74.Option: The return of infection after treatment discontinuation suggests that the antibiotic resistance is developing in the chlamydial population.
Evidence: In the 24 months after the last treatment in 14 of 16 villages, but remained lower than it had been before the first treatment in all 16 villages.

The mean prevalence of infection was reduced from 63.5% at baseline (range 26.3% to 85.7%) to 11.5% six months after the first treatment range (0.0 to 24.4%) as previously described (P<0.0001). The next three biannual distributions continued to reduce the prevalence to 2.6% (range 0.0% to 7.0%), a significant decrease from after a single treatment (P = 0.0004). From 6 to 24 months after the last treatment (the 24 and 42 month visits), infection had returned to an average of 25.2% (range 2.0% to 70.0%).
74.Rationale: The passage describes the return of infection after treatment discontinuation but does not provide evidence of antibiotic resistance. The fact that infection returned to 25.2% (40% of baseline) suggests a resurgence rather than resistance. If resistance were developing, we would expect the infection to return to baseline levels or higher, not a fraction of the original prevalence. The study does not mention any changes in treatment efficacy or resistance patterns, so this inference cannot be drawn from the provided information.

74.Option: Infection may never return if it is brought to a low enough level, analogous to the Allee effect found in population biology.
Evidence: In modestly infected communities, infection may then remain low[5].
In fact, in some areas, infectious trachoma is decreasing even in the absence of treatment[7],[8],[9].

The mean prevalence of infection was reduced from 63.5% at baseline (range 26.3% to 85.7%) to 11.5% six months after the first treatment range (0.0 to 24.4%) as previously described (P<0.0001). The next three biannual distributions continued to reduce the prevalence to 2.6% (range 0.0% to 7.0%), a significant decrease from after a single treatment (P = 0.0004). From 6 to 24 months after the last treatment (the 24 and 42 month visits), infection had returned to an average of 25.2% (range 2.0% to 70.0%).
74.Rationale: The passage mentions that in modestly infected communities, infection may remain low, and that in some areas, trachoma is decreasing without treatment. However, in the study's 16 villages, even after reducing infection to 2.6%, it returned to 25.2% after 24 months. This directly contradicts the claim that infection may never return if brought to a low enough level. The Allee effect, which refers to a critical population threshold below which a population cannot sustain itself, is not mentioned or implied in the passage. The observed resurgence of infection after treatment discontinuation suggests that the Allee effect does not apply in this context.

74.Option: Even after four biannual distributions of azithromycin, infection predictably returns in trachoma-endemic communities, indicating that mass antibiotic distributions cannot be discontinued without risk of resurgence.
Evidence: In the 24 months after the last treatment in 14 of 16 villages, but remained lower than it had been before the first treatment in all 16 villages.

The mean prevalence of infection was reduced from 63.5% at baseline (range 26.3% to 85.7%) to 11.5% six months after the first treatment range (0.0 to 24.4%) as previously described (P<0.0001). The next three biannual distributions continued to reduce the prevalence to 2.6% (range 0.0% to 7.0%), a significant decrease from after a single treatment (P = 0.0004). From 6 to 24 months after the last treatment (the 24 and 42 month visits), infection had returned to an average of 25.2% (range 2.0% to 70.0%).
The 16 villages varied widely in their pre-treatment and post-treatment prevalence, and in how rapidly infection returned after treatments had been discontinued (Figure 2).
74.Rationale: The passage provides clear evidence that infection returns after treatment discontinuation. Even after four biannual distributions, which reduced the mean prevalence to 2.6%, infection returned to 25.2% within 24 months. This pattern was observed in 14 of 16 villages, indicating a predictable resurgence. The fact that the prevalence remained lower than baseline levels suggests that the treatments had some long-term effect, but the return of infection demonstrates that mass antibiotic distributions cannot be discontinued without the risk of resurgence. This inference is directly supported by the data presented in the study.

76.Option: The higher total costs associated with P. vivax infections compared to other Plasmodium species may be explained by longer treatment duration, more frequent clinic visits, and higher caregiver involvement due to the younger age profile of affected patients.
Evidence: P. vivax patients presented with the highest total costs at $199.05 (SD=173.87; Table 4). In contrast to other malaria species, most of the P. vivax costs were direct costs (63%) where hospital and clinic out-of-pocket costs were double the direct costs of malaria due to other Plasmodium species (Table E in S1 Text).
76.Rationale: The passage states that P. vivax had the highest total costs, but attributes this to higher direct costs (63% of total) related to hospital and clinic out-of-pocket expenses, not to longer treatment duration, more frequent clinic visits, or caregiver involvement. The option incorrectly attributes the cost difference to factors not mentioned in the passage. Additionally, the passage does not mention the age profile of P. vivax patients as a contributing factor to higher costs.

76.Option: The use of standardized wage estimates from national surveys to value productivity losses resulted in a 78% increase in total cost per episode, indicating that the base case underestimates the true economic burden of malaria on households.
Evidence: When using the Malaysia household inequities survey for Sabah was utilized (US$389.21 per month) [31], the total cost increased by 78% (US$236.03) when applied to all adults in Scenario 4 and 104% (US$270.41) when applied to all patients in Scenario 5.
76.Rationale: The passage states that using standardized wage estimates from national surveys (specifically the Malaysia Household Inequities Survey) resulted in a 78% increase in total cost per episode. However, the passage does not state that the base case underestimates the true economic burden. The base case uses self-reported wages with imputation for missing data, which may be more accurate for the specific population. The increase in cost with standardized wage estimates could reflect different assumptions rather than an underestimation of the true burden.

76.Option: Households in Sabah face a disproportionate financial burden from malaria due to the high prevalence of zoonotic P. knowlesi transmission among working-age males in rural agricultural areas, which increases both direct and indirect costs.
Evidence: P. knowlesi is most prevalent in males aged between 20 and 40 years in Malaysia, who are the most commonly reported demographic in formal employment [15,18,19].

More than half of P. knowlesi cases nationally have been attributed to agriculture and plantation-based work activities, with many of the remaining cases linked to timber logging and forestry work [22].
The mean total household cost per malaria episode was US$130.53 (SD=102.24) with a median of $96.11 (IQR=$70.73, $153.01) (Table 3).
Working-age males reported higher indirect costs at US$95.66, compared to US$52.62 for others (p<0.001).
76.Rationale: The passage provides evidence that P. knowlesi is most prevalent in working-age males in rural agricultural areas, and that these cases contribute to higher household costs. The higher indirect costs for working-age males, combined with the high prevalence of P. knowlesi in this demographic, supports the inference that households in Sabah face a disproportionate financial burden due to zoonotic P. knowlesi transmission.

76.Option: Severe malaria cases, all due to P. knowlesi infection, were associated with substantially higher indirect costs, primarily due to longer hospital stays and greater productivity losses among working-age males.
Evidence: The seven severe malaria cases (all P. knowlesi) had 91% higher total costs than non-severe cases (US$239.64 versus US$125.27 respectively, p=0.006) (Table 4), predominantly due to substantially higher indirect costs (US$89.25 mean difference) for severe malaria.

The average number of days lost to illness increased to 13.9 (SD = 15.6) for severe P. knowlesi cases, comparatively higher than non-severe P. knowlesi cases at 8.3 days (SD = 6.5, p = 0.141) and all non-zoonotic malaria (P. falciparum, P. vivax and P. malariae) cases at 10.4 days (SD = 5.80, p = 0.477).
The length of hospitalization for severe P. knowlesi cases (mean = 5.6 days, SD = 1.4) was higher than for non-severe malaria cases due to any species (mean = 3.9, SD = 1.4, p=0.004).
76.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that severe P. knowlesi cases had higher total costs, primarily due to higher indirect costs. The evidence also shows that severe cases had longer hospital stays and greater productivity losses, which aligns with the option's claim about the reasons for higher indirect costs. The focus on working-age males is supported by the fact that P. knowlesi is most prevalent in this demographic.

76.Option: Despite the provision of free health-facility-related costs of malaria care by the Ministry of Health, Malaysia, patients in Sabah faced substantial out-of-pocket costs for a single malaria episode, with a mean of $131, and a large proportion of households experienced catastrophic health expenditure, particularly when using lower income thresholds.
Evidence: Malaysia has free universal access to malaria screening, testing, and treatment through public health facilities [6,7].
The mean total household cost per malaria episode was US$130.53 (SD=102.24) with a median of $96.11 (IQR=$70.73, $153.01) (Table 3).

When using the mean monthly self-reported income value of US$148, direct health expenditure for a single malaria episode accounted for 27% of monthly income. Eighty percent (122/152) of patients reported direct health costs above 10% of monthly income, indicating catastrophic health expenditure.
76.Rationale: The passage confirms that while health-facility-related costs are free, households still incur substantial out-of-pocket costs for a single malaria episode, with a mean of $131. It also states that 80% of patients experienced catastrophic health expenditure when using the self-reported income threshold. This directly supports the option's claim about the financial impact despite free clinical care.

77.Option: Negative perceptions of tsetse traps are prone to change over time, particularly after exposure to the technology and through community education, leading to sustained acceptance and active participation in control efforts.
Evidence: Despite many negative first reactions and attitudes towards traps, participants from both groups expressed that these attitudes are prone to change.

Participants from the HAT− group, for instance reported that, on some occasion their first contact with traps was accompanied by the explanation of the trap purpose or general explanation that traps are beneficial.
This communication mostly happened through personnel carrying out trap deployment or another member of community, who had been informed about it previously.
According to participants this information helped them to put aside associations with supernatural powers.
Being able to observe insects being trapped, which was mostly reported by female participants, also seemed to influence positive attitudes.

Participants from HAT+ villages, communicated changed negative- initial attitudes: 'Before we heard of them [traps] we didn't like them; but now we have got to know them [the traps], we like them (…). We like them because since that time they deployed them this has reduced the number of tsetse flies in the area.'
77.Rationale: The passage provides multiple lines of evidence supporting this inference. First, it explicitly states that negative perceptions are prone to change over time. Second, it describes how exposure to the technology (e.g., observing insects being trapped) and community education (e.g., explanations of trap purpose by deployment personnel) led to positive attitude changes. Third, it shows sustained acceptance in HAT+ villages where initial fear was overcome through these mechanisms. The evidence directly supports the claim that negative perceptions change through exposure and education, leading to sustained acceptance and participation.

77.Option: The geographical proximity of HAT+ and HAT− villages suggests that information about tsetse control programs spreads easily between communities, leading to similar levels of acceptance over time.
Evidence: Villages from HAT+ and HAT− areas lie within 10 kilometres of each other.

Villagers from different villages often mix in the local markets and during social events such as celebrations of country independence, women's day, Christmas; hence the differences in views described below were surprising.
77.Rationale: While the passage notes geographical proximity and social mixing between villages, it explicitly states that 'differences in views described below were surprising,' indicating that information about tsetse control did not lead to similar levels of acceptance. The evidence contradicts the claim that proximity leads to similar acceptance levels. The passage actually suggests that despite proximity, different levels of exposure and cultural context led to divergent responses.

77.Option: The effectiveness of tsetse traps in reducing disease incidence is the main reason for community acceptance, regardless of prior exposure or cultural beliefs.
Evidence: Participants from HAT+ villages, communicated changed negative- initial attitudes: 'Before we heard of them [traps] we didn't like them; but now we have got to know them [the traps], we like them (…). We like them because since that time they deployed them this has reduced the number of tsetse flies in the area.'
The initial fear and suspicion toward tsetse traps in HAT− villages were related to associations with supernatural powers.
77.Rationale: The passage shows that while effectiveness in reducing tsetse flies was a factor in HAT+ villages, it was not the main reason for acceptance in HAT− villages where initial fear was driven by cultural beliefs (e.g., associations with witchcraft). The claim that effectiveness is the 'main reason regardless of prior exposure or cultural beliefs' is not supported by the evidence, which shows cultural beliefs significantly influenced acceptance in HAT− villages.

77.Option: Community willingness to participate in tsetse control is primarily driven by financial incentives, as evidenced by men's expressed interest in government involvement and financial motivation.
Evidence: In both groups women expressed more need for empowerment and requested access to traps more often, while men expressed more need for village decision making, financial motivation and government involvement in tsetse control programs.
Regardless of the group, participants expressed willingness and appeared motivated to be involved in tsetse control programs; although the HAT+ group expressed this willingness more frequently.
77.Rationale: The passage states that willingness to participate was expressed by both groups, with men mentioning financial motivation as one of several factors. However, it does not support the claim that financial incentives are the 'primary' driver. The evidence shows willingness was motivated by multiple factors including community benefit, empowerment, and observed effectiveness, not just financial incentives.

77.Option: The initial fear and suspicion toward tsetse traps in HAT− villages are primarily due to the traps' appearance and color, rather than cultural or supernatural associations.
Evidence: The suspicions and fear were not reported to be in any way related to the appearance of traps, but rather its position next to the river, uncertainty about the person who carried out the deployment and the unknown purpose of this action.
Associations of traps with witchcraft and ghosts were reported by participants in HAT− villages.
77.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that fear in HAT− villages was not due to appearance but rather due to cultural and supernatural associations (e.g., witchcraft, ghosts). The claim that appearance and color were the primary causes is directly contradicted by the evidence provided.

78.Option: The persistence of clinical signs of trachoma after the clearance of C. trachomatis infection indicates that the current WHO guidelines for trachoma elimination are ineffective in preventing long-term ocular damage.
Evidence: The correlation between disease and infection is not particularly strong and tends to weaken as the community prevalence drops [5], [6].

At five years TT was found in 6/456 (1.3%) aged 15 years and over. Two were incident cases (compared with baseline), three had TT at baseline (one of which had received surgery) and one was a new resident at five years.
There was a marked reduction in observed fly-eye contacts at the time of examination in 1 to 9 year olds from 18.0% (95% CI 14.7 to 21.5) at baseline to 1.5% (95% CI 0.5–2.5) at 5 years.
78.Rationale: The passage does not provide evidence that WHO guidelines are ineffective in preventing long-term ocular damage. The persistence of clinical signs after infection clearance is acknowledged as a known phenomenon (weak correlation between disease and infection), but the passage does not attribute this to guideline ineffectiveness. The study actually shows a significant reduction in clinical signs (TF%1–9 decreased by 9%) and TT prevalence (1.3% at 5 years). The reduction in fly-eye contacts and unclean faces also suggests environmental improvements contributed to the decline. The passage does not claim that WHO guidelines failed to prevent damage.

78.Option: The lack of a control group in the study design means that the observed decline in C. trachomatis infection cannot be attributed to mass drug administration, and therefore antibiotic treatment should not be used in low prevalence trachoma settings.
Evidence: The study population was comprised of all the individuals living in a geographically defined area 3km by 4km in size in Upper Saloum District, The Gambia.
Antibiotic treatment coverage at baseline was 83.3% (95% CI: 81.4–85.1) of the study area population and 91.3% (95% CI: 89.0–93.7) of 1 to 9 year olds.
Following MDA the prevalence of C. trachomatis infection declined gradually, dropping below 1% by five years (Figure 2 & Table 5).
78.Rationale: The passage does not state that the study lacked a control group. The study was conducted in a defined geographic area with high treatment coverage (83.3% overall, 91.3% in 1-9 year olds). The observed decline in C. trachomatis infection (from 9.7% to <1%) is presented as evidence of the effectiveness of MDA. The passage does not claim that the decline cannot be attributed to MDA or that antibiotic treatment should be avoided in low prevalence settings. The option misrepresents the study design and conclusions.

78.Option: The observed re-infection episode in two villages shortly after mass antibiotic treatment was associated with travel to a religious festival in Senegal, suggesting that large-scale community gatherings may act as critical hubs for the reintroduction of C. trachomatis in low prevalence settings.
Evidence: In contrast, in villages 1 and 3 the prevalence of infection increased at two months (Table 5). This increase was strongly associated with the travel of most village residents to a religious festival in Senegal one month after receiving treatment, which has been previously reported (OR 12.2, p<0.0001, 95% CI 4.0–44.1) [9].
78.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that the re-infection in villages 1 and 3 was strongly associated with travel to a religious festival in Senegal. This directly supports the conclusion that large-scale community gatherings can act as hubs for reintroducing C. trachomatis in low prevalence settings. The evidence is specific and directly links the observed re-infection to the community gathering.

78.Option: The marked reduction in fly-eye contacts and unclean faces observed during the study suggests that health education on facial cleanliness was the primary driver of the decline in trachoma prevalence, independent of antibiotic treatment.
Evidence: There was a marked reduction in observed fly-eye contacts at the time of examination in 1 to 9 year olds from 18.0% (95% CI 14.7 to 21.5) at baseline to 1.5% (95% CI 0.5–2.5) at 5 years.

Similarly there was a large reduction in the proportion of 1 to 9 year olds with unclean faces (nasal or ocular discharge present): 38.4% (95% CI 34.1 to 42.7) at baseline and 5.0% (95% CI 3.1 to 6.8) at 5 years.
Following MDA the prevalence of C. trachomatis infection declined gradually, dropping below 1% by five years (Figure 2 & Table 5).
78.Rationale: While the passage notes a reduction in fly-eye contacts and unclean faces, it does not claim that these were the primary drivers of the decline in trachoma prevalence. The study implemented multiple components of the SAFE strategy, including MDA, surgery, and environmental improvements (latrine installation). The decline in C. trachomatis infection is explicitly attributed to MDA, not just facial cleanliness. The passage does not provide evidence to conclude that health education was the primary driver independent of antibiotic treatment.

78.Option: Applying the current WHO intervention algorithms in low prevalence trachoma settings may lead to unnecessary rounds of mass drug administration in uninfected communities.
Evidence: The correlation between disease and infection is not particularly strong and tends to weaken as the community prevalence drops [5], [6].

After the two-month follow-up TF%1–9 was found to exceed the 10% treatment threshold in several villages on one or more occasions (Table 4). However, in only 4/12 (33%) of these occasions were cases of chlamydial infection detected in the same village (Table 5).

Conversely, after the two-month follow-up chlamydial infection was detected in samples from several villages on one or more occasions (Table 5). However, TF%1–9 exceeded 10% on only 6/16 (37.5%) of these occasions (Table 4).
78.Rationale: The passage provides direct evidence of a mismatch between clinical signs (TF%1–9) and infection status in low prevalence settings. Specifically, it shows that TF%1–9 exceeded the 10% treatment threshold in several villages without corresponding chlamydial infection (33% of cases), and conversely, chlamydial infection was detected without TF%1–9 exceeding the threshold (37.5% of cases). This directly supports the conclusion that using the current WHO algorithm (based on TF%1–9) in low prevalence settings may lead to unnecessary MDA in uninfected communities.

79.Option: The preference for decentralized care stems from the belief that outpatient treatment is more effective in healing BU lesions than hospital-based care.
Evidence: Effective outpatient antibiotic treatment at early stages reduces wound dressings and avoids skin grafts, which are needed for large ulcerations.
More advanced cases often require long-term hospital treatment of indeterminate duration and physical therapy to prevent disability, amputation, and functional limitations after care.
79.Rationale: The passage does not state that outpatient treatment is believed to be more effective than hospital-based care. Instead, it explains that outpatient treatment is effective at early stages but not for advanced cases. The preference for decentralized care is attributed to other factors such as cost, social risks, and household production of health, not perceived effectiveness.

79.Option: The preference for decentralized BU treatment is primarily due to the greater availability of trained health staff at local health stations compared to centralized hospitals.
Evidence: Decentralized management of BU patients is well established in the Atlantic, Ouémé and Couffo regions of Benin. In these regions, most cases of mild to moderate BU (Category I and II) are treated at health stations staffed by nurses. More serious (category II and III) cases are referred to reference hospitals.
In Ouinhi commune, only one of four health stations are presently treating BU cases.
79.Rationale: The passage does not indicate that local health stations have more trained staff than centralized hospitals. In fact, it notes that only one of four health stations in Ouinhi commune treats BU cases, suggesting limited availability. The preference for decentralized care is not attributed to staff availability but to other factors like cost and social risks.

79.Option: Households prefer decentralized care because hospital-based treatment for BU is often delayed due to long waiting lists and limited bed availability.
Evidence: The mission of peripheral centers, which are state run health stations, is to provide accessible care for simple cases of BU (category 1 and 2). Reference centers, like the Catholic mission hospital of Zagnanado, are in charge severe cases.
Hospital administrators in Allada were especially concerned with child abandonment and noted cases where caretakers suddenly just disappeared.
79.Rationale: The passage does not mention long waiting lists or limited bed availability as reasons for preferring decentralized care. Instead, it focuses on the indirect and opportunity costs, social risks, and household production of health as the main factors. There is no evidence provided about delays due to waiting lists or bed availability.

79.Option: The preference for decentralized BU treatment over hospital care is driven not only by geographical accessibility but also by the need to avoid indirect and opportunity costs, preserve social capital, and mitigate social risks such as rumors about moral impropriety.
Evidence: The enabling factors most commonly referenced in interviews about hospital-based BU care were the indirect costs of “free treatment” such as transportation costs, food and incidental costs (soap, mobile phone credit, etc.), and the opportunity cost of lost labor.
Fear of rumor influenced who was chosen to be a patient caretaker in hospital. Daughters who had not yet reached puberty were preferred.

Decentralized care does not disrupt a household’s daily routine by removing a mother from her household. Decentralized treatment, for all but very severe cases of BU, still enables a woman to do chores and watch her children as well as engage in entrepreneurial activities essential for household survival.

A mother does not have to worry about the quality of sibling care and foster care in her absence. She also does not have to worry about pernicious rumors undermining her own or a daughter’s reputation.
Remaining at home during treatment is less stressful. A woman worries less about abandonment.
79.Rationale: The passage provides multiple pieces of evidence supporting this option. It explicitly states that households prefer decentralized care to avoid indirect and opportunity costs, preserve social capital, and mitigate social risks like rumors. These factors are highlighted as key reasons for the preference, along with geographical accessibility.

79.Option: Households prefer decentralized care because it allows for greater involvement of traditional healers in the treatment process, which strengthens cultural continuity and trust.
Evidence: Studies of BU in West Africa have found that biomedical treatment for BU is often delayed for reasons linked to perceptions of causality, fear of surgery and amputation, and the logistics and costs of seeking “free care,”
Djalil initially planned to send the boy to his grandmother’s house in a nearby village during school holidays to be treated by a traditional healer.
79.Rationale: The passage mentions that some households initially consider traditional healers, but the preference for decentralized care is not attributed to the involvement of traditional healers. Instead, the preference is linked to factors like cost, social risks, and household production of health. The involvement of traditional healers is presented as an alternative, not a reason for preferring decentralized care.

80.Option: The model's conclusion that full access to antivenom is cost-saving is contingent on all snakebite victims in ASEAN countries switching from traditional to conventional treatment, which may not be feasible in remote rural areas.
Evidence: In the full access scenario, all snakebite victims who firstly sought traditional healers when access is now full are assumed to switch to conventional treatment.

However, not all victims with snakebite envenoming in ASEAN countries could access to geographically appropriate antivenoms for many reasons including inadequate supplies of antivenom, inefficient supply chain system, and inappropriate treatment seeking behavior.
80.Rationale: The model does assume that victims who initially seek traditional treatment will switch to conventional treatment in the full access scenario, but this is a modeling assumption rather than a condition for the cost-saving conclusion. The passage states that the model's conclusion of cost-saving is robust across sensitivity analyses, including those that incorporate PTSD and increased logistics costs. The feasibility of treatment switching in remote areas is not addressed in the passage and is an external consideration not mentioned in the evidence.

80.Option: Improving access to antivenom in ASEAN countries would be cost-effective only if antivenom effectiveness exceeds a relative risk of death of 1.19 in Myanmar and 0.35 in Lao PDR.
Evidence: Threshold analyses of antivenom effectiveness and costs of antivenom treatment resulted in an ICER of 0 are shown in S4 Table. The lowest level of antivenom effectiveness, presented as a risk ratio of death in indicated victims who were not treated with antivenom compared to those treated with antivenom, was ranging from 0.35 in Lao PDR to 1.19 in Myanmar.
80.Rationale: The threshold analysis shows that even at these lower levels of antivenom effectiveness, the intervention would still be cost-effective. The option incorrectly states that effectiveness must 'exceed' these values, when in fact the model shows that cost-effectiveness is maintained at these lower thresholds. The passage indicates that the intervention remains cost-effective even with reduced effectiveness, not that it requires exceeding these values.

80.Option: The high burden of snakebite in ASEAN countries, including 243,000 victims and 16,000 deaths annually, underscores the urgent need for increased antivenom production to meet demand.
Evidence: Our previous study estimated that there were approximately 243,000 snakebite victims with 16,000 deaths and 950 amputations from snakebite envenoming in seven ASEAN countries, namely, Malaysia, Thailand, Indonesia, Philippines, Vietnam, Lao PDR, and Myanmar.

However, improving access to antivenoms could not be solely done by increasing the production of antivenoms. It requires a multifaceted approach involving strengthening the whole system surrounding the management of snakebite victims, such as accurate informatics, rigorous regulations of antivenoms, efficient supply chain, rational use of antivenoms, appropriate treatment seeking behaviors, and good governance to support a strong healthcare system.
80.Rationale: While the passage does mention the high burden of snakebite, it explicitly states that increasing antivenom production alone is insufficient. The model's findings emphasize a multifaceted approach rather than simply increasing production. The option incorrectly implies that increased production is the primary solution, which contradicts the passage's emphasis on system-wide improvements.

80.Option: The inclusion of post-traumatic stress disorder (PTSD) in the model significantly increased the incremental cost-effectiveness ratio (ICER) per DALY averted, making the intervention less cost-effective.
Evidence: Scenario analyses were performed by incorporating post-traumatic stress disorder (PTSD) in the model as a mental disability occurring in 8% of victims who survived snakebite envenoming.
Scenario analyses found that the base-case conclusions were robust.
80.Rationale: The passage states that incorporating PTSD into the model did not significantly change the cost-effectiveness conclusions. The option incorrectly claims that it 'significantly increased the ICER' and 'made the intervention less cost-effective,' which is not supported by the evidence. The scenario analyses showed that the conclusions remained robust, indicating that the inclusion of PTSD did not substantially alter the cost-effectiveness.

80.Option: Improving access to snake antivenom from the current level to full access in ASEAN countries would result in cost-saving despite higher direct costs, due to substantial reductions in indirect costs from avoided productivity losses.
Evidence: Full access to antivenom had higher direct costs (2 million USD in Philippines to 50 million USD in Indonesia) but less indirect costs (-1,091 million USD in Indonesia to -28 million USD in Philippines) when compared to current access which resulted in total cost savings of 27 million USD in Philippines to 1,040 million USD in Indonesia.

In total, when compared to current access, full access to snake antivenom in ASEAN countries resulted in 9,362 deaths averted (-59%), 230,075 DALYs averted (-59%), and cost savings of 1.3 billion USD (-53%).
80.Rationale: The passage directly states that full access to antivenom results in cost savings despite higher direct costs, primarily due to substantial reductions in indirect costs from avoided productivity losses. This is explicitly mentioned in the base-case analyses section, where the cost savings are attributed to reduced indirect costs. The option accurately reflects this finding from the model.

81.Option: The FP assay's ability to detect compound binding to Brugia Hsp90 is compromised by high background fluorescence from nematode extracts, which necessitates the use of complex normalization procedures in drug screening.
Evidence: The signal amplified with time, reached equilibrium at 8 h and remained stable thereafter, resulting in an excellent assay window of approximately 200 mP (Figure 1A).

To evaluate the applicability of the 96-well FP assay for HTS, the assay performance parameters S:N and Z' were determined to be 32 and 0.87, respectively (Figure 2D). For FP, a S:N value higher than 8 is recommended for reliable readings. The Z' factor is a parameter for the quality of the assay itself without test compounds. Assays with a Z' factor between 0.5 and 1.0 are considered to be reliable, robust, and suitable for HTS [32].
81.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that the FP assay achieved an excellent assay window of approximately 200 mP and that the signal-to-noise ratio (S:N) was 32, which is well above the recommended threshold of 8 for reliable FP readings. The Z' factor of 0.87 also indicates a robust and reliable assay suitable for high-throughput screening. These data directly contradict the claim that the FP assay is compromised by high background fluorescence requiring complex normalization procedures. The passage does not mention any issues with background fluorescence or the need for complex normalization in the context of drug screening.

81.Option: The FP assay is not suitable for identifying selective inhibitors of Brugia Hsp90 because the structural similarity between Brugia and human Hsp90 prevents meaningful discrimination between the two species.
Evidence: While GA interacted with identical affinity with both human and Brugia species, a good selectivity was noted in the PU-series, with a selectivity ratio of 2, 3.5 and 9 for PU-24FCl, PU-H71 and PU-WS10, respectively (Figure 4C). The 3-fold change in selectivity ratio between PU-H71 and PU-WS10, two agents of almost identical chemical structure, further confirms the sensitivity of the Hsp90 pocket to the cellular environment and suggests that it may be possible to identify molecules that specifically target the parasite Hsp90.

The FP assay developed for tumor cell Hsp90 is applicable to the parasitic nematode Brugia, as demonstrated by specific and dose-dependent inhibition of cy3B-GA binding by known Hsp90 inhibitors and the ability to distinguish between Brugia and human Hsp90 species.
81.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that the FP assay can distinguish between Brugia and human Hsp90 species, as demonstrated by the selectivity ratios of PU-series compounds. The data show that while GA interacts with both species with identical affinity, the PU-series compounds exhibit varying selectivity ratios, indicating the assay's ability to identify selective inhibitors. This directly contradicts the claim that the FP assay is not suitable for identifying selective inhibitors due to structural similarity between Brugia and human Hsp90. The passage provides evidence that the assay can detect species-specific differences in Hsp90 binding, which is essential for identifying selective inhibitors.

81.Option: The FP assay developed for tumor cell Hsp90 is applicable to the parasitic nematode Brugia, as demonstrated by specific and dose-dependent inhibition of cy3B-GA binding by known Hsp90 inhibitors and the ability to distinguish between Brugia and human Hsp90 species.
Evidence: The FP assay developed for tumor cell Hsp90 [33]. FP is based on the observation that when a relatively small, fast-tumbling fluorescent labeled compound (in this case a fluorescently labeled GA molecule) is excited with plane-polarized light, the emitted light is random with respect to the plane of polarization, resulting in a lower mP value. When the compound is bound to a bigger molecule (in this case Hsp90), the complex tumbles more slowly and the emitted light is polarized, resulting in a higher mP value. Thus, the change in mP reflects the interaction between the labeled compound (i.e. fluorescent GA) and the protein (i.e. Hsp90). The mP value is proportional to the fraction of bound ligand and the assay is very powerful in measuring real-time protein–inhibitor interactions in solution.

Here we investigated whether the FP assay could be applied to a more complex, multicellular species such as a nematode worm. To investigate this possibility, we prepared extracts from B. pahangi and from the GA-insensitive nematode C. elegans, and probed the affinity of the fluorescent GA molecule, cy3B-GA, for the Hsp90 species characteristic of these two nematodes (Figure 1).

While GA interacted with identical affinity with both human and Brugia species, a good selectivity was noted in the PU-series, with a selectivity ratio of 2, 3.5 and 9 for PU-24FCl, PU-H71 and PU-WS10, respectively (Figure 4C). The 3-fold change in selectivity ratio between PU-H71 and PU-WS10, two agents of almost identical chemical structure, further confirms the sensitivity of the Hsp90 pocket to the cellular environment and suggests that it may be possible to identify molecules that specifically target the parasite Hsp90.

To evaluate the applicability of the 96-well FP assay for HTS, the assay performance parameters S:N and Z' were determined to be 32 and 0.87, respectively (Figure 2D). For FP, a S:N value higher than 8 is recommended for reliable readings. The Z' factor is a parameter for the quality of the assay itself without test compounds. Assays with a Z' factor between 0.5 and 1.0 are considered to be reliable, robust, and suitable for HTS [32].
81.Rationale: The passage provides multiple lines of evidence supporting the applicability of the FP assay to Brugia. First, the FP assay was successfully adapted to detect Hsp90 binding in Brugia extracts, as demonstrated by the high affinity of cy3B-GA for Brugia Hsp90 and the low affinity for C. elegans Hsp90. Second, the assay demonstrated specific and dose-dependent inhibition of cy3B-GA binding by known Hsp90 inhibitors like GA, radicicol, and PU-series compounds. Third, the assay was able to distinguish between Brugia and human Hsp90 species, as evidenced by the selectivity ratios of PU-series compounds. Finally, the assay met the criteria for high-throughput screening with a high S:N ratio and a Z' factor of 0.87. These findings collectively support the conclusion that the FP assay is applicable to Brugia and can identify selective inhibitors.

For FP, a S:N value higher than 8 is recommended for reliable readings. The Z' factor is a parameter for the quality of the assay itself without test compounds. Assays with a Z' factor between 0.5 and 1.0 are considered to be reliable, robust, and suitable for HTS [32].
81.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that the FP assay does not require the production of purified recombinant protein, which directly contradicts the claim that the high-throughput screening capability is limited by this requirement. 86.Option: The melioidosis transmission mode in this endemic area includes environmental aerosol inhalation, as evidenced by the molecular match between B. pseudomallei isolates from contaminated soil, aerosols, and human patients, along with the correlation between aerosol DNA concentration and northwesterly winds during the typhoon season.
Evidence: In humans, a melioidosis outbreak was previously linked to drinking water contaminated with B. pseudomallei [15].
Subcutaneous inoculation is recognized as a major transmission mode because farmers have an occupational risk of melioidosis [16].
Additionally, some melioidosis patients exhibit obvious skin wounds, and athletes can acquire melioidosis from contact with contaminated soil while playing sports [17–18].

Travelers can develop melioidosis (with pneumonia) via inhalation during a helicopter flight [12]. This aerosolization of dry dust associated with the melioidosis incidence was identified in helicopter crews after the Vietnam War [19]. According to epidemiological evidence, pulmonary melioidosis can be acquired by inhalation after heavy rainfalls and strong winds [6,11]. In animal experiments, mice that inhaled aerosols contaminated with B. pseudomallei developed severe inflammation and neutrophil infiltration in the lungs, which mimicked the symptoms of human pulmonary melioidosis [20].

The melioidosis incidence in this endemic area is approximately 10–20 cases per 100,000 persons, which is similar to the annual incidence in Thailand (7.98–21.3 cases per 100,000 persons) and northern Australia (5.4–24.2 cases per 100,000 persons) [22–23].
The incidence of melioidosis (over 70% manifested with pulmonary melioidosis) was associated with the appearance of heavy rainfalls and strong winds such as typhoons [11].
Previously, we demonstrated that aerosols containing B. pseudomallei-specific DNA appeared during the typhoon season but not during the dry season (in which no typhoon occurred) [24].

This endemic area comprises hilly regions in the south and cropped fields in the north [21]. Clusters of melioidosis cases have appeared north of the hills, as far as the cropped fields (approximately 5 km away) [11]. Because a strong northwest wind originating from a counterclockwise cyclone occurred during the typhoon season, we hypothesized that the contaminated aerosols were carried by northwest winds from cropped fields contaminated with B. pseudomallei and enveloped the endemic area during the typhoon season. The number of melioidosis cases was associated with contaminated aerosol inhalation during typhoon seasons due to pathogen-carrying winds. To test this hypothesis, we integrated relevant data, which showed that B. pseudomallei was geographically distributed in northwestern cropped soil, that aerosols contaminated with B. pseudomallei-specific DNA were increased during the typhoon season, that the DNA concentration was related to the incidence of melioidosis and the appearance of a northwesterly wind and that aerosol isolates were genetically identical to human isolates.

The molecular association among soil, aerosol and human isolates was determined using MLST and PFGE. According to the MLST analysis, 6 ST types (ST58, ST704, ST834, ST1001, ST1115 and ST1354) appeared among the strains (S4 Table). Both ST58 and ST1001 were commonly identified in environmental isolates, whereas only ST58 was identified in human isolates. According to PFGE analysis, the soil isolates were mainly categorized into Groups Ia, Ib, Ic and II, and the human isolates were only grouped into Ia (S2 Fig). All analyzed human or aerosol isolates were categorized as PFGE Type Ia (or ST58 by MLST) (Table 3). The molecular type PFGE Ia (ST58) linked the soil isolates in the superficial layer to aerosols and human samples.

The distinct ST types (Taiwanese ST types, shown as green circles; Thai ST types, shown as red circles or localized at the region in pink; Australian ST types, localized at the region in blue) that were identified using eBurst (v3) analysis are shown. ST1354 (singleton) is a novel ST type, shown on the left.

According to a multilevel analysis with a time series and auto-regression, the B. pseudomallei-specific DNA concentration in aerosols was positively correlated with the wind direction (300°-360°, northwesterly to northern wind) (p<0.05) and wind speed of the northwesterly wind (p<0.05) in site G (Table 2).
86.Rationale: The passage provides strong evidence for environmental aerosol inhalation as a transmission mode for melioidosis in this endemic area. The molecular evidence shows that B. pseudomallei isolates from contaminated soil, aerosols, and human patients are genetically identical, indicating a direct transmission link. The correlation between aerosol DNA concentration and northwesterly winds during the typhoon season further supports this conclusion. The study's findings, including the integration of molecular, environmental, and climatic data, demonstrate that contaminated aerosols are carried by northwesterly winds from cropped fields to the endemic area, leading to melioidosis cases. This option correctly summarizes the evidence presented in the passage and is the most accurate inference based on the data.

86.Option: The presence of B. pseudomallei in aerosols is primarily due to contamination from urban green areas and city parks, as these environments support pathogen survival and generate airborne particles during windy conditions.
Evidence: B. pseudomallei was not found in urban grounds or urban green areas. Additionally, B. pseudomallei was not widely distributed in rural areas; rather, it was isolated from cropped fields (n = 4) or wetted but non-cropped fields (n = 2) (S2 Table). All positive isolation sites were located in the northern or northwestern outskirts (ca. 300°-360°) of the core region.

The molecular association among soil, aerosol and human isolates was determined using MLST and PFGE. According to the MLST analysis, 6 ST types (ST58, ST704, ST834, ST1001, ST1115 and ST1354) appeared among the strains (S4 Table). Both ST58 and ST1001 were commonly identified in environmental isolates, whereas only ST58 was identified in human isolates. According to PFGE analysis, the soil isolates were mainly categorized into Groups Ia, Ib, Ic and II, and the human isolates were only grouped into Ia (S2 Fig). All analyzed human or aerosol isolates were categorized as PFGE Type Ia (or ST58 by MLST) (Table 3). The molecular type PFGE Ia (ST58) linked the soil isolates in the superficial layer to aerosols and human samples.

The distinct ST types (Taiwanese ST types, shown as green circles; Thai ST types, shown as red circles or localized at the region in pink; Australian ST types, localized at the region in blue) that were identified using eBurst (v3) analysis are shown. ST1354 (singleton) is a novel ST type, shown on the left.
86.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that B. pseudomallei was not found in urban grounds or urban green areas. Instead, the pathogen was isolated from cropped fields and wetted but non-cropped fields in the northern or northwestern outskirts of the core region. The molecular evidence shows that the genetic type PFGE Ia (ST58) links soil isolates in the superficial layer to aerosols and human samples, indicating that the source of contaminated aerosols is the cropped fields, not urban green areas. The option incorrectly attributes the presence of B. pseudomallei in aerosols to urban green areas and city parks, which is directly contradicted by the evidence presented in the passage.

87.Option: The use of DDT in swamps and sewers during the 1940s and 1950s was a major contributor to the decline of Ae. aegypti in Brisbane, as it effectively eliminated larval habitats and adult populations across the city.
Evidence: The passage does not mention the use of DDT or any chemical insecticides in swamps and sewers during the 1940s and 1950s in Brisbane.
The focus of the passage is on regulatory enforcement, rainwater tank compliance, and surveillance as key factors in the elimination of Ae. aegypti.
87.Rationale: The passage provides no evidence of DDT use in Brisbane during the specified period. The focus is on regulatory measures, rainwater tank compliance, and surveillance as the primary factors in the elimination of Ae. aegypti. The option introduces an unmentioned intervention (DDT) and cannot be derived from the passage body.

87.Option: The elimination of Aedes aegypti from Brisbane was likely driven primarily by the removal of non-compliant rainwater tanks as larval habitat, supported by sustained regulatory enforcement and public compliance.
Evidence: The passage states that rainwater tanks were a key larval habitat for Ae. aegypti and that regulations were introduced in 1911 to prevent mosquitoes from exploiting these tanks.
House-to-house surveys and enforcement of regulations led to a decline in non-compliant tanks, with compliance rates increasing over time.
The estimated number of non-compliant tanks decreased significantly from 1911 to 1971, coinciding with the disappearance of Ae. aegypti.
The probability of the species' presence was calculated as P = 0.042, below the threshold of 0.05, suggesting extinction.
87.Rationale: The passage provides direct evidence that the elimination of non-compliant rainwater tanks, supported by regulatory enforcement and public compliance, was a key factor in the disappearance of Ae. aegypti from Brisbane. The decline in non-compliant tanks and the associated increase in compliance rates are explicitly linked to the species' extinction. The statistical analysis further supports this inference.

87.Option: The improvement of living standards and urban design after the Second World War, including the replacement of backyards with recreational spaces, was the main reason for the disappearance of Ae. aegypti from Brisbane.
Evidence: The passage does not mention any changes in urban design or the replacement of backyards with recreational spaces as a factor in the disappearance of Ae. aegypti.
The focus is on regulatory enforcement, rainwater tank compliance, and surveillance as the primary factors.
87.Rationale: The option introduces a factor (urban design changes) that is not mentioned in the passage. The passage focuses on regulatory measures, rainwater tank compliance, and surveillance as the primary factors in the elimination of Ae. aegypti. This option cannot be derived from the evidence provided.

87.Option: The decline in dengue transmission after 1943 was primarily due to the natural reduction in mosquito populations caused by climatic changes, rather than public health interventions.
Evidence: The passage states that the decline in dengue transmission after 1943 was associated with increased regulatory enforcement and surveillance efforts.
The probability of Ae. aegypti's presence was calculated as P = 0.042, below the threshold of 0.05, suggesting extinction due to public health interventions.
The passage emphasizes the role of regulatory enforcement and compliance in reducing mosquito populations.
87.Rationale: The passage directly contradicts this option by attributing the decline in dengue transmission and the disappearance of Ae. aegypti to public health interventions, including regulatory enforcement and surveillance. There is no evidence in the passage to support the claim that climatic changes were the primary factor.

87.Option: The introduction of a reliable reticulated water supply in 1954 coincided with the disappearance of Ae. aegypti from Brisbane, suggesting that the replacement of rainwater tanks with piped water was the primary factor in the species' elimination.
Evidence: The passage states that the introduction of a large water reservoir in 1954 led to a consistent decline in the mean number of rainwater tanks per dwelling, from over 1 per dwelling in 1912 to less than 1 in 10 in 1971.
The estimated number of non-compliant tanks decreased significantly after 1954, coinciding with the disappearance of Ae. aegypti.
The probability of the species' presence was calculated as P = 0.042, below the threshold of 0.05, suggesting extinction.
87.Rationale: The passage provides evidence that the introduction of a reliable reticulated water supply in 1954 led to a significant reduction in the number of rainwater tanks, which in turn reduced the availability of larval habitats for Ae. aegypti. This reduction in larval habitats is directly linked to the species' elimination. The statistical analysis further supports this inference.

88.Option: The use of itraconazole in PCM patients may have protected them from severe SARS-CoV-2 infection by reducing viral replication rates, explaining the absence of post-COVID-19 PCM cases.
Evidence: All PCM patients were routinely screened for tuberculosis (TB) including smear microscopy (Ziehl-Neelsen stain), culture (Lowenstein-Jensen method), and Xpert MTB/RIF from clinical specimens.
The clinical aspects and possible risk factors for PCM and COVID-19 co-infection are further discussed.
88.Rationale: The passage does not mention any protective effect of itraconazole against SARS-CoV-2. It only states that one patient was treated with itraconazole and trimethoprim/sulfamethoxazole for 35 months due to poor adherence. The absence of post-COVID-19 PCM cases is not discussed in the passage, and there is no evidence provided to support the claim that itraconazole reduces viral replication rates or protects against severe SARS-CoV-2 infection.

88.Option: The high incidence of acute PCM in urban areas of Rio de Janeiro may be directly linked to increased susceptibility to SARS-CoV-2 infection due to immune system alterations in acute PCM patients.
Evidence: Rio de Janeiro state is an important endemic region for this mycosis, and there has recently been an increase in the incidence of acute PCM forms in urban areas.
The acute “juvenile” cases of this mycosis [1].
88.Rationale: While the passage mentions an increase in acute PCM in urban areas of Rio de Janeiro, it does not establish a direct link between this increase and increased susceptibility to SARS-CoV-2 infection. The passage also does not discuss immune system alterations in acute PCM patients or their impact on SARS-CoV-2 susceptibility. The inference about immune system alterations leading to increased susceptibility is not supported by the evidence provided.

88.Option: Co-infection with SARS-CoV-2 and Paracoccidioides manifests in mild to lethal outcomes with increased odds of poor outcomes when the lungs are compromised due to PCM.
Evidence: In the period of study, we identified six patients with COVID-19 among 117 patients evaluated PCM, including the patient we previously reported [7].

Three patients presented with mild symptoms of COVID-19, including low-grade fever, headache, and other common cold symptoms (cough, sneezing and nasal discharge). The other three patients required hospitalization due to hypoxia.

One patient had acute PCM under treatment with pulmonary involvement and we previously reported this case [7]. At the time that we published the description of his case (August 2020) this patient was still hospitalized. Although recovered from acute disease, he subsequently developed a dilated cardiomyopathy as a complication of COVID-19 after discharge, but recovered and was lost follow-up after 17 months of PCM treatment.

The other patient with severe COVID-19 had chronic PCM under post-therapeutic follow-up with severe pulmonary fibrosis, chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD), and, despite treatment in the ICU with mechanical ventilation, corticosteroids, and antibiotics, died four days after admission due to rapid decompensation due to complications of COVID-19.
88.Rationale: The passage provides evidence that co-infection with SARS-CoV-2 and Paracoccidioides can result in a range of outcomes from mild to lethal. Specifically, three patients had mild symptoms, while three required hospitalization, with two of those requiring invasive mechanical ventilation and one dying. The patient with chronic PCM and severe pulmonary fibrosis/COPD died from complications of COVID-19, indicating that lung compromise due to PCM increases the risk of poor outcomes. This directly supports the inference that co-infection can lead to mild to lethal outcomes with increased odds of poor outcomes when the lungs are compromised.

88.Option: The absence of new case reports of PCM and COVID-19 co-infection is primarily due to the high rate of vaccination among PCM patients, which reduced the risk of SARS-CoV-2 transmission.
Evidence: The confounders of overlapping clinical manifestations may explain in part the absence of new case reports of PCM and COVID-19 co-infection.
88.Rationale: The passage attributes the absence of new case reports of PCM and COVID-19 co-infection to the confounders of overlapping clinical manifestations, not to high vaccination rates among PCM patients. There is no mention of vaccination rates or their impact on SARS-CoV-2 transmission in the context of PCM patients. Therefore, the inference about vaccination being the primary reason is not supported by the evidence provided.

88.Option: The immune dysregulation and inflammation occurring in COVID-19 likely leads to a higher risk of symptomatic disease and reactivation of endemic mycoses in individuals with previously latent infections.
Evidence: The confounders of overlapping clinical manifestations may explain in part the absence of new case reports of PCM and COVID-19 co-infection.
The clinical aspects and possible risk factors for PCM and COVID-19 co-infection are further discussed.
88.Rationale: The passage does not discuss immune dysregulation or inflammation in the context of reactivation of endemic mycoses. It mentions the overlap in clinical manifestations between PCM and COVID-19 but does not provide evidence that immune dysregulation from COVID-19 leads to reactivation of latent infections. The inference about immune dysregulation leading to reactivation is not supported by the evidence provided.

89.Option: Stigma associated with ZCL is primarily rooted in the belief that the disease is contagious and poses a public health threat to others.
Evidence: The respondents mentioned the human responsibility for the environmental changes.
The majority considered that ZCL is not hereditary (80%). It was not related to the quality of food (92%), or stress in everyday life (78%).
73% linked the environment (stagnant water) and physical activity (working alongside animals) with the incidence of ZCL.
89.Rationale: The passage does not provide evidence that stigma is rooted in the belief that ZCL is contagious or a public health threat. Instead, it shows that participants attributed ZCL to environmental factors and physical activity, not contagion. The belief that ZCL is not hereditary or related to food or stress further contradicts the idea of contagion as a primary source of stigma. The passage focuses on environmental and behavioral factors, not contagion, as the basis for stigma.

89.Option: The number of facial and body scars is the primary determinant of the level of stigma experienced by ZCL patients, regardless of their location or perception of the disease.
Evidence: There is a moderate correlation and just around the significant level between the total number of body scars and 'rejection experiences' (r = 0.31, p<0.05), and between the number of facial scars and the scores on 'Anticipation and avoidance of stress' (r = 0.29, p = 0.06).

It is important to note that this correlation between the number of scars and stigma indices was at the same threshold of significance when checked against age-related variations. Hence, the number of body scars had a strong link to experience with stigma, regardless of a patient’s age.
89.Rationale: While the passage notes a correlation between the number of scars and stigma, it explicitly states that this correlation is at the same threshold of significance as age-related variations. This suggests that the number of scars is not the primary determinant of stigma, but rather one of several factors. The passage also emphasizes the role of age and perception of the disease in stigma, indicating that the number of scars alone is not the primary determinant.

89.Option: The nature of stigma experienced by ZCL patients is primarily driven by anticipated rejection rather than actual experiences of rejection from society.
Evidence: The descriptive data focused more on anticipation of a negative reaction from others and the efforts made to display anticipatory avoidance behaviour meant to deny such reactions, than on convictions from the presence of negative experiences that patients have had through social interactions on the state of their skin (Table 4).

Rejection experiences and the 'anticipation and avoidance of stress' were respectively, significantly and negatively correlated with age (r = -0.33, p<0.05 and r = -0.31, p<0.05). The younger the subject, the higher the number of experiences tied to stigma; and the older the subject, the more such feelings are dismissed.

The results showed that anticipation and avoidance of stress is strongly correlated with the scores on 'Consequences', 'Coherence' and 'Emotional representation.' These results suggest that the more a person looks at the world anticipating segregation and rejection, the more they perceive ZCL as a disease with harmful effects on them, the more they see it as a mysterious and incomprehensible illness, and the more they talk about suffering and emotional difficulties.
89.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that the descriptive data focused more on anticipation of negative reactions and anticipatory avoidance behavior than on actual experiences of rejection. It also provides statistical evidence showing a significant correlation between anticipation and avoidance of stress and stigma indices, particularly among younger subjects. The strong correlation between anticipation of stress and negative perceptions of the disease further supports the conclusion that anticipated rejection is the primary driver of stigma, not actual experiences of rejection.

89.Option: The most significant factor influencing quality of life among ZCL patients is the physical severity of their skin lesions, particularly facial scarring.
Evidence: Quality of life scores were not significantly correlated with either age, education level, time of illness, or the number of facial or body scars (Table 5).

On the other hand, quality of life scores had interesting correlations with PLSI scores on stigma (Figs 3 and 4). The quality of social life score was significantly and negatively correlated to the 'Anticipation and avoidance of stress' score (r = -0.36, p<0.05) suggesting that the more a person anticipates segregation and psychosocial stress, the lower their quality of life was seen to be.
The quality of knowledge seems therefore to be tied closely to the quality of social life (Table 6).
89.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that quality of life scores were not significantly correlated with the number of facial or body scars. Instead, it highlights the significant correlations between quality of life and stigma-related factors, such as anticipation of stress and social life quality. The passage also notes that knowledge of ZCL is closely tied to social life quality, further indicating that physical severity of lesions is not the most significant factor influencing quality of life.

90.Option: The use of serological markers in population screening studies in Mazán could significantly improve the detection of asymptomatic infections compared to light microscopy, thereby enhancing the effectiveness of active case detection programs.
Evidence: Plasmodium vivax causes the majority of malaria cases in Peru. The detection of cases is based on light microscopy (LM) diagnosis; this may substantially underestimate prevalence as greater than 50% of infections that are detectable by qPCR may be missed by microscopy [6]. Being based on the detection of blood-stage parasites, LM and qPCR do not detect individuals with only liver-stage hypnozoite infections. Therefore, detecting transmission changes using LM or qPCR data may overlook P. vivax ultra-low-density infections [11] and hypnozoite carriers, thus underestimating transmission levels.

New uses of malaria serological tools have been proposed to inform the impact of such control measures [12]. Ideally, the detection of short-lived antibody responses in a given population’s inhabitants will indicate that recent exposure has happened. Recently, new serological markers of exposure to P. vivax have been developed and validated for their use in low transmission settings [13]. These serological markers of exposure were able to detect recent exposure in participants from longitudinal cohorts of road and riverine communities with moderate-to-high transmission of the Peruvian Amazon, although performance was notably lower than in low endemic settings [14].

Mazán and Iquitos are located within the Maynas province, in the department of Loreto. The ICEMR study surveyed seven communities from the Mazán district: Gaminatacocha (GC), Libertad (LI), Primero de Enero (PE), Puerto Alegre (PA), Salvador (SA), Urco Miraño (UM) and Lago Yuracyacu (LY). The CAM study surveyed three communities from Iquitos: Rumococha (RC), Santo Tomás (ST) and Quistococha (QC). Each circle represents a household location. The ICEMR study surveyed seven communities from the Mazán district: Gaminatacocha (GC), Libertad (LI), Primero de Enero (PE), Puerto Alegre (PA), Salvador (SA), Urco Miraño (UM) and Lago Yuracyacu (LY). P. vivax prevalence by LM in Iquitos was not significantly different from Mazán (1.8%, 16/880, vs 1.3%, 13/1024, p = 0.329). Although P. vivax prevalence by qPCR was higher in Iquitos than in Mazán (5.3%, 47/880, vs 2.7%, 28/1024, p = 0.005); Mazán (56.5%, 579/1024) showed higher seroprevalence than Iquitos (38.2%, 336/880, p < 0.001) (Table2). Only 14% (47/336) and 5% (28/579) of seropositive individuals in Iquitos and Mazán respectively had concurrent, qPCR-detectable infections.

When exploring the prevalence of P. vivax by community, we found high heterogeneity within study sites (Fig 2). Both LM and qPCR prevalence had similar spatial patterns in both study sites (Fig 2A and 2B). For better interpretation of prevalent infections in this study, we refer only to the qPCR data from now on. In Iquitos, Rumococha showed higher PCR prevalence (9.83%, 29/295) than Santo Tomás (4%, 12/298) (p = 0.005) and Quistococha (2%, 6/287) (p < 0.001). Conversely, seroprevalence was higher in Santo Tomás (49.3%, 147/298) than Rumococha (35.35%, 104/295) (p < 0.001) and Quistococha (29.6%, 85/287) (p < 0.001).

In Mazán, Libertad had the highest qPCR prevalence among all the communities (8.7%, 16/184), followed by Gamitanacocha (4.2%, 2/48), Puerto Alegre (2.9%, 5/169), Urco Miraño (2.1%, 4/190), and Lago Yuracyacu (1.0%, 1/97). Salvador and Primero de Enero did not have any positive qPCR sample in the survey (0%). On the other hand, Gamitanacocha was the community with higher seroprevalence (87.5%, 42/48), followed by Libertad with 75% (138/184) of seroprevalence, Primero de Enero (69%, 40/58), Urco Miraño (61.0%, 116/190), Puerto Alegre (48.5%, 82/169), Salvador (47.1%, 131/278) and Lago Yuracyacu (30.9%, 30/97).
90.Rationale: The passage provides multiple lines of evidence supporting this conclusion. First, it establishes that P. vivax is the primary malaria-causing parasite in Peru and that light microscopy (LM) significantly underestimates prevalence compared to qPCR, missing over 50% of infections. Second, it explicitly states that serological markers can detect recent exposure and have been validated for use in low transmission settings. Third, the study in Mazán (a riverine community) showed much higher seroprevalence (56.5%) compared to qPCR-detectable infections (2.7%), indicating that serology detects a much larger proportion of infections. The data also show that only 5% of seropositive individuals in Mazán had concurrent qPCR-detectable infections, suggesting that serology is identifying a large number of asymptomatic or low-density infections not captured by traditional methods. This directly supports the inference that serological markers could significantly improve detection of asymptomatic infections in Mazán, thereby enhancing active case detection programs.

Although P. vivax prevalence by qPCR was higher in Iquitos than in Mazán (5.3%, 47/880, vs 2.7%, 28/1024, p = 0.005); Mazán (56.5%, 579/1024) showed higher seroprevalence than Iquitos (38.2%, 336/880, p < 0.001) (Table2). When exploring the prevalence of P. vivax by community, we found high heterogeneity within study sites (Fig 2). Both LM and qPCR prevalence had similar spatial patterns in both study sites (Fig 2A and 2B). For better interpretation of prevalent infections in this study, we refer only to the qPCR data from now on. In Iquitos, Rumococha showed higher PCR prevalence (9.83%, 29/295) than Santo Tomás (4%, 12/298) (p = 0.005) and Quistococha (2%, 6/287) (p < 0.001). Conversely, seroprevalence was higher in Santo Tomás (49.3%, 147/298) than Rumococha (35.35%, 104/295) (p < 0.001) and Quistococha (29.6%, 85/287) (p < 0.001).

In Mazán, Libertad had the highest qPCR prevalence among all the communities (8.7%, 16/184), followed by Gamitanacocha (4.2%, 2/48), Puerto Alegre (2.9%, 5/169), Urco Miraño (2.1%, 4/190), and Lago Yuracyacu (1.0%, 1/97). Salvador and Primero de Enero did not have any positive qPCR sample in the survey (0%). On the other hand, Gamitanacocha was the community with higher seroprevalence (87.5%, 42/48), followed by Libertad with 75% (138/184) of seroprevalence, Primero de Enero (69%, 40/58), Urco Miraño (61.0%, 116/190), Puerto Alegre (48.5%, 82/169), Salvador (47.1%, 131/278) and Lago Yuracyacu (30.9%, 30/97).
90.Rationale: The passage does not provide evidence that serological markers are more sensitive in remote areas. The higher seroprevalence in Mazán compared to Iquitos is explained by the study design and transmission patterns, not by the sensitivity of the test in different geographic contexts. The passage states that serological markers were able to detect recent exposure in moderate-to-high transmission settings but performed notably lower in low endemic settings. The fact that Mazán is a riverine community and Iquitos is peri-urban does not establish that serology is more sensitive in remote areas. The higher seroprevalence in Mazán is likely due to higher transmission intensity rather than test sensitivity. The option incorrectly infers that the geographic remoteness of Mazán is the reason for the higher seroprevalence detected by serology, when the passage provides no evidence for this causal relationship.

90.Option: Because seropositivity is strongly associated with outdoor occupation in Mazán, serological markers should be used to target interventions specifically toward individuals engaged in forest-related work.
Evidence: In Mazán, the risk of being seropositive to P. vivax increased with increasing age (adjusted odds ratio, aOR5-14.9 years = 3.27; 95% CI 1.69–6.31; p < 0.001; aOR15-29 years = 13.68; 95% CI 5.78–32.38; p < 0.001; aOR30-49.9 years = 20.58; 95% CI 8.18–51.79; p < 0.001; aOR≥50 years = 28.34; 95% CI 11.05–72.68; p < 0.001), being male (aOR = 1.72; 95% CI 1.17–2.51; p = 0.01) and working outside (aOR = 2.51; 95% CI 1.43–4.41; p < 0.001). Having domestic animals was associated with a low risk of being seropositive (aOR = 0.59; 95% CI 0.35–0. 98; p = 0.04). There was no association with fever symptom and seropositivity, as well with the travel history in the last month and the type of house (S4 Table). The high ICC value for community, in contrast to households, indicated that seropositivity of participants was mostly affected by community factors or that there was homogeneity within communities, e.g., closeness to water bodies. This possibly indicates a more uniform and stable local transmission pattern (S5 Table).

The association of epidemiological factors with P. vivax exposure (binary) and P. vivax parasitaemia (binary) by qPCR were assessed by mixed-effects logistic regression models with random intercepts per households and communities. The following potential risk factors were assessed as fixed effects: age, sex, fever, travels in the last month, outdoor occupation, education level, livestock inside dwelling, indoor spray, and housing type. Factors in final models were selected by manual backward elimination guided by the minimisation of the Akaike information criterion. Three levels were included in these models: individuals within households, households within communities and communities. Intra-class correlation (ICC) was estimated as a measure of correlation and homogeneity at each level [24]. The ICC quantifies the degree of homogeneity of the outcome within clusters. If ICC = 1, there is no within-class variation. If ICC = 0, the observations do not depend on cluster membership. The lme4 R package was utilised for this analysis [25]. Statistical analysis was done using R 3.4.3 (https://www.r-project.org/).

In the district of Mazán, a representative population-based cross-sectional survey was conducted in July 2018 (end of transmission season), where 1127 individuals were enrolled to participate in the baseline survey by the Amazonia International Center of Excellence in Malaria Research (ICEMR) team [16]. A total of 261 households were sampled in the 6 communities of Mazán described above. Blood samples were collected for LM and qPCR diagnosis (n = 1127). Plasma samples were collected from 1024 individuals for serological investigation (S1 Fig).
The main economic activities are farming, timber extraction and fishing [16].
90.Rationale: The passage provides direct evidence that seropositivity is strongly associated with outdoor occupation in Mazán (aOR = 2.51; 95% CI 1.43–4.41; p < 0.001). This association is presented in the context of a multilevel logistic regression analysis that controlled for other factors. The study also notes that outdoor occupations such as logger, farmer and fisher were more common in Mazán (40.9%) than in Iquitos (8.9%) (p < 0.001). Given that serological markers can detect recent exposure and that outdoor occupation is a significant risk factor, it is a logical epidemiological inference that serological markers should be used to target interventions toward individuals engaged in these high-risk occupations. This would allow for more efficient allocation of resources to those most likely to be contributing to or affected by ongoing transmission.

90.Option: Serological markers are most effective in low transmission settings because they can distinguish between recent and short-lived antibody responses and past infections with high accuracy, making them ideal for monitoring the impact of elimination programs.
Evidence: New uses of malaria serological tools have been proposed to inform the impact of such control measures [12]. Ideally, the detection of short-lived antibody responses in a given population’s inhabitants will indicate that recent exposure has happened. Recently, new serological markers of exposure to P. vivax have been developed and validated for their use in low transmission settings [13]. These serological markers of exposure were able to detect recent exposure in participants from longitudinal cohorts of road and riverine communities with moderate-to-high transmission of the Peruvian Amazon, although performance was notably lower than in low endemic settings [14].

The detection of cases is based on light microscopy (LM) diagnosis; this may substantially underestimate prevalence as greater than 50% of infections that are detectable by qPCR may be missed by microscopy [6]. Being based on the detection of blood-stage parasites, LM and qPCR do not detect individuals with only liver-stage hypnozoite infections. Therefore, detecting transmission changes using LM or qPCR data may overlook P. vivax ultra-low-density infections [11] and hypnozoite carriers, thus underestimating transmission levels.
