P. vivax prevalence by LM in Iquitos was not significantly different from Mazán (1.8%, 16/880, vs 1.3%, 13/1024, p = 0.329). Although P. vivax prevalence by qPCR was higher in Iquitos than in Mazán (5.3%, 47/880, vs 2.7%, 28/1024, p = 0.005); Mazán (56.5%, 579/1024) showed higher seroprevalence than Iquitos (38.2%, 336/880, p < 0.001) (Table2). Only 14% (47/336) and 5% (28/579) of seropositive individuals in Iquitos and Mazán respectively had concurrent, qPCR-detectable infections.

When exploring the prevalence of P. vivax by community, we found high heterogeneity within study sites (Fig 2). Both LM and qPCR prevalence had similar spatial patterns in both study sites (Fig 2A and 2B). For better interpretation of prevalent infections in this study, we refer only to the qPCR data from now on. In Iquitos, Rumococha showed higher PCR prevalence (9.83%, 29/295) than Santo Tomás (4%, 12/298) (p = 0.005) and Quistococha (2%, 6/287) (p < 0.001). Conversely, seroprevalence was higher in Santo Tomás (49.3%, 147/298) than Rumococha (35.35%, 104/295) (p < 0.001) and Quistococha (29.6%, 85/287) (p < 0.001).

In Mazán, Libertad had the highest qPCR prevalence among all the communities (8.7%, 16/184), followed by Gamitanacocha (4.2%, 2/48), Puerto Alegre (2.9%, 5/169), Urco Miraño (2.1%, 4/190), and Lago Yuracyacu (1.0%, 1/97). Salvador and Primero de Enero did not have any positive qPCR sample in the survey (0%). On the other hand, Gamitanacocha was the community with higher seroprevalence (87.5%, 42/48), followed by Libertad with 75% (138/184) of seroprevalence, Primero de Enero (69%, 40/58), Urco Miraño (61.0%, 116/190), Puerto Alegre (48.5%, 82/169), Salvador (47.1%, 131/278) and Lago Yuracyacu (30.9%, 30/97).
90.Rationale: The passage actually states that serological markers 'were able to detect recent exposure in participants from longitudinal cohorts of road and riverine communities with moderate-to-high transmission of the Peruvian Amazon, although performance was notably lower than in low endemic settings.' This directly contradicts the claim that serological markers are most effective in low transmission settings. The option incorrectly asserts that serological markers can distinguish between recent and past infections with high accuracy, but the passage does not make this claim. Instead, it notes that the markers detect short-lived antibody responses indicating recent exposure, but does not quantify their accuracy in distinguishing between recent and past infections. The option also misrepresents the data by suggesting that serological markers are ideal for monitoring elimination programs, when the passage only states they are being proposed for this purpose, not that they are proven to be ideal.

90.Option: In areas where transmission has shifted from moderate-to-high to low levels, serological markers of recent exposure are best suited to detect recent transmission in young people, as high antibody levels in this group are more likely to reflect recent infection rather than cumulative lifetime exposure.
Evidence: Seropositive rate increase with age in both study sites but was more pronounced in Mazán

Antibody levels and seropositivity rate increased with age in both study sites (S3 Fig and S2 and S3 Tables). The proportion of seropositive individuals was significantly higher in participants from Mazán than Iquitos in all age groups (age group < 15: 1.72 times higher; age group 15–29.9: 1.92 higher; age group 30–49.9: 1.54 higher; age group 50+: 1.39 higher; p mean < 0.001) (S3 Fig). There was a notable increase in the proportion seropositive people aged > 15 years in both study sites (S3 Fig). This difference was more pronounced in Mazán, where the SEM seropositivity rate in the age group 15–29.9 (0.69, 95% CI 0.60–0.76) was two times higher than in the age group 0–15 (0.31, 95% CI 0.27–0.35) (p = 0.006) (S3 Fig). Antibody titers to individual SEM also showed an age-trend in both sites for all antigens considered (S4 Fig).

Within each study area, villages had different seropositive rate-age patterns (Fig 3). The lowest seropositive rates in participants aged <15 years were observed in Lago Yuracyacu (0.04, 95% CI 0.00–0.11) in Mazán, and Quistococha (0.15, 95% CI 0.08–0.22) in Iquitos. The highest seropositive rates in participants aged <15 years were observed in Gamitanacocha (0.72, 95% CI 0.50–0.88) in Mazán, and Santo Tomás (0.21, 95% CI 0.13–0.31) in Iquitos. Lago Yuracyacu (0.21, 95% CI 0.00–0.43) in Mazán and Rumococha (0.28, 95% CI 0.18–0.40) in Iquitos had the lowest seropositive rate in participants aged 15–29.9 years, respectively. Seropositivity rate was 0.93 in participants aged 15–29.9 years from the village of Libertad (0.93, 95% CI 0.82–1.00), and 0.42 in Santo Tomás (0.42, 95% CI 0.31–0.53) (Fig 3).

Factors in final models were selected by manual backward elimination guided by the minimisation of the Akaike information criterion. Three levels were included in these models: individuals within households, households within communities and communities. Intra-class correlation (ICC) was estimated as a measure of correlation and homogeneity at each level [24]. The ICC quantifies the degree of homogeneity of the outcome within clusters. If ICC = 1, there is no within-class variation. If ICC = 0, the observations do not depend on cluster membership. The lme4 R package was utilised for this analysis [25]. Statistical analysis was done using R 3.4.3 (https://www.r-project.org/).

In the district of Mazán, a representative population-based cross-sectional survey was conducted in July 2018 (end of transmission season), where 1127 individuals were enrolled to participate in the baseline survey by the Amazonia International Center of Excellence in Malaria Research (ICEMR) team [16]. 90.Rationale: The passage provides evidence that seropositivity increases with age in both study sites, but is more pronounced in Mazán. This age-related increase suggests that older individuals have accumulated more lifetime exposure, while younger individuals with high seropositivity are more likely to have recent infections. The study specifically notes that in Mazán, the seropositivity rate in the 15-29.9 age group was two times higher than in the <15 age group (p = 0.006). This directly supports the inference that in areas where transmission has decreased (as indicated by the lower seroprevalence in younger age groups), high antibody levels in young people are more likely to reflect recent infection rather than cumulative exposure. The data also show that in some communities, young people had very low seropositivity rates, indicating that high antibody levels in this group would be more indicative of recent transmission.

92.Option: Continuity of patient care could be supported by funding a specialist RHD nurse who is capable of answering clinical questions.
Evidence: Most participants were aware that Pacific people were at higher risk of ARF but lacked confidence in the Victorian healthcare system’s capacity to detect cases (except at RCH), and facilitate ongoing case management.

A lack of coordination between treating physicians and restrictions around sharing medical records were identified as barriers to patients transitioning from paediatric to adult care systems without being lost to follow up, and as barriers to primary and tertiary healthcare providers liaising effectively.

Several interviewed clinicians suggested that ensuring patients’ GPs were highly engaged in their treatment. They also postulated that continuing to involve family members in the patient’s care as they transitioned into the adult care system could reduce loss to follow up, and improve the quality of care.
92.Rationale: While the passage mentions the need for better coordination and family involvement, it does not specifically mention the role of a specialist RHD nurse. The option is plausible but not directly supported by the evidence in the passage.

92.Option: Clinicians involving the family in patient care even once the patient reaches adulthood may be an acceptable way to support ongoing treatment and monitoring.
Evidence: Continuing to involve family members in the patient’s care as they transitioned into the adult care system could reduce loss to follow up, and improve the quality of care.

For us, with our community and being an Islander, that age [18 years] means nothing. That support will always be there. Either me or my sister will continue …to take him to his appointments [to have BPG injected].

Older patients and caregivers discussed patients taking on more responsibility for their treatment as they approached adulthood. Such responsibilities included organising and attending appointments alone, paying for BPG and having conversations with clinicians. Despite this, caregivers said they would always be there to support their child to receive the treatment they need.
92.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that continuing to involve family members in the patient’s care as they transition into the adult care system could reduce loss to follow up and improve the quality of care. This is directly supported by quotes from both clinicians and caregivers indicating that family support remains important even after the patient reaches adulthood.

92.Option: Introducing a patient register and a specialist RHD nurse to support best practice patient management may help to improve the continuity of care for Pacific patients with ARF/RHD in Victoria.
Evidence: The Australian ARF/RHD guidelines recognise that Pacific people who live in metropolitan households which are affected by crowding and/or lower socioeconomic status have a high risk of ARF.

In Victoria, ARF and RHD were made notifiable to the state Department of Health on 31 July 2023. There is no centralised RHD register in Victoria to provide a straightforward means of monitoring cases’ BPG uptake.

Several interviewed clinicians suggested that ensuring patients’ GPs were highly engaged in their treatment. They also postulated that continuing to involve family members in the patient’s care as they transitioned into the adult care system could reduce loss to follow up, and improve the quality of care.
92.Rationale: The passage mentions the lack of a centralised RHD register in Victoria and the need for better coordination and engagement of GPs. Introducing a patient register and a specialist RHD nurse aligns with the recommendations from clinicians and the Australian guidelines, which emphasize best practice patient management. This intervention directly addresses the identified challenges of continuity of care and coordination between healthcare providers.

92.Option: A well-resourced register would enable patients to maintain their continuity of care when moving between healthcare providers, and routinely collected data could facilitate future assessments of the effectiveness of BPG delivery.
Evidence: In Victoria, ARF and RHD were made notifiable to the state Department of Health on 31 July 2023. There is no centralised RHD register in Victoria to provide a straightforward means of monitoring cases’ BPG uptake.

A lack of coordination between treating physicians and restrictions around sharing medical records were identified as barriers to patients transitioning from paediatric to adult care systems without being lost to follow up, and as barriers to primary and tertiary healthcare providers liaising effectively.

Several interviewed clinicians suggested that ensuring patients’ GPs were highly engaged in their treatment. They also postulated that continuing to involve family members in the patient’s care as they transitioned into the adult care system could reduce loss to follow up, and improve the quality of care.
92.Rationale: The passage highlights the absence of a centralised RHD register in Victoria and the resulting challenges in monitoring BPG uptake and coordinating care. A well-resourced register would address these issues by enabling continuity of care and providing data for future assessments. This is directly supported by the evidence in the passage.

93.Option: The emergence of streptomycin resistance in S19960127 is likely due to the presence of a novel plasmid, pS96127, which was found in both resistant and susceptible strains.
Evidence: The whole genome of Y. pestis S19960127 (sequence accession number CP045636) consists of a 4.63-megabase chromosome and four plasmids, besides three common virulence plasmid pCD1/pYV, pPCP1/pPst, pMT1/ Fra [24,25], a novel 33.9-kb plasmid (named pS96127) in the Y. pestis S19960127 genome was discovered. The novel plasmid pS96127 shared 99.95% identity and 99.98 coverage with the pTP33 plasmid in Y. pestis strain I-2638 (KT020860.1). Apart from S19960127, another 14 strains sensitive to streptomycin isolated in Shannan Prefecture also carried the pS96127 plasmid.
93.Rationale: The passage states that the novel plasmid pS96127 was found in both the resistant strain S19960127 and 14 other susceptible strains. Since the plasmid is present in both resistant and susceptible strains, it cannot be the direct cause of the streptomycin resistance in S19960127. The resistance in S19960127 is instead attributed to a mutation in the rpsL gene, not the plasmid.

93.Option: The high-level streptomycin resistance in Y. pestis S19960127 is due to a conjugative plasmid carrying the strA and strB genes, similar to strains isolated from Madagascar.
Evidence: None of them, including S19960127, carried these genes. Such results suggested that the high level of streptomycin resistance exhibited in strain S19960127 is not due to the presence of conjugative plasmids or self-transferable plasmids like the isolates from Madagascar [5], [6].
93.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that the streptomycin resistance in S19960127 is not due to the presence of conjugative plasmids or self-transferable plasmids carrying the strA and strB genes, which are responsible for resistance in the Madagascar strains. Therefore, this option is incorrect.

93.Option: The treatment failure in Patient C was primarily due to the ineffectiveness of trimethoprim/sulfamethoxazole and tetracycline, rather than streptomycin resistance.
Evidence: Patient C was given unified combination treatment intramuscular injection streptomycin (1 g, twice daily), oral trimethoprim/sulfamethoxazole (1 g, three times daily); oral tetracycline (0.5 g, three times daily), Such treatment lasted eight days and the patient died on 19 August.
A Y. pestis strain (S19960127) was isolated from necropsy organ samples from Patient C, and the strain was identified with streptomycin resistance in this study.
93.Rationale: The passage indicates that the strain isolated from Patient C was resistant to streptomycin, which is the most effective antibiotic for Y. pestis. The treatment included streptomycin, trimethoprim/sulfamethoxazole, and tetracycline. Since the strain was resistant to streptomycin, the primary treatment, the failure is more likely due to streptomycin resistance rather than the ineffectiveness of the other antibiotics. The passage does not provide evidence that trimethoprim/sulfamethoxazole and tetracycline were ineffective in this case.

93.Option: The emergence of streptomycin resistance in Y. pestis S19960127 is likely due to selective pressure from streptomycin use in patients, despite the lack of direct evidence from the patient who received treatment.
Evidence: The strain S19960127 was isolated from a pneumonic patient who received streptomycin treatment as part of a combination therapy. The high-level streptomycin resistance in S19960127 is due to a mutation in the rpsL gene, which is a chromosomal mutation. Chromosomal mutations can arise spontaneously but are more likely to be selected for in the presence of antibiotic pressure. The passage does not provide direct evidence that the patient who received treatment was the source of the resistant strain, but the presence of the resistant strain in a patient who received streptomycin treatment suggests that selective pressure from streptomycin use could have contributed to the emergence of resistance.
93.Rationale: The passage describes the emergence of a streptomycin-resistant strain in a patient who received streptomycin treatment. While the passage does not provide direct evidence that the resistance developed in the patient, the presence of the resistant strain in a patient who was treated with streptomycin suggests that selective pressure from the antibiotic could have led to the emergence of resistance. This is a plausible epidemiological inference based on the data provided.

93.Option: The streptomycin-resistant Y. pestis strain S19960127 likely originated from a spontaneous mutation in the local marmot population, given the high prevalence of plague in the region.
Evidence: The passage does not provide evidence that the strain S19960127 originated from the local marmot population. The strain was isolated from a human patient, and the passage does not mention marmots in the context of this particular strain. The other 14 strains sensitive to streptomycin isolated in Shannan Prefecture also carried the pS96127 plasmid, but this does not indicate that the resistant strain originated from marmots.
93.Rationale: The passage does not provide evidence that the streptomycin-resistant strain S19960127 originated from the local marmot population. The strain was isolated from a human patient, and the passage does not mention marmots in the context of this particular strain. The high prevalence of plague in the region does not necessarily mean that the resistant strain originated from marmots. The resistance is attributed to a chromosomal mutation in the rpsL gene, not to the plasmid or to the host species.

94.Option: The reduction in bug density achieved through the use of B. bassiana-based attraction-infection traps can significantly lower the potential risk of T. cruzi transmission to humans, with multiple interventions potentially reducing the risk to less than one bite per human per night.
Evidence: The number of bites per night was estimated according to [25] as: The relationship between total bug density and the number of infective bugs was estimated according to [27] as: The average number of humans/house was 5. Both, the infected bug density and the number of bites per night were determined in about 40 houses of small rural infested villages (Santiago del Estero and Córdoba provinces, Argentina), of similar construction characteristics as those described in present study [25]. The three early nymph stages were excluded from the total bug density estimation because of their relatively low infection rates [28].
We also estimated the potential T. cruzi transmission risk index after intervention with B. bassiana-based traps.
94.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that the study estimated the potential T. cruzi transmission risk index after intervention with B. bassiana-based traps. The methodology for calculating the number of bites per night and the relationship between bug density and infective bugs is described, supporting the inference that reducing bug density through these traps can lower transmission risk. The option's claim about multiple interventions reducing risk to less than one bite per night is a logical extrapolation from the study's focus on measuring transmission risk reduction.

94.Option: The high viability of conidia in dry formulation under field conditions (88±3% after 3 months) suggests that a single application of B. bassiana-based traps would be sufficient to achieve long-term elimination of T. infestestans populations in rural villages.
Evidence: Formulation 2: The conidia:DE mix was obtained employing the commercial powder formulation of conidia (1.27×1011 conidia/g, 98% viable). At the dose used, DE produced no mortality.
94.Rationale: While the passage mentions the viability of conidia in dry formulation, it does not provide data on the duration of effectiveness or the long-term elimination of T. infestans populations. The claim about a single application being sufficient for long-term elimination is not supported by the evidence in the passage. The study focuses on the immediate effects of the intervention rather than long-term population dynamics.

94.Option: Since pyrethroid-resistant T. infestans populations show increased cuticular hydrocarbon content and thicker cuticles, B. bassiana-based traps are expected to be less effective in areas with high resistance prevalence.
Evidence: The aim of this research was to characterize the relationship between T. infestans cuticular layer and pyrethroid resistance, and to investigate the potential enhancement of B. bassiana virulence by adaptation to grow on insect-like hydrocarbons.

We also explored a practical vector control approach based on manipulating bug behavior in order to facilitate contact with B. bassiana. This methodology would be particularly useful to control pyrethroid-resistant Chagas disease vectors.
94.Rationale: The passage indicates that the study aimed to investigate the potential enhancement of B. bassiana virulence by adaptation to grow on insect-like hydrocarbons, which suggests that the fungus might be effective against pyrethroid-resistant populations. The claim that B. bassiana-based traps would be less effective in areas with high resistance prevalence is not supported by the evidence provided.

94.Option: The modeling of T. cruzi transmission risk index indicates that the reduction in bug density due to fungal infection is sufficient to eliminate the risk of Chagas disease transmission in the studied villages after a single intervention.
Evidence: We also estimated the potential T. cruzi transmission risk index after intervention with B. bassiana-based traps.
94.Rationale: The passage mentions the estimation of the T. cruzi transmission risk index after intervention but does not state that a single intervention would eliminate the risk. The option's claim about elimination after a single intervention is not supported by the evidence in the passage.

94.Option: The observed horizontal transmission of B. bassiana among triatomine nymphs indicates that the fungus can effectively reduce the overall vector population even in the absence of direct contact with the trap, making trap placement irrelevant.
Evidence: Laboratory assays were performed to evaluate the potential horizontal transmission (autodissemination) of conidia from previously contaminated Py-S insects to healthy insects (third-, fourth-, and fifth-instar).
94.Rationale: The passage describes laboratory assays on horizontal transmission but does not state that trap placement is irrelevant. The claim that trap placement is irrelevant is not supported by the evidence provided. The study's focus on trap placement in field assays suggests that placement is still a relevant factor.

95.Option: The seasonal variation in Leptospira species diversity, particularly the higher proportion of L. kirschneri in spring, indicates that temperature and rainfall patterns directly drive shifts in pathogen species composition.
Evidence: Sequences obtained in summer corresponded to a higher proportion of L. kirschneri (25.5%, 12/47) compared to sequences obtained in the spring (8.0%, 4/50), and all three L. weilii samples were obtained in the spring (Table 2, p-value = 0.02).

The temperature in the preceding week, and rainfall in the preceding month of sample collection were also not statistically significantly associated with the distribution of presumptive Leptospira species (p-value = 0.07 and p-value = 0.12, respectively).
95.Rationale: The passage states that L. kirschneri was more common in summer samples, not spring, and that L. weilii was found only in spring. However, the statistical analysis shows that temperature and rainfall were not significantly associated with species distribution (p-values 0.07 and 0.12). The option incorrectly claims that temperature and rainfall 'directly drive' species shifts, which is not supported by the data. The observed seasonal variation may be real, but the passage does not establish a direct causal link to temperature and rainfall.

95.Option: The presence and diversity of Leptospira species varied substantially at the community level, more than by community type, suggesting that targeted prospective monitoring may be appropriate to identify local mechanisms responsible for enhanced transmission, including critical periods of high risk, and key animal hosts.
Evidence: Community type was not associated with the presence of a particular Leptospira species (p-value = 0.27) (Table 2).
At the community level, there were notable differences in distribution of species (Fig 3).

Community D-4, seemed to have L. interrogans only; 19 of 22 samples were classified as L. interrogans, and the other three, while unclassified, were also suggested to be L. interrogans based on results from the NCBI BLAST. Similarly, all sequences from community U-3 (n = 15) were classified as L. interrogans.

In other communities, diversity was evident despite the small number of sequences. In community U-1, for example, among the three samples sequenced, all three Leptospira species were identified (L. interrogans, L. kirschneri and L. weilii).
95.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that community type was not significantly associated with Leptospira species distribution (p = 0.27), but that there were substantial differences in species distribution at the community level. For example, some communities had only L. interrogans, while others showed high diversity. This supports the inference that local factors (rather than broad community type) drive transmission dynamics, and that targeted monitoring is needed to understand these local mechanisms.

95.Option: The inability to sequence all PCR-positive samples due to poor chromatogram quality indicates that the PCR methods used are unreliable for environmental surveillance.
Evidence: A sample was considered suitable for phylogenetic analysis if the forward and reverse sequences could be aligned, and any mismatches between base pairs could be resolved based on visual inspection of the chromatogram.
From the 153 PCR positive samples, 104 (12.7% of all water samples) had a sequence suitable for phylogenetic analysis using the secY gene (S1 Fig).
95.Rationale: The passage explains that some PCR-positive samples could not be sequenced due to technical limitations in aligning sequences, not because the PCR methods themselves were unreliable. The study used two PCR protocols to detect pathogenic Leptospira, and the inability to sequence all samples does not invalidate the overall method. The option misrepresents the issue as a failure of the PCR methods rather than a limitation in post-PCR sequencing.

95.Option: The higher proportion of L. interrogans in households with seropositive livestock suggests that livestock are the primary source of environmental contamination in this region.
Evidence: In the subset of households with livestock, the water sequences from households with seropositive livestock contained a higher proportion of L. interrogans (95.4%, 21/22) than water samples from households with seronegative livestock (66.7%, 12/18 of L. interrogans) (p-value = 0.04).

The diversity of Leptospira species in water samples in a community did not appear to be associated with the proportion of infected animals in the community (S2 Fig), and in general, L. interrogans, L. kirschneri, and L. weilii were found across the various communities (Fig 3).
95.Rationale: While the passage shows an association between seropositive livestock and L. interrogans in water samples, it does not establish that livestock are the 'primary' source of contamination. The study also found that the diversity of Leptospira species in water was not significantly associated with the proportion of infected animals in the community. Other factors, such as rodents or environmental conditions, may also contribute significantly to contamination.

95.Option: The detection of multiple Leptospira species in urban slum communities, such as in community U-1, suggests that urban areas have higher biodiversity of pathogenic Leptospira due to greater human and animal density.
Evidence: In community U-1, for example, among the three samples sequenced, all three Leptospira species were identified (L. interrogans, L. kirschneri and L. weilii).
Community type was not associated with the presence of a particular Leptospira species (p-value = 0.27) (Table 2).
L. borgpetersenii was found in rodents from rural farms but not in water samples.
95.Rationale: The passage notes that multiple species were found in one urban slum community (U-1), but this does not imply that urban areas have higher biodiversity overall. The study found that all three species were present in all community types, and community type was not significantly associated with species distribution. The option makes an unwarranted generalization from a single example and ignores the lack of statistical support for urban areas having higher biodiversity.

96.Option: Early treatment, higher education, and better knowledge of dengue disease are associated with a reduced probability of developing severe illness.
Evidence: The seeking-early-treatment variable is also statistically significant at the 1% level and decreased the odds of experiencing severe illness for both models. 96.Rationale: The passage provides three key pieces of evidence supporting this option: (1) early treatment is statistically significant at the 1% level and decreases the odds of severe illness; (2) higher education is associated with reduced likelihood of severe illness; and (3) better knowledge of dengue is negatively associated with severe illness. These findings are all derived from the multivariate analysis described in the passage and directly support the stated pattern.

97.Option: S. mansoni infection in HIV-uninfected Ugandan men was associated with an increase in highly HIV-susceptible T cell subsets in the blood, but not the foreskin.

However, these differences did not extend to the foreskin, since S. mansoni-infected and uninfected men had similar frequencies of susceptible Th populations in foreskin tissue.
97.Rationale: The study directly reports that S. mansoni-infected men had significantly higher frequencies of Th1, Th17, and Th22 cells in their blood compared to uninfected men. However, it also explicitly states that these differences did not extend to the foreskin. This conclusion is based solely on the study's reported data without extrapolation.

97.Option: The absence of differences in CCR5 expression and immune activation in the foreskin between S. mansoni-infected and uninfected men suggests that the foreskin is not a relevant site for HIV acquisition in heterosexual Ugandan men.

97.Rationale: While the study found no differences in CCR5 expression or immune activation in the foreskin between S. mansoni-infected and uninfected men, this does not negate the established epidemiological evidence that the foreskin is a major site of HIV acquisition in uncircumcised men. The study's findings only address the specific question of whether S. mansoni infection alters these parameters in the foreskin, not the general relevance of the foreskin as an HIV acquisition site. The conclusion in this option misrepresents the scope of the study's findings.

97.Option: Since S. mansoni infection increases the frequency of polyfunctional Th17 cells in the blood, it likely enhances HIV acquisition risk in heterosexual men by increasing the number of highly susceptible target cells at the site of exposure.

However, S. mansoni infection does not directly involve the urogenital mucosa.

97.Rationale: The study found increased polyfunctional Th17 cells in the blood of S. mansoni-infected men, but it explicitly states that S. mansoni does not directly involve the urogenital mucosa. The option incorrectly assumes that blood-borne changes would translate to increased susceptibility at the genital mucosa, which is the primary site of HIV acquisition in heterosexual men. The study does not provide evidence that these systemic changes lead to increased target cells at the site of sexual exposure.

97.Option: The lack of association between S. mansoni infection and HIV incidence or prevalence in Entebbe, Uganda, suggests that the systemic immune changes observed in this study do not influence HIV transmission risk.
Evidence: The study population consisted of 34 HIV-uninfected men living in fishing communities on the shores of Lake Victoria who were undergoing elective adult circumcision for HIV prevention.
No participant tested positive for malaria, had a symptomatic genital infection, or was taking immunosuppressive therapy at the time of enrolment.
97.Rationale: The study did not collect data on HIV incidence or prevalence in the population. It only examined immune parameters in HIV-uninfected men. The option makes an unsupported inference about population-level HIV transmission risk based on a study of immune cell frequencies in a small sample of uninfected individuals. The study's design does not allow for conclusions about the relationship between S. mansoni infection and HIV incidence or prevalence.

98.Option: Infections caused by HD Class I and Class II strain types appear to differ in ulcer durations and infection resurgence after azithromycin treatment, suggesting important pathophysiological differences between HD Class I and HD Class II.

The prevalence of I.10 in the control arm increased significantly from 5.8% at baseline (Round 1) to 33.3% at Round 4 after MDA (p = 0.0008) while in the experimental arm, the prevalence of I.10 remained constant at 9% at baseline and 9.75% after MDA. 98.Rationale: The passage provides direct evidence that HD Class II causes longer-lasting ulcers compared to Class I (p < 0.001). It also shows that the I.10 strain type (a Class I strain) increased in prevalence after azithromycin treatment in the control arm but not in the experimental arm. These findings suggest important differences in pathophysiology between the two classes, including differential responses to treatment and ulcer duration. The statistical significance of these findings supports the inference of pathophysiological differences.

98.Option: HD Class II strains are more likely to be transmitted in the southern Matalai region due to higher environmental stability compared to Class I strains.
Evidence: Among the 29 villages in the southern part of Namatanai (MAT region), 21 cases of HD infections were found in a total of 9 villages. Similarly, among the 51 villages in central Namatanai (NTI region), there were 79 cases of HD infections in 28 villages. Lastly, among the 73 villages of the northern part of Namatanai (SNA region), we detected 33 cases of HD infections in 16 villages. While there were more Class I HD strains, especially I.3, in the northern part of the Namatanai (SNA region), the southern part (MAT region) exhibited a higher frequency of Class II HD strains (II.3).
98.Rationale: The passage shows a geographic distribution pattern with Class II strains more common in the southern region and Class I in the northern region. However, the option incorrectly attributes this to 'higher environmental stability' without any evidence in the passage about environmental factors affecting transmission. The passage provides no information about environmental stability or its impact on transmission, making this inference unsupported by the data.

98.Rationale: The passage shows a geographic distribution pattern but provides no evidence about adaptability to environmental conditions. The geographic distribution could be due to many factors unrelated to adaptability, such as population movement or historical transmission patterns, which are not discussed in the passage.

98.Option: The increase in the prevalence of the I.10 strain type after azithromycin treatment suggests that this strain has developed resistance to macrolide antibiotics, as evidenced by the presence of point mutations in the 23S rRNA gene.
Evidence: The increased prevalence of I.10 strains in Round 4 prompted us to detect for point mutations in 23S rRNA genes in I.10 samples at baseline (n = 12) and post-mass drug administration (MDA) (n = 18). The region of the I.10 strains spanning nucleotide positions between 1595–2279 of 23S rRNA genes of Class I strains were amplified and compared to the sequence of the Class I strain 35000HP (GenBank acc. no. AE017143.1). Since there are no previous reports of macrolide resistance-encoding mutations in HD, nucleotides in positions where macrolide-resistant point mutations had been found in Haemophilus influenzae were analyzed [15]. While most analyzed sequences were identical to Class I strain 35000HP (GenBank acc. no. AE017143.1), two distinct point mutations were identified in post-MDA in two I.10 samples: G1649A and C2092T. Furthermore, 23S rRNA genes were tested among control samples from Round 4 including I.3, I.4, I.5, and I.9 strain types. The sequences of I.5 and I.4 strain types revealed a C1754T substitution (Table 4). The association of these mutations with resistance is currently unknown.
98.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that the association of these mutations with resistance is currently unknown. While there is evidence of increased prevalence of I.10 and some point mutations in 23S rRNA genes, the passage does not establish a causal link between these mutations and macrolide resistance. The option makes a definitive claim about resistance that is not supported by the data presented.

98.Option: The higher bacterial load associated with the I.10 strain type indicates that it is more virulent and causes more severe clinical outcomes than other HD strains.
Evidence: The average Ct values of individual samples were calculated for 16S rRNA gene amplifications. The remaining 5 plates were excluded from the analysis as a different control sample (T19.ba.1) was used. Plate 11b was chosen as the reference and the samples from the remaining plates were calibrated against T19.ba.2 reference to plate 11b. In our analysis, data was normalised for 104 samples out of 270 HD-typed samples and the average Ct values were calculated for both normalised (n = 104) and non-normalised data sets (n = 270). Variables were tested for normal distribution with the Kolmogorov-Smirnov test and analyzed using a one-sample t-test. The level of significance was set to p ≤ 0.05.

A total of 363 (33.6%) samples were positive for the presence of HD 16S rDNA. In addition to detection of HD positivity, the average Ct values were calculated for HD strain types and ranged from 13 to 33.71.
98.Rationale: The passage provides information about Ct values (which correlate with bacterial load) but does not specifically link I.10 to higher bacterial loads. The option makes a claim about I.10 being more virulent based on bacterial load, but the passage does not provide evidence that I.10 has higher bacterial loads or that higher bacterial load correlates with more severe clinical outcomes. The study does not report clinical outcomes by strain type, so this inference cannot be made from the data presented.

99.Option: The high degree of HAP2 sequence conservation among B. bovis isolates, with no evidence for positive selection, suggests that the protein is under strong immune pressure in the mammalian host, which may explain its low variability.
Evidence: The hap2 gene is highly conserved among the distinct strains (99% to 99.9% aa identity).

The calculated synonymous and non-synonymous S/N ratios (Table 2) with the parameter, ω, (ω = dN/dS), as an indicator of potential selection pressures. In all cases, ω of less than 1 was obtained and revealed no evidence for positive selection for the hap2 gene, suggesting that B. bovis hap2 gene is under no diversifying immune selection.

The data also indicates a low likelihood of selective forces such as immune pressure of the host acting on the evolutionary history of this gene, consistent with low or lack of exposure of the protein to the immune system of the host during infection, which suggests no expression of hap2 in blood stages of the parasite.
99.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that the hap2 gene is highly conserved with no evidence of positive selection (ω < 1). It also directly contradicts the option's claim by stating that the low variability is consistent with 'low or lack of exposure of the protein to the immune system of the host during infection' and 'no expression of hap2 in blood stages of the parasite.' The option incorrectly attributes the conservation to 'strong immune pressure' when the passage indicates the opposite.

99.Option: The successful disruption of the hap2 gene without affecting in vitro blood stage growth suggests that the gene is redundant and not essential for any stage of the parasite’s life cycle.
Evidence: Growth curve analysis demonstrated that both B bovis Tf-hap2KO-gfp-bsd-cln and its wild type parental strain had similar replication kinetics (Fig 3C).

The passage later shows that hap2 is essential for sexual stage development in vitro and in tick midgut. The option misrepresents the scope of the findings by overgeneralizing from in vitro blood stage results.

99.Option: The successful induction of sexual stages in vitro using xanthurenic acid suggests that B. bovis can complete its entire life cycle in culture without requiring a tick vector, making the hap2 gene dispensable for transmission.
Evidence: The hap2 gene is differentially transcribed during B. bovis development within tick midgut, but not during development of parasites within the mammalian host.
The hap2 gene is expressed during in vitro induction of B. bovis sexual stages.
The hap2 gene is essential for transmission to ticks but not for survival in the mammalian host.

The option incorrectly infers that this makes hap2 'dispensable for transmission' when the passage clearly states that hap2 is essential for sexual stage development and transmission to ticks. Evidence: The hap2 gene is differentially transcribed during B. bovis development within tick midgut, but not during development of parasites within the mammalian host.

99.Option: The absence of detectable hap2 transcripts in B. bovis blood stages, combined with the requirement of hap2 expression for sexual stage development in vitro, suggests that the gene is essential for transmission to ticks but not for survival in the mammalian host.
Evidence: The hap2 gene is differentially transcribed during B. bovis development within tick midgut, but not during development of parasites within the mammalian host.
The hap2 gene is expressed during in vitro induction of B. bovis sexual stages.
The hap2 gene is essential for transmission to ticks but not for survival in the mammalian host.

The evidence directly supports the logical connection made in the option.

100.Option: The use of rK39-ICT as a point-of-care test in outbreak settings is justified by its high sensitivity and specificity in HIV-positive patients, despite its lower performance in this group compared to DAT.
Evidence: The estimated sensitivity of rK39 was 78.0% [70.8–85.2] for all 405 suspected VL patients. The estimated sensitivity of DAT was 86.5% [80.5–92.5] for all 405 suspected VL patients... The sensitivity of DAT in HIV-negative VL suspects was 84.2% [76.3–92.1], rising to 91.3% [82.1–100.0] in individuals with underlying HIV infection.

100.Option: The rK39-ICT demonstrated higher sensitivity than the DAT in patients with HIV co-infection, making it a more reliable serological test for clinical diagnosis in this population.
Evidence: The estimated sensitivity of rK39 was 78.0% [70.8–85.2] for all 405 suspected VL patients... The sensitivity dropped to 67.3 [52.7–82.0] in individuals with underlying HIV infection.

The estimated sensitivity of DAT was 86.5% [80.5–92.5] for all 405 suspected VL patients... The sensitivity of DAT in HIV-negative VL suspects was 84.2% [76.3–92.1], rising to 91.3% [82.1–100.0] in individuals with underlying HIV infection.

100.Option: The high rate of false positives in the IFAT among patients with Chagas disease suggests that this test should be avoided in regions where co-infections are common, regardless of its performance in HIV-negative individuals.

101.Option: The SjTR1 real-time PCR assay can reliably detect S. japonicum in human stool samples with as few as 1 egg per gram, making it the most sensitive diagnostic tool currently available for schistosomiasis in endemic regions.

101.Option: The high sensitivity of the SjTR1 assay is primarily due to its ability to amplify a tandemly repeated genomic sequence that is absent in other Schistosoma species, ensuring both high sensitivity and species-specificity.

101.Option: The SjTR1 real-time PCR assay demonstrated superior clinical sensitivity compared to both the Kato-Katz technique and previously published real-time PCR assays, as evidenced by its ability to detect S. japonicum in samples with as few as 3 eggs per gram and to identify additional positive cases in field-collected stool samples.

101.Rationale: While the passage does mention that the Kato-Katz technique has poor sensitivity in low endemicity settings, it does not make a definitive claim that it is no longer reliable for monitoring schistosomiasis control programs. There is no evidence provided that the assay has been validated across multiple animal hosts. The claim about suitability for use in comprehensive elimination programs that require surveillance in both human and reservoir populations is not supported by the evidence in the passage.

102.Option: The co-localization of wBm0152 and actin at the Wolbachia vacuole surface suggests that Wolbachia uses the host cytoskeleton to regulate its own replication cycle through direct contact with actin filaments.
Evidence: The wBm0152 interacts in vivo with the host cytoskeletal proteins.
The results of this study provide a novel molecular perspective on some of the molecular complexes that support the endosymbiotic relationship between B. malayi and Wolbachia.

102.Rationale: The passage provides evidence of co-localization between wBm0152 and actin at the Wolbachia vacuole surface, but it does not provide any direct evidence that this interaction regulates Wolbachia replication. The passage focuses on the structural and biochemical interactions rather than replication processes. The inference about replication regulation is not supported by the data presented.

102.Option: The wBm0152-actin interaction may facilitate the immune evasion of Wolbachia by masking its surface antigens through association with host cytoskeletal proteins.

The Wolbachia surface proteins wBm0432 and wBm0152 co-localize with their corresponding B. malayi binding partners, aldolase and actin, in vivo.

102.Option: The interaction between wBm0152 and actin is likely to be essential for the structural integrity of the Wolbachia-containing vacuole in B. malayi, based on the co-localization of both proteins at the vacuole surface.

Labeling of adult female B. malayi worms using mouse anti-wBm0152 antiserum demonstrated that the protein is present on the surface and in the areas surrounding Wolbachia (Fig. 5D). Rabbit anti actin antibodies appeared to cross-react with a B. malayi actin protein within the tissue surrounding Wolbachia (Fig. 5E). Notably, double labeling of similar cross-sections of B. malayi adult female worms with both antibodies co-localized the corresponding proteins to the surface of the vacuole that surrounds Wolbachia within the cytoplasm of the B. malayi host (Fig. 5F).
102.Rationale: The passage provides direct evidence of co-localization between wBm0152 and actin at the surface of the Wolbachia-containing vacuole. This co-localization suggests a structural relationship. The passage also notes that microfilaments were observed adjacent to the vacuole boundary, which supports the idea that the actin interaction contributes to the structural integrity of the vacuole. This inference is directly supported by the observational data presented.

Evidence: The wBm0152 interacts in vivo with the host cytoskeletal proteins.
The results of this study provide a novel molecular perspective on some of the molecular complexes that support the endosymbiotic relationship between B. malayi and Wolbachia.

The interaction between the two Wolbachia surface proteins and their host binding partners may be connected to each other via the B. malayi aldolase-actin linkage, and/or the possible interaction between the host's enolase and cytoskeletal proteins.
102.Rationale: The passage provides evidence of a physical interaction between Wolbachia surface proteins and host cytoskeletal proteins. The co-localization at the vacuole surface and the potential connection to other molecular complexes suggest that these interactions could support processes like migration and segregation. The passage also notes that Wolbachia's survival and reproduction are dependent on these interactions, which supports the inference about fitness and survival. This conclusion is directly supported by the data presented.

103.Option: The metabolic profile of DTT-treated cells bears some similarities to those treated with AN5568, suggesting that T. b. brucei activates the conserved SLS pathway in response to the benzoxaboroles, although DTT may also impair cellular redox balance and induce other stresses in common with AN5568.
Evidence: Comparing metabolic phenotypes of AN5568 treatment and ER stress

A major target for AdoMet donated methyl groups in T. b. brucei is the spliced leader cap RNA structure that is spliced to all mature messenger RNAs in these cells. Recent publications have highlighted the presence of a unique ER stress response pathway in trypanosomatids [43–45]. In response to unfolding proteins, and to ER stress inducers such as dithiothreitol (DTT), a programmed cell death known as the spliced leader silencing (SLS) pathway is activated, leading to programmed inhibition of spliced leader trans-splicing, as well as a reduction in total RNA, increased cytoplasmic Ca2+ and DNA fragmentation [43]. Presumably, this would also result in significant loss of RNA methylation.

We therefore sought to compare the metabolomic profiles of DTT and AN5568 treated cells in an LC-MS experiment, in order to determine whether the AdoMet signature was a specific response to the benzoxaborole treatment, or a non-specific stress response (Fig 6).

A heatmap was generated to highlight similarities and differences in metabolism after treatment with the ER stress inducer dithiothreitol (DTT) and AN5568. A) AdoMet metabolism was unaffected by DTT-treatment. B) Components of the oxidative stress response pathway were altered after both treatments. In particular, DTT treatment led to a significant decrease in trypanothione disulphide. C) Both DTT and AN5568 treatment result in increased levels of modified lysines and arginines, suggesting these are part of a conserved trypanosomatid stress response.

Modified lysines were increased, in a similar fashion to AN5568 treatment. In particular, there was increased abundance of N6-acetyl-L-lysine (m/z: 188.1162, RT: 11.62 min, 4.26-fold, P = 0.0021), N6-methyl-L-lysine (m/z: 160.1211, RT: 18.99 min, 2.80-fold, P = 0.0004) and N6,N6,N6-trimethyl-L-lysine (m/z: 188.1528, RT: 18.09 min, 1.62-fold, P = 0.013) (Fig 6C), suggesting these changes are not unique to AN5568 treatment, but potential indicators of metabolic responses to stress.
103.Rationale: The passage provides direct evidence of similarities between DTT-treated and AN5568-treated cells, particularly in the increased levels of modified lysines and arginines. The text explicitly states that these changes suggest a conserved trypanosomatid stress response. The passage also notes that DTT treatment leads to a significant decrease in trypanothione disulphide, indicating impaired redox balance. These findings support the inference that T. b. brucei activates the SLS pathway in response to AN5568, while DTT induces additional stresses. The evidence directly supports the conclusion presented in the option.

103.Option: The absence of labelled methyl-arginine in either untreated controls or drug-treated cells was surprising, and suggests that arginine methylation is either a slower process than, for example, lysine methylation, or the methyl groups used to methylate arginine do not originate from L-methionine or indeed AdoMet.
Evidence: Distribution of carbon originating from L-methionine in the T. b. brucei metabolome is not altered after AN5568 treatment

Whilst arginine methylation was not observed in wild-type or drug-treated samples, methylated lysines that were seen to increase in previous experiments did show stable isotope labels (S1 Fig), demonstrating that the carbon atoms in the methyl groups originated from L-methionine.

Lysine methylation has been shown to be involved in the degradation pathway of this amino acid, which is ultimately converted to L-carnitine [39]. However, in neither sample group was L-carnitine found to exhibit 13C labelling (S1 Fig), indicating a separate source of this metabolite in T. b. brucei.
103.Rationale: The passage states that arginine methylation was not observed in either wild-type or drug-treated samples, but it does not express surprise at this finding. The option introduces the idea that this absence is 'surprising,' which is not supported by the passage. The passage also does not provide evidence to support the proposed explanations (slower process or different methyl group source). The inference about the cause of the absence of labelled methyl-arginine is speculative and not directly supported by the evidence presented.

103.Option: The large number of SET domain MTases predicted in the T. brucei genome suggests that AN5568 may target histone-modifying enzymes, which are essential for regulating gene expression during the parasite’s life cycle.
Evidence: In silico analysis of the T. brucei methyltransferome

Another group with many representatives are proteins containing the conserved SET domain (Fig 7A & 7B). These 33 genes are likely to be involved in protein lysine methylation, in particular methylation of histones [53]. This group is far larger than expected (The S. cerevisiae genome encodes 12 SET domain MTases [47]), and could be indicative of the parasite’s need to alter global gene expression throughout the varying life cycle stages.

We attempted to overexpress several of the genes identified in the MTome screen based on their essentiality in an RNAi screen [56] (Gene IDs: Tb927.10.3080, Tb927.10.7560, Tb927.10/7850, Tb927.5.2050, Tb927.8.5040 and Tb927.6.2270). Whilst overexpression of some MTases was achievable, their overexpression did not alter parasite sensitivity to AN5568, although in the case of Tb927.8.5040 and Tb927.10.7850, resistance against sinefungin was noted (S2 Fig).
103.Rationale: The passage notes the presence of many SET domain MTases in T. b. brucei and suggests they may be involved in histone methylation. However, the passage also states that overexpression of these MTases did not alter parasite sensitivity to AN5568. This directly contradicts the inference that AN5568 targets histone-modifying enzymes. The option makes a causal claim that is not supported by the evidence, as the data show no change in sensitivity when these potential targets are overexpressed.

104.Option: The movement of epidemics by viremic individuals is another concern, particularly in the Gulf Coast region of Mexico, which could threaten previously unaffected areas of Mexico and even the U.S.
Evidence: The first VEEV subtype IE isolates from Mexico were obtained by Scherer et al. from sentinel hamsters and mosquito pools in 1963 in the Sontecomapan region of the State of Veracruz [11]. These studies noted the constant enzootic circulation of the subtype IE VEEVs in several lowland coastal habitats in the Gulf Coast region and suggested the potential threat of VEEV expansion mediated by the north-south movement of people and domestic animals in these areas.

In 1965, a human fatality associated with subtype IE VEEV was reported in the village of Jaltipan, Veracruz [12]. Later, in 1966, an equine epizootic attributed to subtype IE VEEV took place in the Tampico region of the northeastern State of Tamaulipas, which borders Texas. During this epizootic, approximately 1,000 equids developed encephalitis and 300 deaths were recorded [13].

A VEE outbreak in 1969–72, caused by an epizootic strain of subtype IAB VEEV spreading northward from Guatemala, affected several coastal regions of Mexico and the U.S. [5]. In September, 1970, this epidemic/epizootic was first reported in the State of Veracruz in the San Andres Tuxtla region, and then, it continued to expand northward during the summer of 1971 through the Tuxpan-Tamiahua region of the State of Veracruz. A few days later, the outbreak spread to the municipality of Soto La Marina in the State of Tamaulipas, about 200 km (124 miles) from the U.S. border, before being reported in southern Texas, where approximately 1,500 horses died and several hundred human cases were documented [5], [14].

The study in Mexico was also conducted under permit no. SGPA/DGVS/03858, issued to Dr. J.G. Estrada-Franco, by the Secretaría del Medio Ambiente Y Recursos Naturales de Mexico (SEMARNAT), and any animal work at the University of Texas Medical Branch (UTMB) was performed in strict accordance with the recommendations in the Guide for the Care and Use of Laboratory Animals of the National Research Council. The animal protocol was approved by the Institutional Animal Care and Use Committee at the UTMB. For the entomological surveys, all owners of private property gave permission for the study to be conducted on their land.

The equine serological data also suggest that VEEV-enzootic regions of the Gulf Coast included the States of Tabasco and Veracruz, particularly lowland areas (Fig. 1, Table 2). Of 79 equids tested in the State of Veracruz, the seroprevalence for VEEV was 72% (57/79). For the municipalities of Minatitlan, A. R. Cabada, and Las Choapas, the seroprevalence for VEEV was 100%. In the State of Tabasco, the seroprevalence for VEEV in equids was 52% (265/506). The southernmost areas, where VEEV-positive sera were detected, included the municipalities of Balancan and Tenosique in the State of Tabasco, which border Guatemala. In these municipalities, equine seroprevalence for VEEV was ≥58%. For the municipalities of Macuspana and Paraiso, the seroprevalence for VEEV was 100%.

The overall seroprevalence for VEEV was 30% (38/126) in bovids and 59% (23/39) in equids. However, for the municipalities of Tuxpan and Tamiahua, the equine seroprevalences for VEEV were as high as 69% and 75%, respectively. Although the sample sizes were small for individual farms (n–4 or 10) in the municipality of Tamiahua, VEEV antibodies were detected in at least 75% of the sampled bovids and/or equids on the Barra and Tamiahua farms.

The study in Mexico was also conducted under permit no. SGPA/DGVS/03858, issued to Dr. J.G. Estrada-Franco, by the Secretaría del Medio Ambiente Y Recursos Naturales de Mexico (SEMARNAT), and any animal work at the University of Texas Medical Branch (UTMB) was performed in strict accordance with the recommendations in the Guide for the Care and Use of Laboratory Animals of the National Research Council. The animal protocol was approved by the Institutional Animal Care and Use Committee at the UTMB. For the entomological surveys, all owners of private property gave permission for the study to be conducted on their land.
104.Rationale: The passage provides multiple lines of evidence supporting the concern about epidemic movement by viremic individuals in the Gulf Coast region. First, historical data from the 1960s explicitly mentions the potential threat of VEEV expansion mediated by the north-south movement of people and domestic animals. Second, the 1969-1972 epidemic/epizootic is described as spreading northward from Guatemala through Veracruz to Tamaulipas and into the U.S., demonstrating the actual historical threat to previously unaffected areas. Third, the high seroprevalence data in equids and bovids in the Gulf Coast region, including areas near the U.S. border, indicates ongoing enzootic circulation. The combination of these factors - historical precedent, current enzootic circulation, and proximity to the U.S. border - directly supports the public health implication that movement of viremic individuals could threaten new areas. This inference is grounded in the epidemiological principle that high prevalence in a region increases the risk of spread through human and animal movement.

104.Option: The presence of VEEV in rodent populations in the Gulf Coast region indicates that these animals are the primary reservoirs responsible for maintaining the virus in nature.
Evidence: In 2008–2009 in the municipality of Minatitlan, 3 species of rodents were trapped in Ejidos Coachapa, in a grassland area near the center of the town (N17°53′25.50″; W94°33′33.30″) and in Ejidos Tacoteno (N18°00′24.62″; W94°30′56.72″). Traps were placed next to swampy pastures and streams of farms where sentinel hamster mortalities and/or positive equine and bovine serology for VEEV had been previously reported. The following rodents were trapped in these locations: Sigmodon toltecus (n–12), Oryzomys chapmani (n–6), and Rattus rattus (n–1). Sera collected from the wild-caught rodents were tested in all cases by both PRNT and HI assays, and the results were consistently either positive or negative for VEEV. The Rattus rattus (n–1) was seronegative for VEEV; however, 4 of the 12 Sigmodon toltecus were seropositive for VEEV, 3 of which were from Ejido Tacoteno and 1 from Ejido Coachapa. Five of the 6 Oryzomys chapmani collected from Ejido Tacoteno were also seropositive for VEEV, suggesting the possible involvement of these rodents in VEEV circulation in this region as in the Pacific Coast [29].
104.Rationale: While the passage does show that some rodent species in the Gulf Coast region have VEEV antibodies, it only suggests 'possible involvement' in VEEV circulation, not that they are the primary reservoirs. The passage does not provide evidence that these rodents are responsible for maintaining the virus in nature. The term 'primary reservoirs' implies a definitive role in the virus's natural cycle, which is not established by the data presented. The passage only notes the presence of antibodies in these rodents, which could indicate exposure but not necessarily a role in maintaining the virus. The conclusion that these animals are the primary reservoirs requires additional evidence not provided in the passage body.

104.Option: The phylogenetic analysis of VEEV isolates from Minatitlan shows that the virus has undergone significant genetic changes since the 1960s, increasing its potential for epizootic spread.
Evidence: The phylogenetic analysis of these strains, Pacific IE genotypes are represented by blue, Gulf/Caribbean IE genotypes are represented by green, and Panama IE genotypes are represented by red. Recent isolates (2008–2010) are printed in bold.

The tree shows the clustering pattern of the sequences obtained from this study as well as representative strains that were available from the GenBank database. VEEV subtype IIIA Mucambo virus was used as the outgroup. Trees generated using maximum parsimony and maximum likelihood (ML) methods had identical topologies. The numbers adjacent to the nodes indicate NJ bootstrap values for 1000 replicates. The scale bar represents the number of nucleotide substitutions per site, and the brackets indicate the genotypes of the subtype IE VEEVs and some geographical/temporal data within the Gulf/Caribbean IE genotype. Sequence names are marked by VEEV subtype, a two-letter abbreviation for the country of origin, a two digit number indicating year of isolation, and the strain name. Pacific IE genotypes are represented by blue, Gulf/Caribbean IE genotypes are represented by green, and Panama IE genotypes are represented by red. Recent isolates (2008–2010) are printed in bold.

The deduced amino acid sequences of the E3 and E2 proteins were also compared across all subtype IE VEEV strains (Table 5). When compared to the Panama and Pacific genotypes, several amino acid differences were distinct for the Gulf/Caribbean IE genotype. Within the Gulf/Caribbean IE genotype, there were no consistent amino acid differences between the 2 temporally distinct isolates from Mexico (1963–69 vs. 2008‐). The amino sequences from 1 sentinel hamster (H60) and 5 mosquito pools (M48, M49, M50, M51, M64), which had been collected from nearby regions in the State of Veracruz in 2009, had a serine→threonine change at position 208 in the E2 protein.
104.Rationale: The passage states that there were no consistent amino acid differences between the 1960s and 2008-2010 isolates within the Gulf/Caribbean IE genotype. While a single amino acid change (serine→threonine at position 208 in the E2 protein) was noted in some 2009 isolates, this is not described as a significant genetic change that would increase the virus's potential for epizootic spread. The phylogenetic analysis shows that the recent isolates cluster within the Gulf/Caribbean genotype but in a distinct clade from the 1960s isolates, but this is not characterized as a significant change in the passage. The conclusion about increased potential for epizootic spread requires additional evidence not provided in the passage body.

104.Option: The high seroprevalence of VEEV in human populations in the Gulf Coast region of Mexico suggests that VEEV is a major cause of febrile illness in this area, comparable to dengue.
Evidence: Between October 2008 and April 2009, sera from a total of 234 febrile persons of all ages were obtained from 44 municipalities broadly representing the State of Veracruz, including Minatitlan. Sera were tested in all cases by PRNT, and in most cases, IgG ELISAs. The results were consistently either positive or negative for VEEV. Serum samples from 2 municipalities (Minatitlan, Villa Acula) were also tested by HI assay, and the results were consistent with the PRNT and IgG ELISA results. PRNT- and IgG-negative serum was also tested for IgM antibodies to VEEV. Fig. 3 only shows the seropositive results based on 24 of the 44 municipalities, where all other municipalities had no seropositive results. Based on the results of the ELISA for IgM or IgG, PRNT, and/or HI, 19.2% (45/234) of the total individuals tested were positive for VEEV antibodies. Among these, 5 were IgM-positive, suggesting recent VEEV infection (Table 4). Interestingly, 4 of the 5 IgM positive individuals also showed IgM antibodies to dengue virus (DENV), suggesting the possibility of co-infection. In the Minatitlan region, 13 of the 24 (54%) tested individuals were seropositive for VEEV. This region was also the area where 17 isolates of VEEV were made from sentinel hamsters and mosquitoes during this same time period (2008‐) (Table 1).
104.Rationale: The passage reports a 19.2% seroprevalence of VEEV in febrile individuals in the Gulf Coast region, with 54% in the Minatitlan region. However, it does not provide any comparative data to dengue or any other febrile illness to support the claim that VEEV is a 'major cause' comparable to dengue. The passage only notes that 4 of the 5 IgM-positive individuals also had IgM antibodies to dengue, suggesting possible co-infection, but this does not establish a comparison of prevalence or impact between VEEV and dengue. The conclusion requires additional evidence not provided in the passage body.

104.Option: The 1969–1971 epidemic/epizootic in the U.S. was caused by the introduction of a new, more virulent strain of VEEV from the Pacific Coast of Mexico.
Evidence: A VEE outbreak in 1969–72, caused by an epizootic strain of subtype IAB VEEV spreading northward from Guatemala, affected several coastal regions of Mexico and the U.S. [5]. In September, 1970, this epidemic/epizootic was first reported in the State of Veracruz in the San Andres Tuxtla region, and then, it continued to expand northward during the summer of 1971 through the Tuxpan-Tamiahua region of the State of Veracruz. A few days later, the outbreak spread to the municipality of Soto La Marina in the State of Tamaulipas, about 200 km (124 miles) from the U.S. border, before being reported in southern Texas, where approximately 1,500 horses died and several hundred human cases were documented [5], [14].
104.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that the 1969-1972 epidemic/epizootic was caused by a strain of subtype IAB VEEV spreading northward from Guatemala, not from the Pacific Coast of Mexico. The passage does not mention the Pacific Coast of Mexico as the source of this strain. The conclusion that it was caused by a new, more virulent strain from the Pacific Coast of Mexico is not supported by the evidence provided in the passage body.

105.Option: The use of metabarcoding technology in this study revealed that the most effective way to prevent mycetoma is through targeted soil decontamination in high-risk land-use areas.
Evidence: To understand the distributions of the causative fungi, it is necessary to comprehensively capture all fungi in an endemic area’s soil.

By applying this metabarcoding technology, it is possible to capture all the causative fungi in the soil for environmental surveillance of mycetoma, which will be critical for eumycetoma prevention measurements and control programs.
105.Rationale: The passage states that metabarcoding technology can comprehensively capture causative fungi in soil for environmental surveillance, which is critical for prevention and control programs. However, it does not claim that targeted soil decontamination is the most effective prevention method. The study's findings about fungal distribution do not directly support the conclusion that soil decontamination is the most effective strategy. The passage focuses on detection and surveillance rather than specific prevention methods like decontamination.

105.Option: The detection of multiple eumycetoma-causative fungi, including rare species, in 83% of soil samples from diverse land uses suggests that these pathogens are ubiquitous in the endemic environment, supporting the need for broad-spectrum preventive measures such as wearing protective footwear.
Evidence: A total of 64 DNA samples with six negative controls were extracted, and 17 of the samples were colored with soil components.

In the BLAST results based on the UNITE database, nine genera and twelve species of causative fungi, including the primary pathogen in Sudan, M. mycetomatis, and Falciformispora senegalensis and Falciformispora tompkinsii, were retrieved (Table 2).
The number of detected species was counted based on land use; there was a significant difference only between road (3.3 ± 1.5) and dryland (1.9 ± 1.0) (p = 0.045) (S1 Fig).
105.Rationale: The passage states that 12 species of causative fungi were detected in soil samples, including rare species, and that these fungi were found in diverse land uses. The detection in 83% of samples (64 out of 77 total samples) supports the idea that these pathogens are widespread in the environment. The need for broad-spectrum preventive measures like protective footwear is a logical epidemiological inference based on the ubiquity of these pathogens in the environment.

105.Option: The presence of PCR inhibitors in soil samples, such as humic acids, significantly reduced the sensitivity of the metabarcoding method, leading to underestimation of the true number of eumycetoma-causing fungi in the environment.
Evidence: For the PCR targeting ribosomal ITS2, ten-fold dilutions of 17 colored DNA extracts were also used to reduce the influence of PCR inhibition (S2 Table).
105.Rationale: The passage mentions that ten-fold dilutions were used to reduce the influence of PCR inhibition, but it does not state that PCR inhibitors significantly reduced the sensitivity of the metabarcoding method or led to underestimation. The study acknowledges the potential for PCR inhibition but does not claim that this caused a significant underestimation of the true number of causative fungi.

105.Option: The significant difference in the number of causative species between roads and drylands indicates that roads are a primary source of eumycetoma transmission in endemic areas.
Evidence: The number of detected species was counted based on land use; there was a significant difference only between road (3.3 ± 1.5) and dryland (1.9 ± 1.0) (p = 0.045) (S1 Fig).
105.Rationale: The passage states that there was a significant difference in the number of causative species between roads and drylands, but it does not conclude that roads are a primary source of transmission. The study only shows a difference in the number of species detected, not that roads are the primary source of transmission. The inference about transmission routes requires additional evidence not provided in the passage.

105.Option: The high detection rate of eumycetoma-causing fungi in soil samples suggests that most patients in the region are infected by multiple fungal species simultaneously.
Evidence: some patients are simultaneously infected with multiple fungi [9–13].

In the BLAST results based on the UNITE database, nine genera and twelve species of causative fungi, including the primary pathogen in Sudan, M. mycetomatis, and Falciformispora senegalensis and Falciformispora tompkinsii, were retrieved (Table 2).
105.Rationale: The passage mentions that some patients are infected with multiple fungi, but it does not state that most patients are infected by multiple species simultaneously. The high detection rate in soil samples does not directly translate to a high rate of multiple infections in patients. The study does not provide data on the proportion of patients with multiple infections, so this conclusion cannot be drawn from the passage.

106.Option: Reduced expression levels of HpHbR are likely to play a role in Tbg1 serum resistance, and the L210S mutation may act synergistically with this mechanism to confer complete resistance to human serum.
Evidence: In Tbg1, the mechanism is better understood and appears to involve reduced expression and altered function of the parasite HpHbR [11].
Sequencing of a few isolates of Tbg and Tbb led Kieft et al. [11] to suggest that mutations in HpHbR may have altered TLF-1 binding in Tbg1.
106.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that Tbg1's resistance mechanism involves both reduced expression and altered function of HpHbR. The L210S mutation is described as a specific amino acid substitution in Tbg1 that differentiates it from other taxa. The inference that these two factors (reduced expression and the L210S mutation) may act synergistically is a logical extension of the evidence presented, as both mechanisms are mentioned in the context of Tbg1's resistance to human serum.

106.Option: The L210S mutation in HpHbR is the only amino acid substitution consistently present in Tbg1 isolates and absent from all other closely related trypanosome taxa, suggesting it may play a key role in conferring serum resistance to Tbg1.
Evidence: The single nucleotide difference in HpHbR that distinguished all isolates of Tbg1 from all other T. brucei isolates sampled in this study was non-synonymous, resulting in the replacement of leucine with serine at position 210 (L210S; Figure 1b, Figure S1).
With one exception, all Tbg1 isolates possessed two copies of HpHbR that coded for just this single amino acid sequence (Z).
106.Rationale: The passage provides direct evidence that the L210S mutation is consistently present in Tbg1 isolates and absent from other T. brucei subspecies. This mutation is described as the single nucleotide difference that distinguishes Tbg1 from other taxa. The inference that this mutation may play a key role in serum resistance is supported by the fact that it is the only such substitution consistently found in Tbg1 and absent elsewhere.

106.Option: The extremely low genetic diversity observed in Tbg1 HpHbR is consistent with prior population genetic studies and suggests that the mutation L210S is likely fixed in the taxon, which could be confirmed in field-circulating Tbg1 using allele-specific PCR or RFLP.
Evidence: Allelic diversity in Tbg1 was relatively low. Allele z1 (identical to the Tbg1 sequence reported in Kieft et al. [2010]) was the most common Tbg1 variant and was recovered from 26 of 30 sampled chromosomes.
The remaining Tbg1 alleles differed by only one nucleotide from this common variant, z1.
Genetic variation in Tbg1 is extremely limited [12], [13]
106.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that Tbg1 exhibits extremely limited genetic diversity and that the L210S mutation is present in the most common allele (z1) found in 26 of 30 sampled chromosomes. The low diversity and the high frequency of the L210S mutation support the inference that this mutation is likely fixed in the Tbg1 population. The suggestion that this could be confirmed using allele-specific PCR or RFLP is a reasonable methodological inference based on the genetic data presented.

106.Option: The L210S mutation in HpHbR is responsible for the altered function of the receptor in Tbg1, which prevents TLF-1 uptake and thereby confers resistance to human serum.
Evidence: The single nucleotide difference in HpHbR that distinguished all isolates of Tbg1 from all other T. brucei isolates sampled in this study was non-synonymous, resulting in the replacement of leucine with serine at position 210 (L210S; Figure 1b, Figure S1).
In Tbg1, the mechanism is better understood and appears to involve reduced expression and altered function of the parasite HpHbR [11].
Sequencing of a few isolates of Tbg and Tbb led Kieft et al. [11] to suggest that mutations in HpHbR may have altered TLF-1 binding in Tbg1.
106.Rationale: The passage provides evidence that the L210S mutation is a key differentiator of Tbg1 and that mutations in HpHbR are associated with altered TLF-1 binding in Tbg1. The inference that this specific mutation is responsible for the altered function of the receptor, which prevents TLF-1 uptake and confers resistance, is a logical conclusion based on the evidence presented. The non-synonymous nature of the mutation and its consistent presence in Tbg1 isolates support this inference.

108.Option: Living in the proximity of VL patients was the strongest VL risk factor, with three out of four cases reporting a household member sick with VL in the past year compared to less than one out of ten controls.
Evidence: As shown in the Table 4, during the year prior the study, 7.4% of controls compared to 71.7% of cases had at least one household member who suffered from VL (p<0.001) and, among those, cases had more often 2 to 4 household members affected (21.8% versus 10.2%).
108.Rationale: The passage provides direct evidence that 71.7% of cases had at least one household member with VL in the past year compared to only 7.4% of controls. This represents a strong association between proximity to VL patients and risk of infection. The p-value of <0.001 confirms statistical significance. This finding directly supports the inference that proximity to VL patients is a major risk factor for transmission.
