It explicitly states that Indian anti-venoms have reduced cross-neutralizing competence against H. hypnale venom. The study's findings, including the failure to neutralize biochemical, pathological, and lethal properties in murine models, are consistent with the observed clinical ineffectiveness reported by Indian medical personnel. The passage also notes that efforts are underway in Sri Lanka to develop specific anti-venoms, while India has not yet taken this step, further supporting the conclusion that current Indian anti-venoms are insufficient.

186.Option: The lack of DNase activity in H. hypnale venom explains why the anti-venoms failed to neutralize its toxic effects, as DNase is a key target for neutralization in snake venoms.
Evidence: DNase activity was determined by performing agarose gel electrophoresis. Briefly, 50 μg of Hhv, Ecv, and Drv were independently incubated with 250 ng of calf thymus DNA for 60 min, at 37°C in a final volume of 30 μl (PBS). The reaction mixture was subjected to electrophoresis on 1.2% agarose gel at 50 V in TAE buffer (40 mM Tris-base and 1 mM EDTA, pH 8.0) for 1 h. DNase 1 (10 U) was used as a positive control.
186.Rationale: The passage describes the methodology for determining DNase activity but does not state that H. hypnale venom lacks DNase activity. The option incorrectly assumes a lack of DNase activity as the reason for anti-venom failure, which is not supported by the evidence provided. The study's findings about anti-venom ineffectiveness are attributed to the venom's composition and cross-neutralizing competence, not the absence of DNase activity.

186.Option: The monovalent anti-venom HhAV is less effective than EcAV and DrAV in neutralizing lethal toxicity, indicating that the immune response to H. hypnale venom is inherently weaker than to the 'big four' species.
Evidence: The study highlighted a markedly reduced cross-neutralizing competence of four commercial Indian anti-venoms against the said neglected snake venoms [4].
The Indian medical personnel too have realized the inaptness [5,10,11].
186.Rationale: The passage does not compare the effectiveness of HhAV with EcAV and DrAV in neutralizing lethal toxicity. The option introduces a comparison that is not supported by the evidence. The study's findings focus on the overall ineffectiveness of Indian anti-venoms against H. hypnale venom, not a relative comparison between different anti-venoms. The claim about the immune response being inherently weaker is speculative and not derived from the passage.

187.Option: The TLA-ELISA demonstrated superior performance in detecting subclinical infections compared to the LPG-ELISA, as it identified a higher proportion of infected dogs in the early stages of disease progression.
Evidence: Regarding the ELISA tests carried out with serum samples from dogs with subclinical infection by L. infantum, the LPG-ELISA identified 9/10 (90%) as positive, while just one sample (1/9) was detected as positive by the TLA-ELISA (Fig 6).
187.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that the LPG-ELISA identified 90% of subclinical infections (9/10) while the TLA-ELISA only identified 10% (1/9). This directly contradicts the claim in option 0 that the TLA-ELISA demonstrated superior performance in detecting subclinical infections.

Evidence: When serum samples from dogs with confirmed infection by T. cruzi or L. braziliensis (with or without clinical signs of disease) were tested, no positive reactions could be seen by either LPG- or TLA-ELISA (Fig 4).

However, when sera from dogs with confirmed infection by Hepatozoon sp., Ehrlichia sp., Babesia sp. or Anaplasma spp. were tested, the TLA-ELISA presented two positive cross reacting results (2/11–18.18%), one for a dog that was infected with Ehrlichia sp. and one for a Babesia sp.-infected dog (Fig 5).
187.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that the LPG-ELISA showed no cross-reaction with T. cruzi or L. braziliensis, and only the TLA-ELISA showed cross-reactions with Ehrlichia and Babesia. The claim in option 1 that the LPG-ELISA's subclinical detection ability is due to cross-reactivity is therefore incorrect. The LPG-ELISA's superior performance is attributed to its specific recognition of LPG, not cross-reactivity.

187.Option: The LPG-ELISA was able to detect 90% of dogs with subclinical L. infantum infection, whereas the TLA-ELISA detected only 10% of such dogs, indicating that the LPG-ELISA is significantly more sensitive for identifying asymptomatic carriers of the parasite.
Evidence: Regarding the ELISA tests carried out with serum samples from dogs with subclinical infection by L. infantum, the LPG-ELISA identified 9/10 (90%) as positive, while just one sample (1/9) was detected as positive by the TLA-ELISA (Fig 6).
187.Rationale: The passage directly states that the LPG-ELISA detected 90% of subclinical infections (9/10) while the TLA-ELISA detected only 10% (1/9). This directly supports the claim in option 2 that the LPG-ELISA is significantly more sensitive for identifying asymptomatic carriers of the parasite.

Regarding dogs with subclinical L. infantum infection, TR DPP-CVL was able to correctly diagnose as positive 06 dogs, and LPG-ELISA presented 09 positive results.

Regarding the ELISA tests carried out with serum samples from dogs with subclinical infection by L. infantum, the LPG-ELISA identified 9/10 (90%) as positive, while just one sample (1/9) was detected as positive by the TLA-ELISA (Fig 6).

This means the LPG-ELISA has a higher sensitivity for subclinical infections (90%) compared to TR DPP-CVL (60%). The claim in option 3 that they are equally effective is therefore incorrect.

Evidence: Regarding the ELISA tests carried out with serum samples from dogs with subclinical infection by L. infantum, the LPG-ELISA identified 9/10 (90%) as positive, while just one sample (1/9) was detected as positive by the TLA-ELISA (Fig 6).

187.Rationale: The passage provides direct evidence that the LPG-ELISA has significantly better sensitivity for detecting subclinical infections (90% vs 10%). The claim in option 4 that the LPG-ELISA's advantages in stability and cost do not translate into better sensitivity is therefore incorrect based on the evidence presented.

188.Option: The inclusion of both individual-level variables and country-level indicators in predictive models enhances the accuracy of identifying migrants at risk of STH infections, particularly for overall STH infections and A. lumbricoides, but the benefit of country-level indicators is less pronounced for T. trichiura.

Pairwise, the difference in the AUCs is significant for M1, M2 and M3. Both scenarios highlight the importance of considering both country-level indicators and individual-level variables in the prediction of infection.

When predicting for individuals from both existing and new countries, M3 has statistically significantly better AUC than both M1 and M2 but had no significant difference in the AUC of M1 and M2. This shows that utilizing a combination of both country-level indicators and individual-level variables is more effective than a model based on either alone.

When predicting for existing countries, the AUC value for M2 is not significantly better than a random classifier. The AUC of M1 is significantly better than that of M2 and M3, and the AUC of M3 is significantly better than M2. This allows us to conclude that in this scenario there is no benefit to including the country-level indicators, and the best model is the one which accounts only for individual-level variables.
188.Rationale: The passage provides clear evidence that models incorporating both individual-level variables and country-level indicators (M3) outperformed models using only one type of variable in most scenarios. Specifically, for overall STH infections and A. lumbricoides, M3 showed significantly better AUC than M1 and M2. However, for T. trichiura, the benefit of country-level indicators was less pronounced, as M2 (country-level only) performed no better than random, while M1 (individual-level only) outperformed M3. This directly supports the inference that combining both types of variables enhances accuracy for most STH infections but not for T. trichiura.

188.Option: Since the time spent in Italy is not a significant predictor of STH infection, individual-level variables such as age and sex are more valuable than country-level indicators for identifying at-risk migrants.
Evidence: The first model (M1) accounts for the individual-level variables and an additional term accounting for the variance between individuals which could not be accounted for by the covariates and will be referred to as the individual random effect. The symbolic representation of this model is: (1)Where all the covariates are individual variables provided by the migrants. Specifically, is their age, is their sex, and is their time spent in Italy.

When predicting for existing countries, the AUC value for M2 is not significantly better than a random classifier. The AUC of M1 is significantly better than that of M2 and M3, and the AUC of M3 is significantly better than M2. This allows us to conclude that in this scenario there is no benefit to including the country-level indicators, and the best model is the one which accounts only for individual-level variables.
188.Rationale: While the passage does show that M1 (individual-level variables) outperformed M2 (country-level indicators) for T. trichiura, it does not state that time spent in Italy is not a significant predictor. In fact, the models included time in Italy as a variable. The claim that individual-level variables are 'more valuable' overall is not supported by the evidence, as M3 (combining both) outperformed M1 for overall STH infections and A. lumbricoides. The option misrepresents the data by generalizing the T. trichiura result to all STH infections.

188.Option: The use of country-level indicators such as the Human Development Index (HDI) and Worm Index can reliably predict STH infection status among migrants, even in the absence of individual-level data, due to their strong inverse relationship with parasite prevalence.
Evidence: The first model (M1) accounts for the individual-level variables and an additional term accounting for the variance between individuals which could not be accounted for by the covariates and will be referred to as the individual random effect.

The second model (M2) accounts for the country-level indicators and an additional term accounting for the variance between countries which could not be accounted for by the covariates which will be referred to as the country random effects.

When predicting for existing countries, the AUC value for M2 is not significantly better than a random classifier. The AUC of M1 is significantly better than that of M2 and M3, and the AUC of M3 is significantly better than M2. This allows us to conclude that in this scenario there is no benefit to including the country-level indicators, and the best model is the one which accounts only for individual-level variables.
188.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that M2 (country-level indicators only) performed no better than random for T. trichiura and was outperformed by M1 (individual-level variables) in that case. For overall STH infections, M2 performed better than M1 but worse than M3. The claim that country-level indicators can 'reliably predict' infection status in the absence of individual data is contradicted by the evidence, particularly for T. trichiura. The inverse relationship with HDI/Worm Index is not sufficient to ensure reliable prediction without individual data.

188.Option: The 'healthy migrant' phenomenon suggests that migrants are inherently less likely to carry STH infections, making country-level indicators redundant for risk prediction in this population.
Evidence: The data consists of 3,830 individuals (migrants) from 64 countries. Of these, 87.4% were males.

Fig 4 shows the prevalence of STH infections in the dataset aggregated by country showing higher prevalence in Africa and South-East Asia than in other WHO regions. In Africa, the country of origin with the highest prevalence is with Guinea Bissau with 25% STH prevalence among migrants. In South-East Asia, the country of origin with the highest prevalence is Bangladesh with 18.6% STH prevalence among migrants.
The overall STH prevalence was 10.1%, specifically 0.4% for A. lumbricoides, 6.4% for hookworms, and 3.3% for T. trichiura.
188.Rationale: The passage provides clear evidence of significant STH prevalence among migrants (10.1% overall), with notable variation by country of origin. The 'healthy migrant' phenomenon is not mentioned, and the data directly contradicts the claim that migrants are 'inherently less likely' to carry STH infections. Country-level indicators were shown to be useful in some scenarios, so the claim that they are 'redundant' is not supported by the evidence.

188.Option: The predictive performance of models using worm indices is superior to all other models, indicating that these indices should be prioritized in future screening programs for migrant populations.
Evidence: When making predictions about new individuals from existing countries, we observe that M3 has a significantly larger AUC than either M4WHO or M4GBD allowing us to conclude that in this scenario the inclusion of the information regarding whether a country is within the top 100 countries ranked by either Worm Index does not improve the model in terms of prediction.

Assessing the performance of the same models under the scenario where we predict for individuals from new countries, suggests that the inclusion of the additional information is associated with an improvement in model prediction. We conclude this since model M4WHO and M4GBD have significantly larger AUCs than M3.
The training sets contained data from only 38 and 36 individuals for the WHO and GBD subsets respectively.
188.Rationale: The passage shows mixed results for worm indices. While M4WHO and M4GBD outperformed M3 for new countries, they underperformed for existing countries. Additionally, the limited data in the WHO and GBD subsets (38 and 36 individuals) raises concerns about generalizability. The claim that worm indices are 'superior to all other models' is not supported, as M3 (combining individual and country-level variables) outperformed them in some scenarios. The evidence does not justify the conclusion that worm indices should be 'prioritized' over other approaches.

189.Option: The rapid shift in CCHFV variant abundance between 2011 and 2012, from A1 to A2 dominance, suggests that the A1 variant was outcompeted by A2 due to a higher intrinsic fitness advantage in the local environment.
Evidence: In 2011, 80% confirmed patients were infected with A1 virus variant (and 20% with A3). On the contrary, in 2012 we detected the A2 virus variant alone (we sequenced 92% confirmed CCHF cases). In 2013, again both A1 and A2 variants were present (9% and 50% confirmed cases, respectively).
189.Rationale: The passage provides evidence of a rapid shift in variant abundance between 2011 and 2012, but it does not provide any evidence about the mechanisms behind this shift. The claim that A2 outcompeted A1 due to a higher intrinsic fitness advantage is a hypothesis that cannot be derived from the data presented. The passage does not discuss viral fitness, competitive advantages, or environmental factors that might explain this shift.

189.Option: The presence of multiple CCHFV genetic variants in Kosovo, including the highly divergent A5 clade, suggests that the virus has been introduced into the region from multiple independent sources over time.
Evidence: We obtained 37 partial CCHFV S segment sequences [...] All sequences clustered in the European CCHF genetic lineage V, along with previously published CCHFV sequences from Kosovo (Figure 1A). [...] The most divergent sequences clustered into group A5. This cluster was also most divergent compared to other sequences in the European genetic lineage V (maximum nucleotide distance within the European genetic lineage V was 2.9, that is to the Turkish GQ337053 sequence).
189.Rationale: The passage describes the presence of multiple CCHFV genetic variants in Kosovo, including the highly divergent A5 clade. However, it does not provide evidence that these variants were introduced from multiple independent sources. The data could be explained by in situ evolution rather than multiple introductions. The passage does not discuss the origin of these variants or provide evidence of multiple introductions.

189.Option: The relative abundances of CCHFV viral variants in Kosovo are dynamic and subject to significant changes over time, which may be influenced by ecological factors such as climate patterns.
Evidence: From 2001 to 2010 the two major phylogenetic groups (A1 and A2) occurred in similar abundances. However, significant shifts in abundances of the two groups occurred in the following years. In 2011, 80% confirmed patients were infected with A1 virus variant (and 20% with A3). On the contrary, in 2012 we detected the A2 virus variant alone (we sequenced 92% confirmed CCHF cases). In 2013, again both A1 and A2 variants were present (9% and 50% confirmed cases, respectively).
189.Rationale: The passage provides clear evidence of dynamic changes in the relative abundances of CCHFV viral variants over time. The significant shifts in variant dominance from 2011 to 2012 and the subsequent changes in 2013 support the inference that these abundances are not static. While the passage does not explicitly mention climate patterns, it is reasonable to infer that ecological factors, including climate patterns, could influence these dynamics given the tick-borne nature of the virus and the known impact of climate on tick populations.

189.Option: The high genetic diversity of CCHFV in Kosovo, particularly in the S segment, indicates that the virus has undergone rapid evolutionary adaptation in response to host immune pressures over the past two decades.
Evidence: We obtained 37 partial CCHFV S segment sequences [...] Overall identity of the sequences ranged from 98.8–100% and we detected three amino acid changes; S272N (present in samples KS153 and KS149), K316R (present in samples KS208, KS213 and KS223) and V327I (present in samples KS172 and KS88)(amino acid positions are numbered relative to the nucleoprotein sequence of CCHFV strain Kosovo Hoti, accession number: AAZ32529).
189.Rationale: The passage describes the presence of some amino acid changes in the S segment, but it does not provide evidence that these changes are the result of rapid evolutionary adaptation in response to host immune pressures. The data on genetic diversity is limited and does not support the claim of rapid evolutionary adaptation over the past two decades. The passage does not discuss host immune pressures or their role in viral evolution.

189.Option: The geographic clustering of CCHFV variants in Kosovo, particularly the concentration of A1 in the south and A2 in the north, indicates that regional differences in healthcare access or reporting bias may be influencing the observed distribution.
Evidence: The two most abundant phylogenetic groups (A1 and A2) are present in almost all studied municipalities. However, sequences from group A1 are present in southern parts in greater abundance than in the northern parts and vice versa for A2. [...] The number of sequences we obtained is comparable to the incidence of CCHF in each municipality. On average we sequenced approximately 50% of total confirmed cases in each municipality. Therefore our results portray a realistic picture of the distribution of viral variants in the endemic area.
189.Rationale: The passage provides evidence of geographic clustering of CCHFV variants in Kosovo, with A1 concentrated in the south and A2 in the north. However, it explicitly states that the number of sequences obtained is comparable to the incidence of CCHF in each municipality, and that approximately 50% of total confirmed cases were sequenced. This suggests that the observed distribution is not due to regional differences in healthcare access or reporting bias, but rather reflects the true geographic distribution of the viral variants.

190.Option: This longitudinal study demonstrates the noisy and unsettled nature of the nasopharyngeal microbiota which does not seem to hold a stable individual pattern such as that described in high-income/low density European populations.
Evidence: The data from Maela do not resemble the studies in European populations. At each age point the mean in-distances were lower than the out-distances, but the difference was not significant (Supplementary S5 Table) meaning that there is great similarity between the microbial communities carried by cohort members.
Some studies have described a wide variation between individuals’ microbiota composition [17] or a “personalised microbiota” [18].
190.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that the microbial community patterns in Maela do not resemble those in European populations, where personalized microbiota patterns are described. The analysis of Jaccard distances shows that the mean in-distances (between samples from the same child) were not significantly lower than out-distances (between different children), indicating a lack of stable individual patterns. This directly supports the inference that the nasopharyngeal microbiota in this cohort is 'noisy and unsettled' with no stable individual pattern as seen in high-income populations.

190.Option: The unclassified Flavobacteriaceae I OTU is observed in 65.2% of samples in this cohort and often at high proportional abundance, and its presence may be linked to respiratory illness episodes.
Evidence: The unclassified Flavobacteriaceae I rises in average abundance until 12 months (Fig 3d).

There is some support for the observation that Dolosigranulum is negatively associated with Streptococcus pneumoniae in the squat distributions seen in (d) however Corynebacterium II does not appear to play a part in that interaction.

The inverse Simpson measure of diversity (Supplementary S1 Table) was not found to change predictably when comparing sequential swabs from before pneumonia to the time of clinical presentation: 20 pneumonia episodes coincided with an increase in the diversity measure while 23 appeared to have a decrease in diversity.
190.Rationale: While the passage confirms the high prevalence of unclassified Flavobacteriaceae I (65.2% of samples) and its increasing abundance with age, it does not establish a direct link between this OTU and respiratory illness episodes. The study found no consistent association between microbiota profiles and pneumonia, and the specific role of Flavobacteriaceae I in illness is not supported by the evidence presented. The claim about its presence being 'linked to respiratory illness episodes' is not derivable from the passage.

190.Option: The high population density and reduced geographical movement in Maela may lead to exposure to a less diverse pool of potential colonisers, which could explain the lack of stable individual microbiota patterns.
Evidence: The data from Maela do not resemble the studies in European populations. At each age point the mean in-distances were lower than the out-distances, but the difference was not significant (Supplementary S5 Table) meaning that there is great similarity between the microbial communities carried by cohort members.
The camp has a population of approximately 40,000 displaced persons from Myanmar, predominantly of Karen ethnicity, living in a 4km2 area.
190.Rationale: The passage does mention the high population density of Maela (40,000 people in 4km²), but it does not discuss population density or geographical movement as factors influencing microbiota patterns. The lack of stable individual microbiota patterns is attributed to the data itself, not to environmental or demographic factors like population density. The inference about population density as an explanatory factor requires external assumptions not supported by the passage.

191.Option: The ZIKV NS1 IgG MIA demonstrated 100% sensitivity and 87.9% specificity, primarily due to cross-reactivity with the sDENV panel, which limits its utility in flavivirus-endemic regions.
Evidence: For the ZIKV NS1 IgG MIA, the overall sensitivity was 100% and specificity 87.9% (Table 2).
191.Rationale: The passage states that the ZIKV NS1 IgG MIA has 100% sensitivity and 87.9% specificity. However, the option incorrectly attributes the 87.9% specificity to cross-reactivity with the sDENV panel. The passage does not make this specific causal claim. The study actually demonstrates that the rMFI ratio approach can overcome cross-reactivity issues, not that cross-reactivity limits utility. The option misrepresents the study's findings.

191.Option: The use of a cutoff rMFI ratio of 0.62 for ZIKV NS1 to DENV1 NS1 was determined by ROC analysis and enables reliable distinction between primary ZIKV and secondary DENV infections.
Evidence: Using the rOD ratio of ZIKV NS1 to DENV1 NS1 with a cutoff at 0.24, we can distinguish ZIKVwprDENV and sDENV panels. Since the same sDENV and ZIKVwprDENV panels recognized both DENV1 and ZIKV NS1 proteins in IgG MIA (Fig 2A and 2B), we calculated the ratio of relative median fluorescence intensity (rMFI) of ZIKV NS1 to that of DENV1 NS1 and found that a cutoff of the rMFI ratio at 0.62, as determined by ROC analysis, can distinguish these two panels with a sensitivity of 88.9% and specificity of 91.7% (Fig 2E).
191.Rationale: The passage describes a cutoff rMFI ratio of 0.62 for ZIKV NS1 to DENV1 NS1 determined by ROC analysis that distinguishes ZIKVwprDENV and sDENV panels. However, the option incorrectly states this cutoff distinguishes primary ZIKV and secondary DENV infections. The study actually focuses on distinguishing ZIKVwprDENV (ZIKV with previous DENV) from sDENV (secondary DENV), not primary ZIKV from secondary DENV. The option misrepresents the specific distinction being made.

191.Option: The combination of DENV1 to DENV4 NS1 IgG MIA increased the sensitivity to 94.3% while maintaining a specificity of 97.2%, suggesting the feasibility and application of this multiplex NS1 IgG MIA to detect DENV infection rather than distinguish DENV serotypes.
Evidence: The overall sensitivity of each DENV (DENV1, DENV2, DENV3) NS1 IgG MIA to detect different DENV infections ranged from 73.6 to 90.1% and specificity from 98.1 to 100% (Table 2). Interestingly, combination of four DENV NS1 IgG MIA increased the sensitivity to 94.5%, while maintaining the specificity of 97.2%, suggesting that this multiplex assay can be applied to detect DENV infections rather than distinguish different DENV serotypes.
191.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that combining DENV1-4 NS1 IgG MIA increased sensitivity to 94.5% while maintaining 97.2% specificity, and that this suggests the multiplex assay can detect DENV infections rather than distinguish serotypes. This directly supports the option's claim about the analytical approach and its application.

191.Option: The multiplex NS1 IgG MIA can distinguish ZIKVwprDENV and sDENV infections using the rMFI ratio of ZIKV NS1 to DENV1 or DENV2 NS1 with a sensitivity of 83.3–94.4% and specificity of 90.9–100.0%.
Evidence: we calculated the ratio of relative median fluorescence intensity (rMFI) of ZIKV NS1 to that of DENV1 NS1 and found that a cutoff of the rMFI ratio at 0.62, as determined by ROC analysis, can distinguish these two panels with a sensitivity of 88.9% and specificity of 91.7% (Fig 2E). Similarly, a cutoff of the rMFI ratio at 0.62 was able to distinguish these two panels with a sensitivity of 94.4% and specificity of 90.9% (Fig 2F). Similar observations were found for post-convalescent-phase sDENV and ZIKVwprDENV panels; these two panels can be distinguished by a cutoff (0.62) of the rMFI ratio for ZIKV NS1 to DENV1 NS1 or DENV2 NS1 with a sensitivity/specificity of 90.0/100% or 83.3/100%, respectively (Fig 2G and 2H).
191.Rationale: The passage provides multiple pieces of evidence showing that the multiplex NS1 IgG MIA can distinguish ZIKVwprDENV and sDENV infections using rMFI ratios of ZIKV NS1 to DENV1 or DENV2 NS1. The sensitivity and specificity values mentioned in the option (83.3-94.4% and 90.9-100.0%) are directly supported by the data presented in the passage.

191.Option: The high-throughput and multiplex capacity of microsphere immunoassay (MIA) allows for simultaneous detection of IgG antibodies against DENV, ZIKV, and WNV, enabling serosurveillance in flavivirus-endemic regions.
Evidence: With its high-throughput and multiplex (up to 100-plex) capacity, microsphere immunoassay (MIA) has been employed in the detection of cytokines, transplantation and transfusion antigens, and various bacterial and viral pathogens [40–43].
Table 1 summarizes the results of all samples tested with different NS1 proteins (DENV1, DENV2, DENV3, DENV4, DENV1, 2, 3 or 4, ZIKV and WNV) in the IgG MIA.
191.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that MIA has high-throughput and multiplex capacity and provides evidence that it can detect IgG antibodies against DENV, ZIKV, and WNV. The study's application of this technology in flavivirus-endemic regions is implied by the context of the research and the specific mention of these regions in the discussion of the need for such tests.

192.Option: The use of human excreta as fertilizer in the Red River Delta is a sustainable practice that supports long-term soil fertility and reduces dependency on imported inorganic fertilizers.
Evidence: The Vietnamese Rice Knowledge Bank recommends spreading inorganic NPK 16-16-8 or 17-12-5 fertilizers at a rate of 415–550 kg/ha for each rice harvest in the RRD [15]. These percentages are substantially higher than all NPK levels observed in organic human excreta, regardless of whether it was fresh or composted (Tables 3 and 4).

We determined that farmers should apply fresh excreta (A) at a rate of 3459 kg/ha to meet but not exceed phosphorus and potassium requirements. At this rate, 425 kg of fresh excreta would be used to fertilize an average sized rice paddy, which is 65% of the total excreta produced by an average RRD family per year. Only 43% of the minimum nitrogen requirement would be met, indicating that farmers would need to supplement a nitrogen-based fertilizer (urea or diammonium phosphate (DAP)) for optimal rice production.
192.Rationale: The passage provides evidence that human excreta is not a complete substitute for inorganic fertilizers, as it lacks sufficient nitrogen content to meet soil requirements. Farmers would need to supplement with inorganic nitrogen fertilizers for optimal rice production. This undermines the claim that human excreta use reduces dependency on imported inorganic fertilizers. Additionally, the study found that composting scenarios (B and C) did not produce sufficient nutrients to meet soil requirements without supplementation, further challenging the sustainability claim.

192.Option: Farmers who use fresh human excreta are at a significantly higher risk of hookworm infection compared to those who use composted excreta or inorganic fertilizers.
Evidence: Our analysis found that farmers who fertilized rice paddies with fresh human excreta were 24% (RR = 1.24, 95% CI: 1.13–1.37, p-value<0.001; Fig 2) more likely to be infected with one or more STHs than farmers who did not handle fresh excreta. However, the infection risks of individual helminths were lower than the risk of any STH, and were not significant. Risk of hookworm infection (RR = 1.02, 95% CI: 0.5–2.06, p-value = 0.96) was lowest compared to other helminth types (A. lumbricoides RR = 1.17, 95% CI: 0.87–1.58, p-value = 0.29; T. trichiura RR = 1.38, 95% CI: 0.79–2.42, p-value = 0.26; Fig 2).
192.Rationale: The meta-analysis found that while farmers using fresh excreta had a 24% higher risk of any STH infection, the risk for hookworm specifically was not statistically significant (RR = 1.02, 95% CI: 0.5–2.06, p-value = 0.96). The option incorrectly states that hookworm risk is significantly higher, when the data actually shows no significant difference. Additionally, the passage states that composting scenarios (B and C) met WHO standards for helminth inactivation, implying these methods would not carry the same infection risk as fresh excreta.

192.Option: Although farmers using fresh human excreta benefitted from the largest cost-savings in fertilizer expenditure, the direct and indirect societal costs incurred due to prolonged STH infection would likely outweigh these savings.
Evidence: Farmers using fresh excreta would need to purchase a nitrogen-based fertilizer (urea or DAP), which would cost an average of 157,000 VND/year if applied up to the minimum recommended level. This implies an annual cost savings of 847,000 VND for scenario A.

Our analysis found that farmers who fertilized rice paddies with fresh human excreta were 24% (RR = 1.24, 95% CI: 1.13–1.37, p-value<0.001; Fig 2) more likely to be infected with one or more STHs than farmers who did not handle fresh excreta.

The Vietnamese government, in collaboration with international aid organizations, has conducted targeted STH elimination campaigns in past decades, and we restricted the time period to avoid overestimating risk.
192.Rationale: The passage provides evidence that using fresh human excreta results in the largest cost savings (847,000 VND/year) compared to other scenarios. However, it also shows that farmers using fresh excreta have a 24% higher risk of STH infection. While the passage does not explicitly calculate the societal costs of STH infection, it is a reasonable epidemiological inference that the direct medical costs and indirect costs (e.g., lost productivity, long-term health impacts) of STH infections could outweigh the fertilizer cost savings. This conclusion is supported by the fact that the Vietnamese government has conducted targeted STH elimination campaigns, indicating that STH infections are a significant public health concern.

192.Option: The health risks associated with using fresh human excreta as fertilizer are minimal because most infected individuals remain asymptomatic and do not require medical treatment.
Evidence: These parasites are particularly prevalent in regions with warm, moist climates, and are included in the category of tropical neglected diseases associated with poverty.

It is common practice for household members to add a handful of kitchen ash after using a latrine, as this reduces smell. A recent study characterizing A. lumbricoides egg die-off during excreta composting suggests that adding lime reliably accelerates egg inactivation so that WHO criteria for safe handling (<1 viable egg/g total solids) are met by 153 days [7].
192.Rationale: The passage does not provide any evidence about the proportion of infected individuals who remain asymptomatic. Instead, it emphasizes that STHs are tropical neglected diseases associated with poverty, implying significant health impacts. The mention of lime accelerating egg inactivation suggests that these parasites are a real health concern requiring specific interventions. The option incorrectly assumes minimal health risks without supporting evidence from the passage.

192.Option: The WHO standard of six-month composting is the most cost-effective method for safe excreta reuse in rice farming communities in northern Vietnam.
Evidence: WHO guidelines recommend that farmers compost human excreta for six months prior to application in order to inactivate STH eggs and larvae, and thereby reduce spread between people [5]. A recent meta-analysis showed that the proportion of Shigella infections that present with dysentery has been decreasing, and that Shigella infections overall had a stronger association with mortality than Shigella-associated cases of dysentery [7]. Furthermore, even in the absence of diarrheal symptoms, Shigella has been associated with impaired linear growth [6,8,9].
199.Rationale: The passage states that Shigella infections are associated with impaired linear growth, but it does not provide any evidence linking the seasonal patterns of Shigella in Tanzania to the seasonality of malnutrition. The option makes a causal claim that cannot be derived from the passage. The passage does not mention malnutrition seasonality in Tanzania or establish a direct causal relationship between Shigella and malnutrition.

199.Option: Because Shigella culture has low sensitivity, especially in young children, and because recent antibiotic use was not associated with reduced detection, the use of culture-based diagnostics should be abandoned in favor of molecular methods in all low-resource settings.
Evidence: Of 30,678 stools tested by both qPCR and culture, 3,372 (11.0%) were positive by qPCR and 280 (0.9%) were positive by culture. Considering qPCR the gold standard, the overall sensitivity of culture was 6.6% (Table 3). Specificity was uniformly high, at more than 99% at all sites.

Culture positivity was strongly associated with age, such that attributable diarrhea stools among younger children were less likely to be culture positive (Table 4). Presence of blood had the strongest association with culture positivity (aRR: 1.84, 95% CI: 1.28, 2.65), but culture still missed more than 70% of dysentery cases. Diarrhea severity was not associated with culture positivity, and caregiver-reported fever was inversely associated with culture positivity. Recent macrolide treatment was also associated with reduced detection by culture (aRR: 0.57, 95% CI: 0.30, 1.08; Table 4).
199.Rationale: The passage provides evidence that Shigella culture has low sensitivity, especially in young children, but it does not provide evidence that recent antibiotic use was not associated with reduced detection. In fact, the passage states that recent macrolide treatment was associated with reduced detection by culture. The option makes a recommendation to abandon culture-based diagnostics in all low-resource settings, which is not supported by the passage. The passage does not provide evidence about the feasibility or cost-effectiveness of molecular methods in all low-resource settings.

199.Option: The presence of Shigella in non-diarrheal stools with no clinical symptoms indicates that most Shigella infections are subclinical and therefore pose no public health concern.
Evidence: The prevalence of Shigella was 11.5% (n = 4744) overall, and was higher in diarrheal stools than non-diarrheal stools (18.4% vs. 10.1%; p<0.0001). Shigella prevalence in non-diarrheal stools varied from 4.9%-17.8% across sites. Almost half (611/1407, 43.4%) of children with Shigella detected in one or more non-diarrheal stools never had a diarrhea episode in which Shigella was detected, and almost two-thirds (900/1407, 64.0%) never experienced a Shigella-attributable diarrhea episode.
Furthermore, even in the absence of diarrheal symptoms, Shigella has been associated with impaired linear growth [6,8,9].
199.Rationale: The passage states that Shigella is associated with impaired linear growth even in the absence of diarrheal symptoms, indicating that subclinical infections do pose a public health concern. The option incorrectly concludes that subclinical infections pose no public health concern, which is directly contradicted by the evidence in the passage.

199.Option: The high burden of Shigella disease documented in MAL-ED highlights the potential utility of Shigella vaccines, as almost all children were exposed to Shigella by two years of age in most sites, suggesting a pathogen-specific population-based prevention strategy is warranted.
Evidence: Shigella is the second leading cause of diarrhea morbidity and mortality among children in low and middle-income countries, accounting for approximately 60,000 deaths in 2016 [1].
By two years of age, 82.0% (n = 1407) of children had been infected with Shigella (Fig 1A), and 29.6% (n = 507) had at least one episode of Shigella-attributable diarrhea (Fig 1B).

Increasing rates of fluoroquinolone and macrolide resistance have highlighted the need for novel interventions, and particularly increased the urgency of the development of a Shigella vaccine [10], which may offer a more sustainable solution.
199.Rationale: The passage provides evidence of the high burden of Shigella disease, with almost all children exposed to Shigella by two years of age in most sites. The passage also states that increasing rates of antibiotic resistance have highlighted the need for novel interventions, particularly the development of a Shigella vaccine. This directly supports the inference that a pathogen-specific population-based prevention strategy, such as a vaccine, is warranted.

199.Option: The high incidence of Shigella-attributable diarrhea in older children suggests that interventions should focus on improving hygiene practices in school-aged children rather than infants.
Evidence: The overall incidence of Shigella-attributable diarrhea was 31.8 cases (95% CI: 29.6, 34.2) per 100 child-years. Incidence of Shigella-attributable diarrhea was higher in older age groups (incidence rate ratio: 1.75, 95% CI: 1.70, 1.81 per 6-month increase in age).

In univariable analysis, unimproved sanitation, crowding (2+ people living in a single room), <10 years of maternal education, having 3+ live children, initiating complementary foods before 3 months, recent diarrhea, antibiotic use (particularly fluoroquinolone use), and lower anthropometric measurements prior to sample collection were also associated with higher risk of Shigella infection (Table 5).
199.Rationale: The passage states that the incidence of Shigella-attributable diarrhea is higher in older age groups, but it does not provide evidence that interventions should focus on school-aged children rather than infants. The passage also provides evidence that risk factors such as unimproved sanitation and crowding are relevant across all age groups. The option makes a recommendation that is not supported by the evidence in the passage.

200.Option: The mating rhythm of Cx. quinquefasciatus is more sensitive to clk gene disruption than that of Ae. albopictus, suggesting that the molecular clock in nocturnal mosquitoes is more dependent on clk for rhythmic output.
Evidence: The mating rate of AalclkΔ293 at night (45.8%) was significantly higher than that of WT group (30.8%; GLM, χ2 = 5.65, d.f. = 1, P = 0.017), but there was no significant difference between AalclkΔ293 and WT group in daytime mating rates (GLM, χ2 = 1.61, d.f. = 1, P = 0.205) (Fig 2E).

The total mating rate of CxqclkΔ98 at night (0.0%) was significantly lower than that of WT group (13.3%; GLM, χ2 = 18.46, d.f. = 1, P < 0.0001). The total mating rate of CxqclkΔ98 during daytime (9.2%) was significantly higher than that of WT group (1.7%; χ2 = 5.23, d.f. = 1, P = 0.022) (Fig 2J).
200.Rationale: The evidence shows that disruption of the clk gene in Cx. quinquefasciatus (CxqclkΔ98) caused a complete loss of nighttime mating activity (0.0% vs 13.3% in WT) and a significant increase in daytime mating (9.2% vs 1.7% in WT). In contrast, disruption of the clk gene in Ae. albopictus (AalclkΔ293) only caused a moderate increase in nighttime mating (45.8% vs 30.8% in WT) with no significant change in daytime mating. This indicates that Cx. quinquefasciatus mating rhythm is more sensitive to clk gene disruption, supporting the inference that nocturnal mosquitoes may be more dependent on clk for maintaining their mating rhythms.

200.Option: The distinct diel mating rhythms of Ae. albopictus and Cx. quinquefasciatus are regulated by the temporal expression of the desat1 gene, which is controlled by the core clock gene clk.
Evidence: The desat1 transcript levels in the heads and bodies of Ae. albopictus exhibited oscillations with expression peaks in both day and night (S3 Fig). The Cx. quinquefasciatus desat1 transcripts had a different oscillation trend with peak abundance at the moment of the light/dark transition (S3 Fig).

The abundance of desat1 transcripts in AalclkΔ293 males was high during light/dark transition at ZT12. The peak and trough values were out of phase compared to WT controls (Fig 3A). The desat1 transcripts profile in CxqclkΔ98 males was also out of phase and had lowest abundance during light/dark transition which was opposite to that of WT controls (Fig 3C).
200.Rationale: The evidence shows that the temporal expression of desat1 is under circadian control (regulated by the core clock gene clk) in both species, but with distinct patterns. In Ae. albopictus, desat1 shows oscillations with peaks in both day and night, while in Cx. quinquefasciatus, desat1 peaks at the light/dark transition. Disruption of the clk gene causes phase shifts in desat1 expression in both species. This supports the inference that the distinct diel mating rhythms are regulated by the temporal expression of desat1, which is controlled by the core clock gene clk.

200.Option: The circadian clock is controlled genetically, and mutation in 'clock genes' can change rhythmic behaviour in animals, including insects, humans and other species.
Evidence: These genetically-encoded circadian clocks can respond to environmental cues (termed zeitgeber) such as light and control the expression of downstream output genes throughout the body, thereby temporally controlling the behavior and physiology to accommodate and exploit daily recurring environmental changes [5,9].
The mating activity rhythm of Ae. albopictus is controlled by an endogenous clock and can be entrained to daily LD cycles.
The mating rhythm of Cx. quinquefasciatus is also controlled by an endogenous clock and can be entrained to daily LD cycles.
200.Rationale: While the passage does establish that circadian clocks are genetically encoded and that mutations in clock genes can affect rhythmic behavior in these mosquito species, it does not provide evidence about humans or other species. The option makes a broad generalization beyond what is supported by the evidence in the passage. The passage only discusses insects (specifically mosquitoes) and does not mention humans or other species in the context of clock gene mutations affecting rhythmic behavior.

200.Option: The desat1 gene directly determines the timing of mating behavior in both mosquito species, independent of the core clock gene clk.
Evidence: The abundance of desat1 transcripts in AalclkΔ293 males was high during light/dark transition at ZT12. The peak and trough values were out of phase compared to WT controls (Fig 3A). The desat1 transcripts profile in CxqclkΔ98 males was also out of phase and had lowest abundance during light/dark transition which was opposite to that of WT controls (Fig 3C).
desat1 knockdown has no effect on the overall mating rhythm of Ae. albopictus and Cx. quinquefasciatus, but significantly reduces mating rate under LD cycles.
200.Rationale: The evidence shows that desat1 expression is regulated by the core clock gene clk, as disruption of clk causes phase shifts in desat1 expression. Additionally, while desat1 knockdown reduces mating rate, it does not affect the overall mating rhythm. This contradicts the claim that desat1 directly determines the timing of mating behavior independent of the core clock gene clk.

200.Option: The phase difference in the temporal expression of desat1 between Ae. albopictus and Cx. quinquefasciatus is due to differences in the light sensitivity of their respective clock genes.
Evidence: The desat1 transcript levels in the heads and bodies of Ae. albopictus exhibited oscillations with expression peaks in both day and night (S3 Fig). The Cx. quinquefasciatus desat1 transcripts had a different oscillation trend with peak abundance at the moment of the light/dark transition (S3 Fig).

The abundance of desat1 transcripts in AalclkΔ293 males was high during light/dark transition at ZT12. The peak and trough values were out of phase compared to WT controls (Fig 3A). The desat1 transcripts profile in CxqclkΔ98 males was also out of phase and had lowest abundance during light/dark transition which was opposite to that of WT controls (Fig 3C).
200.Rationale: The evidence shows that the phase difference in desat1 expression is due to the regulation by the core clock gene clk, not differences in light sensitivity of the clock genes. The passage does not provide any evidence about differences in light sensitivity of the clock genes between the two species. The phase differences are explained by the distinct temporal expression patterns of desat1 under the control of the core clock gene clk.

201.Option: The increase in CFA positivity from 2 to 8 cases in TAS 2, despite passing the threshold, indicates that the WHO criteria for stopping MDA are too lenient and should be revised to prevent resurgence.
Evidence: TAS 1 was conducted in 2015 using a cluster survey of 30 primary schools, and two children were identified as antigen-positive using the rapid filariasis test strip (FTS) (Alere, Scarborough, ME), which detects circulating filarial antigenemia (CFA) for adult worms. This was below the critical cutoff of 2% for regions with Culex vectors, and MDA in the department was stopped. Nippes underwent TAS 2 in 2017 and passed again, however the number of CFA-positive children had increased to eight.

Of the 1,927 participants who completed FTS testing, 29 were CFA-positive, for a total positivity rate of 1.5% (Table 2). Of these, 21 (72%) were from Miragoane, and each index case in this commune led to the identification of CFA-positive participants, with the number found per index case ranging from 6 to 8 (Fig 2).
201.Rationale: The passage provides evidence of an increase in CFA positivity from 2 to 8 cases in TAS 2, but it does not provide evidence that the WHO criteria are too lenient. The passage states that the WHO criteria were met in both TAS 1 and TAS 2, and MDA was stopped. The increase in CFA positivity is presented as a concern for ongoing transmission, not as evidence that the criteria are flawed. The passage also mentions that additional research is needed to better determine the utility of TAS for measuring transmission interruption, but it does not suggest that the criteria should be revised. Therefore, the inference that the WHO criteria are too lenient cannot be directly derived from the passage.

201.Option: The fact that random sampling in neighboring EAs identified only a low number of CFA-positive cases suggests that transmission in Miragoane is isolated and unlikely to spread to surrounding communes.
Evidence: Random sampling in a neighboring EA identified only 3 positive participants for a positivity rate of 0.6% (Table 2).
Random sampling in a neighboring EA found one additional CFA-positive participant for 3 of the 6 index cases.
201.Rationale: The passage provides evidence that random sampling in neighboring EAs identified a low number of CFA-positive cases, but it does not provide evidence that transmission in Miragoane is isolated or unlikely to spread. The low number of CFA-positive cases in neighboring EAs could be due to various factors, such as effective vector control, low population density, or other barriers to transmission. The passage does not make any claims about the likelihood of transmission spreading to surrounding communes. Therefore, the inference that transmission is isolated cannot be directly derived from the passage.

201.Option: The higher CFA prevalence in Miragoane (2.4%) compared to the 2% threshold used to initiate TAS suggests ongoing transmission despite the cessation of MDA, and the presence of CFA-positive children under 7 years old indicates recent infection, which may require targeted intervention.
Evidence: Of the 1,927 participants who completed FTS testing, 29 were CFA-positive, for a total positivity rate of 1.5% (Table 2). Of these, 21 (72%) were from Miragoane, and each index case in this commune led to the identification of CFA-positive participants, with the number found per index case ranging from 6 to 8 (Fig 2).
Two CFA positives were found among participants aged <10 years (positivity 0.8%), both of which resided in Miragoane and were between the ages of 4 and 6 years.

The current World Health Organization (WHO) criteria states that TAS can be initiated once pre-TAS sentinel site and spot checks show <2% LF antigenemia among the population over 5 years old in a given evaluation unit (EU), which is typically a district or a combination of districts [4].
201.Rationale: The passage provides evidence that the CFA prevalence in Miragoane is 2.4%, which is higher than the 2% threshold used to initiate TAS. This suggests that transmission may still be occurring despite the cessation of MDA. The presence of CFA-positive children under 7 years old, who are of sufficient age to develop a mature parasite but young enough for a current infection to indicate recent transmission, further supports the inference of ongoing transmission. The passage also mentions that the WHO encourages program managers to conduct follow-up surveys in communities where antigen-positive children are detected, which aligns with the suggestion that targeted intervention may be required. Therefore, the inference that there is ongoing transmission and the need for targeted intervention can be directly derived from the passage.

201.Option: The presence of CFA-positive children under 7 years in Miragoane, combined with the high seropositivity in older age groups, suggests that the population has experienced a prolonged period of low-level transmission, leading to widespread immune exposure.
Evidence: Two CFA positives were found among participants aged <10 years (positivity 0.8%), both of which resided in Miragoane and were between the ages of 4 and 6 years.

Seropositivity was higher than CFA positivity across every category, however the size of the difference varied (Table 2). The highest number of seropositive participants was found in Miragoane (43, 5.1% positive), but the highest percent seropositive was found in Petit-Trou de Nippes (6.0% positive), which had no CFA positives.

Seropositivity was higher among male (5.8%) than female (3.7%) participants (P = 0.02) and was higher among participants who did not sleep under a bed net (5.1%) than those who did (2.3%) (P = 0.02) (Table 2 and Figs 4 and S3).
201.Rationale: The passage provides evidence of CFA-positive children under 7 years in Miragoane and high seropositivity in older age groups, but it does not provide evidence that the population has experienced a prolonged period of low-level transmission leading to widespread immune exposure. The high seropositivity in older age groups could be due to past exposure during the period of active transmission, but the passage does not provide evidence of a prolonged period of low-level transmission. The presence of CFA-positive children indicates recent infection, but it does not necessarily indicate a prolonged period of low-level transmission. Therefore, the inference about a prolonged period of low-level transmission cannot be directly derived from the passage.

201.Option: The high seropositivity rate among males and older adults in Miragoane suggests that these groups are more likely to have been exposed to LF during the period of active transmission, indicating a higher risk of reactivation of latent infection.
Evidence: Seropositivity was higher among male (5.8%) than female (3.7%) participants (P = 0.02) and was higher among participants who did not sleep under a bed net (5.1%) than those who did (2.3%) (P = 0.02) (Table 2 and Figs 4 and S3).
Seropositivity was higher than CFA positivity across every category, however the size of the difference varied (Table 2).
201.Rationale: The passage provides evidence of high seropositivity rates among males and older adults in Miragoane, but it does not provide evidence that these groups are at a higher risk of reactivation of latent infection. The high seropositivity rate among males and older adults could be due to various factors, such as differences in exposure patterns or immune responses. The passage does not mention reactivation of latent infection as a concern. Therefore, the inference about a higher risk of reactivation cannot be directly derived from the passage.

202.Option: The higher abundance of haemoglobin subunit beta-2 in CBA/Ca mice is directly responsible for their resistance to Ascaris by enhancing oxygen delivery to the liver.
Evidence: The most abundantly expressed proteins observed in CBA/Ca control samples are haemoglobin subunit beta-2, S-methylmethionine, and putative hydroxypyruvate isomerase. The log2 fold change for haemoglobin subunit beta-2 was the highest observed in the dataset, being 13.6.
202.Rationale: While the passage notes that haemoglobin subunit beta-2 is highly abundant in CBA/Ca control samples, it does not establish a causal relationship between this protein and resistance to Ascaris. The option makes a direct causal claim that is not supported by the evidence presented. The passage does not mention oxygen delivery to the liver or any mechanism linking haemoglobin to resistance.

202.Option: The downregulation of ribosomal proteins under infection in both strains indicates that Ascaris actively suppresses host translation to evade immune detection.
Evidence: Under infection however, both CBA/Ca and C57BL/6J mice had significantly lower abundances for many ribosomal proteins (Fig 2). KEGG analysis further confirmed this finding demonstrating that CBA/Ca had more intrinsically abundant ribosomal proteins than C57BL/6J, a difference that became more pronounced under infection (S2B Fig).
202.Rationale: The passage describes a downregulation of ribosomal proteins under infection in both strains but does not attribute this to active suppression by Ascaris. The option introduces a causal mechanism (Ascaris actively suppressing host translation) that is not supported by the evidence. The data only show a correlation, not a causal relationship or an explanation for the downregulation.

202.Option: The higher abundance of mitochondrial proteins and presumed reactive oxygen species (ROS) levels in CBA/Ca mice may create a prohibitive oxidative environment that restricts Ascaris larval migration, contributing to their resistance to infection.
Evidence: Mitochondrial associated proteins were consistently differentially abundant among all samples. Of the 2,307 proteins identified and reported here, 324 were associated with the mitochondrion. A higher abundance of mitochondrial proteins was generally observed in CBA/Ca mice both with and without infection (Table 1; Fig 1C) in comparison to C57BL/6J mice, although an increase in mitochondrial protein abundances was observed for both strains under infection (Fig 2).

KEGG analysis also indicated that there was a generally lower abundance of ETC proteins in C57BL/6J infected mice, compared to their CBA/Ca counterparts. However Complex I proteins, were less abundant in C57BL/6J infected mice, when compared to their controls (S2A Fig).

Proteins of the tricarboxylic acid (TCA) cycle were intrinsically more abundant in CBA/Ca control mice, when compared to their C57BL/6J counterparts. This difference between the two strains was observed also under infection. When infected, both mouse strains had increased abundance of these proteins. However, C57BL/6J infected mice had higher abundances for the entire TCA cycle (S3 Fig).
202.Rationale: The passage provides evidence that CBA/Ca mice have a higher abundance of mitochondrial proteins, including those involved in the electron transport chain and TCA cycle, both in control and infected states. This suggests a higher metabolic activity and potential for ROS production. The option correctly infers that this could create an oxidative environment that restricts Ascaris larval migration, aligning with the observed resistance in CBA/Ca mice. The evidence supports this inference without overreaching.

202.Option: The resistance of CBA/Ca mice to Ascaris infection is primarily due to their higher intrinsic expression of antioxidant enzymes, which directly neutralize Ascaris larvae in the liver.
Evidence: KEGG analysis identified an increase in glutathione metabolic proteins of C57BL/6J control samples, compared to their CBA/Ca counterparts (S4 Fig). This inherent difference was also observed when comparing both strains under infection. Additionally, there was an increase in abundance of glutathione metabolism proteins in C57BL/6J mice when infected, compared to their controls. This increase was less pronounced in CBA/Ca mice.
202.Rationale: The passage indicates that C57BL/6J mice have higher glutathione metabolism proteins, not CBA/Ca mice. The option incorrectly states that CBA/Ca mice have higher antioxidant enzyme expression. The evidence contradicts this claim, making the option incorrect.

202.Option: The lower abundance of immune-associated proteins in CBA/Ca mice compared to C57BL/6J mice suggests that a weaker innate immune response is responsible for their resistance to Ascaris infection.
Evidence: Of these, 20 immune associated proteins were differentially abundant (relative fold difference > 1.5) between infected and control samples (Table 2). Eight proteins: s100-A8; s100-A9; dipeptidyl peptidase 1; coronin-1A; galectin; vitronectin; moesin and plasminogen were more abundantly expressed in both C57BL/6J and CBA/Ca infected mice with respect to their uninfected counterparts indicating a potential conservation of response to Ascaris in both mice strains. The majority of SSDA immune proteins were observed in C57BL/6J Ascaris-infected mice (Table 2). A number of immune proteins demonstrated higher intrinsic abundances in C57BL/6J compared to CBA/Ca (in both infected and uninfected comparisons) and included dipeptidyl peptidase 1, complement C4-B, galectin-3 and the MHC class I molecules H2-D1 and H2-K1 (Fig 2C and 2D).
202.Rationale: The passage shows that C57BL/6J mice have higher intrinsic abundances of immune-associated proteins compared to CBA/Ca mice. The option incorrectly suggests that a weaker innate immune response in CBA/Ca mice is responsible for their resistance. The evidence indicates the opposite, and the option misinterprets the data.

203.Option: The transient decrease in ADAMTS13 activity at H3 and H6 with B. lanceolatus venom suggests a direct endothelial injury mechanism responsible for thrombosis in this envenomation.
Evidence: ADAMTS13 activity was significantly lowered at H3 with B. lanceolatus venom (p = 0.006) and at H6 with the two venoms (p = 0.04 for B. atrox and p = 0.02 for B. lanceolatus), before returning to non-significantly different values at H24 in comparison to the control group.
By contrast, a significant decrease in soluble E-selectin was observed with B. lanceolatus venom at H6 (p = 0.006) and H24 (p = 0.02).
203.Rationale: The passage shows a transient decrease in ADAMTS13 activity with B. lanceolatus venom at H3 and H6, but does not establish a direct causal link between this decrease and thrombosis. The option incorrectly assumes that the ADAMTS13 decrease is the primary mechanism for thrombosis. The passage also notes a significant decrease in soluble E-selectin with B. lanceolatus venom at H6 and H24, which suggests endothelial changes but does not directly support the claim of 'direct endothelial injury' as the mechanism for thrombosis. The evidence provided does not establish a direct causal relationship between ADAMTS13 activity and thrombosis in this context.

203.Option: An initial step of venom-induced hypercoagulability with increased thrombin generation could be considered followed by a secondary step of hypocoagulability with B. atrox venom or persistent hypercoagulability with B. lanceolatus venom, despite their similar proteomic composition and in vitro procoagulant activity.
Evidence: In vitro experiments showed that B. atrox and B. lanceolatus venoms presented dose-dependent procoagulant activities with significant decrease in CT compared to the negative control (p = 0.0008) but no significant difference was observed when compared to one another (p = 0.1) (Figs 1A and S1).

Alterations in TGA occurred early, almost exclusively at H3. Lag time significantly decreased with both venoms at H3 (p < 0.001 for B. atrox and p = 0.01 for B. lanceolatus) and only for B. atrox at H6 (p = 0.019) (Fig 3A). Similarly, the time to peak significantly decreased with both venoms at H3 (p = 0.001 for B. atrox and p = 0.006 for B. lanceolatus) (Fig 3B). In parallel, the peak height significantly increased with the two venoms at H3 (p = 0.04) (Fig 3C). The endogenous thrombin potential only significantly increased with B. atrox venom at H3 (p = 0.02) (Fig 3D).
MCF of venom-treated rats did not differ from controls at H3 and H6. At H24, MCF of B. atrox venom-treated rats was significantly lower than controls (p = 0.004; Figs 2B and S2).

Plasma fibrinogen was significantly lowered with the two venoms at H3 (p = 0.009 for B. atrox and p = 0.005 for B. lanceolatus) and at H6 (p = 0.03 for B. atrox and B. lanceolatus). At the opposite, plasma fibrinogen was significantly increased at H24 with B. lanceolatus venom (p < 0.001; Fig 2D).
203.Rationale: The passage provides evidence of an initial hypercoagulable state at H3 for both venoms, as shown by decreased clotting time (CT), decreased lag time and time to peak in thrombin generation assay (TGA), and increased peak height in TGA. This is followed by a shift to hypocoagulability at H24 for B. atrox venom, as indicated by significantly lower MCF. For B. lanceolatus venom, the passage shows persistent hypercoagulability at H24 with increased fibrinogen levels. Despite similar in vitro procoagulant activity, the in vivo effects diverge over time. This pattern aligns with the option's description of an initial hypercoagulable phase followed by either hypocoagulability or persistent hypercoagulability, depending on the venom type.

203.Option: The early increase in plasma fibrinogen at H24 in B. lanceolatus envenomation is primarily due to direct activation of hepatic fibrinogen synthesis by venom-induced thrombin-like enzymes.
Evidence: Plasma fibrinogen was significantly increased at H24 with B. lanceolatus venom (p < 0.001; Fig 2D).
B. lanceolatus venom dose-dependently clotted purified human fibrinogen, indicating the involvement of a thrombin-like enzyme.
203.Rationale: The passage states that plasma fibrinogen was significantly increased at H24 with B. lanceolatus venom, but it does not provide evidence that this increase is due to direct activation of hepatic fibrinogen synthesis. The option introduces a specific mechanism (direct activation of hepatic synthesis) that is not supported by the passage. The passage only mentions that B. lanceolatus venom can clot fibrinogen via thrombin-like enzymes, but this does not explain the observed increase in plasma fibrinogen at H24. The passage lacks evidence about the source of the increased fibrinogen or the mechanism behind it.

203.Option: The similar proteomic composition and in vitro procoagulant activity of B. atrox and B. lanceolatus venoms imply that their in vivo effects should be identical across all hemostatic parameters.
Evidence: Surprisingly, venom composition of these two Bothrops species are relatively similar with comparable activities on coagulation in vitro in the whole blood and plasma [48].
However, in vitro assays have limitations as they only offer a view of the immediate venom-induced effects whereas envenomation is a complex dynamic process.
MCF of venom-treated rats did not differ from controls at H3 and H6. At H24, MCF of B. atrox venom-treated rats was significantly lower than controls (p = 0.004; Figs 2B and S2).

Plasma fibrinogen was significantly lowered with the two venoms at H3 (p = 0.009 for B. atrox and p = 0.005 for B. lanceolatus) and at H6 (p = 0.03 for B. atrox and B. lanceolatus). At the opposite, plasma fibrinogen was significantly increased at H24 with B. lanceolatus venom (p < 0.001; Fig 2D).
Significant thrombocytopenia was observed with B. atrox venom at H3 (p = 0.04), H6 (p = 0.02), and H24 (p < 0.001) while B. lanceolatus venom had no effect on platelet count (Fig 2C).
203.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that despite similar proteomic composition and in vitro procoagulant activity, the in vivo effects of B. atrox and B. lanceolatus venoms are not identical. The evidence shows differences in MCF at H24, plasma fibrinogen levels at H24, and platelet count changes. The option's implication that in vivo effects should be identical is directly contradicted by the observed differences in these hemostatic parameters. The passage acknowledges the limitations of in vitro assays and emphasizes the complex dynamic nature of envenomation, which explains the divergent in vivo effects.

203.Option: The lack of significant platelet count reduction with B. lanceolatus venom indicates that thrombocytopenia is not a relevant factor in the pathogenesis of thrombosis in this envenomation.
Evidence: Significant thrombocytopenia was observed with B. atrox venom at H3 (p = 0.04), H6 (p = 0.02), and H24 (p < 0.001) while B. lanceolatus venom had no effect on platelet count (Fig 2C).
By contrast, a significant decrease in soluble E-selectin was observed with B. lanceolatus venom at H6 (p = 0.006) and H24 (p = 0.02).
No significant alteration was observed for plasma VWF antigen with any venom at any sampling time (Fig 5A).

ADAMTS13 activity was significantly lowered at H3 with B. lanceolatus venom (p = 0.006) and at H6 with the two venoms (p = 0.04 for B. atrox and p = 0.02 for B. lanceolatus), before returning to non-significantly different values at H24 in comparison to the control group.

211.Option: The recurrence of EV-A71 epidemics in Malaysia is due to the co-circulation of multiple subgenotypes, which increases genetic diversity and facilitates sustained transmission across different age groups.
Evidence: EV-A71 can be divided into three major genotypes, A, B and C, based on a cut-off nucleotide divergence value of 17–22% [4]. Genotypes B and C can be further subdivided into subgenotypes B0-B5 and C1-C5 respectively based on cut-off nucleotide divergence of 10–14% [4,5].

211.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that cyclical patterns of EV-A71 epidemics could be due to the accumulation of a new generation of susceptible children every few years. The data on incidence rates and seroprevalence further support this hypothesis, showing that young children (<5 years) are most affected and that seroprevalence increases during epidemics. About 9.8% (27/275) of the sequences showed E98K, 3.6% (10/275) were E145Q, and 9.1% (25/275) were E145G and these could be observed in different genotypes across different EV-A71 epidemics (S2 Table).

The spatial models for Ag, Wb123 and Bm14 Abs were able to predict the probabilities of these outcomes reasonably well with AUC values of 0.71, 0.70 and 0.70, respectively. Bm33 Ab is the infection marker that is more widespread in American Samoa, and the poor performance of the model could be indicating that sociodemographic and environmental factors may not be important determinants of its distribution across the territory.
217.Rationale: The passage states that Bm33 Ab prevalence increased with tree coverage, but this is not sufficient to conclude that Bm33 antibody testing should be prioritized in high tree density areas. The passage also notes that the Bm33 Ab model performed poorly (AUC 0.60) and that sociodemographic/environmental factors may not be important determinants of its distribution. This suggests that tree coverage may not be a reliable predictor for Bm33 Ab, contradicting the option's recommendation to prioritize testing in high tree density areas.

217.Option: The widespread distribution of Bm33 antibody responses across American Samoa indicates that the parasite is still actively circulating in multiple regions, making it a key marker for identifying ongoing transmission foci.

The models for Ag, Wb123 and Bm14 Abs were able to predict the probabilities of these outcomes reasonably well with AUC values of 0.71, 0.70 and 0.70, respectively. However, the model of Bm33 Ab performed poorly with an AUC value of 0.60 (S8 Fig). Bm33 Ab is the infection marker that is more widespread in American Samoa, and the poor performance of the model could be indicating that sociodemographic and environmental factors may not be important determinants of its distribution across the territory.
217.Rationale: The passage states that Bm33 Ab is widespread but does not provide evidence that this indicates active circulation of the parasite. The poor model performance for Bm33 Ab suggests that its distribution may not be closely tied to current transmission factors. The passage also notes that Bm33 Ab may reflect historical exposure rather than current transmission, which contradicts the option's claim that it indicates active circulation.

217.Option: The high uncertainty in predicted prevalence estimates in tree-covered northern areas suggests that Bm33 antibody testing is less reliable in these regions due to environmental interference with test accuracy.

Also, workers in tuna cannery and outdoor locations had an increase of 56.2% (95% CrI: 3.3–150.1%) and 161.6% (95% CrI: 9.0–534.7%) compared to workers in indoor areas. 217.Rationale: The passage mentions high uncertainty in tree-covered areas but does not attribute this to environmental interference with test accuracy. Instead, it suggests that the poor model performance for Bm33 Ab may be due to sociodemographic and environmental factors not being important determinants of its distribution. The option incorrectly infers that the uncertainty is due to test reliability issues rather than model limitations.

The models for Ag, Wb123 and Bm14 Abs were able to predict the probabilities of these outcomes reasonably well with AUC values of 0.71, 0.70 and 0.70, respectively. However, the model of Bm33 Ab performed poorly with an AUC value of 0.60 (S8 Fig). The spatial distribution of predicted probability and standard deviations of Ag (a and b), Wb123 Ab (c and d), Bm14 Ab (e and f), and Bm33 Ab (g and h) in American Samoa 2016. Base layer from: (https://www.diva-gis.org/gdata/).

The highest predicted probability of all infection makers (≥0.61) was mainly confined in the north-west part, an area that corresponds largely to the coastal villages of Fagali’i and Fagamalo.
