Evidence: The decision to use Magpi was based on several factors, including its widespread acceptance... and because the software supports: a) a wide variety of question types...; b) customized questions; c) off-line data collection (data can be collected off-line and submitted to a central server whenever an active connection becomes available); d) GPS stamping and display of data-points on an electronic map; and e) instantaneous data integration from multiple users.
The accuracy and completeness of the surveys submitted were assessed by a) daily remote data monitoring and b) periodic quality-control visits made to a randomly selected sub-set of households.
230.Rationale: The passage explicitly describes a digital system (Magpi) that supports off-line data collection and remote monitoring, not a paper-based framework. The system is described as 'centralized' in the sense of data aggregation, but the data collection method is electronic, not paper-based. The claim that it is 'error-prone' is also incorrect, as the passage emphasizes quality control measures and high completion rates.

230.Option: The use of GPS stamps and timestamped data in the Magpi system ensures that all health events are accurately geolocated and temporally precise, enabling perfect spatial and temporal mapping of disease outbreaks in real time.
Evidence: However, after comparing our maps (Fig 3) and the data collected (Table 2), it became readily apparent that many geotracers malfunctioned or, most likely, many remote areas had weak or no coverage of cellular communication, causing limitations in acquiring GPS stamps and compromising the ability to produce maps using GPS coordinates.
230.Rationale: The passage explicitly acknowledges limitations in GPS stamping due to weak or no cellular coverage in remote areas. This directly contradicts the claim that the system 'ensures that all health events are accurately geolocated and temporally precise.' The system's real-time mapping capabilities are described as having limitations, not being 'perfect.'

230.Option: The use of minimally trained lay mHealth reporters with basic smartphones enabled real-time monitoring of neurological health data in remote rural communities, allowing for near-real-time identification of disease hotspots, medication shortages, injuries, and deaths with a delay of no more than 1–7 days.
Evidence: The study was not designed or approved to conduct or implement a medical intervention.

Eight male and female Ugandan lay mHealth reporters, age 20–30 years, were recruited and trained. The training covered basic concepts of health and disease... use and maintenance of the equipment (cell phones, solar batteries, bicycles), questionnaire presentation, data collection and submission...

All data acquired and integrated into the Magpi database could be exported, with no particular conversion problems, from the platform into statistical (Excel) or spatial (Magpi maps) analysis software.
The mHealth team completed a mean of 97.3% of surveys administered per week (29.2 ± 0.63 of 30 surveys assigned per mHealth reporter).
The Magpi interface also made it possible to visualize NS health-related data over a defined period of time (Fig 3) with the goal to assess fluctuations and carry out temporal interpretations.
230.Rationale: The passage describes the use of minimally trained lay reporters with basic smartphones to collect and transmit neurological health data. The system enabled real-time monitoring and allowed for the identification of disease hotspots, medication shortages, injuries, and deaths. The high survey completion rate (97.3%) and the ability to visualize data over time support the inference that the system allowed for near-real-time identification with minimal delay. The option is consistent with the evidence provided.

230.Option: The high survey completion rate of 97.3% indicates that the mHealth system is fully independent of network connectivity and can operate without any delays in data transmission, even in areas with poor infrastructure.
Evidence: The most common technical issues were frequent power cuts, poor network coverage, slow upload speeds and unreliable GPS satellites.
On the first day of September, all mobile phones ran out of data because the network provider failed to renew the monthly phone bundle on time.
Some reporters stopped working when they realized their data bundles were exhausted.
230.Rationale: The passage explicitly describes the system's dependence on network connectivity and the challenges faced due to poor infrastructure. The high survey completion rate does not imply independence from network connectivity, as the system required data bundles and faced issues when connectivity was lost. The claim that the system 'can operate without any delays' is directly contradicted by the evidence.

230.Option: The mHealth system's success in data collection and monitoring is primarily due to the high level of formal medical training and clinical expertise of the lay reporters, who were able to accurately diagnose and report neurological conditions without supervision.
Evidence: Eight male and female Ugandan lay mHealth reporters, age 20–30 years, were recruited and trained. The training covered basic concepts of health and disease, the purpose and content of the program, clinical and social aspects of NS, use and maintenance of the equipment (cell phones, solar batteries, bicycles), questionnaire presentation, data collection and submission...
The study was not designed or approved to conduct or implement a medical intervention.
Physical examination of the sample of newly reported and existing NS cases confirmed a diagnosis of NS.
230.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that the mHealth reporters were minimally trained lay individuals, not medically trained professionals. The system's success is attributed to the use of standardized questionnaires and remote monitoring, not to the clinical expertise of the reporters. The physical examination of cases was conducted by a medical diagnostician, not the lay reporters.

231.Option: The high proportion of multibacillary (MB) leprosy cases (up to 74.3% in 2019) suggests that the current leprosy elimination strategies in Yunnan are failing to prevent the progression of early-stage disease to advanced forms.
Evidence: The majority (7,031, 63.6%) were classified as MB leprosy and 4,021 (36.4%) were classified as PB leprosy (Table 2).
The percentage of multibacillary (MB) cases was relatively high in most of the years investigated, ranging from 59.0% in 2008 to 74.3% in 2019 (Table 1).
231.Rationale: The passage provides data on the proportion of MB cases but does not establish a causal link between this proportion and the effectiveness of leprosy elimination strategies. The high proportion of MB cases could be due to various factors, including the natural progression of the disease, the age distribution of cases, or the diagnostic criteria used. The passage does not provide evidence that the current strategies are failing to prevent disease progression. Therefore, this inference cannot be directly drawn from the passage.

231.Option: The observed reduction in the number of newly detected leprosy cases after 2004 can be explained by socioeconomic development, improved access to health services, an effective surveillance system, better travel opportunities, and improved education for the population.
Evidence: The number of newly detected leprosy patients decreased further to 136 cases in 2019, a decrease of 60% in time period 2 (Table 1).

The strategies and interventions implemented to promote early detection of leprosy include a leprosy elimination program, symptom surveillance, contact examination, focused survey, training of medical staff, health education, and financial investments to improve transport and reconstruct the dermatology department and leprosy village.
231.Rationale: While the passage mentions a reduction in cases after 2004 and lists some interventions, it does not explicitly attribute the reduction to socioeconomic development, improved access to health services, better travel opportunities, or improved education. The passage focuses on the implementation of MDT, the establishment of a special fund, and specific public health strategies. The factors mentioned in the option are not directly supported by the evidence in the passage, making this inference speculative rather than directly derivable.

231.Option: The continued presence of leprosy in 17 highly endemic counties indicates that multidrug treatment (MDT) has been ineffective in preventing transmission in remote, mountainous regions of Yunnan.
Evidence: Currently, 17/117 (14.5%) of the counties remain leprosy-endemic regions.

The number of newly detected leprosy patients declined significantly. In 1990, a total of 703 newly detected leprosy patients were reported in Yunnan. This number decreased to 353 cases in 2003, a reduction of 50% in time period 1. After the special fund for leprosy was established by the central government in 2004, the number of newly detected leprosy patients decreased further to 136 cases in 2019, a decrease of 60% in time period 2 (Table 1).
231.Rationale: The passage acknowledges the continued presence of leprosy in 17 counties but does not state that MDT has been ineffective in these regions. The reduction in cases over time, even in these areas, suggests that MDT has had some effect. The passage does not provide evidence that MDT is ineffective in remote regions; instead, it implies that ongoing efforts are needed to address the remaining endemic areas. Therefore, this inference is not supported by the passage.

231.Option: The increase in early detection rates from 53.9% to 60.3% between time periods 1 and 2 suggests that contact examination and health education have been the primary drivers of the reduction in leprosy burden in Yunnan.
Evidence: The percentage of detected cases involving G2D decreased slightly from 22.6% (n = 1550) in time period 1 to 19.2% (n = 812) in time period 2 (P <0.01) (Table 3).

The strategies and interventions implemented to promote early detection of leprosy include a leprosy elimination program, symptom surveillance, contact examination, focused survey, training of medical staff, health education, and financial investments to improve transport and reconstruct the dermatology department and leprosy village.
231.Rationale: The passage mentions an increase in early detection rates and lists contact examination and health education as part of the strategies used. However, it does not explicitly state that these two strategies were the primary drivers of the reduction in leprosy burden. The passage attributes the reduction to a combination of factors, including MDT, the special fund, and various public health interventions. Therefore, the inference that contact examination and health education were the primary drivers is not directly supported by the evidence in the passage.

231.Option: The substantial decline in leprosy case detection and the reduction in leprosy-endemic counties in Yunnan Province over the past 30 years can be attributed to the implementation of multidrug treatment (MDT), the establishment of a special fund for leprosy, and the development of a comprehensive surveillance system.
Evidence: Since 1990, MDT has been used to treat leprosy in every county in Yunnan.
In 2004, a special fund was established by the central government to combat leprosy in the region. The amount given from 2004 to 2019 was 150 million CNY (US$25 million).

The strategies and interventions implemented to promote early detection of leprosy include a leprosy elimination program, symptom surveillance, contact examination, focused survey, training of medical staff, health education, and financial investments to improve transport and reconstruct the dermatology department and leprosy village.

The number of newly detected leprosy patients declined significantly. In 1990, a total of 703 newly detected leprosy patients were reported in Yunnan. This number decreased to 353 cases in 2003, a reduction of 50% in time period 1. After the special fund for leprosy was established by the central government in 2004, the number of newly detected leprosy patients decreased further to 136 cases in 2019, a decrease of 60% in time period 2 (Table 1).
By the end of 2019, 85.5% (100/117) of the leprosy-endemic counties had achieved the leprosy elimination target (Fig 3).
231.Rationale: The passage provides direct evidence linking the decline in leprosy cases and the reduction in endemic counties to the implementation of MDT, the establishment of a special fund, and the development of a comprehensive surveillance system. The data show a significant reduction in cases after the introduction of MDT and the special fund, and the strategies implemented are explicitly mentioned as part of the leprosy elimination program. Therefore, this inference is directly supported by the evidence in the passage.

232.Option: The high proportion of rabies cases in children under 15 years of age suggests that the current surveillance system is more effective at detecting cases in younger populations.
Evidence: Of the 14 cases of human rabies, 12 were children below 15 years of age and 8 were male.
Almost half of cases occur in children under the age of 15 years [3, 5, 6].
232.Rationale: The passage states that 12 out of 14 rabies cases in the outbreak were children under 15, and that globally, almost half of rabies cases occur in this age group. However, this does not indicate that the surveillance system is more effective at detecting cases in children. The high proportion of cases in children is a known epidemiological feature of rabies, not a reflection of surveillance effectiveness. The passage provides no evidence that the surveillance system is more sensitive for children than for adults.

232.Option: The absence of rabies cases in the routine surveillance system during the outbreak indicates that the surveillance system is functioning effectively by excluding false positives.
Evidence: None of the 12 cases of rabies identified from April to July were notified through the routine surveillance system.
232.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that none of the 12 rabies cases identified during the outbreak were reported through the routine surveillance system. This absence cannot be interpreted as evidence of effective exclusion of false positives, as the system failed to detect actual cases. The passage also notes that rabies is under-reported in Mozambique, which is consistent with a failure of the surveillance system rather than a sign of its effectiveness.

232.Option: The poor quality of records at Prophylaxis Centers is the primary reason for the under-reporting of rabies cases in Mozambique.
Evidence: Only 72 (8.7%) of animal bite victims had an animal bite investigation form filled out and the majority of these forms were incomplete.
Information on post-exposure vaccination was available in only 19.0% (156/819) of victims, of whom 7.7% (12/156) were from Maputo City and 92.3% (144/154) from Matola City.
232.Rationale: The passage describes poor record-keeping at Prophylaxis Centers, with only 8.7% of bite victims having completed investigation forms and 19% having information on post-exposure vaccination. While this contributes to under-reporting, the passage does not state that this is the 'primary' reason. Other factors, such as failure to diagnose rabies in clinical settings and lack of awareness among health professionals, are also mentioned as significant contributors. The option overstates the role of record quality as the primary cause.

232.Option: The failure to diagnose rabies in clinical settings, combined with a lack of clinical awareness among health professionals, contributes to the under-reporting of rabies cases in Mozambique.
Evidence: Rabies was not suspected and for this reason, they were not initially recorded as rabies.

No rabies victim received full post-exposure vaccination. In Matola, one victim attended a health center and received only tetanus vaccine and the parents were informed that it was sufficient. Three victims attended the Prophylaxis Center in Matola and the parents were told that there was no vaccine available. In Maputo City, of ten victims who attended health centers, nine were given only tetanus vaccine and parents were told that it was sufficient. They were not referred to the Prophylaxis Center.
232.Rationale: The passage provides direct evidence that rabies was not suspected in some cases, leading to misdiagnosis and failure to record cases. Additionally, health professionals in Matola and Maputo City failed to refer victims to Prophylaxis Centers or administer appropriate post-exposure prophylaxis, indicating a lack of clinical awareness. These factors directly contribute to under-reporting of rabies cases, making this option a valid inference from the passage.

232.Option: None of the cases of human rabies that occurred during the outbreak were notified through the routine surveillance system, showing that rabies is seriously under-reported.
Evidence: None of the 12 cases of rabies identified from April to July were notified through the routine surveillance system.
232.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that none of the 12 rabies cases identified during the outbreak were reported through the routine surveillance system. This directly supports the inference that rabies is seriously under-reported in Mozambique. The absence of cases in the surveillance system is a clear indicator of under-reporting, not a sign of system effectiveness.

233.Option: The failure to sustain a Th17 response beyond 14 days in the murine model indicates that this subset is not essential for long-term fungal control, as its decline coincides with disease remission.
Evidence: The Th17 profile was then followed by Th1 popularization in later stages, coinciding with the remission phase of the disease in this experimental model.

In vivo depletion of CD25+ cells leads to Treg cells’ impairment in draining lymph nodes (dLN), reflected by a reduction in fungal burden after 14 days of infection. Furthermore, fungal cells were not cleared in later stages of the disease, prolonging CBM clinical features in those animals.

In vivo analysis with IL-17A and IFN-γ neutralization hindered fungal cell elimination in the course of the disease. Similarly, in dectin-2 KO animals, Th17 contraction in the course of experimental CBM showed fungal burden impairment in the first 14 days of infection, although it did not affect disease resolution.
233.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that Th17 contraction in dectin-2 KO animals impaired fungal burden in the first 14 days but did not affect disease resolution. This directly contradicts the claim that Th17 is not essential for long-term fungal control. The decline in Th17 coinciding with remission does not imply lack of importance, as the data show Th17 is critical for early control. The passage also shows that Th17 depletion impairs early fungal clearance, indicating its essential role in the initial phase.

233.Option: The initial Th17 response in the footpad is primarily responsible for the recruitment of neutrophils, which are essential for the early elimination of fungal conidia and hyphae, while Th1 cells become critical later for clearing muriform cells.
Evidence: The Th17 profile was then followed by Th1 popularization in later stages, coinciding with the remission phase of the disease in this experimental model.
In vivo analysis with IL-17A and IFN-γ neutralization hindered fungal cell elimination in the course of the disease.
Similarly, in dectin-2 KO animals, Th17 contraction in the course of experimental CBM showed fungal burden impairment in the first 14 days of infection, although it did not affect disease resolution.
233.Rationale: The passage does not mention neutrophils or their recruitment by Th17 cells. The option introduces a mechanism (neutrophil recruitment) that is not supported by the evidence in the passage. While the passage does show that Th17 is important for early fungal control and Th1 for later stages, the specific claim about neutrophils and morphotype-specific clearance is not present in the passage and cannot be inferred from the given evidence.

233.Option: The coordinated action of CD4+ T lymphocyte subpopulations, with initial Th17 polarization followed by Th1 dominance and Treg involvement, is essential for effective fungal clearance and disease remission in experimental chromoblastomycosis.
Evidence: The Th17 profile was then followed by Th1 popularization in later stages, coinciding with the remission phase of the disease in this experimental model.

In vivo depletion of CD25+ cells leads to Treg cells’ impairment in draining lymph nodes (dLN), reflected by a reduction in fungal burden after 14 days of infection. Furthermore, fungal cells were not cleared in later stages of the disease, prolonging CBM clinical features in those animals.
In vivo analysis with IL-17A and IFN-γ neutralization hindered fungal cell elimination in the course of the disease.

Taking into consideration that this is a self-healing animal model of CBM, these results indicate that the increase in the Th17 population in the infection site during the first 14 days, followed by Th1 population increase, closely assisted by a growing Treg population until disease resolution, may represent the ideal balance of these populations when aiming at CBM remission.
233.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that the ideal balance for CBM remission involves initial Th17 polarization followed by Th1 dominance and Treg involvement. The evidence shows that depletion of Treg cells impairs fungal clearance in later stages, and that Th17 and Th1 responses are both necessary at different phases. This directly supports the conclusion that coordinated action of these subpopulations is essential for effective clearance and remission.

233.Option: The chronic inflammatory process observed in human CBM patients may be due to an unbalanced immune response that fails to transition from Th17 to Th1 dominance, resulting in persistent fungal burden and inadequate regulation by Treg cells.
Evidence: In situ studies on CBM patients, as well as in experimental models, show a correlation between Th2 response and disease severity, together with a high fungal load. The milder form of the disease is related to a Th1 profile, with high IFN-γ production, lower levels of IL-10, and lower fungal burden.

On the other hand, low concentrations of TGF-β associated with pro-inflammatory cytokines such as IL-1β, IL-6 and IL-21 and IL-23 allow the differentiation of CD4 + T cells into Th17, inducing RORγT transcription factor.
The Th17 profile was then followed by Th1 popularization in later stages, coinciding with the remission phase of the disease in this experimental model.

In vivo depletion of CD25+ cells leads to Treg cells’ impairment in draining lymph nodes (dLN), reflected by a reduction in fungal burden after 14 days of infection. Furthermore, fungal cells were not cleared in later stages of the disease, prolonging CBM clinical features in those animals.
233.Rationale: The passage provides evidence that in CBM patients, a Th2 response correlates with disease severity and high fungal load, while a Th1 profile is associated with milder disease. The experimental model shows that failure to transition from Th17 to Th1 dominance (as seen in Treg depletion experiments) results in prolonged fungal burden. This supports the inference that chronic inflammation in human CBM may result from an unbalanced immune response failing to make this transition, with inadequate Treg regulation.

234.Option: The identification of genotypes previously described in animals suggests potential zoonotic and anthroponotic transmission of Enterocytozoon bieneusi in Mozambique.
Evidence: Groups 1 and 2 comprise genotypes able to infect a broad range of mammalian species including humans and are, therefore, considered potentially zoonotic.
The nucleotide sequences of the ITS revealed the presence of four distinct E. bieneusi genotypes, three previously reported in humans (Type IV, S2, and Peru11) and a novel genotype (named HhMzEb1).
Genotype S2 nucleotide sequence obtained in this study showed a SNP at the large subunit region when compared with reference sequence (GenBank accession number FJ439678).
234.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that Groups 1 and 2 genotypes are considered potentially zoonotic because they can infect a broad range of mammalian species including humans. The study identified three genotypes (Type IV, S2, and Peru11) previously reported in humans, and the novel genotype HhMzEb1. The presence of genotypes previously described in animals (implied by the zoonotic classification of Groups 1 and 2) supports the inference of zoonotic transmission. Additionally, the fact that some genotypes are found in humans and others in animals (implied by the zoonotic classification) supports the possibility of anthroponotic transmission (human-to-human). This inference is directly supported by the evidence in the passage.

234.Option: The detection of E. bieneusi genotypes in both rural and urban settings suggests that transmission is primarily driven by environmental contamination rather than animal contact.
Evidence: In Zambézia, 807 asymptomatic schoolchildren and 290 children presenting with gastrointestinal complaints seeking for medical care were investigated in 10 and six districts of the province, respectively (Table 2 and S1 and S2 Tables). The overall male/female ratio was 1.0, and children in the age group 6 to 10 years-old represented 60% of the surveyed individuals. Three out of four children investigated lived in rural areas.

In the rural Manhiça district, 109 asymptomatic children and 41 children presenting with gastrointestinal complaints (11 with moderate-to-severe diarrhoea and 30 with less severe diarrhoea) and seeking for medical care were investigated (Table 2 and S3 and S4 Tables). In this subpopulation, the male/female ratio was 1.1, and children in the age group 12–23 months-old represented 40.7% of the surveyed individuals. Most children (62.7%) were from the area covered by the MDH. All these children lived in rural areas.
234.Rationale: The passage only provides evidence that children in both Zambézia and Maputo provinces were studied, and that most children in Zambézia lived in rural areas while all children in Maputo lived in rural areas. There is no evidence in the passage about urban settings. The inference that transmission is primarily driven by environmental contamination rather than animal contact is not supported by the evidence in the passage. The passage does not provide any information about the relative roles of environmental contamination and animal contact in transmission. This conclusion requires external epidemiological knowledge not contained in the passage.

234.Option: The high genetic diversity of E. bieneusi in Mozambique, as evidenced by the identification of a novel genotype, suggests that the parasite is undergoing rapid evolution in this region.
Evidence: The nucleotide sequences of the ITS revealed the presence of four distinct E. bieneusi genotypes, three previously reported in humans (Type IV, S2, and Peru11) and a novel genotype (named HhMzEb1).
Groups 1 and 2 comprise genotypes able to infect a broad range of mammalian species including humans and are, therefore, considered potentially zoonotic.
234.Rationale: The passage states that a novel genotype was identified, but it does not provide any evidence about the rate of evolution of the parasite. The identification of a novel genotype could be due to a variety of factors, including sampling bias or the presence of previously undetected diversity, not necessarily rapid evolution. The inference that the parasite is undergoing rapid evolution in this region is not supported by the evidence in the passage. This conclusion requires external evolutionary biological knowledge not contained in the passage.

234.Option: The presence of multiple E. bieneusi genotypes in both symptomatic and asymptomatic children indicates that clinical symptoms are not reliably associated with specific genotypes.
Evidence: In Zambézia province, E. bieneusi was found in 3 asymptomatic children. None of the symptomatic children investigated in this province tested positive to E. bieneusi.
In Maputo province, E. bieneusi was found in six male children, one presenting with moderate-to-severe diarrhoea and 5 asymptomatic.
234.Rationale: The passage provides evidence that E. bieneusi was found in both symptomatic and asymptomatic children in both provinces. In Zambézia, all three cases were asymptomatic, while in Maputo, one case was symptomatic and five were asymptomatic. This suggests that clinical symptoms are not reliably associated with specific genotypes. The inference is directly supported by the evidence in the passage.

234.Option: The fact that all identified genotypes belong to Group 1 implies that E. bieneusi in Mozambique is primarily transmitted through direct human-to-human contact.
Evidence: Groups 1 and 2 comprise genotypes able to infect a broad range of mammalian species including humans and are, therefore, considered potentially zoonotic.

Phylogenetic analysis revealed that the novel genotype HhMzEB1 clustered within Group 1 when its relationship with other genotypes of human and animal origin previously reported in Africa was evaluated (Fig 1).
234.Rationale: The passage states that Group 1 genotypes are considered potentially zoonotic because they can infect a broad range of mammalian species including humans. The fact that all identified genotypes belong to Group 1 does not imply that transmission is primarily through direct human-to-human contact. In fact, the zoonotic potential of Group 1 genotypes suggests that transmission could also occur through animal contact. The inference that transmission is primarily through direct human-to-human contact is not supported by the evidence in the passage. This conclusion requires external epidemiological knowledge not contained in the passage.

235.Option: The successful completion of five rounds of MDA in Sri Lanka, combined with high MDA compliance rates, ensures that transmission assessment surveys are sufficient to confirm the interruption of lymphatic filariasis transmission.
Evidence: The present study represents a significant expansion of earlier post-MDA surveillance activities in Sri Lanka.
TAS cannot guarantee that LF transmission has been interrupted in evaluation units (EUs), which are typically districts with populations that may exceed 1 million.
235.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that transmission assessment surveys (TAS) cannot guarantee that LF transmission has been interrupted in evaluation units. This directly contradicts the claim that TAS are sufficient to confirm transmission interruption. The mention of high MDA compliance rates does not negate this limitation of TAS.

235.Option: The high rates of antifilarial antibodies in primary school children across most PHIs suggest that transmission assessment surveys are highly sensitive for detecting ongoing filariasis transmission in endemic areas.
Evidence: Antibody rates exceeded the target rate of 2% in 10 of 19 PHIs; five PHIs had borderline elevated antibody rates, and 5 others had higher rates with upper confidence limits >5%.

Community antibody testing was performed in a subset of PHIs that were surveyed in the comprehensive surveillance study (Table S1). Although CFA and Mf rates in these communities were below provisional target levels, community antibody rates were high in all of these PHIs, and this probably reflects high infection rates that were present in these areas prior to implementation of the national MDA program.
235.Rationale: The passage indicates that high antibody rates in school children do not necessarily reflect ongoing transmission but may instead reflect historical exposure to filariasis prior to MDA. This undermines the claim that TAS are highly sensitive for detecting ongoing transmission. The persistence of antibodies after MDA success suggests that antibody testing alone is not a reliable indicator of current transmission.

235.Option: The fact that none of the children with positive CFA tests in TAS surveys had microfilaremia indicates that transmission assessment surveys are reliable indicators of the absence of active filarial transmission.
Evidence: None of the 16 children with positive CFA tests in TAS surveys had microfilaremia.

The detection of filarial DNA in mosquitoes in some PHIs suggests that transmission assessment surveys are overly conservative and should be abandoned in favor of molecular xenodiagnosis for all post-MDA evaluations.
235.Rationale: The absence of microfilaremia in CFA-positive children does not necessarily indicate the absence of active transmission. The passage also shows that filarial DNA was detected in mosquitoes in some PHIs, suggesting ongoing transmission despite TAS results. This directly contradicts the claim that TAS are reliable indicators of transmission absence.

235.Option: The detection of filarial DNA in mosquitoes in some PHIs suggests that transmission assessment surveys are overly conservative and should be abandoned in favor of molecular xenodiagnosis for all post-MDA evaluations.
Evidence: Filarial DNA rates exceeded the target of 0.25% in 10 of 19 PHIs.

Mosquitoes from both PHIs surveyed in Galle district and one in Matara district had parasite DNA rates of more than 1%, and these rates were comparable to those seen in some filariasis endemic areas in Egypt with continued filariasis transmission following one or two rounds of MDA.
235.Rationale: While the detection of filarial DNA in mosquitoes suggests ongoing transmission in some areas, the passage does not support the claim that TAS should be abandoned in favor of molecular xenodiagnosis for all post-MDA evaluations. The study actually uses both TAS and molecular xenodiagnosis as complementary methods, not as mutually exclusive approaches.

235.Option: While transmission assessment surveys may be useful for decisions regarding stopping mass drug administration, they are not sufficient to show that lymphatic filariasis transmission has been interrupted, as evidenced by the detection of filarial DNA in mosquitoes and persistent antigenemia in adults in areas that passed TAS.
Evidence: TAS cannot guarantee that LF transmission has been interrupted in evaluation units (EUs), which are typically districts with populations that may exceed 1 million.
Filarial DNA rates exceeded the target of 0.25% in 10 of 19 PHIs.
Community CFA rates were <2% in 17 of 19 PHIs, but upper confidence limits for CFA were >2% in 5 of 19 PHIs.

CFA rates were higher in males than females when data from all community surveys were considered (1.01% vs. 0.42%, P<0.001) and when localities with no positive CFA tests were excluded from the analysis (1.39% vs. 0.57%, P<0.001) (Table 3).
235.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that TAS cannot guarantee transmission interruption. It also provides evidence of ongoing transmission through the detection of filarial DNA in mosquitoes and persistent antigenemia in adults in areas that passed TAS. This directly supports the conclusion that while TAS may be useful for stopping MDA, they are insufficient to confirm transmission interruption.

236.Option: The integration of mHealth tools with community health volunteers enables the collection of high-quality photographic data for therapeutic response assessment, which is as accurate as in-person evaluation and supports the scalability of digital health interventions in rural settings.
Evidence: In the intervention (app) arm, the study physician evaluated the therapeutic response via photographs taken by the CHV using the phone app at four time points. In-person assessment by the study physician was only undertaken if the app photos were not clear or if it was required for patient management, e.g. for adverse events or concomitant medication.
In the intervention (app) arm, it was not possible to evaluate the induration of lesions via photographs, only their epithelization, but induration is not considered an absolute criterion of cure.
236.Rationale: The passage states that in-person assessment was only used when app photos were unclear or for specific patient management needs, implying that photographic data was not always sufficient. Additionally, it explicitly notes that induration could not be evaluated via photographs, which is a limitation of the method. The claim that photographic data is 'as accurate as in-person evaluation' cannot be supported by the evidence provided.

236.Option: The use of a mobile health app by community health volunteers significantly improved the proportion of patients monitored at target study week 26 compared to standard institution-based follow-up, despite challenges related to connectivity and security.
Evidence: Among these, 26/49 (53.1%) were evaluated by active follow-up at this time in the intervention arm, and none (0/25, 0%) of those in the randomized control arm who, according to the standard-of-care were instructed to return for evaluation at week 13 and 26 (difference = 53.1%, 95% confidence interval 39.1–67.0%, p<0.001).
236.Rationale: The passage provides clear evidence that the intervention arm (using the mHealth app) had a significantly higher proportion of patients monitored at target study week 26 compared to the control group using standard institution-based follow-up. The statistical significance (p<0.001) and the large difference (53.1% vs 0%) directly support this inference. While the passage does not explicitly mention connectivity or security challenges, the context of a remote rural setting with limited infrastructure implies these challenges were present.

236.Option: The mHealth intervention significantly reduced the burden on health facilities by shifting the responsibility for treatment monitoring to community health volunteers, thereby improving access to care in remote areas.
Evidence: The use of mHealth tools by health volunteers offers the opportunity to overcome these barriers.
Travel to the town of Tumaco, where the health centers are located, may take up to 8 hours, which restricts access to health services.
236.Rationale: The passage mentions that mHealth tools offer the opportunity to overcome access barriers but does not provide evidence that the intervention actually reduced the burden on health facilities. The study's primary focus was on improving follow-up rates, not on measuring the impact on facility burden. The claim about reducing facility burden is an extrapolation beyond the evidence presented.

236.Option: Despite the challenges of poor connectivity and mobility, the mHealth intervention led to a complete resolution of treatment non-adherence in the intervention arm, enabling full therapeutic monitoring for all patients.
Evidence: The average number of ampoules administered, as a percentage of the number prescribed, was 88% or higher in each of the three groups.
There was no statistically significant difference in the compliance with the prescribed treatment between the randomized arms.
236.Rationale: The passage states that adherence was 88% or higher in all groups and that there was no significant difference in compliance between the randomized arms. This indicates that the mHealth intervention did not lead to a complete resolution of treatment non-adherence. The claim of 'full therapeutic monitoring for all patients' is also incorrect, as the passage notes that only 53.1% of the intervention arm were monitored at week 26, not 100%.

237.Option: The spatial clustering of high household density and lower SES in coastal Kenya is primarily driven by proximity to main roads and aquatic habitats favorable for Anopheles mosquitoes and snails.
Evidence: Results based on the Gi*(d) test showed that high density households and lower income were clustered in all villages (Figure 5). In Vuga and Kinango, the clustering pattern of both density and low income overlapped.
Low-income houses were clustered closer to sites that were suitable for snail hosts of S. haematobium (Figure 5 and Figure S1 in Text S1).
237.Rationale: While the passage does describe spatial clustering of high household density and lower SES, it does not state that this clustering is 'primarily driven by proximity to main roads and aquatic habitats favorable for Anopheles mosquitoes and snails.' The passage notes that low-income houses are clustered closer to snail host sites for S. haematobium, but this is a correlation, not a causal explanation. The option introduces a causal mechanism (proximity to roads and aquatic habitats) that is not explicitly stated in the passage as the primary driver of the observed spatial patterns.

237.Option: The challenge of polyparasitism is increasingly recognized through i) our increasing appreciation of the health and social burden of chronic/recurrent infections, ii) more sensitive diagnostics, which indicate that concurrent polyparasitism is much more prevalent than previously thought, and iii) new inexpensive approaches to treatment and transmission control that are increasingly more accessible.
Evidence: new advances in diagnostic technology have increased test sensitivity and specificity for these parasites, revealing that in endemic areas, chronic parasitic infection with Schistosoma spp. [24], Plasmodia spp. [25], and/or filaria [26] are much more common than previously thought.

In holoendemic areas such as coastal Kenya or Papua New Guinea, malaria prevalence, as detected by PCR is 60–75%, more than double the previous estimates of 20–33% by blood smear microscopy, with ≥10% carrying two or more malaria species [25], [27].

advances in filaria antigen detection techniques indicate that past community surveys have underestimated prevalence of filariasis by 40% [29], while standard screening techniques for S. haematobium have probably missed 50–60% of low level infections with this parasite [30].
These findings indicate the need to carefully re-evaluate the risk of infection and parasite-related morbidity in exposed populations.
237.Rationale: The passage explicitly supports the first two points of this option: the increased appreciation of the health burden of chronic infections and the role of more sensitive diagnostics in revealing higher prevalence of polyparasitism. The third point about 'new inexpensive approaches to treatment and transmission control' is not directly mentioned in the passage, but the overall context of the study (which aims to inform the design of more effective, population-based control strategies) implies a recognition of the need for improved control measures. The option is therefore supported by the passage as an overarching trend in the understanding and management of these infections.

237.Option: The high prevalence of co-infections in children and young adults is primarily due to the prior mass drug administration (MDA) campaign in 2003, which reduced hookworm and Trichuris infections in the 10–19 age group.
Evidence: Aggregated prevalence data for all villages showed a reduction in malaria rates after age 19, an increase in filaria prevalence with age, a peak in S. haematobium prevalence for ages 10–19, an increase in hookworm infections with age, and a decline in Trichuris infections after age 19 (Figures S2–S6 in Text S1).
In Milalani and Nganja, hookworm infections declined in the 10–19 age group before rising again in the older age groups.
Polyparasitism (two or more infections) was highest in the 10–19 age group.
237.Rationale: The passage describes age-related patterns of infection but does not mention any mass drug administration (MDA) campaign in 2003 or attribute the observed patterns to such a campaign. The option introduces a specific historical event (the 2003 MDA) that is not referenced in the passage, making this inference unsupported by the evidence provided.

237.Option: The integration of socio-ecological data and spatial analysis techniques in this study demonstrates that environmental factors are the dominant drivers of polyparasitism in coastal Kenya.
Evidence: Our study's aim was to detect spatial and demographic patterns of transmission and infection for schistosomiasis, malaria, filariasis, and soil-transmitted helminths (STH) in coastal Kenya through integration of parasitological data with landscape, land use, and socioeconomic risk factors.

The role of the environment is assumed to be critical for vector-borne and soil-transmitted parasite transmission, although the relative non-linear impact of individual environmental factors has not been well-quantified [31], [32].

In contrast to person-to-person contagion of viruses and bacteria, there is a difference between a person's exposure to parasites and her or his ultimate level of parasite infections and related diseases, which is often governed by continued residence in the high-risk environment.
237.Rationale: While the passage emphasizes the importance of environmental factors in parasite transmission and describes the integration of socio-ecological data and spatial analysis techniques, it does not claim that environmental factors are the 'dominant drivers' of polyparasitism. The passage acknowledges that environmental factors are critical but also highlights the role of demographic and socio-economic factors. The option overstates the conclusion by asserting dominance of environmental factors, which is not explicitly supported by the passage.

237.Option: The lack of significant correlation between mosquito abundance and malaria or filariasis cases suggests that vector control measures are ineffective in reducing transmission in coastal Kenya.
Evidence: Results from the GAM applied to the entomological data (Table 4) showed no significant correlation between presence or number of collected mosquitoes and malaria or filariasis cases.
However, not surprisingly, bednet use was protective against malaria.
237.Rationale: The passage states that there was no significant correlation between mosquito abundance and malaria or filariasis cases, but it also notes that bednet use was protective against malaria. This suggests that vector control measures (like bednets) can still be effective, even if mosquito abundance alone is not a strong predictor of infection. The option incorrectly concludes that vector control measures are ineffective, which is not supported by the evidence in the passage.

238.Option: Despite significant creatinine elevation in one patient, significant differences were not recorded in any of mean laboratory values at the end of the therapy, suggesting that routine laboratory assessment is not mandatory during oral miltefosine treatment.
Evidence: Creatinine elevation was the only laboratory data complication which detected in one patient (3.70%) which raised from 0.80 mg/dl in the beginning to 2.0 mg/dl after 14 days.
There were no significant differences in mean values of hematological, hepatic, and renal parameters in treatment groups.
238.Rationale: The passage states that there were no significant differences in mean laboratory values, but this does not imply that routine laboratory assessment is not mandatory. The presence of a single case of significant creatinine elevation suggests that monitoring is still important, especially given the potential for nephrotoxicity. The conclusion that routine assessment is not mandatory cannot be directly inferred from the data provided.

238.Option: The high rate of clinical improvement and low recurrence over 12 months of follow-up in this cohort suggests that oral miltefosine treatment leads to permanent sterilization of L. tropica infection in most patients.
Evidence: In the first follow up visit (three months after treatment) 23 patients (85.18%) gained complete or partial cure, including 14 definitely cured patients and 9 partially cured patients.

In both second and third follow up sessions (sixth and twelfth month post treatment) 22 patients had complete or partial cure (81.48%) including 17 definitely cured patients, corresponding to a cure rate of 68% on a per protocol analysis, and 62.96% according to intention to treat analysis and 5 partially cured patients.
238.Rationale: The passage provides data on clinical improvement and recurrence rates but does not mention sterilization of the infection. The term 'permanent sterilization' implies a complete and lasting elimination of the pathogen, which is not directly supported by the clinical outcomes described. The data show improvement and low recurrence, but not necessarily permanent sterilization.

238.Option: The program to investigate miltefosine for new world cutaneous leishmaniasis (NWCL) was initiated in 2001 with an uncontrolled, open-label, dose-ranging study in Colombia, in which the per-protocol cure rate of 94% was reported by 2.5 mg/kg oral miltefosine per day after four weeks.
238.Rationale: This option introduces information about a study in Colombia that is not mentioned in the passage. The passage focuses on a study in Iran and does not provide any data or references related to studies in Colombia or NWCL. Therefore, this inference cannot be drawn from the passage.

238.Option: The therapeutic effect of oral miltefosine on MA-resistant anthropontic cutaneous leishmaniasis caused by L. tropica in Iran is effective and nearly safe, as evidenced by a high rate of clinical improvement and low recurrence over 12 months of follow-up.
Evidence: Of 27 enrolled patients for systemic miltefosine treatment, 25 patients completed treatment.
In the first follow up visit (three months after treatment) 23 patients (85.18%) gained complete or partial cure, including 14 definitely cured patients and 9 partially cured patients.

In both second and third follow up sessions (sixth and twelfth month post treatment) 22 patients had complete or partial cure (81.48%) including 17 definitely cured patients, corresponding to a cure rate of 68% on a per protocol analysis, and 62.96% according to intention to treat analysis and 5 partially cured patients.
Nausea, but not vomiting, was the most subjective complication during the study which detected in 9 (33.33%) patients.
There were no significant differences in mean values of hematological, hepatic, and renal parameters in treatment groups.
238.Rationale: The passage provides evidence of the effectiveness of oral miltefosine in treating MA-resistant ACL caused by L. tropica in Iran, with high rates of clinical improvement and low recurrence over 12 months. The safety profile is also supported by the low incidence of adverse effects and no significant differences in laboratory values. This directly supports the inference that the treatment is effective and nearly safe.

238.Option: The higher cure rates observed in previous studies of miltefosine for zoonotic CL in Iran may be attributed to the self-limiting nature of L. major infections, which could explain the relatively lower cure rate in this cohort of patients with chronic L. tropica infections.
Evidence: In Iran, as endemic place for OWCL, therapeutic effects of oral miltefosine on ZCL have been studied previously. Mohebali and colleagues (2007) declared miltefosine at least as good as MA for the treatment of ZCL.
238.Rationale: The passage mentions previous studies on miltefosine for ZCL in Iran but does not provide a direct comparison or explanation for the difference in cure rates between L. major and L. tropica infections. The inference about the self-limiting nature of L. major infections is not supported by the data provided in the passage.

239.Option: The majority of V. cholerae isolates from household contacts and water sources within the same household were closely related by MLVA, indicating that transmission occurred either through a shared environmental source or person-to-person contact.
Evidence: Eight households had both clinical and water isolates. When the relatedness of water and clinical isolates from the same household was compared, all households had at least one clinical and water isolate with an identical MLVA genotype or a single locus variant of the same MLVA genotype.

Isolates collected from the same household had significantly fewer pairwise differences in MLVA loci than those from different households (mean: 0.98 pairwise differences in MLVA loci (same household) vs. 1.79 (different household), p<0.0001).
239.Rationale: The passage provides direct evidence that V. cholerae isolates from household contacts and water sources within the same household were closely related by MLVA. The finding that all households with both clinical and water isolates had at least one pair of isolates with identical or nearly identical MLVA genotypes, combined with the significantly fewer pairwise differences in MLVA loci among isolates from the same household, supports the inference that transmission occurred through either a shared environmental source or person-to-person contact. This conclusion is consistent with the study's own findings that 'the findings showed a combination of person-to-person and water-to-person cholera transmission with the proportions of the two modes varying within and between outbreaks.'

239.Option: The presence of V. cholerae in both source and stored water within households indicates that household water contamination is primarily due to poor sanitation infrastructure rather than person-to-person transmission.
Evidence: All households with detectable V. cholerae in their water source had unimproved latrines.
All households with V. cholerae in their source water also had V. cholerae in stored drinking water.
239.Rationale: While the passage does show a correlation between unimproved sanitation and water contamination, it does not establish causation or rule out person-to-person transmission as a contributing factor. The study explicitly found a combination of transmission routes, and the presence of V. cholerae in water sources does not by itself indicate that household water contamination is 'primarily due to poor sanitation infrastructure rather than person-to-person transmission.' The passage provides no evidence to support the claim that one mode of transmission is 'primary' over the other in this context.

239.Option: The fact that stored water samples had a higher proportion of V. cholerae than source water samples indicates that household-level contamination is the primary driver of V. cholerae transmission, independent of the index case.
Evidence: 27% (8) had V. cholerae in stored household drinking water.
13% of households (4) had V. cholerae in their source water during the surveillance period.
239.Rationale: The passage states that 27% of households had V. cholerae in stored drinking water compared to 13% in source water. However, this does not support the inference that household-level contamination is the 'primary driver' of transmission 'independent of the index case.' The study found that 37% of households had at least one household contact with a V. cholerae infection, and the MLVA data showed a combination of person-to-person and water-to-person transmission. The option misrepresents the relationship between stored water contamination and transmission dynamics by suggesting independence from the index case, which is not supported by the evidence.

239.Option: The high diversity of MLVA genotypes observed in clinical and water samples suggests that V. cholerae infections in household contacts are primarily due to multiple independent environmental exposures rather than person-to-person transmission.
Evidence: A total of 230 clinical and water V. cholerae isolates were compared by MLVA: 139 clinical isolates from 40 stool samples from cholera patients and their household contacts; 31 source water isolates from 7 source water samples; and 60 stored water isolates from 11 stored water samples.
We identified 31 MLVA genotypes: 7 MLVA genotypes from both clinical and water isolates, 9 genotypes from only water isolates, and 15 genotypes from only clinical isolates.
239.Rationale: The passage reports the identification of 31 MLVA genotypes, but this diversity does not support the inference that infections are 'primarily due to multiple independent environmental exposures rather than person-to-person transmission.' In fact, the study found that isolates from the same household had significantly fewer pairwise differences in MLVA loci than those from different households, and that many clinical isolates from the same household shared identical or nearly identical MLVA genotypes. This suggests that person-to-person transmission is a significant factor, not that infections are primarily due to independent environmental exposures.

239.Option: The significantly higher odds of symptomatic V. cholerae infection among household contacts with V. cholerae in their water source suggests that waterborne transmission is the dominant mode of infection, with person-to-person transmission playing a minor role.
Evidence: Contacts with V. cholerae in their source water had a significantly higher odds of a symptomatic V. cholerae infection (OR: 5.49, 95% CI: 1.07, 28.08) (25% (V. cholerae in water source) vs. 6% (no V. cholerae in water source)).
239.Rationale: The passage does show that household contacts with V. cholerae in their water source had significantly higher odds of symptomatic infection. However, this does not support the inference that waterborne transmission is the 'dominant mode of infection' with person-to-person transmission playing a 'minor role.' The study explicitly found a combination of transmission routes, and the MLVA data showed that isolates from the same household were closely related, indicating person-to-person transmission. The option overstates the evidence by suggesting that one mode of transmission is dominant over the other, which is not supported by the study's findings.

240.Option: The immunosuppressive effect of M. corti tetrathyridia excretory-secretory products is due to the presence of multiple low-molecular-weight proteins that are selectively secreted during active infection.
Evidence: Glycoproteins are the mediators of the immunosuppressive effects by McES

The data therefore suggested that the DC-suppressing glycoprotein(s) from M. corti tetrathyridia is/are specifically secreted by the larva and not that somatic products were leaking from the larval soma.
Taken together, our bio-activity based fractionation analyses suggest that (a) protein(s) of MW higher than 20 kDa might mediate the immunosuppressive activity of McES.
240.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that glycoproteins are the mediators of immunosuppressive effects and that these proteins have a molecular weight greater than 20 kDa. The option incorrectly claims the proteins are low-molecular-weight, which contradicts the evidence presented in the study.

240.Option: The immunosuppressive effect of M. corti tetrathyridia excretory-secretory products on dendritic cells is mediated by glycoproteins with a molecular weight greater than 20 kDa.
Evidence: Glycoproteins are the mediators of the immunosuppressive effects by McES
Taken together, our bio-activity based fractionation analyses suggest that (a) protein(s) of MW higher than 20 kDa might mediate the immunosuppressive activity of McES.

Active fractions were principally found within the MW group (i) suggesting that the active principle within McES might be of a MW >20kDa since fractions from group ii and iii showed minimal to no activity.
240.Rationale: The study provides direct evidence that the immunosuppressive effect is mediated by glycoproteins with a molecular weight greater than 20 kDa. This is supported by biochemical assays, fractionation analyses, and proteomic data showing that active fractions are in the >20kDa range.

240.Option: The ability of M. corti tetrathyridia to suppress dendritic cell activation is dependent on the metabolic activity of the parasite, as heat-killed larvae failed to recruit CD11c+ cells in vivo.
Evidence: Live M. corti tetrathyridia recruited growing proportion of CD11c+ (12% of the peritoneal exudate cells at day 3 p.i. and 27% at day 7 p.i.)
cells recruited less efficiently by heat-killed M. corti tetrathyridia harbored fewer CD11c+ host cells over time (10% of the peritoneal exudate cells at day 3 p.i. and 4% at day 7 p.i.)
240.Rationale: While the study shows that heat-killed larvae recruit fewer CD11c+ cells, this does not directly address the mechanism of dendritic cell suppression. The option incorrectly infers that metabolic activity is the primary factor in suppression, whereas the study focuses on the molecular characteristics of the secreted glycoproteins.

240.Option: The immunosuppressive effect of M. corti tetrathyridia excretory-secretory products on dendritic cells is primarily due to the presence of cysteine-rich secretory proteins and annexins, which are known to inhibit proteases and modulate membrane dynamics.
Evidence: Among the 55 McES proteins identified were a selection of enzymes (i.e. proteases, phosphatases, enolases, lipases, aldolases, hydroxylases, fucosidases, isomerases, hydrolases, dehydrogenases and kinases), ion-binding proteins, protein transporters, fatty acid-binding proteins, conserved structural proteins (actins, heat shock protein, collagen and actin-binding proteins) and conserved regulatory proteins (ubiquitins, 14-3-3 proteins and endophilins). Protease inhibitors (cystatins, serpins) and annexins were also detected as were a high number of proteins with unknown functions with homologues in other cestodes (conserved cestode proteins) and novel proteins as yet unidentified in other helminths.
Of the 55 proteins identified in the McES, 15 (27%) contained a predicted N-terminal signal peptide

The 5 most represented McES protein entities identified were: a novel hypothetical protein (MCOS_959401, exclusively detected in McES), an annexin homologue (Number 1, MCOS_561301, exclusively detected in McES), a hypothetical protein conserved among parasitic cestodes (Number 2, MCOS_155201, ~3-fold enrichment in McES), a cystatin (MCOS_775601, exclusively detected in McES) and an endophilin (MCOS_968201, 2.4-fold enrichment in McES)
240.Rationale: While the study mentions the presence of annexins and cystatins, it does not establish that these are the primary mediators of immunosuppression. The evidence points to glycoproteins of >20kDa as the key mediators, not specifically cysteine-rich proteins or annexins.

240.Option: The immunosuppressive activity of M. corti tetrathyridia excretory-secretory products is mediated by a combination of proteins and lipids that act synergistically to inhibit IL-12p70 production.
Evidence: Similarly to McES, lipid-free ES significantly diminished the production of LPS-driven IL-12p70 by BMDCs indicating that parasite released lipids are not required for McES-mediated BMDC suppression.
Together, these findings demonstrated that both parasite proteins and carbohydrates are necessary for the immunosuppressive effect of McES.

Whether McES-driven immunosuppression requires a concerted action of a separate protein and carbohydrate entity or whether single or multiple entities combining carbohydrate and protein parts are responsible for the observed McES potency to suppress DC function remained unclear.
240.Rationale: The study shows that lipids are not required for immunosuppression, and that both proteins and carbohydrates are necessary. The option incorrectly suggests a synergistic combination of proteins and lipids, which is not supported by the evidence.

241.Option: Because the PLC primer set is more sensitive than TgsGP and SRA primers, it can detect infections even when the target subspecies DNA is present at extremely low levels.
Evidence: The limit of detection was found to be an estimated 104 trypanosomes/mL for T. b. gambiense and T. b. rhodesiense using purified DNA from culture (S4 Fig). When the performance of the HRM was compared to TgsGP and SRA PCR on the same DNA dilution series, the HRM was as sensitive at detecting T. b. gambiense DNA and tenfold more sensitive for T. b. rhodesiense DNA (S2 and S3 Figs).
241.Rationale: The passage states that the HRM was as sensitive as TgsGP for T. b. gambiense and tenfold more sensitive for T. b. rhodesiense compared to SRA PCR. However, the PLC primer set is not described as being more sensitive than TgsGP and SRA primers. The PLC primer set is used to detect sufficient DNA quantity, not to detect infections at extremely low levels. The option misrepresents the role and sensitivity of the PLC primer set.

241.Option: The PLC primer set improves the overall sensitivity of the assay by amplifying a highly conserved region of the trypanosome genome, enabling detection of all T. brucei s. l. subspecies.
Evidence: The third primer set was designed to identify the presence of sufficient genetic material. These primers amplify a single-copy phospholipase-C (PLC) gene found in all members of the Trypanozoon group [22–25].
241.Rationale: The PLC primer set is described as amplifying a single-copy gene found in all members of the Trypanozoon group, but the passage does not state that this improves the overall sensitivity of the assay. The purpose of the PLC primer set is to determine if sufficient DNA is present for the sub-species primers to work, not to improve sensitivity or detect all subspecies. The option incorrectly attributes a role to the PLC primer set that is not supported by the passage.

241.Option: The inclusion of the PLC primer set enables the assay to detect both T. b. gambiense and T. b. rhodesiense in addition to confirming the presence of sufficient DNA, making it a comprehensive diagnostic tool.
Evidence: The third primer set was designed to identify the presence of sufficient genetic material. These primers amplify a single-copy phospholipase-C (PLC) gene found in all members of the Trypanozoon group [22–25].
Samples found to have sufficient DNA can then be screened using primers specific for T. brucei s. l. subspecies.
241.Rationale: The PLC primer set is used to confirm the presence of sufficient DNA, not to detect T. b. gambiense or T. b. rhodesiense. The detection of these subspecies is done using the TgsGP and SRA primers, respectively. The option incorrectly suggests that the PLC primer set itself detects the subspecies, which is not supported by the passage. The PLC primer set's role is to ensure that there is enough DNA for the subspecies-specific primers to work effectively.

241.Option: The HRM assay's ability to detect sufficient DNA through the PLC primer set allows for more reliable identification of samples that are truly negative for T. b. gambiense or T. b. rhodesiense, reducing the risk of false-negative results due to low DNA quantity.
Evidence: The third primer set was designed to identify the presence of sufficient genetic material. These primers amplify a single-copy phospholipase-C (PLC) gene found in all members of the Trypanozoon group [22–25].

A negative result may indicate the absence of the target species or simply that insufficient DNA is present. To differentiate between these two scenarios, primers have been designed to screen for other single-copy genes [21], namely the single-copy phospholipase-C (PLC) gene, present in all members of T. brucei s. l. [22–25]. Samples found to have sufficient DNA can then be screened using primers specific for T. brucei s. l. subspecies.
241.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that the PLC primer set is used to determine if sufficient DNA is present for the sub-species primers to work. This allows the assay to differentiate between a true negative result and a false negative due to low DNA quantity. By confirming the presence of sufficient DNA, the HRM assay can more reliably identify samples that are truly negative for T. b. gambiense or T. b. rhodesiense, reducing the risk of false-negative results. This option directly aligns with the information provided in the passage.

241.Option: The PLC primer set allows for the differentiation between active and latent infections in tsetse flies by detecting the presence of viable parasite DNA.
Evidence: The third primer set was designed to identify the presence of sufficient genetic material. These primers amplify a single-copy phospholipase-C (PLC) gene found in all members of the Trypanozoon group [22–25].
241.Rationale: The passage does not mention anything about the PLC primer set being used to differentiate between active and latent infections or to detect viable parasite DNA. The purpose of the PLC primer set is to confirm the presence of sufficient DNA for the sub-species primers to work, not to determine the infection status (active vs. latent) of the tsetse flies. The option introduces a concept not supported by the passage.

242.Option: The F1-ATP synthase γ subunit mutations that enable kDNA independence in T. evansi are directly responsible for reduced drug sensitivity in type A strains compared to type B.
Evidence: The specific mutations/deletions in the C-terminal region of F1-ATP synthase γ subunit enable differentiation among the Trypanozoon strains [8].
Once the genetically modified T. brucei are independent from kDNA maintenance and expression, they become multidrug resistant to the diamidine and phenanthridine class of drugs [30].

For isometamidium hydrochloride, the IC50 values of the Ethiopian T. evansi (IC50 0.6–6.2 ng ml-1) fall within the range of T.b. gambiense LiTat 1.3 (IC50 0.1 ng ml-1), T.b. brucei AnTat 1.1E (IC50 7.3 ng ml-1), T. evansi RoTat 1.2 (IC50 7.1 ng ml-1) and T. evansi KETRI 2479 (IC50 5.5 ng ml-1). However, the two Ethiopian T. evansi type A stocks (IC50 4.3–6.2 ng ml-1) appear to be threefold less sensitive that the three type B stocks (IC50 0.6–1.9 ng ml-1).

For suramin, large differences in IC50 values were found among the Ethiopian T. evansi (IC50 15.9–261.5 ng ml-1) stocks and among the other strains: T.b. brucei AnTat 1.1E (IC50 39.5 ng ml-1) and T. evansi RoTat 1.2 (IC50 35.8 ng ml-1) appear highly susceptible, while T.b. gambiense LiTat 1.3 (IC50 134.0 ng ml-1) and T. evansi KETRI 2479 (IC50 222.4 ng ml-1) are less susceptible. The two Ethiopian T. evansi type A (IC50 153.5–261.5 ng ml-1) appear to be tenfold less sensitive than the three type B (IC50 15.9–27.6 ng ml-1).

For diminazene diaceturate, the IC50 values of all Ethiopian T. evansi (IC50 17.5–48.5 ng ml-1) are higher than those of T.b. gambiense LiTat 1.3 (IC50 5.2 ng ml-1) and T. evansi RoTat 1.2 (IC50 13.8 ng ml-1), but similar to T.b. brucei AnTat 1.1E (IC50 39.6 ng ml-1) and T. evansi KETRI 2479 (IC50 24.0 ng ml-1). The two Ethiopian T. evansi type A (IC50 37.4–48.5 ng ml-1) appear to be twofold less sensitive than the three type B (IC50 17.5–25.9 ng ml-1).
242.Rationale: The passage provides evidence that T. evansi type A strains are less sensitive to certain drugs compared to type B strains, but it does not establish a direct causal relationship between F1-ATP synthase γ subunit mutations and this reduced drug sensitivity. The text mentions that F1-ATP synthase γ subunit mutations enable kDNA independence but does not explicitly link these mutations to the observed differences in drug sensitivity between type A and type B strains. The drug sensitivity differences are presented as observed phenomena without a direct attribution to the F1-ATP synthase γ subunit mutations. Therefore, the inference that these mutations are directly responsible for the reduced drug sensitivity in type A strains compared to type B is not directly supported by the evidence in the passage.

242.Option: The progressive loss of kinetoplast DNA during in vitro culture is associated with increased resistance to diamidine and phenanthridine class of drugs in T. evansi strains.
Evidence: Once the genetically modified T. brucei are independent from kDNA maintenance and expression, they become multidrug resistant to the diamidine and phenanthridine class of drugs [30].

DAPI staining was performed on in vivo and in vitro propagated stocks (Fig 4). In vitro culture did not change the percentage of kinetoplastic cells in T.b. gambiense LiTat 1.3 (99%), T.b. brucei AnTat 1.1E (99%) and MCAM/ET/2013/MU/09 (0%). On the other hand, already after 30 days in vitro culture a decrease in the percentage of kinetoplastic cells was observed in T. evansi RoTat 1.2 (89%), T. evansi KETRI 2479 (81%), MCAM/ET/2013/MU/04 (97%), 14 (93%) and 15 (94%) compared to non-in vitro adapted trypanosomes. After 60 days of in vitro culture, the percentage of kinetoplastic cells dropped even further for T. evansi KETRI 2479 (64%), MCAM/ET/2013/MU/04 (89%) and 10 (35%).

No significant correlation was observed between the percentage of kinetoplastid cells of all in vitro adapted T. evansi stocks (including day 30 and day 60) and their IC50 values for melarsomine dihydrochloride (ρ = -0.13, p = 0. 67), isometamidium hydrochloride (ρ = -0.324, p = 0.278), suramin (ρ = -0.097, p = 0.752) and diminazene diacetureate (ρ = -0.355, p = 0.233). These data suggest that among the in vitro adapted Ethiopian T. evansi stocks there is no relation between the drug sensitivity and the presence of kinetoplast DNA.
242.Rationale: The passage provides evidence that in vitro culture leads to a progressive loss of kinetoplast DNA in T. evansi strains, but it does not support the claim that this loss is associated with increased resistance to diamidine and phenanthridine class of drugs. In fact, the data show no significant correlation between the percentage of kinetoplastid cells and drug sensitivity. The text explicitly states that there is no relation between the drug sensitivity and the presence of kinetoplast DNA in the in vitro adapted T. evansi stocks. Therefore, the inference that the progressive loss of kinetoplast DNA is associated with increased resistance to these drugs is not supported by the evidence in the passage.

242.Option: The presence or partial absence of kDNA in the in vitro adapted T. evansi stocks did not correspond with the drug sensitivity phenotype.

These data suggest that among the in vitro adapted Ethiopian T. evansi stocks there is no relation between the drug sensitivity and the presence of kinetoplast DNA.
The loss of kDNA does not seem to influence rodent infectivity since all cryostabilates made from day 60 in vitro cultures remained infective for mice with detectable parasitaemia at 4–5 DPI.

Therefore, the inference that the presence or partial absence of kDNA did not correspond with the drug sensitivity phenotype is directly supported by the evidence in the passage.

After 60 days of in vitro culture, the percentage of kinetoplastic cells dropped even further for T. evansi KETRI 2479 (64%), MCAM/ET/2013/MU/04 (89%) and 10 (35%).

No significant correlation was observed between the percentage of kinetoplastid cells of all in vitro adapted T. evansi stocks (including day 30 and day 60) and their IC50 values for melarsomine dihydrochloride (ρ = -0.13, p = 0. 67), isometamidium hydrochloride (ρ = -0.324, p = 0.278), suramin (ρ = -0.097, p = 0.752) and diminazene diacetureate (ρ = -0.355, p = 0.233). These data suggest that among the in vitro adapted Ethiopian T. evansi stocks there is no relation between the drug sensitivity and the presence of kinetoplast DNA.
The loss of kDNA does not seem to influence rodent infectivity since all cryostabilates made from day 60 in vitro cultures remained infective for mice with detectable parasitaemia at 4–5 DPI.
242.Rationale: The passage provides evidence that the loss of kDNA during in vitro culture leads to a measurable decline in the percentage of kinetoplastic cells over time, but it does not support the claim that this decline correlates with reduced drug sensitivity. 243.Option: The higher infection rates in camels compared to experimental mice and rabbits suggest that camels are more susceptible to 'Ca. Anaplasma camelii' due to their prolonged exposure to multiple vectors, including ticks and biting flies.
Evidence: The prevalence of Anaplasma infection in camels was generally high throughout the year. The rate of infection during dry, wet, and late wet seasons was 70.3% (n = 175/249), 63.9% (n = 179/280), and 77.9% (n = 348/447), respectively (Table D in S1 Text).

243.Rationale: The passage provides data on infection rates in camels (70.3-77.9%) and test animals (47.4-25%), but does not establish a causal relationship between susceptibility and exposure to multiple vectors. The option assumes that camels are more susceptible due to prolonged exposure, but the passage does not provide evidence about the role of multiple vectors in camel susceptibility. The study specifically focuses on camel keds as vectors, not ticks, and the data on ticks is not presented in the passage body.

Evidence: The prevalence of 'Ca. Anaplasma camelii' in keds varied with season, with the percentage prevalence being: 9.9% (dry), 20.8% (wet), and 28.9% (late wet) (Table D in S1 Text).

The success of 'Ca. Anaplasma camelii' transmission to the experimental mice and rabbits by camel keds during blood feeding was influenced by the exposure frequencies to fly bites (OR = 0.6504 (95% CI = 0.4714–0.8373), p = 0.0004, n = 115) (Table A-C in S1 Text).
243.Rationale: The passage shows that keds can transmit the pathogen to experimental animals, but does not provide evidence that keds can sustain and amplify the pathogen in their bodies. The data on ked prevalence (9.9-28.9%) is much lower than camel prevalence (70.3-77.9%), which would be expected if keds were amplifying the pathogen. The study also notes that mechanical transmission of Anaplasma pathogens is thought to be possible only at high parasitaemia, suggesting that the presence in keds may not indicate biological transmission.

243.Option: The low prevalence of 'Ca. Anaplasma camelii' in camel keds, despite high infection rates in camels, suggests that transmission by keds is mechanical rather than biological.

The prevalence of 'Ca. Anaplasma camelii' in keds varied with season, with the percentage prevalence being: 9.9% (dry), 20.8% (wet), and 28.9% (late wet) (Table D in S1 Text).

243.Rationale: The passage provides clear evidence of a significant disparity between camel infection rates (70.3-77.9%) and ked infection rates (9.9-28.9%). This discrepancy, combined with the established epidemiological principle that mechanical transmission requires high parasitaemia, supports the inference that keds are not biologically transmitting the pathogen. The low ked prevalence despite high camel prevalence is more consistent with mechanical transmission where only a subset of keds would acquire the pathogen during feeding on highly parasitemic camels.
