Evidence: The prevalence of Anaplasma infection in camels was generally high throughout the year. The rate of infection during dry, wet, and late wet seasons was 70.3% (n = 175/249), 63.9% (n = 179/280), and 77.9% (n = 348/447), respectively (Table D in S1 Text).
The prevalence of 'Ca. Anaplasma camelii' in keds varied with season, with the percentage prevalence being: 9.9% (dry), 20.8% (wet), and 28.9% (late wet) (Table D in S1 Text).
243.Rationale: While the passage shows that the pathogen is present in both camels and keds across all seasons, it does not establish that keds play a central role in long-term maintenance. The seasonal variation in ked prevalence (9.9-28.9%) is much lower than camel prevalence (70.3-77.9%), suggesting that camels are the primary reservoir. The study also notes that camel keds lack competence to transmit Trypanosoma evansi to mice and cattle keds are incompetent to transmit Anaplasma marginale, indicating that keds may not be effective vectors for long-term maintenance.

243.Option: The seasonal variation in ked infestation levels, with peak numbers during the dry season, suggests that 'Ca. Anaplasma camelii' transmission is most efficient during this period due to increased host-vector contact.

The success of 'Ca. Anaplasma camelii' transmission to the experimental mice and rabbits by camel keds during blood feeding was influenced by the exposure frequencies to fly bites (OR = 0.6504 (95% CI = 0.4714–0.8373), p = 0.0004, n = 115) (Table A-C in S1 Text).
243.Rationale: The passage provides direct evidence of seasonal variation in ked infestation levels, with peak numbers during the dry season. It also shows that transmission success is influenced by exposure frequency to fly bites. These two pieces of evidence together support the inference that transmission is most efficient during the dry season when ked infestation levels are highest, leading to increased host-vector contact and thus higher transmission potential.

244.Option: Epidemiological studies involving nomadic populations need to factor in the fluidity in geographic localisation and household definition that are characteristic of mobile pastoralist populations.
Evidence: The pastoralist communities comprise a heterogeneous group of different ethnicities and other differences, including languages, social organisations, degrees of mobility, sedentariness, livestock ownership, and economic diversification.

Pastoral activity lies on a continuum from sedentary pastoralists who engage in agriculture and other economic ventures, to mobile pastoralists or nomads who practice transhumance with all their livestock.

The basic social unit is the nuclear family, which could be monogamous or polygamous. Some families split up at certain times of the year, and the Imurrani (unmarried young adult men) undertake long distance migration with the livestock, while the rest of the family remains in a more-or-less fixed residence from which they look after sheep and goats.

The homes or settlements are referred to as nkang or manyatta, and they consist of a single circular hut, or multiple huts arranged in a circular manner with a space in the middle, surrounded by an acacia thorn fence. The houses are inexpensively constructed with muds and sticks that can readily be abandoned (midjoni [abandoned homestead where there are still some structures standing]) in case of conflict or to look for grazing land for the remaining livestock. Some families could be split kilometres away at the same time, in different counties or countries, despite constituting the same nuclear family.
244.Rationale: The passage provides extensive evidence of the dynamic nature of nomadic pastoralist populations. It describes how these populations have fluid geographic localization, with families splitting up and migrating seasonally, and how their social units (households) can be reconfigured. The passage also explains that their homes can be easily abandoned and that family members may be located kilometers apart while still constituting the same nuclear family. These factors directly support the conclusion that epidemiological studies must account for this fluidity in geographic localization and household definition.

244.Option: The use of satellite imagery and GIS tools to create a sampling frame can reduce bureaucratic delays and improve the timeliness of research planning in mobile populations.
Evidence: We created a spatial sampling frame by directly identifying and creating data points for all features that looked like a household within the 2018 level 1 global administrative unit boundary for Samburu County, using high-resolution satellite images.

The GPS coordinates of the selected grids and the selected homesteads were loaded into a GIS based mobile application provided by the Environmental Systems Research Institute (ESRI) East Africa called Collector.
244.Rationale: While the passage describes the use of satellite imagery and GIS tools in creating a sampling frame, it does not make any claims about reducing bureaucratic delays or improving the timeliness of research planning. The focus is on the technical aspects of creating a sampling frame for a mobile population, not on the efficiency or speed of the research process. The option introduces a benefit (reducing bureaucratic delays) that is not supported by the passage.

Evidence: The basic social unit is the nuclear family, which could be monogamous or polygamous. Some families split up at certain times of the year, and the Imurrani (unmarried young adult men) undertake long distance migration with the livestock, while the rest of the family remains in a more-or-less fixed residence from which they look after sheep and goats.
Some families could be split kilometres away at the same time, in different counties or countries, despite constituting the same nuclear family.
244.Rationale: The passage explicitly describes the limitations of a fixed household definition in mobile populations. It also notes that family members can be kilometers apart while still constituting the same nuclear family. The option introduces a new association that is not supported by the evidence in the passage.

244.Option: The high rate of household movement during the dry season necessitates conducting data collection during the rainy season to ensure accurate representation of population distribution.
Evidence: Data collection was carried out between December 2019 and March 2020, three months after completing the geospatial cluster and household mapping.

There was an even distribution of selected grids across all sub-counties in Samburu. All grids were identified during the data collection period and data collectors successfully navigated to the 36 selected grids.

245.Option: The observed biological activity of AgCNPs is due to the synergistic interaction between silver and cerium oxide, where silver acts as a co-catalyst that enhances the redox activity of cerium oxide through surface defect engineering.
Evidence: The general bioactivity observed for CNPs is often ascribed to their chemical interaction with the local, biological environment and their catalytic response to (radical) oxygen species.
In particular, catalysis occurs at surface defects, such as oxygen vacancies, and is highly dependent on physicochemical conditions.

Incorporation of the known larvicidal agent, silver, into the CNP structure may allow for an increased total larvicidal response. Similar effects have been observed for industrial applications of metal catalysts on cerium oxide supports.
245.Rationale: The passage provides multiple lines of evidence supporting this inference. Finally, it references similar effects in industrial applications of metal catalysts on cerium oxide supports. These statements collectively support the conclusion that the biological activity results from synergistic interaction between silver and cerium oxide through surface defect engineering.

The general bioactivity observed for CNPs is often ascribed to their chemical interaction with the local, biological environment and their catalytic response to (radical) oxygen species.
In particular, catalysis occurs at surface defects, such as oxygen vacancies, and is highly dependent on physicochemical conditions.

It explicitly states that the ratio of Ce3+ to Ce4+ redox states was 34.7% for AgCNPs, indicating the presence of redox-active Ce3+ species. It also describes how catalysis in CNPs occurs at surface defects like oxygen vacancies and is dependent on physicochemical conditions. The general bioactivity of CNPs is ascribed to their catalytic response to oxygen species. Activating silver for larval killing, through incorporation with cerium oxide nanomaterial, will greatly reduce the silver amount(s) available for release into the environment, thus improving environmental conscientiousness while retaining efficacy as tools for mosquito control personnel.
245.Rationale: The passage provides clear evidence supporting this inference. It explicitly states that negligible free silver remains in the solutions, indicating that the observed effects are due to incorporated silver rather than free Ag+. It also compares the amount of silver needed for larval death in AgCNP versus free silver ion, showing a significant reduction in the AgCNP case. Finally, it notes that incorporating silver with cerium oxide reduces the amount of silver available for release into the environment while retaining efficacy. 245.Rationale: The passage actually shows the opposite of what this option claims. It states that when larval food was present, AgCNPs showed significant larvicidal effect, but when food was withheld, the larvicidal activity was even greater. The authors even hypothesized that the presence of organic matter might blunt the larvicidal activity, which was confirmed by the results showing increased activity when food was withheld. 245.Option: The reduction in egg clutch observed in adult mosquitoes fed AgCNPs via blood meal is due to the physical obstruction of oocytes by undissolved nanoparticles in the reproductive tract.

245.Rationale: The passage provides evidence that the reduction in egg clutch is due to oocyte growth arrest rather than physical obstruction. 247.Option: The identification of SNPs at TNFSF pathways associated with T1R outcomes in populations from Vietnam and Brazil suggests that a universal genetic test for leprosy reactions can be developed with minimal validation across different populations.
Evidence: The present study was designed to investigate by means of survival curves the risk factors associated with reactional episodes by testing Brazilian leprosy patients and the effects of 15 SNPs at 8 genes.

The markers included 11 SNPs selected to replicate the previously reported associations of the TLR1, NOD2, and IL6 genes and the remaining SNPs in 4 candidate genes TNF, LTA, IFNG, and IL10, in reference to markers previously associated with leprosy per se as an outcome [30,31,34–37]. Here, we confirmed the association between IL6 and NOD2 SNPs and leprosy reactions among Brazilians, and demonstrated that the NOD2 genotype associated with a decreased risk for developing a reaction is also related to lower levels of IL-6 in the sera of non-reactional patients.
247.Rationale: The passage only mentions associations in Brazilian populations and does not provide evidence of SNPs in TNFSF pathways being associated with T1R outcomes in Vietnam. The study confirms associations in Brazilians but does not support the claim of a 'universal genetic test' requiring minimal validation across different populations. The inference about universality and minimal validation is not supported by the evidence presented.

247.Option: The failure to detect associations with TLR1 SNPs in this study, despite prior reports, indicates that genetic markers for leprosy reactions are unreliable and should not be pursued further in future research.
Evidence: The markers included 11 SNPs selected to replicate the previously reported associations of the TLR1, NOD2, and IL6 genes and the remaining SNPs in 4 candidate genes TNF, LTA, IFNG, and IL10, in reference to markers previously associated with leprosy per se as an outcome [30,31,34–37].
247.Rationale: The passage does not explicitly state that TLR1 SNPs failed to show associations in this study. It only mentions that TLR1 SNPs were included to replicate previous findings. The option makes a definitive claim about the reliability of genetic markers and suggests they should not be pursued further, which is not supported by the passage. The study's failure to detect an association with TLR1 SNPs is not mentioned, so this conclusion cannot be drawn.

247.Option: The presence of risk alleles for leprosy reactions does not have additive or synergistic effects, suggesting that combining multiple genetic markers may not be feasible for developing a predictive genetic test.
Evidence: The present study was designed to investigate by means of survival curves the risk factors associated with reactional episodes by testing Brazilian leprosy patients and the effects of 15 SNPs at 8 genes.
From the 15 candidate SNPs (total counts and frequencies detailed at S3 Table), 2 showed different Kaplan–Meier curves between genotype groups (log-rank p-value < 0.05).
247.Rationale: The passage does not provide any information about the additive or synergistic effects of risk alleles. The study tested 15 SNPs and found associations with two of them, but it does not discuss whether these effects are additive or synergistic. 247.Option: The lack of association between IL6 rs2069845 and serum IL-6 levels in this study suggests that genetic markers are not useful for predicting leprosy reactions, as they do not correlate with measurable biological outcomes.
Evidence: The cytokine dosage showed a heterogeneous distribution among the patients, and stratified analysis showed no differences between groups in either the patients overall (S2 Fig) or the reactional patients (S3 Fig). The study found that IL6 and NOD2 SNPs were associated with leprosy reactions among Brazilians. The option incorrectly generalizes the lack of association in one aspect to dismiss the utility of genetic markers overall.

247.Option: Since the study confirmed gender and clinical form as risk factors for leprosy reactions, a genetic test should be developed to complement these non-genetic variables in predicting reaction outcomes.
Evidence: As observed, the length of time before a leprosy reaction, as described by survival curves, shows a significant difference when considering the variables of gender (p = 0.002) and clinical form (p < 0.001).

The present study was designed to investigate by means of survival curves the risk factors associated with reactional episodes by testing Brazilian leprosy patients and the effects of 15 SNPs at 8 genes.

Here, we confirmed the association between IL6 and NOD2 SNPs and leprosy reactions among Brazilians, and demonstrated that the NOD2 genotype associated with a decreased risk for developing a reaction is also related to lower levels of IL-6 in the sera of non-reactional patients.
247.Rationale: The passage confirms that gender and clinical form are significant risk factors for leprosy reactions. It also demonstrates that genetic markers (IL6 and NOD2 SNPs) are associated with leprosy reactions. The option suggests developing a genetic test to complement these non-genetic variables, which is a logical inference based on the study's findings. The evidence supports the idea that genetic markers can be used alongside non-genetic variables to improve prediction of reaction outcomes.

248.Option: The high antibody titers observed in geese following DENV2 vaccination, reaching up to 1:6,400,000, suggest that goose-derived IgY could be used as a prophylactic vaccine alternative in humans due to its strong immunogenicity and long-lasting response.
Evidence: The average serum endpoint titer across all weeks post vaccination was 1:924,807 with the highest titer being 1:6,400,000 at 7 weeks post vaccination (Fig 1B).
248.Rationale: While the passage confirms high antibody titers in geese, it does not provide evidence that goose-derived IgY could be used as a prophylactic vaccine in humans. The study focuses on therapeutic use of IgY, not prophylactic vaccination. The passage does not discuss immunogenicity in humans or long-lasting responses in human subjects. The high titers in geese are relevant to the production of therapeutic antibodies, not to the development of a vaccine.

248.Option: The inability of anti-DENV IgY to bind mammalian Fcγ receptors means that it does not induce antibody-dependent enhancement (ADE) of dengue virus infection, making it a promising therapeutic candidate for treating dengue without increasing the risk of severe disease.
Evidence: IgY does not interact with other Fc-binding receptors, which limits its ability to elicit an inflammatory reaction in humans [19–24].
Therefore, the inability of anti-DENV IgY to induce ADE [18].
Neither the anti-DENV IgY nor control IgY sera were enhancing at any dilution tested (Fig 3).
248.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that IgY does not bind mammalian Fcγ receptors and therefore does not induce ADE. This is further supported by the in vitro experiments showing no enhancement of DENV2 infection. The therapeutic potential of IgY is directly tied to its inability to cause ADE, which is a major risk factor for severe dengue. This inference is directly supported by the evidence in the passage.

248.Option: The recognition of novel epitopes on the DENV2 E protein by anti-DENV2 IgY suggests that IgY-based therapies may be effective across all dengue serotypes due to conserved structural motifs.
Evidence: Our results suggest that the majority of the peptide sequences that were recognized were within the E protein, whereas there were few in the NS1 and NS3 proteins, and one with very low mean MFI in the prM protein.
Some of the peptides recognized by the full-length and alternatively spliced anti-DENV2 IgY were similar, while others appeared unique to each of the different antibody populations.

The inference about cross-serotype effectiveness requires additional information about epitope conservation, which is not provided in the passage. 248.Option: The moderate protection observed with control IgY in vivo suggests that IgY may exert non-specific immune activation in mice, potentially enhancing innate immune responses and reducing viral load independently of neutralizing antibodies.
Evidence: 1 mg of control IgY yielded 25% protection (n = 4, p = 0.04 comparing equal amounts of anti-DENV2 IgY and control IgY) (Fig 4A).
248.Rationale: The passage reports that control IgY provided 25% protection, but it does not provide evidence that this protection is due to non-specific immune activation. The study does not investigate the mechanism of this protection, and the inference about non-specific immune activation is speculative. The passage does not support the conclusion that IgY exerts non-specific immune activation.

248.Option: Because IgY does not bind human complement, it is unlikely to contribute to immune enhancement during secondary dengue infections, thereby reducing the risk of vascular leakage and severe disease.
Evidence: Although IgY is able to activate complement in the avian system, its unique structure prevents it from being able to activate human complement [23].
it has been demonstrated that products of complement activation, specifically C3a, C5a, and SC5b-9 are elevated in patients with DHF [25–28].
248.Rationale: The passage states that IgY does not activate human complement, but it does not provide evidence that this prevents immune enhancement during secondary infections. The relationship between complement activation and immune enhancement is mentioned, but the inference that IgY's inability to activate human complement reduces the risk of severe disease is not directly supported by the passage. The passage does not establish a causal link between complement activation and severe disease in the context of IgY therapy.

249.Option: Despite repeated rounds of school-based deworming, hookworm remains prevalent in adult men in Kwale County, suggesting that school-based deworming alone is insufficient to control community-wide hookworm transmission.
Evidence: The survey was conducted in Kwale County, Kenya, which has benefitted from consecutive, annual rounds of deworming implemented by the Ministry of Health and Ministry of Science Education and Technology through the National School Based Deworming Programme (NSBDP) since 2012 [14].

Hookworm prevalence increased with age across both sexes, and was higher in males (Fig 3a). Infection intensity remained fairly constant through childhood, before increasing at age twenty and above in males, and thirty and above in females.

Hookworm-infected individuals were found in 119 of the 120 clusters although considerable heterogeneity in prevalence by cluster was observed (Fig 2b); 16 clusters exhibited a hookworm prevalence below 5%, whereas hookworm prevalence exceeded 50% in three clusters.
249.Rationale: The passage provides evidence that Kwale County has implemented annual school-based deworming since 2012. However, the data show that hookworm prevalence increases with age and is particularly high in adult males. This pattern suggests that school-based deworming, which targets children, is not sufficient to control community-wide transmission, as adult populations remain a significant reservoir of infection. The high prevalence in adults despite deworming in children supports the inference that the current strategy is insufficient for community-wide control.

249.Option: The observed age-related increase in hookworm infection intensity among adults suggests that the current deworming strategy is ineffective because it does not account for the biological differences in susceptibility between age groups.
Evidence: Hookworm prevalence increased with age across both sexes, and was higher in males (Fig 3a). Infection intensity remained fairly constant through childhood, before increasing at age twenty and above in males, and thirty and above in females.

The majority of individual, household and community-level factors investigated were marginally associated with hookworm infection in the univariable analysis (S1 Table). Adjusting for other associated factors, many of these associations remained, with males and older age groups exhibiting greater odds of infection (Table 1).
249.Rationale: While the passage does show an age-related increase in hookworm infection intensity among adults, it does not provide evidence that this is due to biological differences in susceptibility between age groups. 254.Option: The frequent and prolonged interactions between humans and bats in buildings, combined with common attempts to remove bats, create repeated opportunities for pathogen exposure through both direct and indirect contact, as well as through domestic animals that may act as intermediate hosts.

Over 90% of respondents reported indirect contact with bats, mostly through interactions with their feces and urine (n = 98).

254.Rationale: The passage provides multiple lines of evidence supporting this inference. First, it establishes that bat presence in buildings is both frequent and prolonged (70% for >5 years, 88% year-round). Third, it shows that attempts to remove bats (80% of participants) often result in direct contact (47% of removal attempts). All these elements together support the inference about repeated pathogen exposure opportunities.

The inference about 'likely to spread widely within communities' requires additional assumptions not supported by the data presented. Boxplots of daily risk are shown in Fig 3 in log10 scale and reveal the mean risk of waterborne protozoal infection per day, accounting for boiling rates, varied between 0.06% and 1.5% (between about 1/1000 and 15/1000 children infected daily) (see Fig F for risk profiles in S1 Supporting Information).

Scenario two (ENV) assumed the probability of a parasite shed by a human host (H) was directly proportional to the ratio of the estimated environmental load of parasites shed by the population of humans to the total estimated environmental load shed by the population of humans, livestock, and domestic animals in Puri District [15]. Livestock included cattle, buffalo, sheep, and goats and domestic animals included dogs, as these represented the majority of animal species in the study region.

Scenario three (MST) used microbial fecal source tracking (MST) results by year and tubewell type for the same protozoa-tested tubewell water samples [55]. MST uses molecular methods to detect enteric bacteria specific to a host to identify the likely source of fecal contamination.
255.Rationale: While the study does consider the source of parasites (human vs. non-human), it does not mention spatial proximity to latrines as a determinant. Instead, it uses environmental load calculations (ENV scenario) and microbial source tracking (MST scenario) to estimate the fraction of human vs. non-human parasites. The option incorrectly introduces a spatial proximity factor not discussed in the passage.

255.Option: The concentration of Cryptosporidium and Giardia in tubewell water varies significantly between years, which is the primary driver of differences in estimated disease burden between 2012 and 2013.
Evidence: Protozoal concentrations in tubewells.As previously reported [15], Cryptosporidium and Giardia concentrations in deep and shallow tubewells were measured once in 206 tubewells in Odisha Sanitation Trial villages, during the monsoon season (June-August) of either 2012 or 2013. A two-sample Kolmogorov-Smirnov test indicated concentrations were significantly different between years (P-value = < 0.001).

Estimated child diarrhea daily point prevalence is shown in Fig 4 and reveals a wide range of prevalence, from a median as high as 6.5% to as low as < 1% at the population level considering estimated levels of water treatment by boiling. Across all tubewell types and years, assuming all parasites were shed from humans (HUM) increased diarrhea prevalence estimates by two-fold or more, compared to using fractions based on environmental parasite loading estimates (ENV) for Puri District (see Table H in S1 Supporting Information). Comparing years, 2012 had a much higher estimated prevalence of diarrhea compared to 2013 under all scenarios. In 2012, deep tubewell users were estimated to have higher diarrhea prevalence than shallow tubewell users, with Cryptosporidium often causing more symptomatic infections than Giardia across human pathogenicity scenarios.

The simulated 7-day recall diarrhea period prevalence from waterborne infections are compared to the all-cause observed levels reported in the Odisha Sanitation Trial during the monsoon season (~12% in 2012 and ~ 9% in 2013) [21] in Table 4. The fraction of the observed all-cause diarrhea during each monsoon season that can be explained by the estimated tubewell drinking water protozoa infections under each human pathogenicity scenario (HUM, MST, ENV) is presented. The results indicate that as much as 65.8% or as little as 2.9% of the all-cause diarrhea burden in < 5 children can be attributed to waterborne infections from Cryptosporidium and Giardia in tubewell water. Depending on the scenario examined (HUM, MST, ENV), we see a wide range of estimated attributable fractions, but a clear trend that a far greater fraction of diarrhea can be attributed to Cryptosporidium and Giardia contamination in drinking water during the monsoon season in 2012, when child diarrhea prevalence rates were higher (~12% 7-day recall period) compared to 2013 (~9% 7-day recall period).
255.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that concentrations of Cryptosporidium and Giardia in tubewells were significantly different between 2012 and 2013 (p < 0.001). The results also show that 2012 had much higher estimated diarrhea prevalence compared to 2013. The comparison of simulated 7-day recall prevalence with observed levels further supports that the higher concentration in 2012 was a primary driver of the higher disease burden that year. This directly supports the conclusion that concentration variation between years is a key factor.

255.Option: The morbidity ratio of symptomatic infections for Cryptosporidium and Giardia is the most sensitive parameter in the model, leading to the greatest variation in estimated disease burden.
Evidence: Results from the sensitivity analysis for the HUM scenario combined by sample year and tubewell type are shown in Fig 5. Of the parameters examined, Cryptosporidium 7-day recall diarrhea period prevalence estimates were most sensitive to the rate parameter used in the dose-response model, the fraction of detected parasites assumed to be viable, and the method recovery of parasites from tubewell samples. For Giardia, diarrhea estimates were most sensitive to model parameters representing viability, method recovery, and the concentration of Giardia observed in tubewell samples. For both parasites, diarrhea estimates were least sensitive to the volume of water ingested, the fraction of the population that treated water by boiling, the morbidity ratio, and recall bias as these parameters had sensitivity indices generally below 0.2.

To determine if a susceptible child transitioned into the infected state on a given day, first the QMRA model was used to assign a probability of infection, P(i), modeled as a Bernoulli variable (1 = infection, 0 = no infection), to each child. If P(i) = 1, the probability that the infection resulted in a diarrheic symptom (P(s)) was modeled based on published studies of the diarrhea morbidity ratio for children diagnosed with Cryptosporidium [45, 47] or Giardia [9, 46] in developing countries. These included two large scale, multi-country, case-control studies designed to identify primary pathogens responsible for causing diarrhea in children in developing countries (the GEMS and MAL-ED) and two smaller scale regional studies conducted in settings with inadequate access to clean water and sanitation. Variability in the morbidity ratio across studies was modeled as a Bernoulli process with parameter p represented by a uniform distribution between of 0.28–0.68 and 0.49–0.59 for Cryptosporidium and Giardia respectively (see P(s) in Table 1).
255.Rationale: The sensitivity analysis explicitly states that the morbidity ratio had sensitivity indices generally below 0.2 for both parasites, indicating it was not a major driver of variation in the model. The passage also describes the morbidity ratio as being modeled with a uniform distribution based on published studies, but the sensitivity analysis shows it was not the most sensitive parameter. This directly contradicts the claim in the option that it is the most sensitive parameter.

255.Option: The fraction of parasites in drinking water that are from humans versus non-human animal hosts and that are species infectious-to-humans has important implications for infection risk and diarrhea disease burden estimates.
Evidence: Fraction of detected parasites of a species infectious-to-humans.As tubewell samples may have contained a mixture of human and non-human shed parasites, we simulated three different scenarios to estimate the fraction of parasites detected in a tubewell sample that was a species infectious-to-humans (Sp), with Sp determined as follows: (2) where H is the fraction of human shed parasites and  is the fraction of non-human shed parasites of a species infectious-to-humans (i.e. zoonotic fraction), with H calculated for each year (2012–2013), tubewell type (deep and shallow), and parasite (Cryptosporidium and Giardia) when appropriate.

Scenario one (HUM) assumed humans shed all of the detected parasites and 100% of both parasites were species infectious-to-humans. Therefore, H = 1 always and  is irrelevant in the calculation of Sp. The HUM scenario equated to the case with the highest level of parasites infectious-to-humans in tubewells and thus represented the scenario with the highest risk of infection.

Scenario two (ENV) assumed the probability of a parasite shed by a human host (H) was directly proportional to the ratio of the estimated environmental load of parasites shed by the population of humans to the total estimated environmental load shed by the population of humans, livestock, and domestic animals in Puri District [15]. Livestock included cattle, buffalo, sheep, and goats and domestic animals included dogs, as these represented the majority of animal species in the study region.

Scenario three (MST) used microbial fecal source tracking (MST) results by year and tubewell type for the same protozoa-tested tubewell water samples [55]. MST uses molecular methods to detect enteric bacteria specific to a host to identify the likely source of fecal contamination.

Estimated child diarrhea daily point prevalence is shown in Fig 4 and reveals a wide range of prevalence, from a median as high as 6.5% to as low as < 1% at the population level considering estimated levels of water treatment by boiling. Across all tubewell types and years, assuming all parasites were shed from humans (HUM) increased diarrhea prevalence estimates by two-fold or more, compared to using fractions based on environmental parasite loading estimates (ENV) for Puri District (see Table H in S1 Supporting Information).
255.Rationale: The passage explicitly discusses the importance of the fraction of parasites that are from humans versus non-human hosts and that are species infectious-to-humans. It describes three scenarios (HUM, ENV, MST) to estimate this fraction and shows that assuming all parasites are from humans (HUM) significantly increases diarrhea prevalence estimates compared to other scenarios. This directly supports the conclusion that this factor has important implications for infection risk and disease burden estimates.

256.Option: The fact that 74.9% of patients reported trismus indicates that surgical release of ankylosis is ineffective and should be avoided in Noma survivors.
Evidence: The acute phase of the disease has a high mortality, and the evolution is exceptionally rapid, causing the loss of soft and bony facial tissues within days [17,18].
The surgical phase is usually initiated 6 to 18 months after a period of quiescence [20].

Even after modern and sophisticated interventions (particularly reconstructive and plastic surgeries), Noma is associated with considerable morbidity and mortality, and, understandably, psychosocial impacts on the patients remain substantial [21].
256.Rationale: The passage does not provide any evidence regarding the effectiveness or ineffectiveness of surgical release of ankylosis in Noma survivors. While it mentions that 74.9% of patients reported trismus, it does not link this statistic to the effectiveness of surgical interventions. The passage does state that surgical interventions are initiated 6 to 18 months after quiescence and that even with modern interventions, morbidity and mortality remain high. However, this does not imply that surgical release is ineffective or should be avoided. The conclusion in the option is not supported by the evidence provided.

256.Option: The study's findings indicate that Noma is primarily a disease of malnutrition and poor sanitation, and therefore, improving public health infrastructure alone would eliminate the disease in Ethiopia.
Evidence: The major risk factors of ANUG/Noma in children are malnutrition, diarrheal diseases, measles infection, lack of proper sanitation, and poor living conditions.

Ethiopia is one of the countries in the Noma-belt region where people, particularly children, are profoundly affected by the disease. However, attention and resources dedicated to this devastating condition remains inadequate [24].
256.Rationale: While the passage identifies malnutrition and poor sanitation as major risk factors for Noma, it does not state that these are the only factors or that improving public health infrastructure alone would eliminate the disease. The passage also mentions other risk factors such as measles infection and immune defects. Additionally, the passage notes that attention and resources dedicated to Noma remain inadequate, suggesting that even with improved public health infrastructure, other interventions may be necessary. The option overstates the evidence by implying a definitive solution that is not supported by the passage.

256.Option: The presence of dental anarchy in 14 patients suggests that Noma survivors are at increased risk of developing oral cancer due to chronic inflammation and tissue damage.
Evidence: Out of the 109 medical records, 14 have reported different levels of disoriented teeth.
The problems in mastication and swallowing can also further exacerbate or cause malnutrition and as a result, many young patients experience significant stunting of growth [13].
256.Rationale: The passage mentions that 14 patients reported disoriented teeth (dental anarchy) but does not provide any evidence linking this to an increased risk of oral cancer. The passage discusses the impact of Noma on mastication and swallowing leading to malnutrition but does not mention chronic inflammation or tissue damage as risk factors for oral cancer. The option introduces a new hypothesis not supported by the data presented in the passage.

256.Option: The high prevalence of combined facial defects and severe functional limitations in Noma survivors, particularly trismus and dental anarchy, indicates that the disease leads to substantial long-term disability and social marginalization.
Evidence: The majority of the patients in this study have reported varying numbers of missing teeth.
The sequela of the disease reported in the medical records is classified into combined and non-combined. The combined sequela involved two or more anatomical region defects.
The problems in mastication and swallowing can also further exacerbate or cause malnutrition and as a result, many young patients experience significant stunting of growth [13].
In those with more advanced Noma, the lesions and contractures often lead to growth disturbance and result in further facial disfigurement and functional impairment [14].
However, these longer-term effects, including psychosocial aspects, are incompletely understood and an area for further study [23].
Often, those patients that survive are not only severely disfigured, but also rejected from family and society [22].
256.Rationale: The passage provides evidence of high prevalence of combined facial defects and severe functional limitations in Noma survivors, including trismus and dental anarchy. It also mentions that survivors are often rejected by family and society, indicating social marginalization. The passage further notes that the disease leads to long-term disability through functional impairments such as difficulty in mastication and swallowing, which can exacerbate malnutrition and stunting of growth. These findings directly support the inference that Noma leads to substantial long-term disability and social marginalization.

256.Option: The high prevalence of ANUG in children under 10 years in Ethiopia suggests that early intervention for ANUG could prevent the progression to Noma and reduce the overall disease burden.
Evidence: The condition primarily affects children under the age of 10 years [3].
In many cases, the disease begins to develop from acute necrotizing ulcerative gingivitis (ANUG) [2].
The major risk factors of ANUG/Noma in children are malnutrition, diarrheal diseases, measles infection, lack of proper sanitation, and poor living conditions.

The study was initiated to assess the sequela and severity of Noma in Ethiopia. The findings of this study can be used as a baseline for further investigation and help to fill the current knowledge gap.
256.Rationale: The passage states that Noma often begins as ANUG, which primarily affects children under 10 years in Ethiopia. It also lists the major risk factors for ANUG/Noma in children. The inference that early intervention for ANUG could prevent progression to Noma is supported by the evidence that ANUG is a precursor to Noma. The study's purpose to assess Noma's sequela and severity in Ethiopia further implies that understanding and addressing ANUG could help reduce the disease burden.

257.Option: The high level of non-synonymous polymorphisms in gp43 is due to sequencing errors and methodological artifacts, which artificially inflated the dN/dS ratio and led to false detection of positive selection.
Evidence: No differences were found between the linear and the quadratic models, neither for virulence factors (LRT = 2.134, p = 0.144), nor the housekeeping genes (LRT = 0.112, p = 0.7378), nor for the pooled data (LRT = 1.631; p = 0.2015) indicating that the lineal model is more appropriate to explain the relationship between dN and dS. Therefore, mutational saturation is not responsible for the elevated dN/dS ratios observed in the virulence factors.
Because no such pattern is observed in Figure 2, we consider this explanation unlikely.
257.Rationale: The passage explicitly rules out sequencing errors and methodological artifacts as explanations for the observed dN/dS ratios. The mutational saturation analysis showed no significant differences between linear and quadratic models, and the absence of a pattern in Figure 2 indicating sequencing errors further supports this conclusion. Therefore, the claim that sequencing errors caused the observed polymorphisms is not supported by the evidence in the passage.

257.Option: The gp43 gene is under strong purifying selection, as indicated by the low levels of genetic variation and the absence of significant positive selection in the M-K test.
Evidence: Using the one-ratio model (M0), the average value of ω for the gp43 gene was 1.168 - significantly higher than for any of the housekeeping genes [48].

Models of positive selection (discrete, selection, beta and ω models) that allow for sites with ω greater than 1 fit the gp43 data significantly better than the corresponding neutral models (one-ratio, neutral and beta models) (Table 5).
257.Rationale: The passage provides evidence that the gp43 gene has an ω value significantly greater than 1, which is indicative of positive selection rather than purifying selection. Additionally, the models of positive selection fit the data significantly better than neutral models. The option incorrectly claims the gene is under purifying selection, which contradicts the evidence presented.

257.Option: The long time to the most recent common ancestor (TMRCA) of gp43 alleles indicates that the gene has undergone recent positive selection, leading to rapid fixation of beneficial mutations in the P. brasiliensis lineage.
Evidence: The TMRCAs for S1 and PS2 were estimated based on genetic variation at the gp43 locus and seven other nuclear loci. The results showed that the TRMCA for the gp43 alleles is longer than for any other gene in P. brasiliensis (Table 6), indicating that the polymorphism in gp43 is significantly older than the polymorphism in the other genes (Signed rank test; P<0.01). This constitutes evidence for balancing selection [49],[50].
257.Rationale: The passage states that the long TMRCA for gp43 alleles is evidence for balancing selection, not recent positive selection. Balancing selection maintains genetic diversity over long periods, whereas recent positive selection would lead to a shorter TMRCA due to rapid fixation of beneficial mutations. The option misinterprets the TMRCA as evidence of recent positive selection, which is not supported by the passage.

257.Option: The gp43 gene has evolved under positive directional selection, as evidenced by the significant results of the M-K test and the fixation of advantageous amino acid replacements in P. brasiliensis.
Evidence: For the gp43 case, the M-K test yielded no significant results between H. capsulatum and P. brasiliensis (Fischer's exact test, P = 0.40, Table 3).
257.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that the M-K test for gp43 was not significant (P = 0.40), which contradicts the claim in the option that the M-K test provided evidence for positive directional selection. While other tests (e.g., codon-based likelihood analyses) did show evidence of positive selection, the M-K test did not, making this option incorrect.

257.Option: The persistence of non-synonymous intra- and trans-specific gp43 polymorphisms within and between lineages of the P. brasiliensis complex suggests they have been maintained by historical or contemporary selection.
Evidence: The TMRCAs for S1 and PS2 were estimated based on genetic variation at the gp43 locus and seven other nuclear loci. The results showed that the TRMCA for the gp43 alleles is longer than for any other gene in P. brasiliensis (Table 6), indicating that the polymorphism in gp43 is significantly older than the polymorphism in the other genes (Signed rank test; P<0.01). This constitutes evidence for balancing selection [49],[50].
Using the Bayesian Empirical Bayes (BEB) analysis, 19 sites with a posterior probability greater than 95% of having a greater than 1 value were identified.

The results showed that the TRMCA for the gp43 alleles is longer than for any other gene in P. brasiliensis (Table 6), indicating that the polymorphism in gp43 is significantly older than the polymorphism in the other genes (Signed rank test; P<0.01). This constitutes evidence for balancing selection [49],[50].
257.Rationale: The passage provides evidence that the gp43 gene has a long TMRCA and a high number of sites under positive selection, which together suggest that the polymorphisms have been maintained by balancing selection over time. Balancing selection is a form of historical or contemporary selection that maintains genetic diversity. The persistence of these polymorphisms within and between lineages is consistent with the evidence presented, making this option correct.

258.Option: Heterotypic neutralizing antibodies, particularly against DENV-4, were more strongly associated with protection against DENV-2 infection than homotypic antibodies, suggesting that cross-protection may be more effective than serotype-specific immunity in some cases.
Evidence: For DENV-2 clusters, age-adjusted models were better using both homotypic DENV-2 reference NTs (AUC = 0.740 [95% CI 0.460, 0.960]) and heterotypic DENV-4 reference NTs (AUC = 0.880 [95% CI 0.680, 1.000]) than other heterotypic NTs (Table 3).

When different DENV-2 strains were used for PRNT, the AUC using Asian I 1974 strain appeared to have the best fit. The AUC using DENV-2 homologous virus was comparable to that using DENV-2 reference strain.
258.Rationale: The passage provides direct evidence that for DENV-2 clusters, heterotypic DENV-4 NTs showed a stronger association with protection (AUC 0.880) than homotypic DENV-2 NTs (AUC 0.740). This supports the inference that cross-protection from heterotypic antibodies can be more effective than homotypic immunity in some cases. The specific mention of DENV-4 heterotypic antibodies in relation to DENV-2 protection further supports this conclusion.

258.Option: Pre-existing homotypic neutralizing antibody titers measured by PRNT were positively associated with protection against infection by DENV-1, -2, and -4, with the level of protection varying by serotype.
Evidence: When all serotypes were combined, PCR status was significantly associated with the log of homotypic NT both alone and adjusted for age (Table 2).
For DENV-1 clusters, age-adjusted models to predict PCR status were better (based on AIC and AUC) when using homotypic DENV-1 NTs (AUC = 0.833 [95% CI 0.590, 1.000]) than heterotypic NTs (Table 3).
For DENV-4 clusters, age-adjusted models were better using homotypic DENV-4 NTs (AUC = 0.889 [95% CI 0.667, 1.000]) than heterotypic NTs (Table 3).

For DENV-2 clusters, age-adjusted models were better using both homotypic DENV-2 reference NTs (AUC = 0.740 [95% CI 0.460, 0.960]) and heterotypic DENV-4 reference NTs (AUC = 0.880 [95% CI 0.680, 1.000]) than other heterotypic NTs (Table 3).
258.Rationale: The passage provides clear evidence that homotypic NTs were positively associated with protection against DENV-1, -2, and -4. The AUC values for homotypic NTs in each serotype cluster (0.833 for DENV-1, 0.889 for DENV-4, 0.740 for DENV-2) demonstrate this association. The variation in AUC values across serotypes directly supports the claim that the level of protection varies by serotype.

258.Option: The protective effect of neutralizing antibodies is consistent across all dengue serotypes, with similar titers required for protection regardless of the infecting serotype.
Evidence: Homotypic NT cutoff values were 11, 11, 16 and 323 for all serotypes combined, DENV-1, DENV-4, and DENV-2, respectively.

For DENV-1 clusters, the observed odds ratio if homotypic NT was ≥11 was 0 (95% CI 0.000, 1.554). For DENV-4 clusters, the observed odds ratio if homotypic NT was ≥16 was 0 (95% CI 0.000, 0.842). For DENV-2, the odds ratio if homotypic NT was ≥323 was 0 (95% CI 0.000, 1.279).
258.Rationale: The passage explicitly shows that the protective titer thresholds vary significantly across serotypes (11 for DENV-1, 16 for DENV-4, and 323 for DENV-2). This directly contradicts the claim that the protective effect is consistent across all serotypes with similar titers required for protection. The variation in cutoff values and odds ratios clearly demonstrates that the protective effect is not consistent across serotypes.

258.Option: The lack of clinical efficacy against DENV-2 in the recent dengue vaccine trial in Thailand may have partly been due to insufficiently high DENV-2 NTs, suggesting that vaccine-induced antibody levels should be optimized to match natural infection thresholds.
Evidence: Recently, the first human efficacy trial of a dengue vaccine candidate was completed in Thailand showing good neutralizing antibody response to all four DENV serotypes after vaccination, but no clinical efficacy against DENV-2 infection [4].

This was done in order to evaluate possible differences in PRNT titers against different DENV-2 strains given the lack of vaccine efficacy against DENV-2 reported in a recent dengue vaccine trial [4].
Homotypic NT cutoff values were 11, 11, 16 and 323 for all serotypes combined, DENV-1, DENV-4, and DENV-2, respectively.
258.Rationale: The passage provides evidence that the dengue vaccine trial in Thailand showed good neutralizing antibody response but no clinical efficacy against DENV-2. The study's specific focus on DENV-2 strains and PRNT titers in relation to vaccine efficacy, combined with the high cutoff value for DENV-2 (323), supports the inference that insufficient DENV-2 NTs in the vaccine recipients may have contributed to the lack of clinical efficacy. This suggests that vaccine-induced antibody levels should be optimized to match the higher natural infection thresholds observed for DENV-2.

258.Option: The study design using geographic clusters with matched exposed but uninfected individuals provides definitive evidence that neutralizing antibodies are the primary mechanism of protection against dengue virus infection.
Evidence: The study design using geographic cluster studies in which high DENV transmission activity has been demonstrated [18].
We showed an association between homotypic NTs and the likelihood of subsequent infection with DENV-1, -2 and -4.

The study design using geographic clusters with matched exposed but uninfected individuals provides definitive evidence that neutralizing antibodies are the primary mechanism of protection against dengue virus infection.
258.Rationale: While the study design demonstrates an association between neutralizing antibody levels and infection likelihood, it does not provide definitive evidence that neutralizing antibodies are the primary mechanism of protection. The passage explicitly states that the study showed an association, not causation. The study cannot rule out other potential mechanisms of protection that may be correlated with neutralizing antibody levels. The conclusion that this provides 'definitive evidence' of neutralizing antibodies being the primary mechanism is therefore not supported by the passage.

259.Option: Sub-microscopic P. vivax infections are primarily detected in symptomatic patients due to the higher parasite density associated with clinical illness, as shown by the higher PCR prevalence in fever patient surveys.
Evidence: The proportion of individuals with symptoms ranged from 0.0% to 11.4%, averaging 2.8%. This means that between 88.6 and 100.0% (average 97.1%) of individuals with SM P. vivax were asymptomatic.

To ascertain whether these asymptomatic SM carriers will become symptomatic some time later, one study followed 25 LM negative/PCR positive subjects in South America for two months and found they remained asymptomatic over the 2 month duration.
259.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that the vast majority of sub-microscopic P. vivax infections are asymptomatic, with an average of 97.1% of SM P. vivax cases being asymptomatic. The option incorrectly claims that SM P. vivax is primarily detected in symptomatic patients, which contradicts the evidence presented. Additionally, the passage notes that PCR prevalence in fever patient surveys was not significantly higher than LM prevalence, further refuting the claim about higher PCR prevalence in symptomatic patients.

259.Option: The high proportion of asymptomatic sub-microscopic P. vivax infections in the community is primarily due to recent antimalarial drug use, which rapidly reduces parasite density and prevents symptom onset.
Evidence: Only one survey reported that recent antimalarial treatment (<4 weeks prior to survey) was associated with a significant increase in risk of having SM P. vivax infection.
259.Rationale: The passage states that only one survey found an association between recent antimalarial treatment and increased risk of SM P. vivax infection. This is not sufficient evidence to conclude that antimalarial drug use is the primary cause of the high proportion of asymptomatic SM P. vivax infections. The option overstates the role of antimalarial drugs as the primary factor, which is not supported by the data presented.

259.Option: The use of whole blood for DNA extraction in PCR assays significantly increases the risk of false-positive results in detecting sub-microscopic P. vivax infections, which undermines the reliability of PCR-based surveillance.
Evidence: The average prevalence of SM P. vivax in community surveys determined by PCR using whole blood was significantly higher than that using blood from filter papers (Mann Whitney test, P = 0.004).
259.Rationale: The passage indicates that whole blood extraction methods detected a significantly higher prevalence of SM P. vivax compared to filter paper methods. This suggests that whole blood increases sensitivity rather than causing false positives. The option incorrectly interprets this finding as evidence of false positives undermining reliability, when the data actually support the increased detection capability of whole blood methods.

259.Option: The prevalence of sub-microscopic P. vivax infections is significantly higher in areas with high transmission intensity compared to low transmission intensity, as indicated by the higher number of PCR-positive cases in these regions.
Evidence: The negative relationship between LM P. vivax prevalence and proportion of SM P. vivax infections was identified (Fig. 7), with LM P. vivax prevalence identified as a significant factor for predicting the proportion of samples that are PCR positive/LM negative (SM) in cross-sectional surveys where the LM P. vivax prevalence is less than 45%.
The regression equation describing this relationship is: proportion SM P. vivax  =  exp (−0.162–4.163× LM P. vivax prevalence).
259.Rationale: The passage presents a negative relationship between LM prevalence and the proportion of SM P. vivax infections. This means that as transmission intensity (as measured by LM prevalence) increases, the relative proportion of SM infections decreases. The option incorrectly claims the opposite relationship, asserting that SM prevalence is higher in high transmission areas, which contradicts the statistical evidence provided.

259.Option: The relative proportion of sub-microscopic P. vivax infections is significantly higher than that of the sympatric P. falciparum in the same study settings, and this proportion increases in areas with lower transmission intensity.
Evidence: The average relative proportion of SM was significantly higher in P. vivax infections compared to P. falciparum in the same study (Wilcoxon matched-pairs signed rank test, Median difference  = 5.2%, P = 0.045, Fig. 5B).

The negative relationship between LM P. vivax prevalence and proportion of SM P. vivax infections was identified (Fig. 7), with LM P. vivax prevalence identified as a significant factor for predicting the proportion of samples that are PCR positive/LM negative (SM) in cross-sectional surveys where the LM P. vivax prevalence is less than 45%.
259.Rationale: The passage provides direct evidence that the relative proportion of SM P. vivax is significantly higher than SM P. falciparum in the same study settings (5.2% median difference, p=0.045). Additionally, the negative relationship between LM prevalence and SM proportion indicates that as transmission intensity decreases (lower LM prevalence), the proportion of SM P. vivax increases. This matches the option's claim about the proportion increasing in areas with lower transmission intensity. The evidence directly supports both components of the option.

260.Option: The high level of awareness about NTDs among medical and nursing students in Egypt is capable of achieving significant and long-lasting results in controlling these diseases.
Evidence: Only one fourth (26%) of the students knew the meaning of NTDs
Low percentage of the studied medical and nursing students (ranged from 4.3 to 23.9%) correctly identified the names of individual NTDs
Very low percentage of the students (5%) knew specific activities for NTDs prevention and control in Egypt
260.Rationale: The passage clearly states that student knowledge about NTDs is limited, with only 26% knowing the meaning of NTDs and very low percentages identifying specific diseases or prevention activities. The claim of a 'high level of awareness' is directly contradicted by the data presented. The passage does not provide evidence that current student knowledge levels are sufficient to achieve significant disease control.

260.Option: The lack of explicit mention of the term 'neglected tropical diseases' (NTDs) in the epidemiology course materials at Cairo University is associated with a significant knowledge gap among medical and nursing students regarding NTDs and their control activities.
Evidence: Neither the term 'neglected tropical disease' nor the abbreviation 'NTDs' was mentioned explicitly in the course materials or specifications.
Only one fourth (26%) of the students knew the meaning of NTDs
Low percentage of the studied medical and nursing students (ranged from 4.3 to 23.9%) correctly identified the names of individual NTDs
Very low percentage of the students (5%) knew specific activities for NTDs prevention and control in Egypt
260.Rationale: The passage establishes a direct correlation between the absence of the term 'NTDs' in course materials and the observed knowledge gaps. The data shows that only 26% of students knew the meaning of NTDs, and very few could identify specific diseases or prevention activities. This supports the inference that the lack of explicit curriculum content is associated with significant knowledge gaps among students.

260.Option: The lack of NTDs in the curriculum is not a significant factor in the low knowledge levels among students, as most students learned about NTDs through social media and mass media.
Evidence: Only one fourth (26%) of the students knew the meaning of NTDs
The epidemiology course was mentioned as a single source of knowledge by 8 students (4.3%), while it was mentioned with other sources by 6 students (3.3%)
Very low percentage of the students (5%) knew specific activities for NTDs prevention and control in Egypt
260.Rationale: The passage shows that only 4.3% of students cited the epidemiology course as a single source of knowledge about NTDs, and even when combined with other sources, this accounts for only 3.3% of students. The low knowledge levels (26% understanding the term, 5% knowing prevention activities) contradict the claim that alternative sources like social media and mass media are sufficient to offset the lack of curriculum content. The data supports that the absence from the curriculum is a significant factor in the low knowledge levels.

260.Option: The inclusion of NTDs in the epidemiology course curriculum would likely lead to a significant increase in student willingness to participate in NTDs control activities.
Evidence: More than one third of them (34%) expressed their willingness to participate in these control activities
No statistically significant difference (P value = 0.061) was found between medical and nursing students regarding their willingness to participate in the control activities for NTDs
260.Rationale: The passage shows that 34% of students already expressed willingness to participate in NTD control activities, and there is no evidence presented that this willingness is related to knowledge levels. The study does not establish a causal relationship between curriculum content and willingness to participate. The claim that curriculum inclusion would 'likely lead to a significant increase' in willingness is not supported by the data presented.

260.Option: The poor public awareness of NTDs in Egypt is primarily due to insufficient media coverage of control programs, despite adequate knowledge among healthcare students.
Evidence: Only one fourth (26%) of the students knew the meaning of NTDs
Very low percentage of the students (5%) knew specific activities for NTDs prevention and control in Egypt
Nearly half of the students agreed on the poor level of awareness regarding NTDs in Egypt
260.Rationale: The passage does not provide evidence about media coverage of NTD control programs. The data shows that healthcare students themselves have limited knowledge about NTDs, contradicting the claim of 'adequate knowledge among healthcare students.' The study does not establish a causal relationship between media coverage and public awareness, making this inference unsupported by the evidence presented.

261.Option: The method is capable of detecting the effect of benznidazole on T. cruzi epimastigotes at concentrations as low as 0.0195 μg/mL, indicating high sensitivity to drug action.
Evidence: The effect of varying amounts of benznidazole on the activity of the parasites was tested and compared to the pattern obtained from the LIT medium with benznidazole and without parasites.

Figs 12, 13, 14, 15 and 16 show the curves obtained for the activity of the Biospeckle pattern of 4x104 parasites per well, at times of 1, 61, 128 and 204 minutes with six concentrations of benznidazol ranging from 0.0195 to 20μg/mL, and as a comparison, the curves obtained with benznidazole without parasites.

The activity of the Biospeckle pattern as a function of benznidazole concentration shows curves that fit similar Polynomial regressions at all the tested times (Fig 13) with a decrease of the activity as a function of concentration and a clustering of the values (mean of the clustered low concentration values of 48.39±2.91, see S1 Data for details) of the four time curves up to a concentration of benznidazole of 1.25μg/mL.

The results of Figs 12, 13, 14, 15 and 16 suggest that benznidazole produces an instantaneous effect at 1min on T. cruzi that occurs at all the tested concentrations, with a profile that remains almost the same up to at least 61min, as shown by the polynomial tendencies (Fig 13) and the resulting profile of Fig 15.
261.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that the method tested varying concentrations of benznidazole from 0.0195 to 20μg/mL and detected effects at the lowest concentration of 0.0195μg/mL. The results show that the Biospeckle pattern activity decreased with increasing drug concentration, and the method detected an instantaneous effect at 1 minute for all tested concentrations including the lowest one. This directly supports the inference that the method is sensitive to drug action at concentrations as low as 0.0195μg/mL.

261.Option: The Biospeckle pattern is sensitive to changes in parasite motility and aggregation, which may be influenced by drug-induced taxis, suggesting that the method detects early behavioral responses to benznidazole.
Evidence: The activity of the Biospeckle pattern is proportional to the quantity of parasites present in the assay well and remains stable despite desiccation up to around 200 minutes.

The results of Figs 12, 13, 14, 15 and 16 suggest that benznidazole produces an instantaneous effect at 1min on T. cruzi that occurs at all the tested concentrations, with a profile that remains almost the same up to at least 61min, as shown by the polynomial tendencies (Fig 13) and the resulting profile of Fig 15.
261.Rationale: While the passage mentions an instantaneous effect of benznidazole at 1 minute, it does not provide any evidence about the mechanism of this effect (e.g., changes in motility or aggregation). The term 'taxis' (directed movement) is not mentioned or implied in the passage. The inference about detecting early behavioral responses requires assumptions about the biological mechanism that are not supported by the evidence in the passage body. The passage only describes the detection of drug effects, not the specific biological processes being measured.

261.Option: The activity of the Biospeckle pattern is proportional to the quantity of parasites present in the assay well and remains stable despite desiccation up to around 200 minutes.
Evidence: The effect of parasite quantity on the activity Biospeckle pattern was evaluated by conducting two experiments (constant density and variable density) and analyzing in each case, the same video with the three approaches for handling of image data.

In the experiment of constant density 5 different test wells had 4x104 parasites/mL but the volume in each well varied from 13.3 to 133μL and the parasite number varied from 0.532 to 5.32x104 parasites per well. As is shown in Fig 2, the three approaches are able to detect an increase in the activity of the Biospeckle pattern as a function of parasite quantity. Moreover, the plots are nearly linear using the three approaches.

The desiccation of the liquid in the air exposed VDRL wells and its implications on the activity of the Biospeckle pattern was evaluated in several ways. Fig 11 shows the desiccation in terms of remaining volume, after 0, 55, 120 and 270 minutes have elapsed. In the same Figure two assays (Assay 1 and Assay 2) show the activity of the Biospeckle pattern in separate wells with and without parasites and processed under R/SAGA and Eq (3). The results indicate that although there is a marked desiccation effect, the Biospeckle pattern remains stable up to around 200 minutes, indicating that as was seen in Figs 2, 3 and 4 (Effect of Parasite Quantity), the Biospeckle pattern under the conditions of this work, reveals the total number of parasites within the well of the VDRL plate.
261.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that the Biospeckle pattern activity is proportional to parasite quantity in both constant and variable density experiments, as shown in the linear relationships in Figures 2 and 3. It also directly states that the Biospeckle pattern remains stable despite desiccation up to around 200 minutes, as shown in Figure 11. This directly supports the inference about proportionality to parasite quantity and stability during desiccation.

261.Option: The Biospeckle pattern remains stable over time because the VDRL plate's flat-bottomed design minimizes optical distortion and prevents evaporation-induced signal drift.
Evidence: The VDRL plate was recommended as a specific and reproducible system by the World Health Organization in 1980 [11]. The design and dimensions of the VDRL flat test wells are adapted to optical microscopes and magnifiers and they are available in most health centers worldwide.

The desiccation of the liquid in the air exposed VDRL wells and its implications on the activity of the Biospeckle pattern was evaluated in several ways. Fig 11 shows the desiccation in terms of remaining volume, after 0, 55, 120 and 270 minutes have elapsed. In the same Figure two assays (Assay 1 and Assay 2) show the activity of the Biospeckle pattern in separate wells with and without parasites and processed under R/SAGA and Eq (3). The results indicate that although there is a marked desiccation effect, the Biospeckle pattern remains stable up to around 200 minutes, indicating that as was seen in Figs 2, 3 and 4 (Effect of Parasite Quantity), the Biospeckle pattern under the conditions of this work, reveals the total number of parasites within the well of the VDRL plate.
261.Rationale: The passage states that the VDRL plate is recommended for its design and availability, but does not claim that its flat-bottomed design prevents evaporation-induced signal drift. In fact, the passage explicitly acknowledges desiccation occurs in the VDRL wells and evaluates its effects. The claim that the design 'prevents evaporation-induced signal drift' is contradicted by the evidence that desiccation does occur and is measured in the experiments. The stability of the Biospeckle pattern is attributed to the method's ability to detect total parasite quantity, not to the plate's design preventing evaporation.

262.Option: Tungiasis is an important health problem in Vihiga County occasioned by the low economic status of the people affected, and factors that point to poverty contribute to the occurrence of tungiasis in Vihiga County.
Evidence: The poverty levels are high (41%) and the GDP per capita income was reported as US $ 1,103 in 2013 [21].

In the multivariate analysis, the occurrence of tungiasis was significantly associated with variables that indicated low economic status (like monthly income <1000 adjusted OR 27.85 (4.13–187.59) and age (≤14 years) of participant adjusted OR 27.414 (10.02–74.99) (Table 3).

Tungiasis also referred to as jigger infestation is a neglected tropical disease due to the fact that it typically occurs in populations living in poverty, with poor housing, inadequate sanitation and in close contact with domestic animals akin to other neglected diseases.
262.Rationale: The passage provides multiple lines of evidence linking low economic status to the occurrence of tungiasis. It explicitly states that the study area has high poverty levels and low GDP per capita. The multivariate analysis shows a strong association between low income and tungiasis. Additionally, the introduction frames tungiasis as a disease of poverty. This supports the inference that poverty-related factors are key drivers of the disease in this population.

262.Option: The high prevalence of tungiasis among children aged 5–14 years is primarily due to their frequent exposure to infected domestic animals, especially rats, in shared household spaces.
Evidence: In the bivariate analysis, the age group ≤14, ... having rats in the compound ... were shown to be significantly associated with tungiasis (Table 2).
Factors that have been reported to aggravate the problem include ... occurrence of animals like dogs, cats, rats, cows, goats, chicken in the compound [12, 13] and low education level [14].
262.Rationale: While the passage does show an association between children aged ≤14 and tungiasis, and also notes an association with having rats in the compound, it does not establish a causal relationship between these factors. The passage does not provide evidence that the high prevalence among children is 'primarily due to' exposure to infected domestic animals. The option makes a causal claim that cannot be directly inferred from the evidence presented.

262.Option: The lack of knowledge about tungiasis transmission among 45% of participants suggests that community education campaigns should be prioritized to eliminate stigma and improve treatment adherence.
Evidence: When it came to knowledge and practice associated with tungiasis, 45% of the participants interviewed had no knowledge on its transmission.
262.Rationale: The passage states that 45% of participants had no knowledge about tungiasis transmission, but it does not mention stigma or treatment adherence issues. The option introduces new concepts (stigma and treatment adherence) that are not discussed in the passage. While community education might be a reasonable intervention, the specific claim about eliminating stigma and improving treatment adherence cannot be directly inferred from the evidence provided.

262.Option: The absence of spatial autocorrelation in tungiasis cases suggests that environmental factors such as soil type and elevation are the primary drivers of disease transmission in the study area.
Evidence: Visual investigation of the distribution of cases did not reveal any obvious spatial pattern in terms of the changes in elevation. There is similar distribution in both the low and high altitudes (Fig 2).
The semivariogram revealed no spatial autocorrelation which could indicate that tungiasis prevalence in the study area might be random.
The soil type in the three sub locations did not vary, it was mainly observed to be sandy clay (Fig 3).
262.Rationale: The passage states that there was no spatial autocorrelation and that soil type was consistent across the study area. However, this does not support the conclusion that environmental factors are the primary drivers. In fact, the lack of spatial pattern and uniform soil type suggest that environmental factors may not be the main drivers. The option misinterprets the evidence presented in the passage.

262.Option: The use of GIS and spatial interpolation techniques such as inverse distance weighting (IDW) allows for accurate prediction of tungiasis hotspots in small-scale endemic areas, enabling targeted public health interventions.
Evidence: GIS analysis was carried out using Arc GIS 10.5 from Esri. ... The tungiasis case concentration map was produced by interpolating the number of cases recorded per site using the inverse distance weighting (IDW) algorithm tool found in ArcGIS Spatial Analyst tool...
The semivariogram revealed no spatial autocorrelation which could indicate that tungiasis prevalence in the study area might be random.
262.Rationale: While the passage describes the use of GIS and IDW techniques, it also states that the semivariogram revealed no spatial autocorrelation, suggesting that the distribution might be random. This undermines the claim that these techniques allow for 'accurate prediction of tungiasis hotspots.' The evidence does not support the conclusion that these methods enable targeted interventions in this specific context.

263.Option: The increasing dengue incidence from 1999 to 2008, coupled with a stable median age of infection, suggests that the force of infection has declined, leading to fewer infections in children and a higher proportion of symptomatic cases in adults.
Evidence: In the quasi-Poisson model there was no statistically significant yearly increase in age adjusted incidence when all years were included (table 1). Since dengue characteristically produces large epidemics interspersed by many years, these unusually large epidemic years may mask underlying trends in the data. Therefore we also ran the quasi-Poisson model with the two epidemic years of 1998 and 2009 excluded. In this analysis of years 1999-2008, the age adjusted dengue incidence increased 1.38 fold annually (table 1).

The unadjusted median and mean age of notified cases were 23 and 26.3 years respectively, and 53% of cases were male. The majority of notified cases (85%) occurred in individuals aged over 15 years (figure 2A). Although the average age of notified dengue cases increased over the study period, from a median (mean) age of 20 (22.4) years in 1998 to 24 (26.8) years in 2009, this was not statistically significant after adjusting for changes in the population age structure (figure 2B).
263.Rationale: The passage states that age-adjusted dengue incidence increased 1.38 fold annually from 1999 to 2008. However, the median age of infection did not decrease but rather increased slightly, though not statistically significant after adjusting for population age structure. The option incorrectly claims the force of infection has declined, which contradicts the observed increase in incidence. Additionally, the option's claim about fewer infections in children is not supported by the data, as the passage does not provide evidence of declining infections in children.

263.Option: Despite a significant increase in age-adjusted dengue incidence in Hanoi from 1999 to 2008, the median age of notified cases did not decrease, suggesting that the force of infection may not have increased and that clinical case reporting patterns may be influenced by factors such as adult susceptibility to symptomatic disease.
Evidence: In the quasi-Poisson model there was no statistically significant yearly increase in age adjusted incidence when all years were included (table 1). Since dengue characteristically produces large epidemics interspersed by many years, these unusually large epidemic years may mask underlying trends in the data. Therefore we also ran the quasi-Poisson model with the two epidemic years of 1998 and 2009 excluded. In this analysis of years 1999-2008, the age adjusted dengue incidence increased 1.38 fold annually (table 1).

The unadjusted median and mean age of notified cases were 23 and 26.3 years respectively, and 53% of cases were male. The majority of notified cases (85%) occurred in individuals aged over 15 years (figure 2A). Although the average age of notified dengue cases increased over the study period, from a median (mean) age of 20 (22.4) years in 1998 to 24 (26.8) years in 2009, this was not statistically significant after adjusting for changes in the population age structure (figure 2B).
263.Rationale: The passage confirms a significant increase in age-adjusted dengue incidence from 1999 to 2008. However, the median age of notified cases did not decrease, which is consistent with the idea that the force of infection may not have increased. The option correctly suggests that clinical case reporting patterns may be influenced by factors such as adult susceptibility to symptomatic disease, which is supported by the high proportion of cases in adults (85% over 15 years). This aligns with the epidemiological concept that older individuals may be more likely to present with symptomatic disease due to lack of prior immunity.

263.Option: The increase in dengue incidence from 1999 to 2008 is likely due to improved case detection and reporting, as evidenced by the rising proportion of IgM-positive cases over time.
