For example, Fig 1 shows that if 37% of heavy-intensity hookworm infections become light-intensity infections after the first year of preventive chemotherapy, the same proportion (37%) of the remaining heavy-intensity infections will become light-intensity infections after the second year of the intervention.

The mean discrepancy for any of the three STH parasites between the observed and the predicted prevalence was 3.16 percentage points (SD, 4.162) for OM, 4.8 percentage points (SD, 6.075) for SM1 and 8.4 percentage points (SD, 12.484) for SM2; these differences are marginally statistically different (p = 0.0301).

The accuracy of the models (proportion of predictions within 10 percentage points) for any of the STH parasites was 94% for OM, 84% for SM1 and 72% for SM2. In general the models preformed best for T. trichiura (43% of predictions within 1 percentage point) and least well for hookworm (only 26% of predictions within 1 percentage point).
290.Rationale: The passage states that the Markov model assumes stationarity of the transition probabilities (TP) if the intervention remains the same. This means that the TPMS are assumed to remain constant throughout the duration of the control programme. The reduced accuracy for hookworms is not attributed to a loss of stationarity over time, but rather to other factors such as the poor sensitivity of the Kato-Katz method. The passage does not provide evidence that the transition probabilities for hookworms were not updated annually, leading to a loss of stationarity over time.

291.Option: The use of visual analogue scores in the LeishCOM_LCL tool ensures that both clinician and patient perspectives are captured in a reliable and repeatable manner, which enhances the tool's validity for assessing treatment response in LCL.
Evidence: To measure the impact of the treatment on the “skin problem” at the time of assessment a Visual Analogue Score (VAS) ranging from 0–10 (0 = “completely clear skin”, 10 = “severely affected skin”) was described for both investigator (VASi) and patient (VASp) (CRF Section 6.3).
Each line was 10 cm long. Each score is to be allocated according to the nearest whole cm.
The Treatment Impact domain was recorded on each day of assessment by capturing the perception by both the investigator and the patient on a visual analogue score.
291.Rationale: The passage explicitly describes the use of Visual Analogue Scores (VAS) to capture both clinician (VASi) and patient (VASp) perspectives on treatment impact. The VAS is described as a 10 cm line with scores allocated based on the nearest whole cm, indicating a standardized and repeatable method. This dual-perspective approach directly supports the claim that the tool captures both clinician and patient perspectives in a reliable manner, enhancing its validity for assessing treatment response.

291.Option: The development of the LeishCOM_LCL tool represents the first standardized and validated approach to assess core outcome domains in localized cutaneous leishmaniasis, enabling consistent evaluation of treatment response across clinical trials and routine care.
Evidence: The overall aim of this work was to adopt a standardized and validated approach to assess agreed clinical aspects of LCL and response to treatment in a measurable manner for use in clinical trials and routine patient care which were then reflected in a practical tool.

The development of a validated scoring system for LCL based on harmonized methodologies would allow assessment of treatment response in routine clinical settings and enable comparison of the efficacy of existing or new drugs as well as novel alternative interventions and would subsequently support meta-analysis.

Once developed, the LeishCOM_LCL was incorporated into a case report form (CRF) and the process of face and content validation was undertaken. Further comprehensive validation of the final outcome measure instrument has been conducted through a clinical study.
291.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that the LeishCOM_LCL was developed as a standardized and validated tool for assessing LCL in both clinical trials and routine care. It emphasizes the harmonized methodologies and the validation process, including face and content validation, as well as a clinical study. This directly supports the claim that it is the first standardized and validated approach for LCL, enabling consistent evaluation across settings.

291.Option: The LeishCOM_LCL tool was developed through a multi-stakeholder consensus process that included representation from all major LCL-endemic regions, ensuring global applicability and cultural relevance.
Evidence: A multi-institutional, multi-disciplinary stakeholder panel of international experts from Sri Lanka, India, Brazil, and the United Kingdom comprising of dermatologists, general physicians and parasitologists were included along with a moderator (n = 20).
291.Rationale: While the passage mentions stakeholder representation from Sri Lanka, India, Brazil, and the UK, it does not explicitly state that these countries represent 'all major LCL-endemic regions.' The claim about 'global applicability and cultural relevance' is not directly supported by the evidence provided, as the passage does not address cultural relevance or global applicability in detail. The option makes a broader claim than what is substantiated by the passage.

291.Option: The inclusion of open-ended questions on HRQoL in the LeishCOM_LCL tool allows for the systematic capture of patient-reported outcomes, which is essential for developing a patient-centered approach to LCL management.
Evidence: The impact of the HRQoL had not been previously considered in LCL and therefore two open-ended questions were included as a preliminary step to try and capture the most important aspects for the patient with a view to informing a novel tool at a later date.
The CRF also includes two open-ended questions aimed at capturing some aspects of Health-related quality of life (HRQoL).

Information on the two open-ended questions was gathered from patients and thematic analysis was carried out during the validation process in the downstream application of the CRF in a dermatology clinic in Sri Lanka.
291.Rationale: The passage explicitly describes the inclusion of two open-ended questions in the CRF to capture HRQoL aspects, which were used to gather patient-reported outcomes during validation. This systematic capture of patient perspectives supports the development of a patient-centered approach, as the tool aims to inform future HRQoL assessments based on patient input.

291.Option: The LeishCOM_LCL tool enables the assessment of both clinical efficacy and long-term sequelae, which allows for the evaluation of treatment impact on patient quality of life and psychological well-being.
Evidence: The Core Outcome Domains identified during the development and judgment process are given in Table 1.
Clinical sequelae.Pigmentary change (Hypo/hyper) and scarring (atrophic or hypertrophic) were recognized as the parameters to capture for the clinical sequelae assessment domain.

The psychological impact improved in line with treatment response over a 6 month period from baseline, however, 30% of patients expressed psychological concerns as a result of sequelae such as pigment changes and scarring (See S2 Table for Baseline raw data).
291.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that the LeishCOM_LCL includes domains for assessing clinical efficacy (e.g., re-epithelialization, induration) and long-term sequelae (e.g., scarring, pigmentation). It also notes that psychological concerns related to sequelae were captured during validation, indicating that the tool evaluates treatment impact on quality of life and psychological well-being. This directly supports the option's claim.

292.Option: The national hotline 117 was widely used and trusted by Sierra Leoneans during the 2014–2015 outbreak, as evidenced by the high number of calls received and the rapid response times.
Evidence: Only three participants had ever called 117. One person reported that he called 117 for his neighbor and the neighbor became very angry with him. Another person reported calling six times, not receiving a response, and then seeking other health care resources for the suspected case.
292.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that only three participants had ever called 117, and provides examples of negative experiences with the hotline. This directly contradicts the claim that the hotline was 'widely used and trusted.' The evidence shows low usage and reports of non-response and negative outcomes, not high usage or trust.

292.Option: The use of chlorine spray by ambulance workers was widely perceived as a necessary and effective public health measure, with most participants expressing confidence in its safety and efficacy.
Evidence: About one-third of participants expressed the belief that chlorine was harmful to people.

Chlorine suffocates patients. If a patient has been vomiting and toileting then in the end he or she is suffocated with chlorine, because when they spray the patient in the vehicle they usually close the door where the patient will be placed and no air enters the cabin.
Two respondents even believed it might be the chlorine spray that was killing people and not Ebola.
292.Rationale: The passage provides clear evidence that a significant portion of participants (about one-third) believed chlorine was harmful, with specific quotes describing how it 'suffocates patients' and even that it 'might be the chlorine spray that was killing people.' This directly contradicts the claim that most participants expressed confidence in its safety and efficacy.

292.Option: The high Ebola-related fatality rate in 2014 in Sierra Leone, combined with the delayed reporting of test results, likely led to widespread public skepticism about the accuracy and reliability of the Ebola laboratory test.
Evidence: As of January 2016, Ebola resulted in 3,956 cumulative confirmed deaths in Sierra Leone.
Returning results on an Ebola test took a minimum of 48 hours.

Many respondents expressed concern or even disbelief that the testing process was valid: 'I hear they take the blood specimen of people but even there it is not clear to me. They can take the blood of somebody under the pretext that he/she has Ebola but at the end of the day you will hear that that person has been discharged.'
292.Rationale: The passage provides evidence of both high fatality rates and delayed test results (minimum 48 hours). It also includes direct quotes from participants expressing skepticism about the testing process, including concerns about its validity and accuracy. The combination of these factors supports the inference that public skepticism about the test's accuracy and reliability was widespread.

292.Option: Participants’ reluctance to call 117 was primarily due to financial barriers and lack of access to transportation, rather than fear or mistrust of the response system.
Evidence: With 117, the ambulance takes people away and sometimes they never come back … I think this is what frightens people.

Participants were more willing to call 117 if they observed symptoms in another person. However, about one-third of participants stated they were afraid of community members’ anger if they were to call.
One participant said that he could not afford to go to a health center and so he would pray.
292.Rationale: While the passage does mention one participant who could not afford to go to a health center, the primary reasons cited for reluctance to call 117 were fear (e.g., 'they never come back') and mistrust (e.g., fear of community anger). The evidence shows that financial barriers were not the primary reason for reluctance, contradicting the claim in this option.

292.Option: Despite high reported intentions to use the Ebola response system, participants in this study expressed significant apprehension about calling the national hotline and using the response system, which may have contributed to low actual usage rates during the outbreak.
Evidence: In December 2014, 95% agreed that a person had a higher chance of survival if s/he immediately went to a health facility.
In response to the question 'what would you do if you had a fever?' very few participants said they would call 117 right away.
Only three participants had ever called 117.

Participants were more willing to call 117 if they observed symptoms in another person. However, about one-third of participants stated they were afraid of community members’ anger if they were to call.
292.Rationale: The passage provides evidence of high reported intentions (95% believed going to a health facility increased survival chances) but low actual usage (only three participants had ever called 117). It also includes direct quotes about apprehension (e.g., fear of community anger) that may have contributed to this discrepancy between intentions and actual behavior.

293.Option: Because ZIKV immunology is still in its infancy, we hypothesize that, similar to other flaviviruses (e.g., Yellow fever virus (YFV), Japanese encephalitis virus (JEV), and Tick-borne encephalitis virus (TBEV)), nAbs are a critical component of the protective immune response.
Evidence: Serum neutralizing antibody responses also were measured by plaque reduction neutralization tests (PRNT90), and all animals exhibited neutralizing antibody (nAb) titers ≥20 as early as 21 dpi (Fig 4D).
293.Rationale: The passage does mention that serum neutralizing antibody (nAb) titers were measured and that all animals exhibited nAb titers ≥20 as early as 21 dpi. However, the passage does not explicitly state that nAbs are a critical component of the protective immune response. The passage also does not compare ZIKV immunology to other flaviviruses like YFV, JEV, and TBEV. The option introduces a hypothesis about the role of nAbs in ZIKV immunity that is not directly supported by the passage. The passage provides evidence of nAb presence but does not establish their critical role in protection.

293.Option: The continuing efficacy of YFV vaccines in reducing yellow fever disease in both Africa and South America suggests that the antigenic differences between individual YFV strains do not facilitate resistance to vaccine-induced immunity, i.e., diversity of YFV sequences appears to have little consequence for the use of currently formulated vaccines and/or the development of new YFV vaccines.
293.Rationale: The passage does not mention YFV vaccines or their efficacy in reducing yellow fever disease in Africa and South America. The option introduces information about YFV vaccines that is not present in the passage. The passage focuses on ZIKV and its immunology, not on YFV vaccines or their effectiveness. Therefore, this option cannot be derived from the passage.

293.Option: The protection against detectable viremia from homologous and heterologous rechallenge suggests that the immunologic barrier for complete protection may be comparatively low, such that vaccines with acceptable safety may have desirable efficacy even if they are not highly immunogenic.
Evidence: All three animals were productively infected with MR766, with detectable plasma viremia one day post inoculation (dpi) in two of the three animals and in all three by two dpi using a qRT-PCR that amplifies MR766 and ZIKV-FP with essentially identical efficiency (S1 Fig).
Viral RNA was undetectable in plasma (Fig 2B), saliva, and urine at all timepoints through 21 days post re-challenge.
293.Rationale: The passage describes that all three animals were productively infected with MR766 and that viral RNA was undetectable in plasma, saliva, and urine after re-challenge. However, the passage does not discuss the immunologic barrier for complete protection or suggest that vaccines with acceptable safety may have desirable efficacy even if they are not highly immunogenic. The option introduces a hypothesis about vaccine efficacy and immunogenicity that is not directly supported by the passage. The passage provides evidence of protection against detectable viremia but does not make inferences about the immunologic barrier or vaccine characteristics.

293.Option: The protection from detectable viremia observed in animals previously infected with African ZIKV suggests that immunity elicited by any Asian ZIKV infection should be sufficient to confer complete protection against all Asian ZIKV strains, similar to what has been described for YFV, TBEV, and JEV.
Evidence: All three animals were productively infected with MR766, with detectable plasma viremia one day post inoculation (dpi) in two of the three animals and in all three by two dpi using a qRT-PCR that amplifies MR766 and ZIKV-FP with essentially identical efficiency (S1 Fig).
Viral RNA was undetectable in plasma (Fig 2B), saliva, and urine at all timepoints through 21 days post re-challenge.

There was a significant increase in protection against infection with Asian ZIKV (Exact unconditional test) in animals previously infected with African ZIKV (n = 3) as compared to animals only challenged with Asian ZIKV (n = 8, p = 0.0016).
All 11 animals were challenged with the same dose of the Asian ZIKV (1x104 PFU/ml).

However, we did observe an increase in nAb titers after re-challenge despite the lack of detectable viremia in the animals (Fig 4D) suggesting that low level virus replication did occur, indicating protection from disease rather than sterilizing immunity.
Finally, nAb titer was similar when serum from the original challenge was screened against African versus Asian ZIKV (S5 Fig).
293.Rationale: The passage provides evidence that animals previously infected with African ZIKV (MR766) were protected against detectable viremia when re-challenged with Asian ZIKV (ZIKV-FP). The passage states that there was a significant increase in protection against infection with Asian ZIKV in animals previously infected with African ZIKV compared to animals only challenged with Asian ZIKV. The passage also mentions that nAb titers were similar when serum from the original challenge was screened against African versus Asian ZIKV. This suggests that immunity elicited by African ZIKV infection provides protection against Asian ZIKV, and by extension, that immunity elicited by any Asian ZIKV infection should be sufficient to confer complete protection against all Asian ZIKV strains. The option draws a parallel to what has been described for YFV, TBEV, and JEV, which is a reasonable inference given the context of flaviviruses and the evidence provided in the passage.

294.Option: Mebendazole treatment administered during pregnancy may have masked the true effect of prenatal helminth infection on infant cognitive development due to high cure rates and reduced reinfection.
Evidence: Women were also given a total of 600 mg of mebendazole (100 mg twice daily for 3 days) at the first ANC visit and again if they were tested positive for helminths at second ANC visit.
294.Rationale: The passage mentions that mebendazole was administered during pregnancy, but it does not provide any evidence about the effectiveness of the treatment in curing helminth infections or reducing reinfection. The study's findings show a significant association between maternal helminth infection and infant cognitive development despite the treatment. Therefore, the option cannot be derived from the passage.

294.Option: The lack of association between maternal helminth infection and infant helminth infection suggests that prenatal helminth exposure does not influence the child's susceptibility to infection.
Evidence: The prevalence (95% CI) of helminth infection among children by age one was 32.8% (26.0%-39.6%).
294.Rationale: The passage does not state that there is a lack of association between maternal helminth infection and infant helminth infection. It only provides the prevalence of helminth infection among children by age one. The option makes an inference that is not supported by the evidence in the passage.

294.Option: The association between maternal helminth infection and infant cognitive development is likely mediated by maternal anemia and low birth weight, as these are known risk factors for poor neurodevelopment.
Evidence: Over the past decades, many studies have confirmed helminth infection during pregnancy as a risk factor for maternal iron deficiency (ID) and anemia.
Evidence remains limited on the effects on adverse birth outcomes such as low birth weight (LBW) which is known to be associated with poorer cognitive function in children.
294.Rationale: The passage mentions that maternal anemia and low birth weight are known risk factors for poor neurodevelopment, but it does not provide evidence that these factors mediate the association between maternal helminth infection and infant cognitive development. The study adjusted for these variables in sensitivity analyses, but the association remained significant. Therefore, the option cannot be derived from the passage.

294.Option: Prenatal helminth infection, particularly with hookworms, is associated with poorer cognitive and gross motor functions in one-year-old children, independent of infant helminth infection and other known risk factors.
Evidence: The difference in mean ELC scores between children whose mothers were infected with helminths at first ANC visit and those whose mothers were not infected with any helminth remained significant after adjusting for maternal education, child sex and HOME score (p = 0.013).

Pregnant women who were infected with helminths at least once during pregnancy had children with poorer ELC scores, thus-4.4 (95% CI: -7.2 to-1.5) compared to those of mothers who were never infected during pregnancy after adjustment.

After adjusting for gravidity, maternal education, family possession, child sex and HOME score, helminth infection at first ANC visit was negatively associated with infant gross motor function (p = 0.028).

We observed that mothers who were infected with hookworms during the first ANC visit had children who scored less in the gross motor scale, -4.9 (95% CI: -8.6 to-1.1), compared to those whose mothers were never infected with hookworms at first ANC visit.
294.Rationale: The passage provides evidence that prenatal helminth infection, particularly with hookworms, is associated with poorer cognitive and gross motor functions in one-year-old children, even after adjusting for infant helminth infection and other known risk factors. The study's findings show a significant association between maternal helminth infection and infant cognitive development, independent of these factors.

294.Option: The low sensitivity of the Kato-Katz technique for helminths may have resulted in measurement error, which could have biased the observed association between maternal helminth infection and infant cognitive development toward the null.
Evidence: Thick smears of retrieved stool samples were immediately prepared and assessed using the Kato-Katz technique as described by the WHO.
294.Rationale: The passage mentions the use of the Kato-Katz technique for assessing helminth infection but does not provide any evidence about its sensitivity or the potential for measurement error. The study's findings show a significant association between maternal helminth infection and infant cognitive development, so the option cannot be derived from the passage.

295.Option: The detection of Bunyamwera virus in Anopheles gambiae and Culex univittatus in the Lake Victoria region indicates that these species are the primary vectors responsible for human outbreaks of Bunyamwera fever in East Africa.
Evidence: Bunyamwera virus (Orthobunyavirus) was the only vertically transmitted arbovirus (pathogenic to vertebrates) detected. It was identified by HRM analysis (Fig 2A), culture, and DNA sequencing (143 nt; 100% identity to GenBank accession KM507344, S1 Fig) from female Anopheles gambiae from Luanda Nyamasare (1/77 pools) and Cx. univittatus from Rusinga (2/31 pools) (Table 2) that were reared from larvae sampled in November 2012.
295.Rationale: The passage confirms the presence of Bunyamwera virus in Anopheles gambiae and Culex univittatus in the Lake Victoria region. However, it does not provide evidence that these species are the primary vectors responsible for human outbreaks of Bunyamwera fever in East Africa. The study does not compare the vector competence of these species to other potential vectors, nor does it establish a link between these species and human outbreaks. The term 'primary vectors' implies a level of dominance in transmission that is not supported by the data presented.

295.Option: The vertical transmission of Bunyamwera virus in Anopheles gambiae and Culex univittatus indicates that these mosquito species are more likely to transmit the virus to humans than other known vectors in the region.
Evidence: Bunyamwera virus (Orthobunyavirus) was the only vertically transmitted arbovirus (pathogenic to vertebrates) detected. It was identified by HRM analysis (Fig 2A), culture, and DNA sequencing (143 nt; 100% identity to GenBank accession KM507344, S1 Fig) from female Anopheles gambiae from Luanda Nyamasare (1/77 pools) and Cx. univittatus from Rusinga (2/31 pools) (Table 2) that were reared from larvae sampled in November 2012.
295.Rationale: The passage confirms vertical transmission of Bunyamwera virus in Anopheles gambiae and Culex univittatus. However, it does not provide evidence that these species are more likely to transmit the virus to humans than other known vectors in the region. The study does not compare the vector competence of these species to other potential vectors, nor does it establish a link between vertical transmission and increased likelihood of human transmission. The inference about relative transmission likelihood is not supported by the data presented.

295.Option: The presence of Bunyamwera virus in adult mosquitoes reared from field-collected larvae suggests that the virus is primarily maintained in the environment through contamination of aquatic breeding sites rather than through vertical transmission.
Evidence: Bunyamwera virus (Orthobunyavirus) was the only vertically transmitted arbovirus (pathogenic to vertebrates) detected. It was identified by HRM analysis (Fig 2A), culture, and DNA sequencing (143 nt; 100% identity to GenBank accession KM507344, S1 Fig) from female Anopheles gambiae from Luanda Nyamasare (1/77 pools) and Cx. univittatus from Rusinga (2/31 pools) (Table 2) that were reared from larvae sampled in November 2012.
295.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that Bunyamwera virus was detected in adult mosquitoes reared from field-collected larvae and that it is a vertically transmitted arbovirus. This directly contradicts the option's claim that the virus is primarily maintained through contamination of aquatic breeding sites rather than vertical transmission. The evidence presented supports vertical transmission as the mechanism of persistence, not environmental contamination.

295.Option: The absence of Bunyamwera virus in Baringo County samples suggests that the virus is not capable of persisting in mosquito populations outside the Lake Victoria region due to climatic or ecological differences.
Evidence: However, no vertically transmitted pathogenic arbovirus was detected in Baringo County samples.
295.Rationale: The passage states that no vertically transmitted pathogenic arbovirus was detected in Baringo County samples. However, it does not provide evidence that the virus is not capable of persisting in mosquito populations outside the Lake Victoria region due to climatic or ecological differences. The absence of detection in Baringo County could be due to various factors such as sampling limitations, low prevalence, or other methodological constraints. The option makes an unsupported generalization about the virus's capability to persist in other regions based on a single negative finding.

295.Option: The detection of Bunyamwera virus in adult mosquitoes reared from field-collected larvae indicates that this pathogenic arbovirus can persist through transstadial development and may be maintained in mosquito populations via vertical transmission, suggesting a potential reservoir role for Anopheles gambiae and Culex univittatus in the Lake Victoria region.
Evidence: Bunyamwera virus (Orthobunyavirus) was the only vertically transmitted arbovirus (pathogenic to vertebrates) detected. It was identified by HRM analysis (Fig 2A), culture, and DNA sequencing (143 nt; 100% identity to GenBank accession KM507344, S1 Fig) from female Anopheles gambiae from Luanda Nyamasare (1/77 pools) and Cx. univittatus from Rusinga (2/31 pools) (Table 2) that were reared from larvae sampled in November 2012.
295.Rationale: The passage provides direct evidence that Bunyamwera virus was detected in adult mosquitoes reared from field-collected larvae, indicating that the virus can persist through transstadial development. The study also explicitly states that this is a vertically transmitted arbovirus. The detection in Anopheles gambiae and Culex univittatus supports the suggestion of a potential reservoir role for these species in the Lake Victoria region. This inference is directly supported by the evidence presented in the passage.

296.Option: The inter-population redundancy of amino acid sequences in V1 and V4 indicates that these regions are functionally constrained and therefore less likely to contribute to immune evasion in syphilis patients.
Evidence: In eight cases, two or more amino acid sequences were found to be identical in one sample although they were coded by different nucleotide sequences (S3 and S4 Tables).
V1 and V4 presented a strong shared sequence capacity. The sequence IASDGGAIKH in V1 was observed in five strains (5/14) and DVGHKKENAANVNGTVGA in V4 was shared across seven strains (7/14).
296.Rationale: The evidence shows that V1 and V4 have shared sequences across multiple strains, which could suggest functional constraints. However, the passage also states that the tprK gene is 'acknowledged to have a pivotal role in immune evasion and pathogen persistence.' The shared sequences in V1 and V4 could represent conserved epitopes that are important for immune evasion rather than being functionally constrained in a way that limits immune evasion. The option incorrectly assumes that sequence redundancy implies reduced contribution to immune evasion, which is not supported by the passage.

296.Option: The low frequency of minor variants in tprK (mostly 1–5%) indicates that these variants are non-functional and do not contribute to the antigenic diversity of T. pallidum during primary infection.
Evidence: The frequency of all detected minor variants was almost below 20% (231/237) (Fig 2A).
The major proportion of the variants in primary syphilis samples was in the 1–5% (181/237) range, and the lowest was in the 10–60% (22/237) range.
296.Rationale: The passage states that minor variants exist at low frequencies but does not provide evidence that they are non-functional. In fact, the study's purpose is to understand tprK diversity, and the presence of minor variants at any frequency suggests they may contribute to antigenic diversity. The option makes an unsupported claim about functionality based solely on frequency, which is not addressed in the passage.

296.Option: The high frequency of predominant sequences in V1 and V6 regions suggests that these regions are under stronger purifying selection compared to other variable regions.
Evidence: There was a predominant sequence in each V region of ten samples directly from primary syphilis patients, and the proportion of this sequence was almost above 80%.
The sequence IASDGGAIKH in V1 was observed in five strains (5/14) and DVGHKKENAANVNGTVGA in V4 was shared across seven strains (7/14).
296.Rationale: While the passage notes high frequency of predominant sequences in V1 and V6, it does not provide evidence about the type of selection (purifying vs. balancing) acting on these regions. The option assumes purifying selection without supporting evidence from the passage. The study focuses on antigenic variation, which is more consistent with diversifying selection rather than purifying selection.

296.Option: The length variation in tprK sequences, particularly in V3, V6, and V7, is likely due to random insertions and deletions during PCR amplification, which introduces artifacts into the sequencing data.
Evidence: The length of the captured sequences within each V region was also found to be variable, particularly in V3, V6 and V7, with 11 or 12 forms.
In this regard, the lengths of V1, V4 and V5 appeared to vary in intervals of 6 bp.
296.Rationale: The passage describes length variation as a biological phenomenon, not as an artifact of PCR. The study used NGS with specific methods to capture variable regions, and the length variations were systematically analyzed. The option incorrectly attributes biological variation to technical artifacts without evidence from the passage.

296.Option: The presence of a high proportion of a predominant sequence and numerous low-frequency minor variants in the tprK gene of primary syphilis patients suggests a fitness-based evolutionary strategy where dominant sequences are optimized to evade immune recognition, while minor variants may represent transient diversity that could be selected for under immune pressure.
Evidence: The tprK gene is acknowledged to have a pivotal role in immune evasion and pathogen persistence.
The frequency of all detected minor variants was almost below 20% (231/237) (Fig 2A).
The major proportion of the variants in primary syphilis samples was in the 1–5% (181/237) range, and the lowest was in the 10–60% (22/237) range.
The observed distribution of sequence variants suggests a fitness-based evolutionary strategy.
296.Rationale: The passage provides evidence that tprK is crucial for immune evasion and that the observed sequence distribution includes a dominant sequence and many low-frequency variants. This pattern is consistent with a fitness-based evolutionary strategy where dominant sequences are well-adapted to evade immune recognition, while minor variants represent potential diversity that could be selected for under changing immune pressure. The evidence directly supports this inference about evolutionary dynamics under immune selection pressure.

297.Option: The fact that leprosy prevalence remains high in this region is likely due to the underreporting of PKDL cases, as both diseases share similar clinical presentations and diagnostic challenges.
Evidence: An important condition in the differential diagnosis of PKDL is leprosy.
The three PKDL cases were all from different villages.
297.Rationale: The passage states that PKDL and leprosy are in the differential diagnosis, but it does not suggest that underreporting of PKDL is causing high leprosy prevalence. The low number of PKDL cases (3 confirmed out of 4 suspects) and their spatial distribution in different villages indicate that PKDL is not a major confounding factor for leprosy prevalence in this study. The high leprosy prevalence is attributed to other factors, not PKDL underreporting.

297.Option: The observed prevalence of leprosy in the study population (17.1 per 10,000) is consistent with national elimination targets, suggesting that the disease has been effectively controlled at the local level.
Evidence: India was among the countries that by 2005 had achieved an overall leprosy prevalence of less than 1 per 10,000, an enormous reduction from the 57.8 per 10,000 observed in 1983.
The states of Bihar, Maharashtra and West Bengal, which had earlier achieved the elimination threshold, were again above the threshold at the end of 2013.
297.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that the national elimination target for leprosy is less than 1 per 10,000. The observed prevalence of 17.1 per 10,000 in this study is 17 times higher than the elimination target. This directly contradicts the claim that the prevalence is consistent with national elimination targets. The passage also notes that Bihar had previously exceeded the elimination threshold, supporting the conclusion that the disease is not effectively controlled in this region.

297.Option: The low prevalence of PKDL (1.1 per 10,000) in the studied region indicates that the elimination of visceral leishmaniasis has been successfully achieved and sustained in this area.
Evidence: In the Muzaffarpur Health and Demographic Surveillance System (HDSS), situated in a VL endemic area in Bihar, India, comprising of 50 contiguous villages and with a population of approximately 90,000, 339 new VL cases were reported from 2007 to 2018, but only 5 cases of PKDL.
297.Rationale: The passage states that 339 new VL cases were reported in the area from 2007 to 2018, indicating ongoing transmission of visceral leishmaniasis. The low PKDL prevalence does not indicate successful elimination of VL but rather may reflect underreporting of PKDL. The study period (2017-2018) is after the 2007-2018 reporting period, so the low PKDL prevalence cannot be used to infer that VL has been successfully eliminated and sustained in the area.

297.Option: The high spatial clustering of leprosy cases within 25 meters of an affected household suggests that leprosy transmission is primarily driven by close household contact, making household-based screening sufficient for effective case detection.
Evidence: Four out of 44 cases (9.1%) were found among 402 persons living in the same household as a leprosy patient, who make up 1.2% of the population.
Among the neighbors at less than 25 meters of an active leprosy case, who make up 3.5% of the total population, 15.9% of the leprosy patients were found.
297.Rationale: The data show that 15.9% of leprosy cases were found within 25 meters of an affected household, which is significantly higher than the 9.1% found within the same household. This suggests that transmission extends beyond the household to the immediate neighborhood. The Poisson model also showed a rate ratio of 6.3 for household contacts and 3.6 for neighbors within 25 meters, indicating that both household and neighborhood contacts are important for transmission. Therefore, household-based screening alone would not be sufficient for effective case detection.

297.Option: Leprosy is far from eliminated and requires intensified case finding approaches, as evidenced by a prevalence rate of 17.1 per 10,000 in a rural area of Bihar, India, which is 17 times the elimination target.
Evidence: India was among the countries that by 2005 had achieved an overall leprosy prevalence of less than 1 per 10,000, an enormous reduction from the 57.8 per 10,000 observed in 1983.
The states of Bihar, Maharashtra and West Bengal, which had earlier achieved the elimination threshold, were again above the threshold at the end of 2013.
The observed prevalence rate of leprosy was 17.1 per 10,000.
297.Rationale: The passage provides clear evidence that the national elimination target for leprosy is less than 1 per 10,000. The observed prevalence of 17.1 per 10,000 in this study is 17 times higher than the target, indicating that leprosy is far from eliminated in this region. The passage also notes that Bihar had previously exceeded the elimination threshold, supporting the need for intensified case finding approaches. This inference is directly supported by the data presented in the passage.

298.Option: Monoclonal antibodies that bind to the N- and C-terminus of GP1 and GP2 are sufficient to provide complete protection in guinea pigs when administered at any time after infection.
Evidence: The MAbs tested so far are not 100% efficacious in all animal models therefore further research is needed for more effective antibodies.
The PBS control treated animals all died with a mean time to death of 7 days. In contrast, all of the MAb treated groups demonstrated complete survival, except for 10C8 (83%).
298.Rationale: The passage does not mention anything about the N- and C-terminus of GP1 and GP2, nor does it state that MAbs binding to these regions provide complete protection at any time after infection. The evidence shows that some MAbs provided complete protection in guinea pigs when administered at specific times (e.g., 2 dpi), but not at any time after infection. The claim about 'any time after infection' is not supported by the data presented.

298.Option: The failure of KZ52 to protect non-human primates despite success in guinea pigs suggests that monoclonal antibodies are unlikely to be effective in higher animal models.
Evidence: One neutralizing MAb KZ52 was 100% efficacious in guinea pigs, but did not protect NHPs [26], [35].
298.Rationale: The passage states that KZ52 failed to protect NHPs but does not generalize this to all monoclonal antibodies. The evidence does not support the conclusion that MAbs are 'unlikely to be effective in higher animal models.' The failure of one specific MAb in NHPs does not imply a general ineffectiveness of all MAbs in higher models.

298.Option: The ability of monoclonal antibodies to improve survival in guinea pigs is primarily due to their neutralizing capacity, as demonstrated by the complete protection seen with the combination of neutralizing MAbs 1H3, 2G4, and 4G7.
Evidence: The MAbs tested so far are not 100% efficacious in all animal models therefore further research is needed for more effective antibodies.
The PBS control treated animals all died with a mean time to death of 7 days. In contrast, all of the MAb treated groups demonstrated complete survival, except for 10C8 (83%).
298.Rationale: The passage does not state that the ability of MAbs to improve survival is 'primarily due to their neutralizing capacity.' While the combination of neutralizing MAbs provided complete protection, the passage also notes that the ability of MAbs to neutralize the virus is not essential for improving survival. Therefore, the inference that neutralizing capacity is the primary factor is not supported by the evidence.

298.Option: A combination of neutralizing monoclonal antibodies targeting different regions of the Ebola virus glycoprotein provides complete protection in guinea pigs when administered up to 2 days after infection, representing a clinically feasible treatment window.
Evidence: The combination of neutralizing MAbs (1.5 mg 1H3+1.5 mg 2G4+2 mg 4G7) was given to guinea pigs either 1 day before, or 1, 2 or 3 days after a 1000 LD50 GA-ZEBOV infection, and survival followed. The PBS control group all died, with a mean time to death of 6.58±0.59 days. In contrast all animals receiving the neutralizing MAb combination at 2 dpi. survived.
The PBS control treated animals all died with a mean time to death of 7 days. In contrast, all of the MAb treated groups demonstrated complete survival, except for 10C8 (83%).
298.Rationale: The evidence shows that a combination of neutralizing MAbs provided complete protection in guinea pigs when administered up to 2 days after infection. This treatment window is considered clinically feasible. The data directly support the inference that a combination of neutralizing MAbs targeting different regions of the GP can provide complete protection within this timeframe.

298.Option: The most effective monoclonal antibody treatment protocol involves administering a single dose of neutralizing MAbs 1 day before infection, as this timing maximizes survival in both mice and guinea pigs.
Evidence: In the majority of cases, treatment 1 day before infection resulted in lower survival rates than 4 days before infection.
The most effective MAbs for pre-treatment were 5D2, 5E6, and 7C9. They had the highest survival rates (73–90%) and worked almost equally well on both days −4 and +1.
298.Rationale: The evidence indicates that treatment 1 day before infection did not maximize survival in all cases and that some MAbs were more effective when administered 4 days before infection. The claim that administering a single dose 1 day before infection is the most effective protocol is not supported by the data, which shows variability in effectiveness depending on the MAb and timing.

299.Option: The urine-based ELISA and dipstick tests show significant clearance of anti-Leishmania antibodies in 90% of treated VL patients within six months, suggesting their potential as a test of cure.
Evidence: Out of 18 urine samples, 16 showed a significant decrease (p<0.0001) in the urine IgG titre only after six months (day > 180) since the treatment started (Fig 5).
Out of 18 follow-up samples, 16 were entirely negative on and after six months of the treatment initiation (day >180).
299.Rationale: The passage provides direct evidence that 16 out of 18 (88.9%) treated VL patients showed significant clearance of anti-Leishmania antibodies in urine after six months, as demonstrated by both ELISA and dipstick tests. This directly supports the inference that urine-based tests can serve as a test of cure. The longitudinal data specifically tracks antibody clearance over time, which is the key evidence for this conclusion.

299.Option: Urine-based ELISA and dipstick tests are superior to serum-based rK39-RDTs for diagnosing VL in all endemic regions, including East Africa, due to their higher sensitivity and specificity.
Evidence: rK39-RDTs for sero-diagnostics were also tested with urine samples in several studies and found sensitivities and specificities of 96.40% and 66.2–100%, respectively [19].
In comparison to urine dipstick test, serum-based rK39-RDT showed 95.87% sensitivity (93/97) and 97.33% specificity (73/75).
299.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that rK39-RDTs have variable performance in different regions, with lower sensitivity in East Africa. While the urine-based tests in this study showed 100% sensitivity and specificity, the passage does not claim superiority in all regions, especially East Africa. The evidence does not support the universal claim made in this option.

299.Option: The dipstick test's high specificity and sensitivity in detecting VL and PKDL make it a reliable tool for diagnosing relapse in patients who remain positive after six months of treatment.
Evidence: The dipstick test showed positivity in all VL urine samples, acquiring a sensitivity of 100% (97/97). None of the urine samples taken from negative controls showed reactivity with the dipstick assay, resulting in 100% specificity (75/75) of the test.

Out of 18 follow-up samples, 16 were entirely negative on and after six months of the treatment initiation (day >180). Two samples remained positive after 6 months of treatment till the data reported (S1 Table).
299.Rationale: The passage provides evidence that the dipstick test has 100% sensitivity and specificity for detecting VL and PKDL. The longitudinal data also shows that most patients become negative after six months, while those who remain positive could indicate relapse. This supports the inference that the dipstick test can reliably detect relapse in patients who remain positive after six months of treatment.

299.Option: The delayed decline in urine antibody titres after treatment suggests that the immune response to Leishmania is slower to resolve in urine than in serum, indicating a longer duration of immune activity in the urinary system.
Evidence: Out of 18 urine samples, 16 showed a significant decrease (p<0.0001) in the urine IgG titre only after six months (day > 180) since the treatment started (Fig 5).
There is no significant decline in the titre just after completing the treatment (day 30).
299.Rationale: The passage only provides data about urine antibody titres, not serum antibody titres. The comparison between urine and serum immune responses is not supported by the evidence. The option makes an unsupported claim about serum antibody resolution that cannot be derived from the passage.

299.Option: The persistence of anti-Leishmania antibodies in serum for several years after treatment makes serum-based tests unsuitable for monitoring treatment response, but urine-based tests overcome this limitation by detecting antibodies only during active infection.
Evidence: Leishmania-specific antibodies remain within the infected blood for years after treatment. Thus serum-based antibody detection tests cannot serve as a test of cure [12].
299.Rationale: The passage states that serum-based tests cannot serve as a test of cure because antibodies persist for years after treatment. However, the passage does not provide evidence that urine-based tests detect antibodies only during active infection. The claim about urine-based tests overcoming this limitation is not supported by the evidence provided.

300.Option: The fact that 72% of dog owners adopted offspring of free-roaming dogs indicates that the rural population is generally accepting of stray dogs, suggesting that community-based dog adoption programs could be implemented without significant resistance.
Evidence: The characteristics of the dog owners in this study are presented in Table 8. Dog-owners who had a negative perception of FRD were less likely to seek veterinary attention (OR 0.3, 95%CI 0.1–1, p = 0.047) for their pets. Dog owners who adopted their pets “off the street” were less likely to get their dogs vaccinated than those who either purchased them or were given them (OR 0.08, 95%CI 0.01–0.4, p = 0.001).
300.Rationale: The passage does not state that 72% of dog owners adopted offspring of free-roaming dogs. The only information provided about adoption is that dog owners who adopted pets 'off the street' were less likely to vaccinate them. The claim about 72% adoption rate is not supported by the passage. Additionally, the passage indicates that adoption from stray dogs is associated with lower vaccination rates, which would be a concern for community-based adoption programs. The option makes an unsupported claim and misrepresents the relationship between adoption and community acceptance.

300.Option: Respondents from the high/middle socio-economic level had more positive attitudes towards free-roaming dogs, including being more likely to feed them and take injured dogs to a veterinarian, which could be leveraged to promote responsible dog ownership and population management.
Evidence: Participants from the high/middle socio-economic level considered FRD were useful (OR 3.09, 95%CI 1.06–8.97, p = 0.03), were likely to feed them (OR2.81, 95%CI 1.34–5.88, p = 0.005), and would take an injured stray dog to a veterinarian (OR 2.33 95%CI 1.0–5.48, p = 0.04).
300.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that high/middle socio-economic level respondents had more positive attitudes toward free-roaming dogs, including being more likely to feed them and take injured dogs to a veterinarian. This directly supports the option's claim that these attitudes could be leveraged for responsible dog ownership and population management. The statistical significance (p-values) and odds ratios provided in the passage confirm the strength of this association, making this a valid epidemiological inference.

300.Option: Because younger respondents were less likely to view free-roaming dogs as a health threat, public health campaigns should prioritize youth education to reduce fear and promote coexistence with stray dogs.
Evidence: The younger respondents (≤34 years) did not consider FRD a threat to human health (OR 0.2, 95% CI 0.04–0.97, p = 0.05), and were more likely to feed them (OR 2.2, 95%CI 1.1–4.5, p = 0.04) than older participants.
300.Rationale: While the passage does state that younger respondents were less likely to view free-roaming dogs as a health threat, the option misinterprets this as a need to 'reduce fear' when the data actually suggests a lack of awareness of the threat rather than fear. The passage does not provide evidence that public health campaigns should focus on youth education to promote coexistence. In fact, the lack of perceived threat among younger people could be a barrier to effective rabies prevention rather than an opportunity for promotion of coexistence.

300.Option: The lack of significant factors influencing favorable practices for rabies elimination suggests that community-wide interventions should focus on improving access to post-exposure prophylaxis rather than changing attitudes toward free-roaming dogs.
Evidence: The association between the various descriptive variables and the attitudes and practices of the participants towards rabies are presented in Table 4. Pet ownership (p = 0.11), dog ownership (p = 0.1), and poultry ownership (p = 0.19) were offered to the multivariable logistic regression model. A stable multivariate logistic regression model with significant p-values could not be generated.
300.Rationale: The passage does not state that there is a 'lack of significant factors influencing favorable practices for rabies elimination.' It only mentions that a stable multivariate model could not be generated for attitudes and practices towards rabies. This does not imply that there are no significant factors influencing these practices. The option makes an unsupported claim about the lack of significant factors and draws an incorrect conclusion about intervention priorities.

300.Option: Since poultry owners were more likely to perceive free-roaming dogs as a threat due to attacks on backyard poultry, targeted rabies awareness campaigns should focus on livestock-owning households to improve preventive practices.
Evidence: A significant number of poultry owners reported that FRD attacked their backyard poultry for food (OR 3.2, 95%CI 1.07–12.1, p = 0.02).
300.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that poultry owners were more likely to perceive free-roaming dogs as a threat due to attacks on their backyard poultry. This provides a clear rationale for targeting livestock-owning households in rabies awareness campaigns, as these households already have a perceived threat from free-roaming dogs that could be leveraged to improve preventive practices. The statistical significance (p = 0.02) and odds ratio (3.2) support the strength of this association.

301.Option: The detection of Rickettsia felis DNA in the cerebrospinal fluid of two fatal cases of acute neurological disease, combined with the absence of other identifiable pathogens and the presence of rickettsial-like CSF findings, suggests that R. felis may be a causative agent of severe neurological disease in humans.
Evidence: Rickettsia positive specimens were confirmed using multi-locus sequence typing semi-nested PCR targeting the 17-kDa, ompB, and gltA genes
Results were consistent with only Rickettsia felis
Amplicons were confirmed to be R. felis by Sanger sequencing
Two of 38 CSF samples (patients E-059 and E-063) were, however, positive for Rickettsia DNA as determined by genus-specific 23S rRNA qPCR
Insufficient sera were available from these two cases for Rickettsia PCR
All CSF and serum samples were negative for Leptospira and O. tsutsugamushi by qPCR
Retroactive testing for virus was negative for both [12]
301.Rationale: The passage provides multiple lines of evidence supporting this conclusion. First, Rickettsia felis DNA was specifically detected in the cerebrospinal fluid of two fatal cases using both genus-specific and species-specific molecular methods. Second, no other pathogens were identified in these cases through comprehensive testing for Leptospira, O. tsutsugamushi, and various viruses. Third, the CSF findings in these cases (mononuclear pleocytosis in one case) are consistent with rickettsial infection patterns. The combination of direct pathogen detection, absence of alternative explanations, and clinical context of neurological disease provides strong epidemiological support for R. felis as a causative agent in these cases.

301.Option: The seasonal co-circulation of Rickettsia felis with other rickettsial pathogens in Southeast Asia may compound healthcare system burden, particularly in pediatric intensive care units.
Evidence: The study was conducted in North Sulawesi, Indonesia
Testing was done on specimens collected from cases of acute neurological disease suspected to have infectious etiologies
301.Rationale: While the study was conducted in Southeast Asia, the passage contains no information about seasonal patterns of R. felis co-circulation with other rickettsial pathogens. Additionally, the study focused on adult patients (aged >15 years) and did not specifically address pediatric intensive care units. The conclusion about healthcare system burden requires assumptions not supported by the evidence in the passage.

301.Option: The 100% sequence identity of the 17-kDa and ompB genes between the Indonesian cases and the R. felis reference strain URRWXCal2 confirms that R. felis is a zoonotic pathogen with a stable transmission cycle in human populations.
Evidence: The 17-kDa and ompB sequences were 100% identical to the R. felis reference strain URRWXCal2 [3]
Rickettsia felis was first identified as a R. typhi-like organism in the midgut epithelium of a commercial colony of cat fleas, Ctenocephalides felis [1]
301.Rationale: While the sequence identity confirms the species as R. felis, it does not provide evidence about transmission cycles in human populations. The passage states that R. felis is naturally passaged generationally in fleas but does not provide evidence about its transmission cycle in human populations. The conclusion about a 'stable transmission cycle in human populations' requires additional evidence not presented in the passage.

301.Option: The absence of skin rash, eschar, or regional lymphadenopathy in both cases suggests that Rickettsia felis infection typically presents without classical rickettsial signs.
Evidence: Neither patient was noted on the charts to have skin rash, eschar, or regional lymphadenopathy
301.Rationale: The absence of classical rickettsial signs in these two cases does not establish that R. felis typically presents without these signs. The passage only describes two cases and does not provide comparative data about typical presentations. The conclusion requires generalizing from a small sample size, which is not supported by the evidence in the passage.

301.Option: The failure of standard TB therapy in both cases, combined with negative acid-fast staining, provides definitive evidence that Rickettsia felis is the primary cause of death.
Evidence: TB meningitis was initially suspected for both cases but neither CSF was positive by acid-fast bacilli stain and neither responded to presumptive TB treatment
Capacity to culture TB at the hospital was not available
301.Rationale: While the failure of TB treatment and negative acid-fast staining support that TB was not the cause, they do not provide definitive evidence that R. felis was the primary cause of death. The passage states that R. felis DNA was detected, but does not establish a direct causal relationship between the detection and the fatal outcomes. The conclusion requires making a definitive causal claim that is not fully supported by the evidence presented.

302.Option: The high in-hospital case fatality rate among elderly patients with severe dengue in this study, despite high-quality ICU care, suggests that age-related physiological decline and comorbidities are major contributors to poor outcomes.
Evidence: The mean age of the 75 patients was 72.3±9.3 years (ranging from 50.7 to 88.5 years, Table 1).
The most common comorbidities included hypertension (72.0%), diabetes (43.7%), and chronic kidney disease (22.7%).

Old patients with DF are often associated with atypical presentations, longer hospitalization and more underlying diseases [14]. In particular, diabetes mellitus, hypertension, and aging are associated with a higher risk of DHF/DSS and a worse outcome [6, 15–18].
302.Rationale: The passage provides direct evidence of the high age (mean 72.3 years) and high prevalence of comorbidities (hypertension, diabetes, chronic kidney disease) in the cohort. It also references prior literature establishing that aging and comorbidities like diabetes and hypertension are associated with worse outcomes in dengue. The high case fatality rate (41.3%) despite ICU care supports the inference that age-related physiological decline and comorbidities are major contributors to poor outcomes.

302.Option: The high in-hospital case fatality rate in elderly patients with severe dengue is due to the high prevalence of bloodstream infections, particularly late-onset candidemia, which contributed significantly to mortality.
Evidence: Nineteen bacterial and three fungal pathogens were identified in the bloodstream of 18 (23.7%) patients.
Notably, two patients experienced recurrent fever at the eighth and ninth day after the onset of DF, respectively, which was caused by candidemia.
302.Rationale: While the passage mentions bloodstream infections in 23.7% of patients and specifically notes two cases of candidemia, it does not establish that these infections were a major contributor to the overall high case fatality rate. The passage states that bloodstream infections were identified in 18 patients, but the leading causes of death were multiple organ failure (45.2%), refractory shock (22.6%), and intracranial hemorrhage (19.4%). The evidence does not support the claim that bloodstream infections, including candidemia, were a primary driver of mortality in this cohort.

302.Option: The high in-hospital case fatality rate among elderly patients with severe dengue in this study is primarily due to delayed access to medical care and inadequate fluid resuscitation, as evidenced by the high rate of cardiac arrest before ICU admission.
Evidence: Seven (9.3%) patients experienced cardiac arrest before ICU admission.
The median resuscitation time was 10 minutes, ranging from 2–38 minutes.
302.Rationale: The passage states that 9.3% of patients experienced cardiac arrest before ICU admission, but it does not provide evidence that this was due to delayed access to care or inadequate fluid resuscitation. The passage also notes that five of these patients were admitted to the ICU immediately after resuscitation, and one was admitted the next day. The evidence does not support the claim that delayed care or inadequate resuscitation was the primary cause of the high case fatality rate.

302.Option: The high in-hospital case fatality rate in this cohort is attributable to the limited availability of invasive life-supporting interventions such as hemodialysis and endotracheal intubation for elderly patients in Taiwan, which restricts their recovery potential.
Evidence: Approximately three-quarters (57, 76.0%) of all patients required ventilator support during the ICU stay.
12 (29.3%) of whom needed renal replacement therapy during the ICU stay.
302.Rationale: The passage indicates that a significant proportion of patients required ventilator support and renal replacement therapy, but it does not mention any limitations in the availability of these interventions. The evidence does not support the claim that limited availability of life-supporting interventions was a factor in the high case fatality rate.

302.Option: The high in-hospital case fatality rate in this elderly cohort is primarily driven by the high APACHE II and SOFA scores, which reflect the severity of organ failure and are the most significant predictors of mortality.
Evidence: The median APACHE II score and SOFA score at the ICU admission was 20 and 11, respectively.

In the multivariate analysis, cardiac arrest before ICU admission (hazard ratio [HR] 6.26 [1.91–20.54]), AKI at ICU admission (HR 2.48 [1.07–5.74]), and initial APTT longer than 48 s (HR 3.91 [1.69–9.07]) were independent predictive factors for in-hospital fatality after adjusting for gender, cardiac arrest before ICU admission, respiratory failure at ICU admission, presence of AKI at ICU admission and initial APTT longer than 48 s (Table 3). APACHE II and SOFA scores include many clinical parameters and were not included in the final model of multivariate Cox analysis as they demonstrated collinearity with other variables.
302.Rationale: The passage states that APACHE II and SOFA scores were not included in the final multivariate model because they demonstrated collinearity with other variables. The independent predictive factors identified in the multivariate analysis were cardiac arrest before ICU admission, AKI at ICU admission, and initial APTT longer than 48 s. The evidence does not support the claim that APACHE II and SOFA scores were the most significant predictors of mortality in this cohort.

303.Option: The availability of miltefosine in East Africa for visceral leishmaniasis treatment creates a strong foundation for immediate implementation of miltefosine as a first-line therapy for L aethiopica without further clinical evaluation.
Evidence: Miltefosine has been successfully used in more than 50 CL cases due to L aethiopica in Addis Ababa, but the findings have not been published (personal communication, Asrat Hailu).
The available in vitro data relating to these drugs suggest a good susceptibility of L aethiopica to miltefosine, paromomycin, pentamidine and amphotericin B (Table 5).
303.Rationale: The passage mentions that miltefosine has been successfully used in over 50 cases of L aethiopica in Addis Ababa, but this information is based on unpublished personal communication. The passage also states that the evidence base for L aethiopica treatment remains extremely limited, with most studies being small and non-randomized. The option incorrectly suggests that miltefosine can be implemented as a first-line therapy without further clinical evaluation, which contradicts the passage's emphasis on the need for randomized clinical trials and the lack of high-quality evidence.

303.Option: The national and WHO guidelines recommend intramuscular paromomycin as the preferential first-line treatment for LCL caused by L aethiopica, supported by strong clinical evidence from well-designed trials.
Evidence: Paromomycin ointment was highly effective in three Ethiopians treated in Israel.
The available in vitro data relating to these drugs suggest a good susceptibility of L aethiopica to miltefosine, paromomycin, pentamidine and amphotericin B (Table 5).
303.Rationale: The passage states that paromomycin ointment was highly effective in three cases in Israel, but there is no mention of national or WHO guidelines recommending it as a first-line treatment. Additionally, the passage emphasizes the lack of well-designed trials and the limited evidence base for L aethiopica treatment. The option incorrectly implies strong clinical evidence from well-designed trials, which is not supported by the passage.

303.Option: The high genetic diversity of L aethiopica and its frequent association with mucocutaneous and diffuse forms suggest that treatment outcomes are inherently unpredictable and that no standardized therapy can be developed.
Evidence: Clinical manifestations of L aethiopica are particularly diverse and pleotropic, and a high genetic diversity has been documented as well [12].
L aethiopica has particular features (the frequent occurrence of DCL and MCL) that imply a potentially higher need of systemic therapy, as compared to other species such as L major or L tropica.
303.Rationale: The passage mentions the high genetic diversity of L aethiopica and its association with mucocutaneous and diffuse forms, but it does not conclude that treatment outcomes are inherently unpredictable or that no standardized therapy can be developed. Instead, the passage emphasizes the need for randomized clinical trials to identify potentially efficacious medications. The option misrepresents the passage's content by suggesting an inherent unpredictability rather than a need for further research.

303.Option: The consistent use of antimonials in clinical practice in Ethiopia indicates that their efficacy and safety have been well-established through long-term observational use.
Evidence: In vitro data generally suggested a low susceptibility of L aethiopica to antimonials (Table 5).

In a study from Tigray, clinical non-response was observed in 23 (15%) of the 154 treated (intralesional or systemic SSG). Failure rates were particularly high in MCL and DCL cases and all of the HIV cases relapsed.
The available in vitro data relating to these drugs suggest a good susceptibility of L aethiopica to miltefosine, paromomycin, pentamidine and amphotericin B (Table 5).
303.Rationale: The passage states that in vitro data suggest low susceptibility of L aethiopica to antimonials and that clinical studies show high failure rates, particularly in MCL and DCL cases. The option incorrectly claims that the efficacy and safety of antimonials have been well-established through long-term observational use, which is not supported by the passage's findings of poor efficacy and high failure rates.

303.Option: The evidence base for treatment of cutaneous leishmaniasis due to L aethiopica remains extremely limited, with most studies being small, non-randomized, and lacking rigorous methodology, despite the availability of certain drugs in clinical practice.
Evidence: The evidence base remains extremely limited. A Cochrane review published in 2008 identified not a single randomized clinical trial dedicated to CL in Ethiopia [14].

Out of a total of 95 studies or reports identified and screened, only 27 provided information relevant to our topic (Fig 1). This comprised 13 case series and seven case reports, generally clinical information collected as part of clinical practice. Most of these were reported over 30 to 40 years ago.

Most studies had obvious limitations, beyond the descriptive nature. Definitions of treatment outcomes varied across studies and were often not clearly defined. Sample size was often small, follow-up short and information on patient characteristics often limited. In formal quality assessment, the non-randomized studies performed poorly, with a median score of three (maximum score is eight), see Table 3.
303.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that the evidence base for L aethiopica treatment is extremely limited, with most studies being small, non-randomized, and lacking rigorous methodology. It also mentions that a Cochrane review found no randomized clinical trials dedicated to CL in Ethiopia and that most studies were case series or case reports with significant limitations. The option accurately reflects the passage's findings and is directly supported by the evidence provided.

305.Option: The lack of association between IL18 haplotypes and infection risk suggests that the functional effects of the gene are not relevant in the immune response to T. cruzi.
Evidence: The haplotype model did not better explain the IL18 association to risk of infection than the model considering the SNPs independently (likelihood P-value = 0.1454), indicating no additive effects (that is, the associated haplotypes were a consequence of the independent associations of the considered variants).
305.Rationale: The passage states that the haplotype model did not better explain the association than the model considering SNPs independently, but this does not imply that the functional effects of the gene are irrelevant. The study still found significant associations between specific SNPs and infection risk. The lack of haplotype effect simply suggests that the associations are not additive, not that the gene's functional effects are unimportant in the immune response.

305.Option: The promoter polymorphism rs360719 is likely the causal variant underlying the association between IL18 gene variation and susceptibility to infection by T. cruzi in the Colombian population.
Evidence: The results of these analyses pointed to rs360719 as the most likely causative variant of the IL18 association with Chagas disease, as the statistical significance of both rs2043055 and rs1946518 were clearly lost after conditioning to it, and a trend was maintained when conditioning rs360719 on these two latter variants (Table 2).
305.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that rs360719 is the most likely causative variant. The conditional logistic regression analysis showed that when rs360719 was considered, the significance of the other SNPs (rs2043055 and rs1946518) was lost, indicating that rs360719 is the primary variant driving the association. This supports the conclusion that rs360719 is the causal variant in the Colombian population.

305.Option: The observed association between IL18 variants and infection susceptibility is likely due to population stratification in the Colombian cohort.
Evidence: The six IL18 SNPs were in Hardy-Weinberg equilibrium in all the analyzed subgroups (P>0.01), suggesting that a possible inbreed in Guanentina and Comunera provinces is not likely.
305.Rationale: The passage states that the SNPs were in Hardy-Weinberg equilibrium, which suggests that population stratification is not a significant factor in the observed associations. Hardy-Weinberg equilibrium is a key indicator that the population is not experiencing significant stratification or inbreeding, which would otherwise confound the results. Therefore, the association is more likely to be genuine rather than due to population stratification.

305.Option: The functional role of rs360719 in IL-18 expression is likely to be independent of the transcription factor OCT-1, based on the observed genetic associations.
Evidence: an additional promoter variant (rs360719) that has been described to interact with the transcription factor OCT-1 [28]
305.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that rs360719 has been described to interact with the transcription factor OCT-1. The observed genetic associations do not provide evidence to suggest that this interaction is not occurring. Therefore, the option that the functional role is independent of OCT-1 is incorrect.

305.Option: The IL18 gene variants rs2043055 and rs1946518 are independently associated with increased risk of chronic Chagas cardiomyopathy in the Colombian cohort.
Evidence: No statistically significant differences were observed between asymptomatic individuals and chronic Chagasic cardiomyopathy patients for any of the analyzed polymorphisms.
305.Rationale: The passage clearly states that no statistically significant differences were observed between asymptomatic individuals and chronic Chagas cardiomyopathy patients for any of the analyzed polymorphisms, including rs2043055 and rs1946518. Therefore, these variants are not independently associated with increased risk of chronic Chagas cardiomyopathy.

In 2019 infected individuals had low positive ODs indicating a low number of adult worms.

307.Option: The high seroprevalence of Leptospira IgM in urban areas of the DRC suggests that leptospirosis is primarily an urban zoonotic disease driven by poor sanitation and overcrowding, with transmission linked to flooding and exposure to contaminated water.

62 (71%) were found in areas with a tropical savanna climate.
The odds of finding Leptospira IgM was almost three times higher in areas with tropical savanna climate compared to areas with humid subtropical climate (Crude OR 2.81; 95% CI 1.37–5.76).
307.Rationale: The passage provides strong evidence that leptospirosis is more prevalent in urban areas of the DRC. Besides the rainy season (Adjusted OR 3.34; 95% CI 1.34–8.29), multivariable logistic regression showed that male sex (Adjusted OR 4.41; 95% CI 1.87–10.39) and rainy season (Adjusted OR 3.14; 95% CI 0.38–25.49) were associated with positive leptospiral IgM in the urban setting.

The higher prevalence in urban areas and among young adults contradicts the claim that it is primarily a rural disease affecting children. 308.Option: The widespread use of insecticide-treated curtains (ITCs) in the intervention arm led to a significant reduction in the number of adult female Aedes aegypti mosquitoes, which likely contributed to the lower seroconversion rates observed in the control arm.
Evidence: There was a significant difference in overall seroconversion rates (seroconversion to any individual serotype or multiple serotypes; p<0.0001) between the intervention and control arms. The intervention arm had an average seroconversion rate of 50.6 per 100 person-years (PY) (CI: 29.9–71.9) and those in the control arm had an average seroconversion rate of 37.4 per 100 PY (CI: 15.2–51.7).

Overall, entomological indices were similar across treatment and control arms over the course of the study (Table 3). There were no significant differences detected between the intervention and control arms for any of the adult or immature Ae. aegypti indices that were measured.

308.Option: The lack of sustained insecticidal efficacy of insecticide-treated curtains (ITCs) and the emergence of deltamethrin resistance in the local Aedes aegypti population likely contributed to the failure of ITCs to reduce dengue seroconversion in the intervention arm.
Evidence: After hanging for 6 months (May 2010), a representative sample of 18 curtains was collected from randomly selected houses, according to a matrix of characteristics (6 of each colour, exposed to either sun or shade and washed 0, 1 or >1 times). Results were highly variable and ranged from 34%-100% mortality, with 8 of the curtains falling below the 80% mortality threshold, with no discernible pattern attributable to curtain color, sun exposure, or washing frequency.

After hanging for 8 months (July 2010), a further 18 ITCs were selected for testing using the same methodology. Results showed further declines in bioefficacy, with 13 of the curtains falling below the 80% mortality threshold (range: 14%-100%).
After hanging for 11 months (October 2010), the same process was repeated and all except for 1 curtain fell below the 80% mortality threshold (range: 32%-98%).

Resistance to deltamethrin was first detected during February 2011, when 24-hour mortality using the WHO bioassay dropped to 79.7% and was at a similar level (74.8%) during April 2011. Mortality fell further to 68.3% (using the CDC bottle bioassay) during August 2011.

There was a significant difference in overall seroconversion rates (seroconversion to any individual serotype or multiple serotypes; p<0.0001) between the intervention and control arms. The intervention arm had an average seroconversion rate of 50.6 per 100 person-years (PY) (CI: 29.9–71.9) and those in the control arm had an average seroconversion rate of 37.4 per 100 PY (CI: 15.2–51.7).
308.Rationale: The passage provides evidence that the insecticidal efficacy of ITCs declined over time, with many curtains falling below the 80% mortality threshold. Additionally, deltamethrin resistance emerged in the local Ae. aegypti population, leading to reduced mortality rates. The higher seroconversion rates in the intervention arm compared to the control arm suggest that ITCs failed to reduce dengue transmission effectively. These factors together support the inference that the lack of sustained efficacy and resistance contributed to the failure of ITCs.
