The study results show that the BrucellaCapt test with a titre ≥320 had a lower seroprevalence (7.3%) compared to the standard RBT (9.0%), indicating that the BrucellaCapt test is more specific and likely more sensitive in detecting true cases. This supports the conclusion that the BrucellaCapt test is superior to the standard RBT for diagnosing brucellosis in Uganda.

331.Option: The identification of compounds that inhibit both TGR and Prx2 in the dual-enzyme assay suggests that the most effective therapeutic strategy for schistosomiasis may involve dual-target inhibition of the parasite's redox cascade.
Evidence: The TGR/Prx work described here is the result of the first project officially accepted by the MLI in 2005. Since inhibiting either TGR or Prx can potentially lead to schistosome death [22], we chose to screen both enzymes in one assay as a reconstituted redox cascade.

Assays were performed at 25°C in 0.1 M potassium phosphate, pH 7.4, 10 mM EDTA using 100 µM NADPH. The Prx2 assay was based on the reduction of H2O2 by Prx2 in the presence of GSH measured by the reduction of the GSSG produced in a coupled assay with yeast glutathione reductase monitored by observing the decrease in A340 nm due to consumption of NADPH (ε340 nm = 6.22 mM−1 cm−1) during the first three minutes [18]. The activity of TGR was determined with 3 mM 5,5′ dithiobis(2-nitrobenzoic acid) (DTNB, Ellman's reagent) [29] following the increase in A410 nm due to the production of 2-nitro-5-thiobenzoic acid (ε412 nm = 13.6 mM−1 cm−1) [17],[18].

Target deconvolution assays: After establishing the activities of primary hits and new analogues against the screened dual-enzyme system, the compounds were further subjected to target deconvolution experiments. When tested against Prx2 in a GR-coupled hydrogen peroxide reduction assay none of the selected compounds showed activity up to the 50 µM top concentration tested (and by extension, none were active against GR, an enzyme related to TGR). Prx2 was therefore ruled out as the target of any of the actives identified in the screen. Results from the TGR assay are shown in the last column of Figure 4. The majority of active compounds demonstrated approximately the same, and in some instances improved (most notably with 7 and 8), potency against the isolated TGR as they did against the dual-enzyme system. These results not only confirm the initial findings from the screen, but also further support the hypothesis of TGR being the sole target of these actives.
331.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that TGR was identified as the sole target of the active compounds, and that Prx2 was ruled out as a target. The dual-enzyme assay was used to increase screening robustness, but the results indicate that TGR inhibition alone is sufficient for activity. The claim that dual-target inhibition is the most effective strategy is not supported by the evidence, which instead shows that TGR is the primary target.

331.Option: The successful identification of potent inhibitors of TGR, combined with the lack of inhibition of human GR, suggests that these compounds may be used as broad-spectrum antiparasitic agents effective against multiple neglected tropical diseases.
Evidence: In contrast, humans possess two distinct enzymes, glutathione reductase (GR) and thioredoxin reductase (TrxR), which specifically recognize GSH and Trx as substrates, respectively [16]. The apparent replacement of two human enzymes by one dual-specificity worm enzyme has created a metabolic and regulatory bottleneck in which the inactivation of a single target, TGR, might have an enhanced deleterious effect on both the maintenance of parasite's redox balance and on its “antioxidant firewall”.

Target deconvolution assays: ... Results from the TGR assay are shown in the last column of Figure 4. The majority of active compounds demonstrated approximately the same, and in some instances improved (most notably with 7 and 8), potency against the isolated TGR as they did against the dual-enzyme system. These results not only confirm the initial findings from the screen, but also further support the hypothesis of TGR being the sole target of these actives.

The TGR/Prx work described here is the result of the first project officially accepted by the MLI in 2005. Since inhibiting either TGR or Prx can potentially lead to schistosome death [22], we chose to screen both enzymes in one assay as a reconstituted redox cascade.
331.Rationale: The passage establishes that TGR is a unique target in S. mansoni compared to human enzymes, but it does not provide evidence that these compounds are effective against other neglected tropical diseases. The claim about broad-spectrum antiparasitic activity requires extrapolation beyond the evidence presented in the passage, which is limited to schistosomiasis. The lack of inhibition of human GR supports selectivity for the parasite enzyme, but does not imply efficacy against other parasites.

331.Option: The identification of several novel structural series of TGR inhibitors, several of which are highly potent, provides both mechanistic tools for probing the redox balance in S. mansoni and starting points for developing medicinal leads for much-needed new treatments for schistosomiasis.
Evidence: Analysis of the qHTS results revealed 39 actives characterized by full concentration-response curves and IC50 values of better than 10 µM. After exclusion of antimony-containing compounds, as well as various mercury- and other heavy metal-containing molecules, the following series and singletons were selected for further studies after SAR analysis (Figure 4): oxadiazole 2-oxides (5 actives out of 29 analogues in collection, IC50 potency range between 8 µM and inactive), phosphinic amides (two compounds in the collection, one inactive and one active at 37 nM), phosphoramidite (singleton active, IC50 of 560 nM), and isoxazolone (singleton active, IC50 of 530 nM). In addition, a weaker series, quinolinyl sulfonamides (8 actives out of 47 total analogues in collection, IC50 potency range between 0.6 µM and inactive), was identified but noted to contain a number of both active and inactive members which were strongly fluorescent as judged by the extreme intercept values recorded during the screen. In contrast, neither the oxadiazole series nor the singleton actives exhibited any detectable autofluorescence (Figure 5).

Series expansion and confirmation: ... The comparison of qHTS results, where applicable, and re-test results from independently acquired powder samples are shown in Figure 4, first and second data columns, respectively. The overall confirmation rate was excellent with the exception of the sulfonamide series of actives, which showed wide shifts between qHTS and confirmatory assay (results not shown). The apparent lack of confirmation for this series was consistent with the aberrant fluorescent values associated with many of its members. An analogue series built around a lower-potency benzoindolone singleton (NCGC00038549, IC50 of 3.9 µM) failed to yield activity against the screening assay and against both TGR and Prx2 individual assays. The original activity of that singleton was therefore deemed an artifact.

Gratifyingly, the previously-untested analogues of the phosphinic amides (compounds 1–4), phosphoramidite (13–16), and isoxazolone (10–12) actives all showed activity with various degrees of potency, supporting and expanding the qHTS findings. Specifically, the “gap” in potency between the highly active 3 (NCGC00042730, qHTS IC50 of 37 nM, confirmed at 25 nM on re-test) and the inactive distant analogue 4 (NCGC00064648) was filled partially by the newly-acquired analogues 1 (NCGC00093512, IC50 of 247 nM) and 2 (NCGC00093512, IC50 of 23 µM). Similarly, increased potency was achieved by the addition of analogues to the phosphoramidite (from a singleton IC50 of 0.5 µM to a range of 0.2–2 µM) and the isoxazolone (from a singleton IC50 of 0.5 µM to a range of 0.1 µM to 9 µM).

Target deconvolution: ... The majority of active compounds demonstrated approximately the same, and in some instances improved (most notably with 7 and 8), potency against the isolated TGR as they did against the dual-enzyme system. These results not only confirm the initial findings from the screen, but also further support the hypothesis of TGR being the sole target of these actives.
331.Rationale: The passage provides direct evidence of multiple novel structural series of TGR inhibitors with varying potencies, including highly potent compounds like the phosphinic amide with an IC50 of 37 nM. The confirmation of these compounds through series expansion and target deconvolution experiments supports their potential as both mechanistic tools and medicinal leads. The passage also emphasizes the urgent need for new treatments for schistosomiasis, making this inference directly supported by the evidence presented.

332.Option: The expansion of CD4+CD25+FOXP3+ T cells in hookworm-infected individuals is primarily driven by the direct induction of FOXP3 expression in naïve T cells by excretory-secretory products of nematodes.
Evidence: Regulatory T cells were identified by flow cytometry as CD4+ T cells expressing both CD25 and FOXP3 marker (Figure 1A) and are reported as frequency (Figure 1B) and absolute numbers of cells per mm3 (Figure 1C). Analysis of PBMCs from Necator-infected individuals showed a significant increase in frequency (p<0.0001, Figure 1C) and absolute numbers (p = 0.0018, Figure 1B) of circulating CD4+CD25+FOXP3+ T cells (21.4±15.4%, 477.4±131.8 cells/mm3) when compared with non-infected naive individuals (2.3±0.6%, 78.9±11.2 cells/mm3).

The study was conducted in areas endemic for N. americanus in the Northeast Minas Gerais State, Brazil. Ten volunteers between the ages of 18 and 76 were recruited over the course of two months (Table 1). These volunteers reside in areas of moderate N. americanus transmission and presented with low to moderate (up to 872 epg) intensity of Necator infection.
332.Rationale: The passage provides evidence of increased CD4+CD25+FOXP3+ T cell frequency and absolute numbers in infected individuals, but does not establish the mechanism by which this expansion occurs. The option claims the expansion is 'primarily driven by the direct induction of FOXP3 expression in naïve T cells by excretory-secretory products of nematodes,' but the passage does not provide evidence for this specific mechanism. The study mentions excretory-secretory products in antigen stimulation experiments but does not directly link them to FOXP3 induction in naïve T cells as the primary driver of Treg expansion.

332.Option: The absence of changes in T cell numbers or Treg counts in human primo-infection with N. americanus suggests that Treg-mediated immunosuppression is not a primary mechanism of hookworm survival in early infection.
Evidence: The study was conducted in areas endemic for N. americanus in the Northeast Minas Gerais State, Brazil. Ten volunteers between the ages of 18 and 76 were recruited over the course of two months (Table 1). These volunteers reside in areas of moderate N. americanus transmission and presented with low to moderate (up to 872 epg) intensity of Necator infection.

Regulatory T cells were identified by flow cytometry as CD4+ T cells expressing both CD25 and FOXP3 marker (Figure 1A) and are reported as frequency (Figure 1B) and absolute numbers of cells per mm3 (Figure 1C). Analysis of PBMCs from Necator-infected individuals showed a significant increase in frequency (p<0.0001, Figure 1C) and absolute numbers (p = 0.0018, Figure 1B) of circulating CD4+CD25+FOXP3+ T cells (21.4±15.4%, 477.4±131.8 cells/mm3) when compared with non-infected naive individuals (2.3±0.6%, 78.9±11.2 cells/mm3).
332.Rationale: The passage does not provide information about T cell numbers or Treg counts in human primo-infection (first infection) with N. americanus. The study focuses on individuals with chronic infection (as indicated by the presence of low to moderate intensity Necator infection) rather than primo-infection. Therefore, the option's claim about primo-infection cannot be evaluated based on the evidence provided in the passage.

332.Option: The reduction in CD4+CD25+FOXP3+IL-17+ cells after hookworm antigen stimulation indicates that IL-17 production is directly suppressed by parasite antigens, leading to a permanent shift toward a regulatory phenotype.
Evidence: In order to determine the possible effect of hookworm antigens on the expression of cell surface markers (CTLA-4 and GITR) and cytokines (IL-17, TGF-β, and IL-10) by CD4+CD25+FOXP3+ regulatory T cells in N. americanus-infected donors, whole blood cultures were stimulated with either hookworm crude antigen or ES products. When crude antigen was added to the in vitro cultures, the percentage and the absolute counts of CD4+CD25+FOXP3+ regulatory T cells co-expressing IL-17 was significantly reduced (p<0.0030) (Figure 3). Although there was a tendency in increase on the expression of IL-17 in ES stimulated blood it was not statistically significant (p = 0.3562) (Figure 3). No differences in the proportion of cells expressing CTLA-4, GITR, TGF-β, IL-10, (Supplementary Figure S3) and median intensity of fluorescence were seen after hookworm antigen stimulation (p>0.05 for all).
332.Rationale: The passage shows that hookworm antigen stimulation reduces the percentage of CD4+CD25+FOXP3+IL-17+ cells, but it does not establish that this is a 'permanent shift toward a regulatory phenotype.' The option's claim about a 'permanent shift' is not supported by the evidence, which only describes an in vitro effect of antigen stimulation. Additionally, the passage does not provide evidence that IL-17 production is 'directly suppressed by parasite antigens' as the mechanism for this reduction.

332.Option: Hookworm-induced Treg activity may be able to control and divert selective proliferative and cytokine responses to numerous disorders, such as intestinal inflammation, airways inflammation/hyper-reactivity, diabetes, and multiple sclerosis.
Evidence: CD4+CD25+ Treg cells modulates antigen-specific PBMC proliferation in N. americanus-infected individuals. Chronic human N. americanus infection is classically associated with a profound ablation of cell proliferation. In order to determine the possible effect of Treg cells on the immune response during hookworm infection, functional assays were designed to evaluate whether CD4+CD25+FOXP3+ Treg cells could modulate the in vitro cellular proliferation of CD4+ and CD8+ lymphocytes after parasite antigen stimulation. Hookworm antigen-stimulated PBMCs from infected individuals showed a naturally impaired proliferative response, which was not further suppressed by co-incubation with Tregs (data not shown). However, in vitro cultures, where Tregs cells were depleted (dPBMC), showed a significant increase on the CD4+ cell proliferative response induced by crude (p = 0.0039) and ES (p = 0.0012) hookworm antigenic stimulation (Figure 4A). Interestingly, depletion of Treg cells significantly augmented the proliferation of CD8+ PBMCs of infected donors in response to hookworm ES products (p = 0.0039), but not to crude antigen (Figure 4B). The depletion of Tregs elicited the increase of IL-2 and lower levels of IL-10 in supernatants of dPBMCs with or without antigenic stimulation although statistical significance was not achieved (Supplementary Figure S4). No differences were also observed for IFN-γ and IL-5 (Supplementary Figure S4) after CD4+CD25+ depletion. These results suggest that T regulatory cells do have the capacity to modulate the in vitro proliferative response in hookworm-infected patients.

The discovery of regulatory T lymphocytes (Treg) that are actively involved in maintaining immune tolerance has recently led to new insights into mechanisms of tolerance breakdown and/or immunoregulation in human diseases, including those resulting from allergic, autoimmune, or infectious causes [14]. These cells have been shown to suppress cellular immune responses through direct contact with immune effector cells and by the production of regulatory cytokines, including TGF-β and IL-10 [15], [16].
332.Rationale: The passage provides evidence that Treg cells modulate immune responses in hookworm-infected individuals by suppressing antigen-specific proliferation. This is demonstrated by the significant increase in CD4+ and CD8+ cell proliferation when Tregs are depleted. The passage also notes that Tregs are known to suppress immune responses in various diseases, including allergic, autoimmune, and infectious conditions. This supports the inference that hookworm-induced Treg activity could have broader immunological impacts beyond hookworm infection, potentially controlling or diverting immune responses in other disorders.

332.Option: The increased expression of GITR and CTLA-4 on Treg cells in hookworm-infected individuals is sufficient to fully account for the observed suppression of antigen-specific proliferation, independent of cytokine production.
Evidence: Specific peripheral blood subpopulations of CD4+CD25+FOXP3+ T cells are elevated in N. americanus-infected individuals. Once observed the elevated number of Treg cells in the peripheral blood of hookworm infected donors, we further characterized this cell population by evaluating the expression of molecules and cytokines associated with cell modulation. Surface expression of the GITR molecule and intracellular expression of CTLA-4, IL-10, TGF-β and IL-17 cytokines, were assessed by flow cytometry. Infection of N. americanus significantly increased the proportion of cells expressing CTLA-4 (p = 0.0002) and GITR (p<0.0001). Flow cytometric analysis also showed a significant augmentation of CD4+CD25+FOXP3+ cells producing IL-10 (p<0.0001), TGF-β (p<0.0001) and IL-17 (p = 0.0003) in hookworm infected individuals (Figure 2). Similar results were found when frequency of cells were analyzed (Supplementary Figure S2).

CD4+CD25+ Treg cells modulates antigen-specific PBMC proliferation in N. americanus-infected individuals. Hookworm antigen-stimulated PBMCs from infected individuals showed a naturally impaired proliferative response, which was not further suppressed by co-incubation with Tregs (data not shown). However, in vitro cultures, where Tregs cells were depleted (dPBMC), showed a significant increase on the CD4+ cell proliferative response induced by crude (p = 0.0039) and ES (p = 0.0012) hookworm antigenic stimulation (Figure 4A). Interestingly, depletion of Treg cells significantly augmented the proliferation of CD8+ PBMCs of infected donors in response to hookworm ES products (p = 0.0039), but not to crude antigen (Figure 4B). The depletion of Tregs elicited the increase of IL-2 and lower levels of IL-10 in supernatants of dPBMCs with or without antigenic stimulation although statistical significance was not achieved (Supplementary Figure S4). No differences were also observed for IFN-γ and IL-5 (Supplementary Figure S4) after CD4+CD25+ depletion.
332.Rationale: The passage shows that Treg cells in hookworm-infected individuals express increased levels of GITR and CTLA-4, as well as cytokines like IL-10 and TGF-β. The suppression of antigen-specific proliferation is observed, but the option claims this is 'sufficient to fully account for the observed suppression' and 'independent of cytokine production.' However, the passage demonstrates that cytokine production (e.g., IL-10 and TGF-β) is also increased in Treg cells and that depletion of Tregs leads to increased proliferation and changes in cytokine levels (e.g., increased IL-2 and decreased IL-10). This indicates that cytokine production is a significant component of the suppression mechanism, contradicting the option's claim of independence from cytokine production.

333.Option: In endemic areas of intestinal schistosomiasis with low-intensity infections, the actual prevalence can be underestimated by up to 4.7 times when measured by the recommended standard procedure.
Evidence: The reference standard was used to evaluate the efficacy of each of the diagnostic methods tested. For the fecal techniques, the best performance was obtained with the modified Helmintex method, which identified schistosome eggs in feces of 88 individuals (40.4% of prevalence). This parasitological method showed a high sensitivity (86.6%) and the highest degree of concordance in relation to the reference standard (kappa = 0.84) (Table 3). The analysis also demonstrated that the sensitivity of the KK method increased from 41.4% with two slides from one fecal sample to up to 66.7% with six slides from three fecal samples. In comparison, if only one fecal sample was processed, the sensitivity remained around 60%, even when the number of examined slides was increased to 12 or 14 (Table 3 and Fig 3). The improved performance of the KK method due to an increased number of examined slides (14 slides) or increased sampling effort (three fecal samples) is shown in Fig 3.

Using the reference standard, we found that children and young adults (11–20 years of age) had the highest prevalence (55%) for S. mansoni infection. In contrast, the prevalence was reduced to less than 50% in the other age groups, being further reduced in the elderly (older than 60 years of age). Importantly, the prevalence found in each age group, considering the combination of all parasitological exams (reference standard), was 1.7 to 4.7 times higher than the prevalence obtained with the recommended two KK slides from one fecal sample (Fig 4).
333.Rationale: The passage provides direct evidence that the standard diagnostic method (two KK slides from one fecal sample) significantly underestimates the true prevalence of intestinal schistosomiasis in this community. The reference standard, which combined multiple more sensitive methods, found that the prevalence in each age group was 1.7 to 4.7 times higher than what was detected by the standard method. This directly supports the inference that the standard procedure underestimates prevalence by up to 4.7 times in this context of low-intensity infections following long-term control interventions.

334.Option: The hyaluronidase from Loxosceles venom is essential for the activation of phospholipase-D, thereby enabling the toxin to exert its dermonecrotic effects.
Evidence: The molecular mechanism by which brown spider venom induces gravitational spreading of skin lesions and systemic involvement is not fully understood.
A fundamental requirement for venom to induce local spreading of lesions and systemic involvement is the presence of venom components that are able to degrade tissue barriers.
The delivery of venom toxins to neighboring bite sites and into systemic circulation is assisted by molecules that degrade extracellular matrix constituents such as proteases and hyaluronidases.
334.Rationale: The passage does not provide evidence that hyaluronidase activates phospholipase-D. While phospholipase-D is mentioned as a venom component, the passage only describes hyaluronidase as a spreading factor that facilitates toxin diffusion, not as an activator of other toxins. The claim about activation requires a causal relationship not established in the passage.

334.Option: The hyaluronidase from Loxosceles venom contributes to dermonecrotic injury by directly degrading collagen fibers and disrupting blood vessel walls in the dermis.
Evidence: Histologically, the lesion is reported as a collection of inflammatory cells in and around the blood vessels and diffusely distributed in the dermis. It is also possible to observe degeneration of blood vessel walls, disorganization of collagen fibers with edema, hemorrhage into the dermis, necrosis of cells, and destruction of tissue structures.

The idea that brown spider venom hyaluronidases play a role in the gravitational spreading of dermonecrosis and/or systemic diffusion of venom toxins, and then act as “spreading factors” comes from its degradative activity on hyaluronic acid and other glycosaminoglycans that mediate tissue integrity and stability.

By degrading glycosaminoglycans, the hyaluronidase reduces the viscosity of hyaluronic acid and renders the extracellular matrix less rigid. This change makes the matrix more permeable to other toxins and facilitates the spread of other venom constituents and inflammatory cell mediators.
334.Rationale: The passage attributes collagen fiber degradation and blood vessel wall disruption to the overall dermonecrotic process, not specifically to hyaluronidase. Hyaluronidase is described as acting on hyaluronic acid and glycosaminoglycans, not directly on collagen or blood vessels. The option misrepresents the mechanism by which hyaluronidase contributes to dermonecrosis.

334.Option: The hyaluronidase from Loxosceles venom acts as a spreading factor that enhances the diffusion of other venom toxins, such as phospholipase-D, through the extracellular matrix, thereby increasing the area of dermonecrotic lesions and promoting gravitational spreading of skin injury.
Evidence: The idea that brown spider venom hyaluronidases play a role in the gravitational spreading of dermonecrosis and/or systemic diffusion of venom toxins, and then act as “spreading factors” comes from its degradative activity on hyaluronic acid and other glycosaminoglycans that mediate tissue integrity and stability.

By degrading glycosaminoglycans, the hyaluronidase reduces the viscosity of hyaluronic acid and renders the extracellular matrix less rigid. This change makes the matrix more permeable to other toxins and facilitates the spread of other venom constituents and inflammatory cell mediators.

Injections of L. intermedia crude venom, recombinant dermonecrotic toxin [33] (positive controls), and dermonecrotic toxin plus Dietrich's Hyaluronidase all induced macroscopic erythema, ecchymosis and dermonecrosis in rabbit skin, which was followed by 3, 6 and 24 h of observation (Fig. 6A and Fig. 6B).

The length of dermonecrosis lesions in the rabbit skin of Dietrich's Hyaluronidase concomitantly injected with dermonecrotic toxin was substantially larger than for dermonecrotic toxin alone after 24 h (Fig. 6B).
The macroscopic gravitational spreading and edema induced by the two recombinant enzymes injected together were very similar to whole venom (Fig. 6C).
334.Rationale: The passage explicitly describes hyaluronidase as a 'spreading factor' that degrades hyaluronic acid and glycosaminoglycans, making the extracellular matrix more permeable to other toxins. Experimental evidence shows that when hyaluronidase is combined with dermonecrotic toxin, the lesions are larger and exhibit gravitational spreading similar to whole venom. This directly supports the option's claim about hyaluronidase enhancing toxin diffusion and lesion progression.

334.Option: The hyaluronidase from Loxosceles venom functions as a major allergen that triggers anaphylactic reactions in susceptible individuals, contributing to the systemic symptoms observed in loxoscelism.
Evidence: Systemic involvement has also been reported including fever, malaise, weakness, nausea, vomiting and in severe cases, intravascular coagulation, hemolysis and acute renal disturbance.
The molecular mechanism by which brown spider venom induces gravitational spreading of skin lesions and systemic involvement is not fully understood.
334.Rationale: The passage does not describe hyaluronidase as an allergen or link it to anaphylactic reactions. Systemic symptoms are mentioned but not attributed to hyaluronidase. The option introduces a mechanism (allergen-mediated anaphylaxis) that is not supported by the evidence in the passage.

334.Option: The hyaluronidase from Loxosceles venom is primarily responsible for the direct induction of dermonecrotic lesions and systemic toxicity in brown spider bites.
Evidence: The idea that brown spider venom hyaluronidases play a role in the gravitational spreading of dermonecrosis and/or systemic diffusion of venom toxins, and then act as “spreading factors” comes from its degradative activity on hyaluronic acid and other glycosaminoglycans that mediate tissue integrity and stability.

Injections of L. intermedia crude venom, recombinant dermonecrotic toxin [33] (positive controls), and dermonecrotic toxin plus Dietrich's Hyaluronidase all induced macroscopic erythema, ecchymosis and dermonecrosis in rabbit skin, which was followed by 3, 6 and 24 h of observation (Fig. 6A and Fig. 6B).

The length of dermonecrosis lesions in the rabbit skin of Dietrich's Hyaluronidase concomitantly injected with dermonecrotic toxin was substantially larger than for dermonecrotic toxin alone after 24 h (Fig. 6B).
334.Rationale: The passage shows that hyaluronidase alone does not induce dermonecrotic lesions (negative controls showed no lesions). Dermonecrosis requires the presence of other toxins like phospholipase-D. Hyaluronidase's role is to facilitate the spread of these toxins, not to directly induce lesions. The option incorrectly states that hyaluronidase is 'primarily responsible' for direct induction.

335.Option: The higher odds of cysticercosis in breeding female pigs compared to males indicates that reproductive status directly increases susceptibility to Taenia spp. infection.
Evidence: The final model for presence of Taenia spp. antigen in pigs (Table 5) indicated that breeding sows had significantly higher odds of antigen presence compared to male pigs (OR = 10.35, p = 0.01), and flooding agricultural land and grassland demonstrated a positive association with the outcome (OR = 1.04, p = 0.004).
335.Rationale: The passage states that breeding sows had significantly higher odds of antigen presence compared to male pigs, but it does not provide evidence that reproductive status directly increases susceptibility to Taenia spp. infection. The observed association could be due to other factors such as differences in behavior, management practices, or exposure to contaminated environments. The passage does not establish a causal relationship between reproductive status and infection risk, so this inference cannot be drawn from the evidence provided.

335.Option: Environmental factors, including land cover and precipitation, influence the spatial distribution of Taenia spp. infections in humans and pigs, with flooding agricultural and grassland areas showing a non-linear association with cysticercosis risk.
Evidence: The majority of environmental covariates, when included in a univariable model as non-linear terms, did not significantly improve model fit in comparison to inclusion as linear terms (Tables A and B in S3 File). A non-linear relationship (quadratic) was indicated between the percentage of land that was flooding agricultural and grassland, and presence of Taenia spp. antigen in humans (p = 0.04). Thus, subsequent analysis of human data included the percentage of land that was flooding agricultural and grassland as a quadratic term.
Precipitation was negatively associated with the outcome (OR = 0.998, p = 0.02).

The final model for presence of Taenia spp. antigen in pigs (Table 5) indicated that breeding sows had significantly higher odds of antigen presence compared to male pigs (OR = 10.35, p = 0.01), and flooding agricultural land and grassland demonstrated a positive association with the outcome (OR = 1.04, p = 0.004).
335.Rationale: The passage provides evidence that environmental factors, including land cover and precipitation, influence the spatial distribution of Taenia spp. infections in humans and pigs. Specifically, it mentions a non-linear association between the percentage of land that was flooding agricultural and grassland and the presence of Taenia spp. antigen in humans, as well as a positive association between flooding agricultural land and grassland and the presence of Taenia spp. antigen in pigs. Additionally, precipitation was negatively associated with the outcome in humans. These findings support the inference that environmental factors play a role in the spatial distribution of Taenia spp. infections.

335.Option: The spatial clustering of cysticercosis cases in households near tapeworm carriers is the primary driver of infection risk, overshadowing the influence of environmental factors.
Evidence: Spatial analyses have demonstrated significant clustering of taeniasis, porcine cysticercosis and human cysticercosis, with evidence of aggregation of human and porcine cysticercosis cases within close proximity to human tapeworm carriers [15–18].

The influence of socio-economic, behavioural and environmental variables was assessed for human and porcine cysticercosis. Evidence for the influence of environmental factors on the distribution of Taenia spp. infections may provide the basis for further epidemiological research, to support the development and targeting of control programmes.
335.Rationale: The passage acknowledges the spatial clustering of cysticercosis cases near tapeworm carriers but also emphasizes the need to assess the influence of environmental factors. The study aimed to evaluate the combined effects of localized transmission cycles and environmental conditions on the spatial distribution of cysticercosis. The results show that environmental factors, such as land cover and precipitation, do influence the distribution of Taenia spp. infections. Therefore, the inference that spatial clustering near tapeworm carriers is the primary driver, overshadowing environmental factors, is not supported by the evidence provided.

335.Option: The lack of correlation between microscopy and copro-antigen ELISA results in this study suggests that the copro-antigen ELISA is unreliable for detecting taeniasis, and thus, the observed spatial patterns in cysticercosis may be influenced by diagnostic inaccuracies.
Evidence: The correlation between the two diagnostic methods for taeniasis (microscopy and copro-antigen ELISA) was zero: four participants were positive for taeniasis via microscopy only, 397 were positive for taeniasis via copro-antigen detection only and none were positive using both methods.

The prevalence of taeniasis, based on direct observation of Taenia spp. eggs (i.e. using only the microscopy results), was 0.2% (95% confidence interval [CI]: 0.05%–0.5%, note that this includes both T. saginata and T. solium and the methods used cannot differentiate between them). Based on the lack of correlation between the two diagnostic methods, and unexpectedly high number of positive results, the taeniasis results were not used in further statistical analyses as a precautionary measure.
335.Rationale: The passage states that the correlation between the two diagnostic methods for taeniasis was zero, but it does not suggest that the copro-antigen ELISA is unreliable for detecting taeniasis. Instead, the lack of correlation and the unexpectedly high number of positive results led to the decision not to use the taeniasis results in further statistical analyses as a precautionary measure. The study focused on cysticercosis, and the diagnostic methods for cysticercosis (HP10 antigen ELISA) were used to detect the presence of Taenia spp. antigen. Therefore, the observed spatial patterns in cysticercosis are not influenced by diagnostic inaccuracies related to taeniasis detection.

335.Option: The negative association between precipitation and cysticercosis risk suggests that rainfall reduces environmental contamination, thereby decreasing transmission of Taenia spp. eggs.
Evidence: Precipitation was negatively associated with the outcome (OR = 0.998, p = 0.02).

Egg survival is influenced by temperature and humidity, with tropical regions being particularly suitable for transmission [10]. Surface moisture and humidity are thought to be the main constraining factors for Taenia spp. eggs in the environment: the eggs are vulnerable to desiccation and survival is greatly reduced under dry conditions, regardless of temperature [11].
335.Rationale: The passage provides evidence that precipitation was negatively associated with the presence of Taenia spp. antigen in humans. This negative association, combined with the information that Taenia spp. eggs are vulnerable to desiccation and survival is greatly reduced under dry conditions, supports the inference that rainfall reduces environmental contamination, thereby decreasing transmission of Taenia spp. eggs. The negative association between precipitation and cysticercosis risk suggests that higher rainfall may lead to lower infection rates, possibly due to reduced egg survival in the environment.

336.Option: The high confidence in domestically-made vaccines and the preference for them over foreign-made vaccines indicate that public trust in China’s vaccine regulatory system has been fully restored after past scandals.
Evidence: The vast majority of the study participants reported that they were confident (48.7%, 95%CI 47.0 to 50.3) or completely confident (46.1%, 95%CI 44.5 to 47.8) in domestically-made COVID-19 vaccines.
In contrast, 20.4% (95%CI 19.1 to 21.8) reported that they were completely confident and 57.4% (95%CI 55.8 to 59.1) reported that they were confident in foreign-made vaccines.

Findings on the preference of domestically-made over foreign-made COVID-19 vaccines revealed that 64.2% (95%CI 62.6 to 65.8) reported a preference for domestically-made and 11.9% (95%CI 10.8 to 13.0) preferred foreign-made COVID-19 vaccines.
336.Rationale: The passage provides evidence of high confidence in domestically-made vaccines compared to foreign-made vaccines, but it does not state that public trust in China’s vaccine regulatory system has been 'fully restored' after past scandals. The data shows a preference for domestic vaccines, but this does not necessarily imply full restoration of trust in the regulatory system. The passage explicitly mentions that vaccine hesitancy and distrust in the country’s immunization program still exist due to past vaccine scandals and reports of serious side effects. The conclusion that trust has been 'fully restored' is an overinterpretation of the evidence presented.

336.Option: The perception of susceptibility to COVID-19 was not a significant predictor of vaccination intention, indicating that public health campaigns should focus on increasing fear of infection to drive vaccine uptake.
Evidence: The participants had low perceptions of susceptibility. The majority disagreed that there was a great chance of getting COVID-19 in the next few months (68.7%), were also not worried about the likelihood of getting COVID-19 (56.8%), and disagreed that it was currently possible that they would get COVID-19 (72.0%).

Most of the constructs in the HBM model were significantly associated with having a definite intention for COVID-19 vaccination in the univariate analysis. Perception that vaccination decreases the chances of getting COVID-19 under the perceived benefit construct (OR = 3.14, 95% CI 2.05–4.83) was the strongest predictor for a definite intention.
336.Rationale: The passage states that perception of susceptibility was low among participants, but it does not claim that perception of susceptibility was not a significant predictor of vaccination intention. In fact, the passage indicates that most constructs in the HBM model, including perceived susceptibility, were significantly associated with vaccination intention in univariate analysis. The conclusion that public health campaigns should focus on increasing fear of infection is not supported by the passage, as the data does not establish that perception of susceptibility was not a significant predictor in the multivariable analysis.

336.Option: The significant positive moderating effect of income level on the relationship between perceived susceptibility, severity, and cues to action with WTP suggests that public health interventions should prioritize higher-income groups to maximize vaccine uptake.
Evidence: The results of the moderating effect of income level on the relationship between the constructs of HBM and WTP are shown in Table 4. A significant positive moderating effect of income level on the relationship perceived susceptibility and WTP was found (β = 3.007, p = 0.045). Income level was also found significantly moderated the relationship between perceived severity and WTP (β = 3.980, p = 0.026). This moderation effect of income level was not significant for the relationship between perceived benefit (β = 5.597, p = 0.064) and WTP. The level of income also had no moderating effect on the relationship between barriers (β = 0.743, p = 0.551) and WTP. Income level showed a strong statistically significant moderating effect on the relationship between cues to action (β = 0.313, p = 0.551) and WTP.
336.Rationale: The passage states that income level had a significant positive moderating effect on the relationship between perceived susceptibility, severity, and cues to action with WTP. However, it does not suggest that public health interventions should prioritize higher-income groups to maximize vaccine uptake. The conclusion that interventions should prioritize higher-income groups is not supported by the evidence, as the passage does not provide data on vaccine uptake by income level or suggest that higher-income groups have higher vaccination rates. The moderating effect on WTP does not necessarily translate to higher vaccine uptake among higher-income groups.

336.Option: The fact that only 28.7% of respondents reported a definite intention to vaccinate suggests that the majority of the population in China is unwilling to accept a COVID-19 vaccine, necessitating mandatory vaccination policies.
Evidence: On the whole, a total of 2,950 (83.5%; 95%CI 82.3–84.8) participants responded yes to COVID-19 vaccine intent, while only 582 (16.4%; 95%CI 15.2–17.7) responded no. By a more specific breakdown, the majority responded probably yes (54.8%; 95% CI 53.2–56.5) followed by definitely yes (28.7%; 95% CI 27.3–30.3%). Only 4.5% (95% CI 3.9–5.3) responded definitely no and 11.9% (95% CI 10.8–13.9) reported probably no.
336.Rationale: The passage states that 83.5% of respondents reported vaccine intent, with 28.7% reporting a definite intention. The conclusion that the majority of the population is unwilling to accept a vaccine is incorrect, as the majority (83.5%) expressed some level of vaccine intent. The suggestion that mandatory vaccination policies are necessary is not supported by the evidence, as the data does not indicate widespread unwillingness to accept the vaccine. The passage also does not discuss the feasibility or necessity of mandatory vaccination policies.

336.Option: Despite concerns about the safety and efficacy of the vaccine, the perception of benefits and the provision of adequate information were strong predictors of a definite intention to receive the COVID-19 vaccine.
Evidence: Under the perceived barriers construct, concerns about the COVID-19 vaccine being faulty/fake and affordability were reported by 82.0% and 75.1%, respectively. Concerns about safety (72.6%) and efficacy (71.2%) were also notable.

Most of the constructs in the HBM model were significantly associated with having a definite intention for COVID-19 vaccination in the univariate analysis. Perception that vaccination decreases the chances of getting COVID-19 under the perceived benefit construct (OR = 3.14, 95% CI 2.05–4.83) was the strongest predictor for a definite intention.
Participants who disagreed with taking the vaccine unless it had been taken by many in the public were associated with having a higher definite intention to vaccinate (OR = 1.88, 95% CI 1.52–2.33).
336.Rationale: The passage provides evidence that concerns about safety and efficacy were notable among participants, but it also shows that perception of benefits and cues to action (such as the vaccine being taken by many in the public) were strong predictors of definite vaccination intention. The data supports the conclusion that despite concerns, the perception of benefits and adequate information are key drivers of vaccine acceptance. This inference is directly supported by the statistical analysis presented in the passage.

337.Option: The multivariate regression model incorporating HI and socioeconomic/climatic variables is superior to simulation models in predicting DHF incidence, suggesting that real-world data can fully capture the dynamics of antibody-dependent enhancement.
Evidence: Although incorporation of socioeconomic/variables improved the goodness of fit, this multivariate predictive model still failed to reproduce the very wide variation in the observed incidence (compare Figure 2B vs Figure 5).

The goodness of fit in predicting incidence by R0 showed remarkable differences between simulation with cross-immunity and those without cross-immunity (Figure 8). The predictability was much worse in simulation with cross-immunity than in other simulations.
337.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that the multivariate regression model failed to reproduce the wide variation in observed incidence and that the predictability was worse in simulations with cross-immunity. This directly contradicts the claim that the multivariate model is superior to simulation models. Furthermore, the passage does not support the assertion that real-world data can 'fully capture' ADE dynamics, as the simulations with cross-immunity (which model ADE) showed different predictability patterns.

337.Option: The observed non-monotonic relationship between DHF incidence and HI is primarily driven by age-dependent differences in the probability of manifesting severe illness, independent of cross-serotype immunity.
Evidence: The present study aims to provide an empirical example of this non-monotonic relationship between the incidence of DHF and transmission intensity, with transmission intensity represented by vector abundance.

A mathematical model recently predicted that, due to the presence of transient cross-serotype immunity, the incidence of DHF and transmission intensity will be correlated negatively at high transmission intensities [22].
The simulation results showed that the non-monotonic relationship in the simulations with cross-immunity (Figure 7A), while the age-dependency simulations showed a negative relationship (Figure 7E).
337.Rationale: The passage presents evidence that the non-monotonic relationship is predicted by a mathematical model incorporating cross-serotype immunity and is supported by simulation results. The simulation with age-dependency alone showed a negative relationship, not a non-monotonic one. The passage does not support the claim that the non-monotonic relationship is 'independent of cross-serotype immunity.'

337.Option: The negative response of DHF incidence to transmission intensity at areas of intense transmission has important public health implications, regardless of its underlying mechanism.
Evidence: This model hypothesized that a cross-protected individual will be seroconverted to an infecting viral serotype, while he/she is protected from manifesting severe illness. Under this assumption, which is consistent with results from experiments on monkeys [23], the individual would acquire immunity to nearly all serotypes while being cross-protected from clinical illness, at very intense transmission.
Our findings have major implications for future epidemiological surveys and dengue control programs.
The negative response of DHF incidence to transmission intensity at areas of intense transmission has important public health implications, regardless of its underlying mechanism.
337.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that the negative correlation between DHF incidence and transmission intensity at high transmission levels has important public health implications. This is supported by the model's hypothesis about cross-protection leading to reduced severe illness at high transmission intensities. The option correctly states that these implications are 'regardless of its underlying mechanism,' which aligns with the passage's focus on the practical significance of the observed relationship.

337.Option: Since HI is strongly correlated with vector abundance, reducing HI to below 30 will inevitably lead to a significant reduction in DHF incidence across all transmission settings.
Evidence: Univariate analysis of GAM revealed that the correlation between HI and incidence was positive below about HI = 30, while the correlation was negative above this HI value (Figure 2B; Figure 3).
As HI decreases from 70 to 30, for example, the log incidence would increase by 0.35 (Figure 3), which is equivalent to an increase of 40% in incidence, since exp (0.35) = 1.4.
The multivariate analysis estimated that, as HI decreases from 70 to 30, log incidence would increase by 0.6 (Figure 4A), which corresponds to an increase of 80%.
337.Rationale: The passage clearly shows that reducing HI below 30 leads to an increase in DHF incidence, not a decrease. The data demonstrates a positive correlation between HI and incidence below HI=30, meaning lower HI values are associated with higher incidence in this range. The option's claim that reducing HI to below 30 will 'inevitably lead to a significant reduction' is directly contradicted by the evidence presented.

337.Option: The high variability in DHF incidence across districts is primarily due to differences in the number of public large water wells, which serve as the main breeding sites for Aedes mosquitoes.
Evidence: Although incorporation of socioeconomic/variables improved the goodness of fit, this multivariate predictive model still failed to reproduce the very wide variation in the observed incidence (compare Figure 2B vs Figure 5).

The final regression model obtained from the multivariate GAM analysis (Table 1) is presented. Log incidence of DHF reported between 2002 to 2004 was regressed against House Index and socioeconomic/variables. The per capital number of public large wells was assigned with df = 3, while other covariates were assigned with df = 2.
337.Rationale: The passage states that even with the inclusion of socioeconomic variables (including public large water wells), the multivariate model still failed to explain the wide variation in DHF incidence. This directly contradicts the claim that the variability is 'primarily due to' public large water wells. The passage does not support the assertion that these wells are the 'main breeding sites' for Aedes mosquitoes in this context.

338.Option: The 'valley of death' in diagnostic development is primarily caused by insufficient funding for large-scale prospective studies, which prevents promising early-phase candidates from being validated in real-world settings.
Evidence: Eighty-six out of the 96 tests (89%) were evaluated in phase I and/or II only.
Seven tests (6 antigens) in phase III are evaluated on less than 600 specimens, while 3 antigens/tests are evaluated on 601 to 5000 specimens and no antigen is evaluated on >5000 specimens.
338.Rationale: While the passage does show that most tests remain in early phases and few reach large-scale phase III studies, it does not provide any evidence about the reasons for this pattern. The option introduces the concept of 'insufficient funding' as a cause, which is not mentioned or implied in the passage. The passage only describes the distribution of studies across phases without attributing it to funding limitations. Therefore, this conclusion cannot be derived from the passage.

338.Option: The diagnostic accuracy of rK28-based immunochromatographic tests in East Africa is consistently high across multiple studies, suggesting that this antigen is a reliable candidate for widespread deployment in the region.
Evidence: RDTs with rK28 antigen have been evaluated in four studies in Sudan and Ethiopia with promising results for the test manufactured by CTK Biotech (sensitivities between 94 and 98%, and specificities between 95 and 98%) while sensitivities and specificities based on RDTs by other manufacturers and in ELISA were more variable (sensitivities between 89 and 100%, and specificities between 81 and 99%).

With rk28 having been evaluated on a relatively small number of specimens (VL suspected patients: 285), there is a risk that its performance turns out to be variable based on the population it is evaluated on.
338.Rationale: The passage states that rK28-based tests showed promising results in four studies in East Africa, but also explicitly notes that the evaluation was on a relatively small number of specimens (285) and that there is a risk of variable performance across different populations. The option claims 'consistently high diagnostic accuracy across multiple studies,' but the passage actually highlights variability in performance depending on the manufacturer and test format. Therefore, the conclusion that rK28 is a 'reliable candidate for widespread deployment' cannot be drawn from the passage.

338.Option: The diagnostic development pipeline for visceral leishmaniasis serodiagnostic tests is characterized by a high proportion of early-phase evaluations, with most candidates not advancing to large-scale prospective studies despite showing high diagnostic accuracy in initial assessments.
Evidence: Eighty-six out of the 96 tests (89%) were evaluated in phase I and/or II only.

Seventeen of the tests in phase I, 11 in phase II and 1 test in phase III (rK28 immunochromatographic test [ICT] from CTK) consistently have sensitivities above 94% and specificities above 97% in all studies analysed (green in Fig 2 and grey in Table 1).
338.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that 89% of tests remain in phase I and II, indicating a high proportion of early-phase evaluations. It also notes that 17 tests in phase I and 11 in phase II consistently meet high diagnostic accuracy thresholds. This directly supports the conclusion that many candidates with high initial accuracy do not advance to large-scale phase III studies. The pattern described in the option is clearly supported by the data presented in the passage.

338.Option: The majority of serodiagnostic tests for visceral leishmaniasis are not advanced to phase III because they fail to meet minimum diagnostic accuracy thresholds in early-phase evaluations.
Evidence: Eighty-six out of the 96 tests (89%) were evaluated in phase I and/or II only.

Seventeen of the tests in phase I, 11 in phase II and 1 test in phase III (rK28 immunochromatographic test [ICT] from CTK) consistently have sensitivities above 94% and specificities above 97% in all studies analysed (green in Fig 2 and grey in Table 1).
338.Rationale: The passage states that 89% of tests remain in phase I and II, but it also shows that 17 tests in phase I and 11 in phase II consistently meet high diagnostic accuracy thresholds. This indicates that many tests with high accuracy do not advance to phase III, not because they failed to meet thresholds, but for other reasons. The option incorrectly attributes the lack of advancement to failure to meet accuracy thresholds, which is not supported by the passage.

338.Option: The lack of standardized production methods for native antigens with carbohydrate and lipid moieties contributes to high batch-to-batch variation, which limits their potential for commercialization and widespread use in low-resource settings.
Evidence: Fifty-three antigens (59%) were expressed in E. coli or on phages or were synthetic peptides and thus do not contain eukaryotic glycosylated or lipid moieties.

The remaining 34 (38%) are divided in three categories. In 14, the nature of the antigen could not be deducted from its isolation procedure... Fifteen antigens contain both a carbohydrate and a lipid moiety as they are made from whole parasites, whole parasite lysates, the entire secretome, the parasite membrane or lipophosphoglycan (LPG).
338.Rationale: The passage discusses the characteristics of antigens, including whether they contain carbohydrate or lipid moieties, but it does not mention anything about standardized production methods or batch-to-batch variation. The option introduces a new claim about production methods that is not supported by the passage. Therefore, this conclusion cannot be derived from the passage.

339.Option: Participants who reported trying to do as many physical and social activities as possible were more likely to experience a faster recovery and return to normal functioning, suggesting that active coping strategies are clinically effective.
Evidence: Participants using emotional coping strategies went less often to a physician, used less medications and reported trying to do as many physical/social activities as possible. For example, they tried to mobilize by performing the physical exercises or movements they could do. With this in mind, people were more willing to accept that symptoms would eventually subside.
339.Rationale: The passage does not provide evidence that active coping strategies (trying physical/social activities) led to faster recovery or return to normal functioning. It only states that individuals using emotional coping strategies engaged in these activities and were more willing to accept symptoms would subside. There is no mention of clinical effectiveness or faster recovery times for these individuals.

339.Option: Since effective long-term treatments are not available yet for chronic chikungunya, the high uptake of (alternative) medicines and frequent physician visits will normally not lead to success, resulting in dissatisfaction and further emotional stress.
Evidence: Frequent physician visits and different medical treatments was a pattern of problem-focused coping, which was illustrative for people who perceived severe impairment from chikungunya, which they could hardly accept. Frequent visits of health care providers as coping strategy led to unmet expectations for cure or treatment, resulting in stress (negative impact on QoL).

Some participants did not visit a physician. These participants did not recognize visiting a physician as a problem-focused coping strategy, because they would not be treated with more than the regular symptom relief-providing medications, i.e. paracetamol and nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs.
339.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that frequent physician visits and use of alternative medicines (problem-focused coping) led to unmet expectations for cure or treatment, resulting in stress and negative impact on quality of life. This directly supports the inference that these behaviors, while not leading to successful treatment outcomes, contribute to dissatisfaction and emotional stress.

339.Option: The universal health care program in Curaçao minimized out-of-pocket expenditures and reduced the financial burden on individuals, which may have contributed to higher healthcare utilization among chikungunya patients.
Evidence: The financial impact due to chikungunya on participant’s personal situation was generally perceived as limited. In Curaçao, chikungunya patients could rely on an insurance system, which covered health care costs, which in turn minimized out-of-pocket expenditures.
339.Rationale: While the passage confirms that the insurance system minimized out-of-pocket expenditures, it does not provide evidence that this led to higher healthcare utilization. The passage only states that financial impact on individuals was limited, but does not make a causal link between the insurance system and healthcare utilization rates.

339.Option: Problem-focused coping strategies aimed at physical healing induced a high uptake of (alternative) medicines and consultation of physicians, whereas emotional coping strategies focused on acceptance of the situation were linked to lower perceived impacts on HRQoL and less uptake of medical care.
Evidence: Within the community, problem-focused coping was commonly performed by using (natural) medicines and by visiting a physician to cope with the physical impact of chikungunya.
Participants using emotional coping strategies went less often to a physician, used less medications and reported trying to do as many physical/social activities as possible.

Frequent physician visits and different medical treatments was a pattern of problem-focused coping, which was illustrative for people who perceived severe impairment from chikungunya, which they could hardly accept.
339.Rationale: The passage clearly distinguishes between problem-focused coping (using medicines and visiting physicians) and emotional coping strategies (acceptance, less medical care). It explicitly states that problem-focused coping was associated with higher use of medical care while emotional coping was associated with lower medical care utilization and lower perceived impact on HRQoL. This directly supports the inference in the option.

340.Option: The increase in the median age of hospitalized dengue cases over time indicates that younger patients are being under-treated or under-referred to hospitals.
Evidence: The median age of dengue cases remained about the same from 2007 onwards (Fig 3).
The median age among hospitalized cases increased from 38 years in 2007 to 43 years in 2017 (p < 0.0005) (Fig 3).
340.Rationale: The passage states that the median age of dengue cases remained stable from 2007 onwards, but the median age of hospitalized cases increased from 38 to 43 years over the 15-year period. This suggests a shift in the age distribution of hospitalized patients, not necessarily under-treatment or under-referral of younger patients. The passage does not provide evidence that younger patients are being under-treated or under-referred; the increase in median age of hospitalized cases could be due to other factors such as changes in admission criteria or demographic shifts in the population of dengue cases.

340.Option: The reduction in hospitalization rates for dengue cases is primarily due to improved early diagnosis and management in primary care settings, which has led to fewer patients requiring hospital admission.
Evidence: The proportion of dengue cases hospitalized plummeted from 97.3% in 2003 to an all-time low of 25.6% in 2014 (p < 0.0005).

The Singapore Ministry of Health (MOH) sent out circulars to hospitals, government primary care clinics and medical practitioners to apprise them of the dengue situation during epidemic periods, and to provide periodic updates on guidelines for the management of dengue [14–19].

In 2007, a medical expert committee established the criteria for immediate referral to hospitals... Another comprehensive set of hospital referral and admission criteria was also instituted during the 2013 dengue epidemic...
340.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that the reduction in hospitalization rates is due to the implementation of more selective admission criteria by the MOH and medical expert committees, not primarily due to improved early diagnosis and management in primary care. The passage mentions that MOH provided guidelines and criteria for hospital referral, which were updated over time. The reduction in hospitalizations is directly linked to these changes in admission criteria, not to improvements in primary care management.

340.Option: The implementation of more selective admission criteria for dengue has not led to an increase in adverse outcomes, despite a significant reduction in the proportion of cases hospitalized.
Evidence: The proportion of dengue cases hospitalized plummeted from 97.3% in 2003 to an all-time low of 25.6% in 2014 (p < 0.0005).
The proportion of deaths among hospitalized patients ranged from 0.14% in 2003 to 0.77% in 2009.
The CFR ranged from 0% in 2017 to 0.27% in 2006.
340.Rationale: The passage provides evidence that the implementation of more selective admission criteria has not led to an increase in adverse outcomes. The case fatality rate (CFR) and the proportion of deaths among hospitalized patients did not increase despite the significant reduction in hospitalization rates. This suggests that the more selective admission criteria have not compromised patient outcomes, as the CFR remained low and stable over the study period.

340.Option: The proportion of dengue cases hospitalized in the elderly aged ≥65 years has been highest since 2013, which may be attributed to both the MOH guideline to refer for hospital evaluation those aged ≥65 years as well as a shift in demographic profile of dengue cases towards older adults.
Evidence: The highest proportions were in older adults aged 45–64 years from 2003 to 2012 (range: 50.8% to 100.0%) and elderly persons aged ≥65 years from 2013 to 2017 (range: 47.2% to 55.4%).
In the majority of years after 2007, the hospitalization rate per 100,000 population was highest in the elderly ≥65 years of age.

The Singapore Ministry of Health (MOH) sent out circulars to hospitals, government primary care clinics and medical practitioners to apprise them of the dengue situation during epidemic periods, and to provide periodic updates on guidelines for the management of dengue [14–19].
340.Rationale: The passage states that the proportion of dengue cases hospitalized in the elderly aged ≥65 years has been highest since 2013. This is supported by the data showing that the hospitalization rate per 100,000 population was highest in the elderly ≥65 years of age in the majority of years after 2007. The MOH guidelines, which include criteria for referring elderly patients for hospital evaluation, likely contributed to this trend. Additionally, the demographic shift in the population of dengue cases towards older adults may also be a factor.

340.Option: The implementation of more selective admission criteria by hospitals has led to a significant reduction in hospital bed utilization, even during large dengue epidemics.
Evidence: The total number of hospital bed-years due to dengue halved from 136 in 2005 to 69 in 2013 (Fig 4).
The proportion of dengue cases hospitalized plummeted from 97.3% in 2003 to an all-time low of 25.6% in 2014 (p < 0.0005).

The Singapore Ministry of Health (MOH) sent out circulars to hospitals, government primary care clinics and medical practitioners to apprise them of the dengue situation during epidemic periods, and to provide periodic updates on guidelines for the management of dengue [14–19].
340.Rationale: The passage provides evidence that the implementation of more selective admission criteria has led to a significant reduction in hospital bed utilization. The total number of hospital bed-years due to dengue halved from 2005 to 2013, and the proportion of dengue cases hospitalized decreased significantly from 2003 to 2014. This reduction in hospitalizations is directly linked to the changes in admission criteria implemented by the MOH, which were aimed at reducing unnecessary hospital admissions.

341.Option: The decline in anti-filarial IgG4 antibody prevalence after MDA occurred more slowly than the decline in microfilaria prevalence, indicating that antibody levels persist longer after infection clearance and may reflect past exposure rather than current transmission.
Evidence: The antibody to MF ratio was 3.1 at baseline, but increased dramatically after MDA. This means MF rates fell more rapidly after MDA than antibody rates.

Antibody clearance tends to occur faster after treatment in children than in adults, since children tend to have shorter term exposure and lighter infections. Therefore, adults may have persistent antibodies years after effective therapy.
341.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that the antibody-to-MF ratio increased after MDA, meaning that microfilaria (MF) rates fell more rapidly than antibody rates. This directly supports the inference that antibody levels persist longer after infection clearance. The additional evidence about antibody clearance being slower in adults due to longer exposure further supports the idea that antibodies may reflect past exposure rather than current transmission.

341.Option: The increase in the antibody-to-MF ratio after MDA reflects a higher sensitivity of the Brugia Rapid test in detecting past infections compared to the membrane filtration method used for microfilariae.
Evidence: The antibody to MF ratio was 3.1 at baseline, but increased dramatically after MDA. This means MF rates fell more rapidly after MDA than antibody rates.
341.Rationale: The passage explains the increase in the antibody-to-MF ratio as a result of the faster decline in microfilaria rates, not as a reflection of the Brugia Rapid test's sensitivity. The passage does not compare the sensitivity of the Brugia Rapid test to the membrane filtration method. This option introduces a comparison not supported by the passage.

341.Option: The persistence of anti-filarial antibodies in children after MDA cessation indicates that transmission of B. timori was not fully interrupted in the study population.
Evidence: In 2010 the antibody rates had strongly declined. The rates varied with age (4.9% for subjects less than 21 years of age versus 8.7% for older subjects) (Fig. 3). However, this difference between the two age groups was not significant.
The prevalence (45%) was significantly higher than the baseline prevalence (34%) prior to MDA (p<0.01).
341.Rationale: The passage states that antibody rates in children declined significantly and were not significantly different from those in adults. The rebound in Ascaris prevalence is unrelated to filarial transmission. The persistence of antibodies in children is better explained by the slower clearance of antibodies in adults rather than ongoing transmission. The passage does not provide evidence that transmission of B. timori was not fully interrupted.

341.Option: The rebound in STH infection rates after MDA cessation suggests that anti-filarial antibodies may also reappear in the population if transmission resumes.
Evidence: The prevalence (45%) was significantly higher than the baseline prevalence (34%) prior to MDA (p<0.01).
Hookworm rates rebounded to 7% at 34 months after the last MDA.
341.Rationale: The rebound in STH infection rates is a separate phenomenon from the persistence of anti-filarial antibodies. The passage does not establish a direct relationship between STH rebound and anti-filarial antibody reappearance. This option makes an unsupported inference by linking two unrelated outcomes.

341.Option: The rapid decline in microfilaria prevalence following MDA suggests that the Brugia Rapid test is a more sensitive marker for active infection than for past exposure.
Evidence: The antibody to MF ratio was 3.1 at baseline, but increased dramatically after MDA. This means MF rates fell more rapidly after MDA than antibody rates.
We used the Brugia Rapid dipstick test as a marker for ongoing or prior infection, because there is no adult worm antigen detection test for Brugia spp.
341.Rationale: The passage states that the Brugia Rapid test is used as a marker for ongoing or prior infection, not specifically for active infection. The rapid decline in microfilaria prevalence is explained by the effectiveness of MDA, not by the sensitivity of the Brugia Rapid test. This option misinterprets the purpose and sensitivity of the test as described in the passage.

342.Option: The high level of amino acid variation (36.4%) in mitochondrial protein-coding genes between human-Trichuris and pig-Trichuris is consistent with recent cross-infection events between the two host species.
Evidence: The amino acid sequence differences ranged from 13.6–62.5%, with COX1 being the most conserved protein, and ATP8 the least conserved.
The magnitude of sequence variation in each gene and NCR between human-Trichuris and pig-Trichuris ranged from 24.6–47.4%.
342.Rationale: The passage states that the amino acid sequence differences between human-Trichuris and pig-Trichuris range from 13.6–62.5%, with the average variation being much higher than the 36.4% mentioned in the option. High genetic variation is more indicative of long-term evolutionary divergence rather than recent cross-infection events. Recent cross-infection would typically result in lower genetic divergence between the populations.

342.Option: The genetic similarity in the rrnL gene between human-Trichuris and pig-Trichuris suggests that they are closely related and likely represent a single, host-generalist species.
Evidence: The magnitude of sequence variation in each gene and NCR between human-Trichuris and pig-Trichuris ranged from 24.6–47.4%.

The sequence variation in part of the rrnL gene was assessed among 16 individuals of Trichuris from humans and pigs... These differences included 15 indels, 16 purine transitions (A<->G) and 19 transversion (A<->T).
342.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that the sequence variation in the rrnL gene between human-Trichuris and pig-Trichuris is significant, with 89 nucleotide differences including indels and transitions/transversions. This level of variation is inconsistent with the suggestion that they are closely related and represent a single species. The data instead support genetic divergence between the two groups.

342.Option: The substantial genetic divergence observed in mitochondrial protein-coding genes and ribosomal RNA genes between human-Trichuris and pig-Trichuris supports the hypothesis that they represent distinct species.
Evidence: The magnitude of sequence variation in each gene and NCR between human-Trichuris and pig-Trichuris ranged from 24.6–47.4%.

The sequence variation in part of the rrnL gene was assessed among 16 individuals of Trichuris from humans and pigs... These differences included 15 indels, 16 purine transitions (A<->G) and 19 transversion (A<->T).
Phylogenetic analyses of the amino acid sequence datasets... reflected the clear genetic distinctiveness between human-Trichuris and pig-Trichuris.
342.Rationale: The passage provides multiple lines of evidence supporting the genetic divergence between human-Trichuris and pig-Trichuris. The sequence variation in mitochondrial protein-coding genes and ribosomal RNA genes is substantial (24.6–47.4%), and phylogenetic analyses show clear genetic distinctiveness between the two groups. These findings support the hypothesis that they represent distinct species.

342.Option: The high A+T content and AT-rich non-coding regions in the mitochondrial genomes of human-Trichuris and pig-Trichuris indicate that these parasites have evolved a shared mechanism for mitochondrial DNA replication.
Evidence: The nucleotide composition of the entire mt genome is biased toward A and T, with T being the most favoured nucleotide and G being least favoured.
